AS  EXCELLENT   GREEN   PEAS   SOUP. 

Take  at  their  ftJlest  size,  but  before  they  are  of  bad  colour  or 
worm-eaten,  three  pints  of  fine  large  peas,  and  boil  them  as  for 
table  (see  Chapter  XYIL)  with  half  a  teaspoonful  of  carbonate  of  soda 
in  the  water,  that  they  may  be  veiy  green.  When  they  are  quite 
tender,  drain  them  well,  and  put  them  into  a  couple  of  quarts  of 
boiling,  pale,  but  good  beef  or  veal  stock,  and  stew  them  in  it  gently 
for  half  an  hour ;  then  work  the  whole  through  a  fine  hair-sieve, 
pot  it  into  a  clean  pan  and  bring  it  to  the  point  of  boiling ;  add  salt, 
should  it  be  needed,  and  a  small  teaspoonml  of  pounded  suear;  clear 
off  the  scum  entirely,  and  serve  the  soup  as  not  as  possible.  An 
elegant  variety  of  it  is  made  by  adding  a  half  pint  more  of  stock  to 
the  peas,  and  about  three  quarters  of  a  pint  of  as])aragus  points, 
boiled  apart,  and  well  drained  before  they  are  thrown  into  it,  which 
should  be  done  only  the  instant  before  it  is  sent  to  table. 

Green  peas,  3  pmts :  boiled  25  to  30  minutes,  or  more.  Veal  or 
beef  stock,  2  quarts  (with  peas)  :  i  an  hour.  Sugar,  one  small  tea- 
spoonful  ;  salt,  if  needed. 

Obs. — When  there  is  no  stock  at  hand,  four  or  five  pounds  of 
flliin  of  beef  boiled  slowly  down  with  three  quarts  of  water  to  two,  and 
well  seasoned  with  savoury  herbs,  young  carrots,  and  onions,  will 
serve  instead  quite  well.  A  thick  slice  of  lean,  undressed  ham,  or  of 
Jewish  be^  would  improve  it. 

Should  a  common  English  peas  soup  be  wished  for,  make  it  some- 
what thinner  than  the  one  above,  and  add  to  it,  just  before  it  is 
dished,  fmrn  half  to  three  quarters  of  a  pint  of  young  peas  boU^ 
tender  and  well  drained.

in  from  three  to  four  ounces  of  butter,*  three  large,  or  four  smaR

Green  peas,  1  quart ;  water,  5  pints :  cucumbers,  3  to  6 ;  lettuces,

Ob9, — We  must  repeat  that  the  peas  for  tnese  soups  must  not  be 
o2dy  as  when  they  are  so,  their  fine  sweet  flavour  is  entirelv  lost,  and 
the  dried  ones  would  have  almost  as  good  an  effect ;  nor  snould  they 
be  of  inferior  kinds.  Freshly  gathered  marrowfats,  taken  at  nearly 
or  quite  their  full  growth,  will  give  the  best  quality  of  soup.  We 
are  credibly  informed,  but  cannot  assert  it  on  our  own  authority, 
that  it  is  often  made  for  expensive  tables  in  early  spring,  with  the 
youn^  tender  plants  or  halms  of  the  peas,  when  they  are  about  a 
foot  m  height.  They  are  cut  off  close  to  the  ground,  like  small 
salad,  we  are  told,  then  boiled  and  pressed  through  a  strainer,  and 
mixed  with  the  stock.    The  flavour  is  affirmed  to  be  excellent. 

A  CHEAP  GREEN  PEAS  SOUP. 

Wash  very  clean  and  throw  into  an  equal  quantity  of  boiling 
water  salted  as  for  peas,  three  quarts  of  the  snells,  and  in  from 
twenty  to  thirty  minutes,  when  tney  vrill  be  quite  tender,  turn  the 
whole  into  a  large  strainer,  and  press  the  pods  strong^ly  with  a  wooden 
spoon.  Measure  the  liquor,  put  two  quarts  of  it  mto  a  dean  deep 
saucepan,  and  when  it  boils  add  to  it  a  quart  of  Aill  grown  peas,  two 
or  even  three  large  encumbers,  as  many  moderate-siscd  lettuces 
freed  from  the  coarser  leaves  and  cut  small,  one  large  onion  (or  more 
if  liked)  sliced  extremely  thin  and  stewed  for  half  an  hour  in  a 
morsel  of  butter  before  it  is  added  to  the  soup,  or  gently  fried  with- 
out  being  allowed  to  brown ;  a  branch  or  two  of  parsley,  and,  when 
the  flavour  is  liked,  a  dozen  leaves  of  mint.  Stew  these  softly  for  an 
hour,  with  the  addition  of  a  small  teaspoonful,  or  a  larger  quantity 
if  required  of  salt,  and  a  good  seasoning  of  fine  white  pepper  or  ok

Ohg, — The  cucumbers  should  be  pared,  quartered,  and  freed  from

ought  to  be  selected  for  the  liquor. 

RTCH   PEAS  BOUP. 

Soak  a  quart  of  fine  yellow  split  peas  for  a  night,  drain  them  well, 
and  put  them  mto  a  large  soup-pot  with  five  quarts  of  ffood  brown 
gravy  stock ;  and  when  they  have  boiled  gently  for  htSf  an  hour, 
add  to  the  soup  three  onions,  as  many  carrots,  and  a  turnip  or  two,  all 
sliced  and  fried  carefully  m  butter ;  stew  the  whole  somy  until  the 
peas  are  reduced  to  pulp,  then  add  as  much  salt  and  cayenne  as  may 
oe  needed  to  season  it  well,  give  it  two  or  three  minutes*  boil,  and 
pass  it  through  a  sieve,  pressing  the  vegetables  with  it.  Put  into  a 
dean  saucepan  as  much  as  may  be  required  for  table,  add  a  little 
fresh  stock  to  it  should  it  be  too  thick,  and  reduce  it  by  quick  boil- 
mg  if  too  thin ;  throw  in  the  white  part  of  some  fresh  celery  sliced 
a  quarter  of  an  inch  thick,  and  when  this  is  tender  send  the  soup 
ouickly  to  table  with  a  dish  of  small  fried  or  toasted  sippets.  A 
dessertspoonful  or  more  of  currie-powder  greatly  improves  peas 
ionp :  it  should  be  smoothly  mixed  with  a  few  spoonsful  of  it,  and 
soured  to  the  remainder  when  this  first  begins  to  boil  after  having 
Deen  strained. 

Split  i^eas,  1  quart :  soaked  one  night.  Grood  brown  gravy  soup, 
6  quarts:  30  minutes.  Onions  and  carrots  browned  in  butter, 
9  of  each;  turnips,  2:  2^  to  34  hours.  Cayenne  and  salt  as 
needed.  Soup,  6  pints ;  celery,  sliced,  1  large  or  2  snudl  heads :  20 
minutes. 

Obt. — When  more  convenient,  six  pounds  of  neck  of  beef  well 
Koredand  equally  and  carefully  browned,  may  be  boiled  gently  with 
the  peas  and  fried  vegetables  in  a  gallon  of  water  (which  should  be 
poured  to  them  boiling)  for  four  or  five  hours. 

COMMON  PEAS  SOUP. 

Wash  well  a  anart  of  good  split  peas,  and  float  off  such  as  remain 
m  the  surfiice  oi  the  water  ;  soak  tnem  for  one  night,  and  boil  them

with  a  bit  of  soda  the  size  of  a  filbert  in  just  sufficient  water  to  allow 
them  to  break  to  a  mash.  Put  them  into  from  three  to  four  quarts 
of  ffood  beef  broth,  and  stew  them  in  it  gently  for  an  hour ;  then 
work  the  whole  through  a  sieve,  heat  imresh  as  much  as  may  be

clear  it  perfectly  from  scum,  and  send  it  to  table  with  fried  or  toasted 
bread.  Gelenr  sliced  and  stewed  in  it  as  directed  for  the  rich  peas 
soup,  will  be  found  a  great  improvement  to  this. 

Peas,  1  quart :  soaked  1  night ;  boiled  in  2  quarts  or  rather  more 
of  water,  2  to  2^  hours.  Beef  broth,  8  to  4  quarts:  1  hour.  Salt 
and  cayenne  or  pepper  as  needed :  8  minutes. 

PEAS  SOUP   WITHOUT   MEAT. 

To  a  pint  of  peas,  freed  from  all  that  are  worm-eaten,  and  well 
washed,  put  five  pints  of  cold  water,  and  boil  them  tolerably  tender; 
then  add  a  couple  of  onions  (more  or  less  according  to  the  taste),  a 
couple  of  fine  carrots  grat^  one  large  or  two  moderate-sized 
turnips  sliced,  all  gently  fried  brown  in  butter ;  half  a  teaspoonful 
of  black  pepper,  and  three  times  as  much  of  salt.  Stew  these  softly, 
keeping  them  often  stirred,  until  the  vegetables  are  sufficiently  tender 
to  pass  through  a  sieve ;  then  rub  the  whole  through  one,  put  it  into 
a  clean  pan,  and  when  it  boils  throw  in  a  sliced  head  of  celery, 
heighten  the  seasoning  if  needful,  and  in  twenty  minutes  serve  the 
soup  as  hot  as  possible,  with  a  dish  of  fried  or  toasted  bread  cut  into 
dice.  A  little  chili  vinegar  can  be  added  when  liked :  a  larger  pro- 
portion of  vegetables  also  may  be  boiled  down  with  the  peas  at  plea- 
sure. Weak  broth,  or  the  liquor  in  which  a  joint  has  been  boiled^ 
can  be  substituted  for  the  water;  but  the  soup  is  very  palatable  as 
we  have  given  the  receipt  for  it.  Some  persons  like  it  flavoured 
with  a  little  mushroom  catsup.  All  peas  soup  is  rendered  more 
wholesome  by  the  addition  of  a  small  quantity  of  currie-paste  or 
powder. 

Split  peas,  1  pint ;  water,  6  pints :  2  hours  or  more.  Onions,  2 ; 
carrots,  2  ;  large  turnip,  1 ;  pepper,  J  teaspoonful ;  salt,  IJ  teaspoon- 
ful:  1  to  H  hour.    Celery,  1  head :  20  minutes. 

OX-TAIL   SOUP. 

An  inexpensive  and  very  nutritious  soup  may  be  made  of  ox-taOs, 
but  it  vrill  be  insipid  in  flavour  without  the  addition  of  a  little  ham, 
knuckle  of  bacon,  or  a  pound  or  two  of  other  meat.  Wash  and  soak 
three  tails,  pour  on  them  a  gallon  of  cold  water,  let  them  be  brought 
gradually  to  boil,  throw  in  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  salt,  and  clear  off 
the  scum  carefully  as  soon  as  it  forms  upon  the  surface ;  when  it 
ceases  to  rise,  add  four  moderate-sized  carrots,  from  two  to  four 
onions,  according  to  the  taste,  a  large  faggot  ot  savoury  herbs,  a  bead 
of  celery,  a  couple  of  turnips,  six  or  eight  cloves,  and  a  half-teaspoon* 
ful  of  peppercorns.    Stew  these  gently  from  three  hours  to  three  and

t  half,  if  the  tafla  be  very  large ;  lift  them  out,  strain  the  liquor,  and 
skim  off  all  the  &t ;  divide  the  tails  into  joints,  and  put  them  into  a 
couple  of  quarts  or  rather  more  of  the  stock ;  stir  in,  when  these 
begm  to  boil,  a  thickening  of  arrow-root  or  of  rice  flour  (see  page  4), 
mixed  with  as  much  cayenne  and  salt  as  may  be  required  to  £your 
the  soap  well,  and  serve  it  very  hot.  If  stewed  down  until  the  flesh 
fiiils  away  from  the  bones,  the  ox-tails  will  make  stock  which  will  be

be  a  superior  soup  to  the  above.  A  richer  one  still  may  be  made  by 
pouring  good  beef  broth  instead  of  water  to  the  meat  in  the  first  in-

Ox-tails,  3 ;  water,  1  gallon;  salt,  1}  oz. ;  carrots,  4 ;  onions,  2  to

boors.  (Ham  or  gammon  of  bacon  at  pleasure,  with  other  flavour- 
ings.) 

Obt. — ^To  increase  the  savour  of  this  soup  when  the  meat  is  not 
served  in  it,  the  onions,  turnips,  and  carrots  may  be  gently  firied  until 
of  a  fine  light  brown,  before  they  are  added  to  it. 

▲   CHEAP  AND   GOOD  STEW   SOUP. 

?at  from  four  to  five  pounds  of  the  gristly  part  of  the  shin  of  beef 
into  three  quarts  of  cold  water,  and  stew  it  very  softly  indeed,  with 
the  addition  of  the  salt  and  vegetables  directed  for  houiUon  (see  paee 
7),  until  the  whole  is  yery  tender;  lift  out  tiie  meat,  strain  the 
liiquor,  a^  put  it  into  a  large  clean  saucepan,  add  a  thickening  of 
lice-flour  or  arrowroot,  pepper  and  salt  if  needed,  and  a  tablespoonful 
iif  mushroom  catsup.  In  the  mean  time,  cut  all  the  meat  into  small, 
thick  slices,  add  it  to  the  soup,  and  serve  it  as  soon  as  it  is  very  hot. 
The  thickening  and  catsup  may  be  omitted,  and  all  the  vegetables, 
pressed  through  a  strainer,  ma^  be  stirred  into  the  soup  instead,  be- 
fise  the  meat  is  put  back  into  it. 

SOUP   IN   HASTE. 

Chop  tolerably  fine  a  pound  of  lean  beef,  mutton,  or  yeal,  and  when 
it  is  partly  done,  add  to  it  a  small  carrot  and  one  small  turnip  cut  in 
dices,  half  an  ounce  of  celery,  the  white  part  of  a  moderate-sized 
leek,  or  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of  onioiL  Mince  all  these  together,  and 

a  the  whole  into  a  deep  saucepan  with  three  pints  of  cold  water 
!n  the  soup  boils  take  off  the  scum,  and  aod  a  little  salt  and 
pepper.  In  half  an  hour  it  will  be  ready  to  serve  with  or  without 
ttnuning :  it  may  be  flavoured  at  will,  with  cayenne,  catsup,  or  aught 
else  xhSi  is  preferred,  or  it  ina^  be  conyerted  into  French  spring 
broth,  by  passing  it  through  a  sieve,  and  boiling  it  again  for  five  or 
■X  minutes,  with  a  handful  of  young  and  well  washed  sorrel. 

Meat,  1  lb.;  carrot,  2  oz. ;  turnip,  l{  oz. ;  celery,  J  oz.;  onion,  \  oa. ; 
water,  8  pints  ;  half  an  hour.    Little  pepper  and  salt.

Ohi. — ^Three  pounds  of  beef  or  mutton,  with  two  or  three  siloes  of

TEAL   OR   MUTTON   BROTH. 

To  each  pound  of  meat  add  a  quart  of  cold  water,  bring  it  gently 
to  boil,  skim  it  very  dean,  add  salt  in  the  same  proportion  as  for 
bouillon  (see  page  7),  with  spices  and  vegetables  also,  unless  us* 
^wmred  broth  be  required,  when  a  few  peppercorns,  a  blade  or  two 
of  mace,  and  a  bunch  of  savoury  herbs,  will  be  sufficient ;  though  for 
some  purposes  even  these,  with  the  exception  of  the  salt,  are  better 
omitted.  Simmer  the  broth  for  about  four  hours,  unless  the  quan« 
tity  be  very  small,  when  from  two  and  a  half  to  three,  will  be  suffi- 
cient. A  uttle  rice  boiled  down  with  the  meat  will  both  thicken  the 
broth,  and  render  it  more  nutritious.  Strain  it  off  when  done,  and 
let  it  stand  till  quite  cold  that  the  fat  may  be  entirely  cleared  from  it: 
this  is  especially  needful  when  it  is  to  be  served  to  an  invalid. 

Veal  or  mutton,  4  lbs. ;  water,  4  quarts ;  salt.  (For  vegetables,  kc^ 
see  page  7 ;)  rice  (if  used),  4  oz. :  4  hours  or  more. 

MILK  SOUP   WITH   VERMICELLI. 

Throw  into  five  pints  of  boiling  milk  a  small  quantity  of  salt,  and 
then  drop  lightly  mto  it  five  ounces  of  good  fresh  vermicelli ;  keep 
the  milk  stirred  as  this  is  added,  to  prevent  its  gathering  into  lumps, 
and  continue  to  stir  it  very  frequently  from  fifteen  to  twenty  minutea> 
or  until  it  is  perfectly  tender.  The  addition  of  a  little  pounded  sugar 
and  powdered  cinnamon  renders  this  a  very  agreeable  dish.  In  Ca- 
tholic countries,  milk  soups  of  various  kinds  constantly  supply  the 
place  of  those  made  with  meat,  on  maigre  days ;  and  with  us  they 
are  sometimes  very  acceptable,  as  ^ving  a  change  of  diet  for  the  nur- 
sery or  sick  room.  Rice,  semoulma,  sago,  cocoa-nut,  and  maccaroni 
may  all  in  turn  be  used  for  them  as  directed  for  other  soups  in  thb 
chapter,  but  they  will  be  required  in  rather  smaller  proportions  with 
the  milk. 

Milk, 5  pints ;  vermicelli,  6  oz.:  15  to 20  minutes. 

CHEAP   RICE   80UP. 

Place  a  gallon  of  water  on  the  fire  (more  or  less  according  to  the 
c^uantity  of  soup  required),  and  when  it  boils,  throw  in  a  moderate- 
Bised  tablespoonful  of  salt,  and  two  or  three  onions,  thickly  sliced, 
a  faggot  of^  sweet  herbs,  a  root  of  celery,  and  three  or  four  lAqje 
carrots  split  down  into  many  diviuons,  and  out  into  short  lengths, 
l^il  these  gently  for  an  hour  and  a  half,  or  two  hours,  and  then  strain 
the  liquor  from  them.    When  time  will  permit,  let  it  become  oold ;

then  for  each  qnart,  take  from  three  to  four  onnces  of  well  washed 
rice,  ponr  the  soup  on  it,  heat  it  very  slowly,  giving  it  an  occasional 
stir,  and  stew  it  gently  nntil  it  is  perfectly  tender,  and  the  potage 
qmte  thick.  A  moderate  seasoninff  of  pepper,  and  an  ounce  or  two 
of  fresh  butter  well  blended  with  a  teaspoonfiil  of  flour,  may  be 
thoroughly  stirred  up  with  the  soup  before  it  is  served ;  or,  in  lieu  of 
the  butter,  the  yolks  of  two  or  three  new-laid  eggs,  mixed  with  a  little 
milk,  may  be  carefully  added  to  it. 

It  may  be  more  quickly  prepared  by  suhstitnting  vermioelli,  semou- 
lina,  or  soujee  for  the  nee,  as  this  last  will  require  three  Quarters  of

thirty  minutes ;  and  two  ounces  will  thicken  sufficiently  a  quart  ol 
broth. 

A  large  tablespoonfnl  of  Captain  Whitens  currie-paste,  and  a  small 
one  of  flour,  diluted  with  a  spoonful  or  two  or  two  of  the  broth,  or 
vith  a  little  milk  or  cream,  if  perfectly  mixed  with  the  rice  and  stewed 
with  it  for  fifteen  or  twenty  minutes  before  it  is  dished,  render  it 
excellent :  few  eaters  would  discover  that  it  was  made  without  meat. 

Good  beef  or  mutton  broth  can  be  used  instead  of  water  for  the 
above  soup,  and  in  that  case  the  vegetables  sliced  small,  or  rubbed 
through  a  strainer,  may  be  added  to  it  before  it  is  served. 


CARROT  SOUP   MAIGRE. 

Throw  two  ounces  of  salt  into  a  gallon  of  boiling  water,  then  add 
three  or  four  carrots  quartered  or  thickly  sliced,  one  onion  or  more 
according  to  the  taste,  and  a  faggot  of  parsley,  or  some  parsley  roots. 
When  these  have  boiled  gently  for  upwards  of  an  hour,  strain  off  the 
liquor  and  put  it  back  into  the  saucepan.  Have  ready  more  carrots, 
nicely  scraped  and  washed ;  split  them  down  into  strips  about  the 
size  of  large  macaroni  and  cut  them  into  half  finger  lengths.  Two 
quarts  of  these  will  not  be  too  much  for  persons  who  like  the  soup 
well  filled  with  the  vegetable ;  boil  them  perfectly  tender,  and  turn 
them  with  their  liquor  into  the  tureen,  first  adding  pepper  sufiicient 
to  season  it  properly,  and  more  salt  if  needed.  The  proportion  of 
carrots  may  be  diminished,  and  a  quart  or  more  of  Brussels  sprouts, 
boiled  and  drained,  may  be  substituted  for  part  of  them.  Some  per- 
sons have  these  soups  thickened,  or  enriched  as  they  think,  with  flour 
and  butter;  but  the  latter  ingredient  should  at  least  be  sparingly  used; 
and  any  other  kind  of  thickening  is  more  wholesome.  A  few  ounces 
of  vermicelli  stewed  in  them  for  twenty  minutes  or  rather  longer,  will 
be  found  a  very  good  one.  Celery,  leeks,  and  turnips  may  be  boiled 
down  in  the  carrot^stock,  or  added  when  the  fresh  vegetables  have 
beoi  stewed  in  it  for  about  ten  minutes. 


46  HODEBN  COOKERY.  [cnxv.  i. 

CHEAP  FISH  SOUPS. 

An  infinite  yariety  of  excellent  soaps  may  be  made  of  fisb,  which 
may  be  stewed  down  for  them  in  precisely  tne  same  manner  as  meat, 
and  with  the  same  addition  of  yegetables  and  herbs.  When  the  skin 
is  coarse  or  rank  it  should  be  carefully  stripped  off  before  the  fish  is 
used ;  and  any  oily  particles  which  may  float  on  the  surface  should 
be  entirely  remoyed  from  it. 

In  France,  Jersey,  Cornwall,  and  many  other  localities,  the  conger  eel, 
diyested  of  its  skin,  is  sliced  up  into  thick  cutlets  and  made  into  soup, 
which  we  are  assured  by  Engush  families  who  haye  it  often  seryed  at 
their  tables,  is  extremely  good.  A  h^-grown  fish  is  best  for  the  pur- 
pose. After  the  soup  has  been  strained  and  allowed  to  settle,  it  must 
be  heated  afresh,  and  rice  and  minced  parsley  may  be  added  to  it  as 
for  the  turkey  soup  of  page  32 ;  or  it  may  be  thickened  with  rice- 
flour  only,  or  seryed  clear.  Curried  fish-soups,  too,  are  much  to  be 
recommended. 

When  broth  or  stock  has  been  made  as  aboye  with  conger  eel, 
common  eels,  whitings,  haddocks,  codlings,  fresh  water  fish,  or  any 
common  kind,  which  may  be  at  hand,  flakes  of  cold  salmon,  cod  fiahf 
John  Dories,  or  scallops  of  cold  soles,  plaice,*  &c.,  may  be  heated  and 
seryed  in  it ;  and  the  remains  of  crabs  or  lobsters  mingled  with 
them.  The  large  oysters  sold  at  so  cheap  a  rate  upon  the 
coast,  and  which  are  not  much  esteemed  for  eating  raw,  serye 
admirably  for  imparting  flayour  to  soup,  and  the  softer  portions  of 
them  may  be  seryed  in  it  after  a  few  minutes  of  gentle  simmering. 
Anchoyy  or  any  other  store  fish-sauce  may  be  added  with  good  effect 
to  many  of  these  pottages  if  used  with  moderation.  Prawns  and 
shrimps  likewise  would  generally  be  considered  an  improyement  to 
them. 

For  more  sayoury  preparations,  fry  the  fish  and  y^tables,  lay 
them  into  the  soup-pot,  and  add  boiling,  instead  of  cmd  water  to 
them.

CHAP,  n.]  FISH.  49 

igreeable.  When  all  these  marks  are  reyersed,  and  the  e3'es  are 
tQnken,  the  gills  very  dark  in  hue,  the  fish  itself  flabby  and  of  offen- 
sive odour,  it  is  bad,  and  should  be  avoided. 
The  chloride  of  soda,  will,  it  is  true,  restore 
it  to  a  tolerably  eatable  state,*  if  it  be  not 
very  much  over-kept,  but  it  will  never  resem- 
ble in  quality  and  wholesomeness  fish  which 
is  fresh  from  the  water. 

A  good  turbot  is  thick,  and  fVill  fleshed,  and 
the  under  side  is  of  a  pale  cream  colour  or 
yellowish  white ;  when  tnis  is  of  a  bluish  tint, 
and  the  fish  is  thin  and  soft,  it  should  be  re- 
Madtcrei  Eettte.        jected.    The  same  observations  apply  equfdly 
to  soles. 

The  best  salmon  and  cod  fish  are  known  by  a  small  head,  very 
thick  shoulders,  and  a  small  tail ;  the  scales  of  the  former  should  he 
bright,  and  its  flesh  of  a  fine  red  colour ;  to  be  eaten  in  perfection  it 
should  be  dressed  as  soon  as  it  is  caught,  before  the  curd  (or  white 
nihrtanoe  which  lies  between  the  flakes  of  flesh)  has  melted  and  ren- 
dered the  fish  oily.  In  that  state  it  is  really  crimp,  but  continues  so 
only  for  a  very  few  hours ;  and  it  bears  therefore  a  much  higher  price 
in  the  London  market  then,  than  when  mellowed  by  having  been  Kept 
a  ^  or  two. 

The  flesh  of  cod  fish  should  be  white  and  clear  before  it  is  boiled, 
whiter  still  after  it  is  boiled,  and  firm  though  tender,  sweet  and  mild 
in  flavour,  and  separated  easUy  into  large  flakes.  Many  persons  con- 
sider it  rather  improved  than  otherwise  by  having  a  little  salt  rubbed 
along  the  inside  oi  the  backbone  and  lettmgit  lie  from  twenty-four  to 
ibrty-eight  hours  before  it  is  dressed.  It  is  sometimes  served  crimp 
like  salmon,  and  must  then  be  sliced  as  soon  as  it  is  dead,  or  within  the 
shortest  possible  time  afterwards. 

Herrings,  mackerel,  and  whitings,  unless  newly  caught,^are  quite 
noatable.  When  they  are  in  good  condition  their  natural  colours 
irill  be  very  distinct  and  their  whole  appearance  glossy  and  fresh. 
The  herring  when  first  taken  from  the  water  is  of  a  silvery  bright- 
ness ;  the  Inck  of  the  mackerel  is  of  a  bright  green  marked  with  dark 
itripes ;  but  this  becomes  of  a  coppery  colour  as  the  fish  grows  stale. 
The  w^ting  is  of  a  pale  brown  or  fawn  colour  with  a  pinkish  tint ; 
Vat  appears  dim  and  leaden-hued  when  no  longer  fresh. 

£eis  should  be  alive  and  brisk  in  movement  when  they  are  purchased, 
hut  the  ^  horrid  barbarity,**  as  it  is  truly  designated,  of  skinning  and 
dividing  them  while  they  are  so,  is  without  excuse,  as  they  are  easily 
destroyed  ""  by  piercing  the  spinal  marrow  close  to  the  back  part  of  the 
sknll  with  a  anarp  pointed  kmfe  or  skewer.  K  this  be  done  in  the  right

place  all  motion  will  instantly  oease.**  We  quote  Dr.  Kitcbcner*k

Lobsters,  prawns,  and  shrimps,  are  very  stiff  when  freshly  boiled, 
and  the  tails  turn  strongly  inwfurds ;  when  these  relax,  and  the  fish  are 
soft  and  watery,  they  are  stale ;  and  the  smell  will  detect  their  being 
so,  instantly,  even  if  no  other  symptoms  of  it  be  remarked.  If  bought 
alive,  lobsters  should  be  chosen  by  their  weight  and  ^^  liveliness."  The 
hen  lobster  is  preferred  for  sauce  and  soups,  on  account  of  the  coral ; 
but  the  flesh  of  the  male  is  generally  considered  of  finer  flavour  for 
eating.  The  vivacity  of  their  leaps  will  show  when  prawns  and 
shrimps  are  fresh  from  the  sea. 

Oysters  should  close  forcibly  on  the  knife  when  they  are  opened : 
if  the  shells  are  apart  ever  so  little  they  are  losing  their  condition,  and 
when  they  remain  far  open  the  fish  are  dead,  and  fit  onlv  to  be  thrown 
away.  Small  plump  natives  are  very  preferable  to  ine  larger  and 
coarser  kinds. 

TO   CLEAN   FISH. 

Let  this  be  always  done  with  the  most  scrupulous  nicety,  for  nothioff 
can  more  effectually  destrov  the  appetite,  or  disgrace  the  cook,  than  fish 
sent  to  table  imperfectly  cleaned.  Handle  it  lightly,  and  never  thivw 
it  roughly  about,  so  as  to  bruise  it ;  wash  it  well,  but  do  not  leave  it 
longer  in  the  water  than  is  necessary ;  for  fish,  like  meat,  loses  its  fla- 
vour from  being  soaked.  When  the  scales  are  to  be  removed,  lay  the 
fish  flat  upon  its  side  and  hold  it  firmly  with  the  left  hand,  while  they 
are  scraped  off  ¥rith  the  right ;  turn  it,  and  when  both  sides  are  done, 
pour  or  pump  sufficient  water  to  float  off  all  the  loose  scales ;  then 
proceed  to  empty  it ;  and  do  this  without  opening  it  more  than  is  abso' 
IvJtely  necessary  for  the  purposes  of  cleaminess.  Be  sure  that  not  the 
slightest  particle  of  offensive  matter  be  left  in  the  inside ;  wash  out  the 
blood  entirely,  and  scrape  or  brush  it  away  if  needful  from  the  back- 
bone. This  may  easily  be  accomplished  without  opening  the  fish  so 
much  as  to  render  it  unsightly  wnen  it  is  sent  to  table.  When  the 
scales  are  left  on,  the  outside  of  the  fish  should  be  well  washed  and 
wiped  with  a  coarse  cloth,  drawn  gently  from  the  head  to  the  tai]«  'Ef^B 
to  be  wholesome  should  be  skinned,  but  they  are  sometimes  dressed 
without ;  boiling  water  should  then  be  poured  upon  them,  and  they 
should  be  left  in  it  from  five  to  ten  minutes  before  they  are  cut  up.  llie 
dark  skin  of  the  sole  must  be  stripped  off  when  it  is  firied,  but  it 
should  be  left  on  it  like  that  of  a  turbot  when  the  fish  is  boiled,  and  it 
should  be  dished  with  the  white  side  upwards.  Whitings  are  skinned 
before  thev  are  e^ged  and  crumbed  for  frying,  but  fov  boiling  or 
broiling,  the  skin  is  kfit  on  them.  The  gills  of  all  fish  (the  red  mul* 
let  sometimes  except^),  must  be  taken  out.     The  Jms  qf  a  turho^

wbek  an  canridered  a  great  delicacy^  shawld  be  left  wnttmched;  bat 
those  of  most  other  fish  most  be  cut  off. 

TO   KEEP   FISH. 

We  find  that  all  the  smaller  kinds  of  fish  keep  best  if  emptied  and 
dnned  as  soon  as  they  are  brought  in,  then  wiped  gently  as  dry  as 
thejr  can  be,  and  hung  separately  by  the  head  on  the  hooks  in  th^ 
oiljog  of  a  oool  larder,  or  in  the  open  air  when  the  weather  will 
aUov.  When  there  is  danger  of  their  being  attacked  by  flies,  a 
inre  safe,  placed  in  a  strong  draught  of  air,  is  better  adapted  to  the 
Purpose.  Soles  in  winter  will  remain  good  for  two  days  when  thus 
l^cpared ;  and  eyen  whitings  and  mackerel  may  be  kept  so  without 
Josiiig  any  of  their  excellence.  Salt  may  be  rubbed  slightly  over  cod  fish, 
md  well  along  the  back-bone ;  but  it  injures  the  flavour  of  salmoi;^ 
the  inside  of  which  may  be  rubbed  with  vinegar  and  peppered  instead. 
When  excessive  sultriness  renders  all  of  these  modes  unavailing,  the 
fish  fflust  at  once  be  partially  cooked  to  preserve  it,  but  this  snould 
be  avoided  if  possible,  as  it  is  very  rarely  so  good  when  this  method  is 
Rurtedto. 

TO   SWEETEN   TAINTED   FISH. 

The  application  of  strong  vinegar,  or  of  acetic  acid  (which  may  be 
pnnhasod  at  the  chemists*),  will  effect  this  when  the  taint  is  but 
sHght  The  vinegar  should  be  used  pure ;  and  one  wineglassful  of 
the  add  should  be  mixed  with  two  of  water.  Pour  either  of  these 
orer  the  fish,  and  rub  it  on  the  parts  which  require  it ;  then  leave  it 
ontooched  for  a  few  minutes,  and  wash  it  afterwards  well,  changing 
the  water  two  or  three  times.  When  the  fish  is  in  a  worse  state 
the  ehloride  pf  soda,  from  its  powerful  anti-putrescent  properties, 
^  have  more  effect :  it  may  be  diluted,  and  applied  in  the  same 
Biaoer  as  the  acid.

There  is  no  better  way  of  dressing  a  good  turbot,  brill,  John  Doiy, 
or  cod's  head  and  shoulders,  than  plain  but  careful  boiling.  Salmon 
is  excellent  in  almost  every  mode  m  which  it  can  be  cook^  or  used. 
Boiled  entire  or  in  crimped  slices ;  roasted  in  a  cradle-spit  or 
Dutch  oven ;  baked  ;  fried  in  small  collops  ;  collared ;  potted;

Soles  may  be  either  boiled,  or  baked,  or  fried  entire,  or  in  fillets ; 
eurried;  stewed  in  cream;  or  prepared  by  any  of  the  directions 
given  for  them  in  the  body  of  this  chapter. 

Plaice,  unless  when  in  full  season  and  very  fresh,  is  apt  to  be 
watery  and  insipid ;  but  taken  in  its  perfection  and  carefully  cooked, 
it  is  very  sweet  and  delicate  in  flavour.  If  large,  it  may  be  bculed 
with  advantage  either  whole  or  in  fillets ;  but  to  many  tastes  it  is 
very  superior  when  filleted,  dipped  into  egg  and  bread  crumbs,  and 
fried.  The  flesh  may  also  be  curried ;  or  the  plaice  may  be  converted 
into  water-souchy,  or  soupe'tnaigre :  when  small  it  is  often  fried 
whole. 

Red  mullet  should  always  be  baked,  broiled,  or  roasted:  it  should 
on  no  occasion  be  boiled. 

Mackerel,  for  which  many  receipts  will  be  found  in  this  chapter, 
when  broiled  ottxfewAo^e,  as  we  have  directed,  or  freed  from  the  bones, 
divided,  eggeo,  crumbed,  and  fried,  is  infinitely  superior  to  the  same 
fish  cooked  in  the  ordinary  manner. 

The  whiting,  when  very  fresh  and  in  season,  is  always  delicate  and 

food ;  and  of  all  fish  is  considered  the  best  suited  to  invalids.  Fer- 
aps  quite  the  most  wholesome  mode  of  preparing  it  for  them,  is  to 
open  it  as  little  as  possible  when  it  is  cleansed,  to  leave  the  skin  on, 
to  dry  the  fish  well,  and  to  broil  it  gently.  It  should  be  sent  very 
hot  to  table,  and  will  require  no  sauce :  twenty  minutes  will  usuaUy 
be  required  to  cook  it,  if  of  moderate  siie. 

The  haddock  is  sometimes  very  large.  We  have  had  it  occasionally 
ftom  our  southern  coast  between  two  and  three  feet  in  length,  and  it 
was  then  remarkably  good  when  simply  boiled,  even  the  day  after  it 
was  caught,  the  white  curd  between  the  flakes  of  flesh  being  like  that 
of  extremely  fresh  salmon.  As  it  is  in  full  season  in  mid- winter,  it  can 
be  sent  to  a  distance  without  injury.  It  is  a  very  firm  fiish  when 
large  and  in  season ;  but,  as  purchased  commonly  at  inland  markets, 
18  often  neither  fine  91  size  nor  quality.  One  of  the  best  modes  of 
C0oking  it  is,  to  take  the  flesh  entire  from  the  bones,  to  divide  it,  d^

Other  receipts  for  it  will  be  found  in  the  body  of  this  cnapter. 

The  flesh  of  the  gurnard  is  exceedingly  dry,  and  somewhat  aver

Portions  only  of  the  skate,  which  is  frequently  of  enormoiu  size,  ane

^  Trout,  which  is  a  ddicious  fish  when  stewed  in  grayy,  either  quite

Pike,  of  which  the  flesh  is  extremely  dry,  is  we  think  better  baked

Carp  should  eitner  be  stewed  whole  in  ihe  same  manner  as  trout, 
or  seryed  cut  in  slices,  in  a  rich  sauce  called  a  matelote. 

Smelts,  sand-eels,  and  white-bait,  are  always  fried ;  the  last  two 
sometimes  after  being  dipped  into  batter. 

THE   BEST   MODE   OF   BOILJNO  FISH. 

We  haye  left  unaltered  in  the  following  receipts  the  greater  number 
of  our  original  directions  for  boiling  fish,  which  were  found  wheneare- 
folly  followed,  to  produce  a  good  result ;  but  Baron  Liebeg  and  other 
scientific  writers  explain  clearly  the  principles  on  which  the  nutriment 
contained  in  fish  or  fiesh  is  best  retained  by  bringing  the  surface  of 
either  when  it  is  cooked,  into  immediate  contact  with  boiling  water ; 
and  then  (after  a  few  minutes  of  ebullition)  lowering  the  temperature 
by  the  additbn  of  cold  water,  and  keeping  it  somewhat  below  the 
boiling  point  for  the  remainder  of  the  process.  This  method  is  at 
least  worthy  of  a  trial,  eyen  if  it  be  attended  with  a  slight  degree 
more  of  trouble  than  those  in  general  use ;  but  when  fish  is  seryed 
with  a  yariety  of  other  dishes,  the  escape  of  some  portion  of  its 
nutritious  juices  is  of  less  importance  than  when  it  forms  the  principal 
Sood  of  any  part  of  the  community :  in  that  case,  the  preseryation  of 

•  We  bsT«  been  inftmned  by  Mr.  Howitt,  the  well-known  ftnthor  of  sevenl 
lii^y  interesting  works  on  Germany,  that  this  fish,  when  boiled  the  instant  it 
was  eaaoghi — as  he  had  eaten  it  often  on  the  banks  of  some  celebrated  Qerman 
txoiii4tream»— was  most  excellent,  especially  when  it  was  of  large  size ;  bnt,  as  a 
general  mk^  almost  any  other  mode  of  eooking  is  to  be  recommended  for  It  ia 


54  MODERN  COOKERY.  [chap,  a 

all  the  nourishmeiit  which  can  be  deriyed  from  it,  is  of  real  conse- 
quence. 

Directions. — Throw  into  as  much  water  as  will  coyer  the  fish 
entirely,  a  portion  of  the  salt  which  is  to  be  added  in  cooking  it,  and 
when  it  boils  quickly  take  off  the  scum,  lay  in  the  fish,  and  let  it 
boil  moderately  fast  from  three  to  ten  minutes,  according  to  its  weight 
and  thickness ;  then  pour  in  as  much  cold  water  as  there  is  of  the 
boiling,  take  out  a  part,  leaying  sufficient  only  to  keep  the  fish  well 
coyered  until  it  is  ready  to  senre ;  add  the  remainder  of  the  salt, 
draw  the  fish-kettle  to  the  side  of  the  fire,  and  keep  the  water  jut 
simmering,  and  no  more,  until  the  fish  is  done. 

The  cook  will  understand  that  if  a  gallon  of  water  be  required  to 
eoyer  the  fish  while  it  is  cooking,  that  quantity  must  be  made  to  boil ; 
and  that  a  gallon  of  cold  must  be  added  to  it  after  the  fish  has  been 
laid  in,  and  kept  boiling  for  a  yery  few  minutes.  For  example :— A 
lar^  turbot  or  cod*s  head  for  ten  minutes ;  a  moderate-si«d  plaice 
or  John  Dory,  about  fiye ;  and  whitinffs,  codlings,  and  other  small 
fish,  from  three  to  four  minutes.  Tnat  one  ^Uon  must  then  be 
taken  out  of  the  kettle,  which  should  immediatdy  be  drawn  from  the 
fire,  and  placed  at  the  side  of  the  stoye,  that  the  fish  may  be  gra- 
dually heated  through  as  the  water  is  brought  slowly  to  the  point  of 
simmering. 

The  whole  of  the  salt  may  be  added  after  a  portion  of  the  water  is 
withdrawn,  when  the  cook  cannot  entirely  depend  on  her  own  judg- 
ment  for  the  precise  quantity  required. 

O&s.— This  is  the  best  practical  application  that  We  can  giye  of 
Baron  Liebeg*s  instructions. 

BRINE   FOR   BOILING  FISH. 

Fish  is  exceedingly  insipid  if  sufficient  salt  be  not  mixed  with  the 
water  in  which  it  is  boiled,  but  the  precise  Quantity  required  for  it  will 
depend,  in  some  measure,  upon  the  Kind  oi  salt  which  is  used.  Fine 
common  salt  is  that  for  which  our  directions  are  giyen ;  but  when  the 
Maldon  salt,  which  is  yery  superior  in  strength,  as  well  as  in  other 
qualities,  is  substituted  for  it,  a  smaller  quantity  must  be  allowed. 
About  four  ounces  to  the  gallon  of  water  will  be  sufficient  for  small 
fish  in  general ;  an  additional  ounce,  or  rather  more,  will  not  be  too 
much  for  cod  fish,  lobsters,  crabs,  prawns,  and  shrimps ;  and  sabnon 
will  require  eight  ounces,  as  the  brine  for  this  fish  should  be  strong: 
the  water  should  always  be  perfectly  well  skimmed  from  the  moment 
the  scum  begins  to  form  upon  the  surface. 

Mackerel,  whiting,  and  other  small  fish,  4  ounces  of  salt  to  a  gallon 
of  water.  Cod  fish,  lobsters,  crabs,  prawns,  shrimps,  5  to  6  ox. 
Salmon,  8  ozs. 

TO  RENDER  BOILED  FISH  FIRM. 

Put  a  small  bit  of  saltpetre  with  the  salt  into  the  water  in  which  it 
IB  boiled :  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  will  be  sufficient  for  a  gallon.

to  KKOW  WHEN   FISH   IS  SUFFICIENTLY    BOILED,    OR   0THERWI8B 

COOKED. 

If  the  thickest  part  of  the  flesh  separates  easily  from  the  back -bone, 
H  is  quite  ready  to  serve,  and  should  be  withdrawn  from  the  pan 
without  delay,  as  further  cookii^  would  be  injurious  to  it.  This  test 
csn  easily  be  applied  to  a  fish  which  has  been  divided,  but  when  it  is 
entire  it  should  be  lif^d  from  the  water  when  the  flesh  (tf  the  tail 
Iwesks  from  the  bone,  and  the  eyes  loosen  from  the  head. 

TO   BAKE   FISH. 

A  gentle  oven  may  be  used  with  advantage,  for  cooking  almost 
every  kind  of  fish,  as  we  have  ascertained  from  our  own  observation ; 
but  it  must  be  subjected  to  a  mild  degree  of  heat  onjy.  This  pene- 
tntes  the  flesh  gradually,  and  converts  it  into  wholesome  succulent 
food;  whereas,  a  hot  oven  evaporates  all  the  juices  rapidly,  and 
Koden  the  fish  hard  and  dry.  When  small,  they  should  be  wrapped 
in  oiled  or  buttered  paper  before  thejr  are  baked ;  and  when  filleted, 
or  left  in  any  other  form,  and  placed  m  a  deep  dish  with  or  without 
any  liquid  before  they  are  put  into  the  oven,  a  buttered  paper  should 
rtill  be  laid  closely  upon  them  to  keep  the  surface  moist.  Large  pieces 
of  salmon,  conger  eel,  and  other  fish  of  considerable  size  are  some- 
times in  conunon  cookery  baked  like  meat  over  potatoes  pared  and 
haired. 

FAT   FOR   FRTINO   FISH. 

This,  whether  it  be  batter,  lard,  or  oil  should  always  be  excellent 
in  qoafihr,  for  tfae  finest  fish  will  be  rendered  unfit  for  eating  if  it  be 
filed  in  fat  that  is  rancid.  When  good,  and  used  in  sufficient  quan- 
tity, it  will  serve  for  the  same  purpose  several  times,  if  strained  after 
csch  flying,  and  put  carefully  away  in  a  clean  pan,  provided  always 
that  it  has  not  been  smoked  nor  burned  in  the  using. 

Lard  renders  fish  more  crisp  than  butter  does ;  but  fresh,  pure 
plire-oil  (talad  ot2,  as  it  is  commonly  called  in  England)  is  the  best 
ingredient  which  can  be  used  for  it,  and  as  it  will  serve  well  for  the 
nne  purpose,  many  times  in  succession,  if  strained  and  carefully 
stored  as  we  have  a&eady  stated,  it  is  not  in  reality  so  expensive  as 
Bught  be  supposed  for  tliis  mode  of  cookinff .  There  should  always 
be  a&  ample  quantity  of  it  (or  of  an^  other /nficrff*)  in  the  pan,  as  the 
fish  ihouid  be  nearly  covered  with  it,  at  the  least ;  and  it  should  cease 
to  bubble  before  either  fish  or  meat  is  laid  into  it,  or  it  will  be  too 
much  absorbed  by  the  flesh,  and  will  impart  neither  sufScient  firm- 
ves,  nor  sufficient  colour.

for  a  fish  of  the  same  weight,  the  cook  must  watch  it  attentively,  and 
lift  it  out  88  soon  as  its  appearance  denotes  its  being  done.

LoDfler,  if  of  unusual  size.

into  bailing  water,  and  to  prevent  its  breaking,  tie  it  with  a  cloth 
tightly  to  Uie  fish-plate. 

TURBOT  1  LA   CRl^MB. 

Raise  carefully  from  the  bones  the  flesh  of  a  cold  turbot,  and  dear 
it  from  the  dark  skin ;  cut  it  into  small  squares,  and  put  it  into  an 
exceedingly  clean  stewpan  or  saucepan ;  then  make  and  pour  upon  it 
the  eream  sauce  of  Chapter  Y.,  or  make  as  much  as  may  be  required 
for  the  fish  b^  the  same  receipt,  with  equal  proportions  of  milk  and 
cream  and  a  httle  additional  flour.  Heat  the  turbot  slowly  in  the  sauce, 
but  do  not  allow  it  to  boil,  and  send  it  very  hot  to  table.  The  white 
skin  of  the  fish  is  not  usually  added  to  this  dish,  and  it  is  of  better  ap- 
pearance without  it;  but  for  a  family  dinner,  it  may  be  left  on  the 
flesh,  wiien  it  is  much  liked.  No  acid  must  be  stirred  to  the  sauce 
until  the  whole  is  ready  for  table. 


TURBOT  AU  BECHAMEL,   OR,   IN   BECHAMEL  SAUCE. 

Prepare  the  cold  turbot  as  for  the  preceding  receipt,  but  leave  no 
portion  of  the  skin  with  it.  Heat  it  in  a  ridb  bechamel  sauce,  and 
serve  it  in  a  vol-au-vent^  or  in  a  deep  dish  with  a  border  of  fried  bread 
cut  in  an  elegant  form,  and  made  with  one  dark  and  one  light  sippet, 
plaoed  altematelv.  The  surface  may  be  covered  with  a  half-inch 
layer  of  delicately  fried  bread-crumbs,  perfectly  well  drained  and 
dned;  or  they  may  be  spread  over  the  fish  without  being  fried, 
thai  nunatened  with  clarified  butter)  and  browned  with  a  sala-

SMALL  JOHN  DORIES  BAKED. 

(Author' t  Receipt— good.) 

We  have  faund  these  fish  when  they  were  too  small  to  be  worth 
cooking  in  the  usual  way,  excellent  when  quite  simply  baked  in  the 
following  manner,  the  nesh  bdng  remarkably  tneet  and  tender,  much 
more  so  than  it  becomes  by  frying  or  broiling.  After  they  have 
been  cleaned,  dry  them  in  a  cloth,  season  the  inddet  slightly  with 
fine  salt,  dredge  a  little  flour  on  the  fish,  and  stick  a  few  very  small 
bits  of  butter  on  them,  but  only  just  sufficient  to  prevent  their 
becoming  dry  in  the  oven ;  lay  them  singly  on  a  flat  dish,  and  bake 
them  very  gently  from  fourteen  to  sixteen  minutes.  Serve  them 
with  the  same  sauce  as  baked  solet. 

When  extremely  fresh,  as  it  usually  is  in  the  markets  of  the  coast, 
fish  thus  simply  dressed  an  four  ii  preferable  to  that  more  elaborately 

Srepared  by  adding  various  condiments  to  it  after  it  is  placed  in  a 
eep  dish,  and  covering  it  with  a  thick  layer  of  Iwead  cmmlx,  hmhs- 
lened  with  clarified  butter. 

The  appearance  of  the  John  Dories  is  improved  by  talcing  off  the 
heads,  and  cutting  away  not  only  the  fins  but  the  filaments  of  the 


A  fresh  and  Aill-nied  brill  always  ranks  high  in  the  list  of  fish,  as

quires less  cooking  than  the  turbot,  even  when  it  is  of  equal  size;

not  the  same  rich  glutinous  skin  as  that  fish,  nor  are  thafinseateemcd-

They  mast  be  ent  off  wben  the  brill  is  cleaned ;  and  it  may  be  put

and  drain  it  well  upon  the  fish-plate  when  it  is  lifted  out ;  dish  it  on  a

TO   BOIL  SALMON. 

[In  ftdl  seaaon  from  May  to  Aagost:  may  be  bod  macb  earlier,  bat  is  scarce 

and  dear.] 

To  preserve  the  fine  colour  of  this  fish,  and  to  set  the  curd  when  it 
Is  quite  freshly  caught,  it  is  usual  to  put  it  into  boiling^  instead  of  into 
cold  water.  Scale,  empty,  and  wash  it  with  the  greatest  nicety,  and 
be  especially  careful  to  cleanse  all  the  blood  from  the  inside.  Stir 
into  tne  fish-kettle  eight  ounces  of  common  salt  to  the  gallon  of  water, 
let  it  boil  quickly  for  a  minute  or  two,  take  off  all  the  scum,  put  in 
the  salmon  and  boil  it  moderately  fast,  if  it  be  small,  but  more  gently 
ihould  it  be  very  thick ;  and  assure  yourself  that  it  is  quite  sufficiently 
done  before  it  is  sent  to  table,  for  nothing  can  be  more  distasteful, 
even  to  the  eye,  than  fish  which  is  under  dressed. 

From  two  to  three  pounds  of  the  thick  part  of  a  fine  salmon  will 
leqnire  half  an  hour  to  boil  it,  but  eight  or  ten  pounds  will  be  done 
enonsh  in  little  more  than  double  that  time ;  less  in  proportion  to  its 
veight  should  be  allowed  for  a  small  fish,  or  for  the  thin  end  of  a 
iaiige  one.  Do  not  allow  the  salmon  to  remain  in  the  water  afler  it  is 
Rady  to  serve,  or  both  its  flavour  and  appearance  will  be  injured. 
Dish  it  on  a  hot  napkin,  and  send  dressed  cucumber,  and  anchovy, 
shrimp,  or  lobster  sauce,  and  a  tureen  of  plain  melted  butter  to  table 
with  it 

To  each  gallon  water,  8  oz.  salt  Salmon,  2  to  3  lbs.  (thick), 
Ihour ;  8  to  10  lbs.,  1^  hour ;  small,  or  thin  fish,  less  time. 

SALMON  X  LA   GENBVESE. 

A  fashionable  mode  of  serving  salmon  at  the  present  day  is  to 
divide  the  larger  portion  of  the  body  into  three  equal  parts ;  to  boil 
them  in  water,  or  in  a  marinade ;  and  to  serve  them  dished  in  a  line, 
but  not  doee  together,  and  covered  with  a  rich  Genevese  sauce  (for 
vhkh  see  Chapter  V.^  It  appears  to  us  that  the  skin  should  be 
stripped  from  any  fisn  over  which  the  sauoe  is  poured,  but  in  this 
Ctte  It  is  not  customary.

CRIMPED  SALMON. 

Gut  into  slices  an  incli  and  a  half,  or  two  inches  thick,  the  body  of 
a  salmon  quite  newly  caught;  throw  them  into  strong  salt  and  water 
as  they  are  done,  but  do  not  let  them  soak  in  it ;  wash  them  well, 
lay  them  on  a  fish-plate,  and  put  them  into  fast  boiling  water,  salted 
and  well  skimmed.  In  from  ten  to  fifteen  minutes  they  will  be  done. 
Dish  them  on  a  napkin,  and  send  them  very  hot  to  table  with 
lobster  sauce,  andplam  melted  butter ;  or  with  the  caper  fish-sauce 
of  Chapter  Y.  Tne  water  should  be  salted  as  for  admon  boiled  in 
the  ordmary  waj,  and  the  scum  should  be  cleared  off  with  great  care 
af^er  the  fish  is  m. 

In  boiling  water,  10  to  15  minutes. 

SALMON    1   LA    ST.    MARCEL. 

Separate  some  cold  boiled  salmon  into  flakes,  and  free  them  en- 
tirely from  the  skin ;  break  the  bones,  and  boil  them  in  a  pint  of 
water  for  half  an  hour.  Strain  off  the  liquor,  put  it  into  a  clean 
saucepan  and  fltir  into  it  by  degrees  when  it  b^ns  to  boil  quickly, 
two  ounces  of  butter  mixed  with  a  large  teaspoonful  of  flour,  and 
when- the  whole  has  boiled  for  two  or  three  minutes  add  a  teaspoonful 
of  essence  of  anchovies,  one  of  good  mushroom  catsup,  half  as  much 
lemon-juice  or  chili  vinecar,  a  half  saltspoonful  of  pounded  mace, 
some  cayenne,  and  a  yery  little  salt.  Shell  from  half  to  a  whole  pint 
of  shrimps,  add  them  to  the  salmon,  and  heat  the  fish  very  slowly 
in  the  sauce  by  the  side  of  the  fire,  but  do  not  allow  it  boil.  When 
it  is  very  hot,  dish  and  send  it  quickly  to  table.  French  cooks,  ivhen 
they  re-dress  fish  or  meat  of  any  kind,  prepare  the  flesh  with  great 
nicety,  and  then  put  it  into  a  stewpan,  and  pour  the  sauce  upon  it, 
which  is,  we  think,  better  than  tne  more  usual  English  mode  of 
laying  it  into  the  boiling  sauce.  The  cold  salmon  may  also  be  re- 
heated in  the  cream  sauce  of  Chapter  Y.,  or  in  the  Maitre  dTHtad 
sauce  which  follows  it;  and  will  be  found  excellent  with  either. 
This  receipt  is  for  a  moderate  sized  dish. 

SALMON   BAKED   OyER   MASHED    POTATOES. 

We  are  informed  by  a  person  who  has  been  a  resident  in  Irelaiid, 
that  the  middle  of  a  salmon  is  there  often  baked  over  mashed 
potatoes,  from  which  it  is  raised  by  means  of  a  wire  stand,  as  meat  is 
m  England.  We  have  not  been  able  to  have  it  tried,  but  an  in- 
genious cook  will  be  at  no  loss  for  the  proper  method  of  preparing, 
and  the  time  of  cooking  it.  The  potatoes  are  sometimes  merely  pared 
and  halved ;  the  fiish  is  then  laid  upon  them. 

SALMON  PDDDINO,   TO   BE   SERVED   HOT   OR  COLD. 

(A  Scotch  Receipt — OoodJ) 
Pound  or  chop  small,  or  rub  through  a  sieve  one  pound  of  cold

bofled  salmon  freed  entunely  from  bone  and  skin;  and  blend  it 
lightly  but  thoroughly  with  half  a  pound  of  fine  bread-crumbs,  a

aeaaoaing  of  fine  ndt  and  cayenne,  and  four  well  whisked  eggs.

TO  BOIL   COD   FISH. 

[In  highest  seaaon  from  October  to  the  beginning  of  Febmazy;  in  perfection 

about  Chriatmas.] 

When  this  fish  is  large  the  head  and  shoulders  are  snfiicient  for  a 
handsome  dish,  and  they  contain  all  the  choicer  portion  of  it,  though 
not  so  much  substantial  eating  as  the  middle  of  the  body,  which,  m 
SQQseqnence,  is  generally  preferred  to  them  by  the  frugal  house- 
keeper. Wash  Uie  fish,  and  cleanse  the  inside,  and  the  bi^k-bone  in 
particular,  with  the  most  scrupulous  care ;  lay  it  into  the  fish-kettle 
sad  cover  it  well  with  cold  water  mixed  with  five  ounces  of  salt  to 
the  giUon,  and  about  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of  saltpetre  to  the  whole. 
Place  it  over  a  moderate  fire,  clear  off  the  scum  perfectly,  and  let  the 
fish  boil  gently  until  it  is  done.  Drain  it  well*  and  disn  it  carefully 
upon  a  very  hot  napkin  with  the  liver  and  the  roe  as  a  garnish.  To 
these  are  usually  added  tufts  of  lightly  scraped  horse-radish  round 
the  edge.  Serve  well-made  oyster  sauce  and  plain  melted  butter 
with  it ;  or  anchovy  sauce,  when  oysters  cannot  be  procured.  The 
cream  sauce  of  Chapter  Y.,  is  also  an  appropriate  one  for  this  fish. 

Moderate  sise,  20  to  30  minutes,    ^arge,  t  to  i  hour. 

SLICES  OF   COD   FISH   FRIED. 

Cut  the  middle  or  tail  of  the  fish  into  slices  nearly  an  inch  thick, 
season  them  with  salt  and  white  pepper  or  cayenne,  flour  them  well, 
and  fry  them  of  a  clear  equal  brown  on  both  sides ;  drain  them  on  a 
sieve  before  the  fire,  and  serve  them  on  a  well-heated  napkin,  with 
plenty  of  crisped  parsley  round  them.  Or,  dip  them  into  beaten 
egg,  and  then  into  fine  crumbs  mixed  with  a  seasoning  of  salt  and 
pepoer  (some  cooks  add  one  of  minced  herbs  also),  before  they  are 
fried.    Send  melted  butter  and  anchovy  sauce  to  table  with  them. 

8  to  12  minutes.

8TEWED   COD. 

Put  iuto  boiling  water,  salted  as  usual,  about  three  pounds  of  fresh 
cod  fish  cut  into  slices  an  inch  and  a  half  thick,  and  boil  them 
gently  for  five  minutes ;  lift  them  out,  and  let  ttiem  drain.  Have 
ready  heated  in  a  wide  stewpan  nearly  a  pint  of  veal  gravy  or  of 
very  good  broth,  lay  in  the  nsh,  and  stew  it  for  five  minutes,  then 
add  four  tablespoonsful  of  extremely  fine  bread-crumbs,  and  simmer 
it  for  three  minutes  longer.  Stir  well  into  the  sauce  a  large  tea- 
spoonful  of  arrow-root  quite  free  from  lumps,  a  fourth  part  as  much 
of  mace,  something  less  of  cayenne,  and  a  tablespoonful  of  essence  of 
anchovies,  mixed  with  a  slass  of  white  wine  and  a  dessertspoonful  of 
lemon  juice.  Boil  the  whole  for  a  couple  of  minutes,  lift  out  the  fidi 
carefully  with  a  slice,  pour  the  sauce  over,  and  serve  it  quickly. 

Cod  fish,  3  lbs. :  boiled  5  minutes.  Gravy,  or  strong  broth,  nearly 
1  pint:  5  minutes.  Bread-crumbs,  4  tablespoonsful:  3  minutes.

will  to  many  tastes  vary  it  very  agreeably. 

STEWED   COD   FISH,   IN   BROWN  SAUCE. 

Slice  the  fish,  take  off  the  skin,  flour  it  well,  and  fry  it  quickly  a 
fine  brown ;  lift  it  out  and  drain  it  on  the  back  of  a  sieve,  arrange 
it  in  a  clean  stevrpan,  and  pour  in  as  much  good  boiling  brown  gravy 
as  will  nearly  cover  it ;  add  from  one  to  two  glasses  of  port  wine, 
or  rather  more  of  claret,  a  dessertspoonful  of  Cnili  vinegar,  or  the 
juice  of  half  a  lemon,  and  some  cayenne,  with  as  much  salt  as  may  be 
needed.  Stew  the  fish  very  softly  until  it  just  begins  to  break,  lut  it 
carefully  with  a  slice  into  a  very  hot  dish,  stir  into  the  gra^y  an 
ounce  and  a  half  of  butter  smoothly  kneaded  with  a  large  teaspoonfol 
of  flour,  and  a  little  pounded  mace,  give  the  sauce  a  minute's  boil, 
pour  it  over  the  fish,  and  serve  it  immediately.  The  wine  may  be 
omitted,  good  shin  of  beef  stock  substituted  for  the  gravy,  and  a' tea- 
spoonful of  soy,  one  of  essence  of  anchovies,  and  two  tablespoonsful 
of  Uarvey*s  sauce  added  to  flavour  it. 

TO   BOIL  SALT   FISH. 

When  very  salt  and  dry,  this  must  be  long  soaked  before  it  is 
boiled,  but  it  is  generally  supplied  by  the  fishmongers  nearly  or  quite 
ready  to  dress.     When  it  is  not  so,  lay  it  for  a  ni^ht  into  a  large 

Suantity  of  cold  water,  then  let  it  lie  exposed  to  the  air  for  some  time, 
len  again  put  it  into  water,  and  continue  thus  until  it  is  well

softened  Brush  it  very  clean,  wash  it  thoroughly,  and  put  it  with

SALT   FISH,   X  LA   mIiTRB   d'HOTEL. 

Boil  the  fish  by  the  foregoing  receipt,  or  take  the  remains  of  that 
which  has  been  served  at  table,  flake  it  off  clear  from  the  bones,  and 
strip  away  every  morsel  of  the  skin ;  then  lay  it  into  a  very  clean 
saucepan  or  stewpan,  and  pour  upon  it  the  sharp  MaUre  d  Hotel 
sauce  of  Chapter  IV. ;  or  dissolve  gently  two  or  three  ounces  of 
butter  with  four  or  five  spoonsful  of  water,  and  a  half-teaspoonful  of 
floor;  add  some  pepper  or  cayenne,  very  little  salt,  and  a  dessert- 
spoonful or  more  oi  minoed  parsley.  Heat  the  fish  slowly  quite 
tnrough  in  either  of  these  sauces,  and  toss  or  stir  it  until  the  whole 
is  well  mixed ;  if  the  second  be  used,  add  the  juice  of  half  a  lemon, 
or  a  small  ammtity  of  Chili  vinegar  just  before  it  is  taken  from  the 
file.  The  nsh  ^us  prepared  may  be  served  in  a  deep  dish,  with  a 
border  of  mashed  parsneps  or  potatoes. 

TO   BOIL   CODS*   SOUNDS. 

Should  they  be  highly  salted,  soak  them  for  a  night,  and  on  the 
following  day  rub  off  entirely  the  discoloured  skin ;  wash  them  well, 
lay  them  into  plenty  of  cold  milk  and  water,  and  boil  them  gently 
from  thirty  to  forty  minutes,  or  longer  should  they  not  be  quite 
tender.  Clear  off  the  scum  as  it  rises  with  great  care,  or  it  will  sink 
and  adhere  to  the  sounds,  of  which  the  appearance  will  then  be 
epmled.  Drain  them  well,  dish  them  on  a  napkin,  and  send  egg 
nuoe  and  plain  melted  butter  to  table  with  them. 

TO  FRY  cods'   bounds   IN   BATTER. 

Boil  them  as  directed  above  until  they  are  nearly  done,  then  lift  them 
out,  lay  them  on  to  a  drainer,  and  let  them  remain  till  they  are  cold : 
cut  them  across  in  stripe  of  an  inch  deep,  curl  them  round,  dip  them 
into  a  good  French  or  English  batter,  fry  them  of  a  fine  pale  brown* 
.dnin  and  dry  them  well,  dish  them  on  a  hot  napkin,  and  garnish 
them  with  cnsped  parsley.

TO  FRY  SOLES. 
[In  season  all  the  year.] 

All  fish  to  fry  well  must  be  not  only  fresh  but  perfectly  free  from 
moisture,  particularly  when  they  are  to  be  drened  with  egg  and 
bread-crumbs,  as  these  will  not  otherwise  adhere  to  them.  Empty, 
skin,  and  wash  the  soles  with  extreme  nicety,  from  one  to  two  hours 
before  they  are  wanted  for  table ;  and  afler  having  cleansed  and  wiped 
them  very  dry  both  inside  and  out,  replace  the  roes,  fold  and  press 
them  gently  in  a  soft  clean  cloth,  and  leave  them  wrapped  in  it  until 
it  is  time  to  fry  them ;  or  suspend  them  singly  upon  hooks  in  a  current 
of  cool  air,  wnich  is,  perhaps,  the  better  method  of  proceeding  when 
it  can  be  done  convemently.  Cover  them  equally  in  every  part,  first 
with  some  beaten  egg,  and  then  with  fine  dry  crumbs  of  bread,  mixed 
with  a  very  little  flour  to  make  them  adhere  with  more  certainty :  a 
small  teaspoonful  will  be  sufficient  for  two  large  soles.  Melt  in  a 
large  and  exceedingly  clean  firing  pan  over  a  brisk  and  clear  fire,  as 
much  very  pure-flavoured  lard  as  will  float  the  fish,  and  let  it  be  suffi- 
ciently hot  before  they  are  laid  in  to  brown  them  quickly;  for  if 
this  bie  neglected  it  will  be  impossible  to  render  them  crisp  or  dry. 
When  the  fat  ceases  to  bubble,  throw  in  a  small  bit  of  bread,  and 
if  it  takes  a  good  colour  immediately  the  soles  may  be  put  in  vdthout 
delay.  An  experienced  cook  will  Know,  without  this  test,  when  it  is 
at  the  proper  point;  but  the  learner  will  do  better  to  avail  herself  of 
it  until  practice  and  observation  shall  have  rendered  it  unnecessary  to 
her.  Before  the  fish  are  laid  into  the  pan,  take  them  by  the  head 
and  shake  the  loose  crumbs  from  them.  When  they  are  firm,  and 
of  a  fine  amber-colour  on  one  side,  turn  them  with  care,  passing  a 
slice  under  them  and  a  fork  through  the  heads,  and  brown  them  on 
the  other.  Lift  them  out,  and  either  dry  them  well  on  a  soft  cloth 
laid  upon  a  sieve  reversed,  before  the  fire,  turning  them  often,  or 
press  them  lightly  in  hot  white  blotting  paper.  Dish  them  on  a 
drainer  covered  with  a  hot  napkin  and  send  them  to  table  without 
delay  with  shrimp  or  anchovy  sauce,  and  plain  melted  butter. 

Very  small  soles  will  be  done  in  six  minutes,  and  large  ones  in 
about  ten.  They  may  be  floured  and  fried,  without  being  ^ged  and 
crumbed,  but  this  is  not  a  very  usual  mode  of  serving  them. 

Small  soles,  6  minutes ;  large,  about  10  minutes. 

TO   BOIL  SOLES. 

The  flesh  of  a  fine  fresh  sole,  when  boiled  with  care,  is  remarkably 
sweet  and  delicate :  if  very  large  it  may  be  dressed  and  served  as  turbot, 
to  which  it  will  be  found  little  inferior  in  flavour.  Empty  it,  take  out 
the  gills,  cut  ofif  the  fins,  and  cleanse  and  wash  it  wiUi  great  nicety, 
but  do  not  skin  it ;  then  either  lay  it  into  cold  water  in  wh^  ^e  usual 
proportion  of  salt  has  been  dissolved,  and  heat  it  rather  slowly,  and 
then  simmer  it  from  five  to  ten  minutes,  according  to  its  nw ;  or  boil 


oup.  n.j  ViSHt  65 

it  in  the  manner  directed  in  tlie  first  pages  of  this  chapter.  Brain  it 
veil  on  the  fish-plate  as  it  is  lifted  ont,  and  dish  it  on  a  napkin,  the 
white  aide  upwards,  and  serve  it  qoickly  with  anchovy,  shrimp,  or 
lobster  sance.  It  may  also  he  sent  to  tahle  thickly  covered  witn  the 
Ciesm  ilsh  Sauce,  Caper  Fish  Sance,  or  Lady's  Sauce,  of  Chapter  VI. ; 
though  this  is  a  mode  of  service  less  to  be  recommended,  as  the  sauce 
cook  more  speedily  when  spread  over  the  surface  of  the  fish :  it  is, 
howerer,  the  continental  fasnion,  and  will  therefore  find  more  favour 
vith  some  persons. 

Yery  large    sole,  5   to   10  minutes  ;  moderate  siied,  4  to  6 
nmmtes. 

FILLETS  OF  SOLES. 

The  word  fSUt^  whether  applied  to  fish,  poultry,  game,  or  butcher's 
meat,  means  simply  the  flesh  of  either  (or  of  certain  portions  of  it), 
nised  clear  from  the  bones  in  a  handsome  form,  and  divided  or  not, 
M  the  manner  in  which  it  is  to  be  served  may  require.  It  is  an 
el^ant  mode  of  dressing  various  kinds  of  fish,  and  even  those  which 
sre  not  the  most  highly  esteemed,  afford  an  excellent  dish  when  thus 
pxeptred.  Soles  to  be  filletted  with  advantage  should  be  large ;  the 
oesn  may  then  be  divided  down  the  middle  of  the  back,  next,  sepa- 
nted  from  the  fins,  and  with  a  very  sharp  knife  raised  clear  from  the 
hones.*  When  thus  prepared,  the  fillets  may  be  divided,  trinuned 
hito  a  good  form,  egged,  covered  with  fine  crumbs,  fried  in  the  usual 
«vay,and  served  wiSi  the  same  sauces  as  the  whole  fish ;  or  each  fillet 
nay  he  rolled  up,  in  its  entire  length,  if  very  small,  or  after  being 
onoe  divided  if  la^i^  and  fastened  with  a  slight  twine,  or  a  short  thin 
skewer;  dien  egged,  crumbed,  and  fried  in  plenty  of  boiling  lard ;  or 
merely  well  floured  and  fried  from  eight  to  ten  minutes.  When  the 
fish  are  not  very  large,  they  are  sometimes  boned  without  being 
parted  in  the  middle,  and  each  side  is  rolled  from  the  tail  to  the  head, 
>fter  being  first  spr«d  with  pounded  shrimps  mixed  with  a  third  of 
their  volume  of  butter,  a  few  bread-crumbs,  and  a  hish  seasoning  of 
mace  and  cayenne ;  or  with  pounded  lobster  mixed  with  a  large  por- 
tion of  the  coral,  and  the  same  seasoniuff,  and  proportion  of  butter  as 
the  shrimps ;  then  laid  into  a  dish,  wiUi  the  maredients  directed  for 
the  kIUm  au  plat;  well  covered  with  crumbs  of  bread  and  clarified 
hotter,  and  baked  from  twelve  to  sixteen  minutes,  or  until  the  crumbs 
ue  coloured  to  a  fine  brown  in  a  moderate  oven. 

The  fillets  may  likewise  be  cut  into  small  strips  or  squares  of  uni- 
form sixe,  lightly  dredged  with  pepper  or  cayenne,  salt  and  flour,  and 
fiied  in  butter  over  a  brisk  fire ;  then  well  drained,  and  sauced  with 
«  good  heekoatel^  flavoured  with  a  teaspoonful  of  minced  parsley.

SOLES  AU  PLAT. 

Clftrifjr  from  two  to  three  ounces  of  fresh  butter,  and  poor  it  into

glasses  of  sherry,  or  of  any  other  dry  white  wine ;  lay  in  a  couple  of

01$. — The  soles  are,  we  think,  better  without  the  wine  in  this

BAKED  SOLES. 

(A  simple  but  excellent  Receipt.) 

Fresh  large  soles,  dressed  in  the  following  manner,  are  remarkaUy 
tender  and  delicate  eating ;  much  more  so  than  those  which  are  fined. 
After  the  fish  has  been  skinned  and  cleansed  in  the  usual  way,  wipe  it 
dry,  and  let  it  remain  for  an  hour  or  more,  if  time  will  permit,  closely 
folded  in  a  clean  cloth ;  then  mix  with  a  slightly  beaten  egg  about  an 
ounce  of  butter,  just  liquefied  but  not  heated  at  the  mouth  of  the 
oven,  or  before  the  fire ;  brush  the  fish  in  every  part  with  this  mix- 
ture, and  cover  it  with  very  fine  dry  bread-crumbs,  seasoned  with  a 
little  salt,  cayenne,  pounded  mace,  and  nutmeg.     Pour  a  teaspoon^ol 
or  two  of  li<juid  butter  into  a  flat  dish  which  will  contain  the  fish 
well ;  lay  it  m,  sprinkle  it  with  a  little  more  butter,  press  the  bread- 
crumbs lightly  on  it  with  a  broad-bladed  knife,  and  bake  it  in  & 
moderate  oven  for  about  twenty  minutes.    If  two  or  more  soles  are 
required  for  table  at  the  same  time,  they  should  be  placed  separatdy, 
quite  flat,  in  a  large  dish,  or  each  fish  should  be  laid  on  a  dish  by 
itself.    ()n  our  first  essay  of  this  receipt,  the  fish  dressed  by  it  (it  vas 
baked  for  twenty-five  minutes  in  a  very  slack  iron  oven)  proved  in- 
finitely nicer  than  one  of  the  same  size  which  was  fried,  and  served 
with  it.     The  difierence  between  them  was- very  marked,  especially 
as  regarded  the  exceeding  tenderness  of  the  flesh  of  that  which  ima 
baked;  its  appearance,  however,  would  have  been  somewhat  im. 

S roved  by  a  rather  quicker  oven.  When  ready  to  serve,  it  should 
e  gently  glided  on  to  the  dish  in  which  it  is  to  be  sent  to  table. 
About  three  ounces  of  bread-crumbs,  and  two  and  a  half  of  butter, 
will  be  sufficient  for  a  large  pair  of  soles.    They  will  be  more  per-

Mj  eaerosted  with  the  bread  if  dipped  into,  or  tprinkled  with  it  a 
Koood  time,  after  the  first  coating  has  been  well  moistened  with  the 

batter. 

SOLES  STEWED  IX  CBEAaf. 

Prepare  some  very  fresh  middling  8i2ed  soles  with  exceeding  nicety, 
imt  them  into  boiliof  water  slightly  salted,  and  simmer  them  for  two 
mmiites  only ;  lift  tnem  out,  and  let  them  drain ;  lay  them  into  a 
wide  stewpan  with  as  much  sweet  rich  cream  as  wm  nearly  cover 
them ;  add  a  good  seasoning  of  pounded  maoe,  cayenne,  and  salt ;  stew 
the  fish  softly  from  six  to  ten  minutes,  or  until  tne  flesh  parts  readily 
from  the  bones ;  dish  them,  stir  the  juice  of  half  a  lemon  to  the 
■mce,  poor  it  oyer  the  soles,  and  send  them  immediately  to  table. 
Some  lemon-iind  may  be  boiled  in  the  cream,  if  approved ;  and  a 
anall  teaspoonful  of  arrow-root,  very  smoothly  mixed  with  a  little 
xoilk,  may  be  stirred  to  the  sauce  (should  it  require  thickening)  before 
the  Jemon-juice  is  added.  Turbot  and  brill  also  may  be  dressed  by 
thii  receipt,  time  proportioned  to  their  size  being  of  course  allowed 
fixrthem. 

Soles,  3  or  4:  boiled  in  water  2  minutes.  Cream,  ^  to  whole 
pint;  salt,  noaoe,  cayenne:  fish  stewed,  6  to  10  minutes.  Juice  of 
half  a  lemon. 

Ohs,— In  Cornwall  the  fish  is  laid  at  once  into  thick  clotted  cream, 
and  stewed  entirely  in  it ;  but  this  method  gives  to  the  sauce,  which 
ooght  to  be  extremely  delicate,  a  coarse  fishy  flavour  which  the  pre- 
^ioos  boil  in  water  prevents. 

At  Penzance,  grey  mullet,  after  being  scaled,  are  divided  in  the 
niddle,  Just  covered  with  cold  water,  and  softly  boiled,  with  the  ad- 
ditum  of  branches  of  parsley,  pepper  and  salt,  until  the  flesh  of  the 
hack  parts  easdly  from  the  bone ;  clotted  cream,  minced  parsley,  and 
lemoQ-jmce  are  then  added  to  the  sauce,  and  the  mullets  are  dished 
with  the  heads  and  tails  laid  even  to  the  thick  parts  of  the  back, 
where  the  fish  were  cut  asunder.  Hake,  too,  is  there  divided  at  every 
joint  (having  previously  been  scaled),  dipped  into  egg,  then  thickly 
covered  with  fine  bread-crumbs  mixed  witn  plenty  of  minced  parsley, 
sod  filed  a  fine  brown ;  or,  the  back-bone  being  previously  taken 
oat,  the  fish  is  sliced  into  cutlets,  and  then  fried. 

TO  FHY  WHITINGS. 
[In  foil  season  firom  MiohaelmaB  to  beginning  of  Febraftry.l 

Clean,  skin,  and  dry  them  thoroughly  in  a  cloth,  fasten  their  tails

FILLETS   OF   WHITINGS. 

Empty  and  wash  thoroughly,  but  do  not  skin  the  fish.  Take  off 
the  fiesh  on  both  sides  close  to  the  bones,  passing  the  knife  from  the 
tail  to  the  head ;  divide  each  side  in  two,  trim  the  fillets  into  good 
shape,  and  fold  them  in  a  cloth,  that  the  moisture  may  be  well 
absorbed  from  them ;  dip  them  into,  or  draw  them  through,  some 
beaten  egg,  then  dip  them  into  fine  crumbs  mixed  with  a  small  por- 
tion of  flour,  and  fry  them  a  fine  light  brown  in  lard  or  clanfied 
butter ;  drain  them  well,  press  them  in  white  blotting-paper,  dish 
them  one  over  the  other  in  a  circle,  and  send  the  usual  sauce  to  table 
with  them.  The  fillets  may  also  be  broiled  afler  being  dipped  into 
eggs  seasoned  with  salt  and  pepper,  then  into  crumbs  or  bre«d,  next 
into  clarified  butter,  and  a  second  time  into  the  bread-crumbs  (or,  to 
shorten  the  process,  a  portion  of  clarified  butter  may  be  mixed  with 
the  eggs  at  first),  and  served  with  good  melted  butter,  or  thickened 
veal  gravy  seasoned  with  cayenne,  lemon-juice,  and  chopped  parsley. 

Five  minutes  will  fry  the  fillets,  even  when  very  laige :  rather 
more  time  will  be  required  to  broil  them.

lO  BOIL   MACKEREL. 

[In  fan  aeMQn  in  May,  Jane,  and  July ;  may  be  had  also  in  early  spring.] 

Open  the  fish  sufficiently  to  admit 
of  the  insides  being  perfecuy  cleansed^ 
hut  not  more  than  is  necessary  for 
this  purpose ;  empty  them  with  care, 
lay  the  roes  apart,  and  wash  both 
them  and  the  mackerel  delicately 
clean.  It  in  customary  now  to  lay  these,  and  the  greater  number  of 
other  fish  as  well,  into  cold  water  when  they  are  to  be  boiled ;  for- 
merly all  were  plunged  at  once  into  fast-boiling  water.  For  such  as 
are  small  and  delicate,  it  should  be  hot;  they  should  be  brought 
gently  to  boil,  and  simmered  until  they  are  done ;  the  scum  should 
be  cleared  off  as  it  rises,  and  the  usual  proportion  of  salt  stirred  into 
the  water  before  the  mackerel  are  put  in.  The  roes  are  commonly 
replaced  in  the  fish ;  hut  as  they  sometimes  require  more  boiling  than 
the  mackerel  themselves,  it  is  better,  when  they  are  very  large,  to 
lay  them  upon  the  fish-plate  by  their  sides.  From  fifteen  to  twenty 
mmutes  will  generally  be  sufficient  to  boil  a  full-sized  mackerel 
some  wiU  he  done  in  less  time ;  but  they  must  be  watched  and  lifted 
ant  as  soon  as  the  tails  split,  and  the  eyes  are  starting. 

Dish  them  on  a  napKin,  and  send  fennel  or  gooseberry  sauce  to 
table  with  them,  and  plain  melted  butter  also. 
Small  mackerel,  10  to  15  minutes ;  large,  15  to  20  minutes. 

TO  BAKE  MACKEREL.

derate oven  wfll  hake  them.  Oyster  forcemeat  is  always  appropriate 
ibr  any  kind  of  fish  which  is  in  season  while  the  oysters  are  so ;  but  the

BAKED   MACKEREL,   OB  WHITINGS. 

(jCindereUcCs  Receipt— good,) 

The  fish  for  this  receipt  should  be  opened  only  so  mnch  as  will 
permit  of  their  being  emptied  and  perfectly  cleansed.  Wash  and 
\7ipe  them  dry,  then  rold  them  in  a  soft  doth,  and  let  them  remain  in 
it  awhile.  Replace  the  roes,  and  put  the  fish  into  a  baking-dish  of 
suitable  size,  with  a  tablespoonful  of  wine,  a  few  drops  of  cnili  vine* 
gar,  a  little  salt  and  cayenne,  and  about  half  an  ounce  of  butter,  well- 
blended  with  a  saltspoonful  of  flour,  for  each  fish.  They  must  be 
turned  round  with  the  heads  and  tails  towards  each  other,  that  they 
may  lie  compactly  in  the  dish,  and  the  backs  should  be  placed  down- 
wards, that  the  sauce  may  surround  the  thickest  part  of  the  fiesh. 
Lay  two  buttered  papers  over,  and  press  them  down  upon  them ;  set 
the  dish  into  a  gentle  oven  for  twenty  minutes,  take  off  the  papers, 
and  send  the  fish  to  table  in  their  sauce. 

A  few  minutes  more  of  time  must  be  allowed  for  mackerel  when  it 
is  large,  should  the  oven  be  very  slow. 

Full-sized  whitings  are  excellent  thus  dressed  if  carefully  managed, 
and  many  eaters  would  infinitely  prefer  mackerel  so  prepared,  to 
boiled  ones.  The  writer  has  port -wine  always  used  for  tne  sauce,  to 
which  a  rather  full  seasoning  of  chili  vinegar,  cayenne,  and  pounded 
mace,  is  added ;  but  shen^,  Bucellas,  or  any  other  dry  wine,  can  be 
used  instead ;  and  the  various  condiments  added  to  it,  can  be  varied 
to  the  taste.  This  receipt  is  a  very  convenient  one,  as  it  is  prepared 
with  little  trouble,  and  a  stove-oven,  if  the  heat  be  properly  mode- 
rated, will  answer  for  the  baking.  It  is  an  advantage  to  take  off 
the  heads  of  the  fish  before  they  are  dressed,  and  they  may  then  be 
entirely  emptied  without  being  opened.  When  preferred  so,  they 
can  be  re-dished  for  table,  and  me  sauce  poured  over  them. 

Ohs, — The  dish  in  which  they  are  bakei,  should  be  buttered  before 
they  are  laid  in. 

FRIED  MACKEREL. 

(jConmum  French  Receipt,) 

After  the  fish  have  been  emptied  and  washed  extremely  clean,  cut 
off  the  heads  and  tails,  split  the  bodies  quite  open,  and  take  out  tlie 
backbones  (we  recommend  in  preference  that  the  flesh  shoold  be 
taken  off  the  bones  as  in  the  following  receipt),  wipe  the  mackerel 
very  dry,  dust  fine  salt  and  pepper  (or  cayenne)  over  them,  floor 
them  well,  fry  them  a  fine  brown  in  boiling  lard,  drain  them  tlio- 
roughly,  and  serve  them  with  the  following  sauce : — ^Dissolve  in.  a 
small  saucepan  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  butter  smoothly  mixed  with  a 
teaspoonful  of  fiour,  some  salt,  pepper,  or  cayenne;    shake  these 
over  a  gentle  fire  until  they  are  lightly  coloured,  then  add  by  slow 
degrees  nearly  half  a  pint  of  good  broth  or  gravy,  and  Ae  juice   of 
one  large  lemon ;  boil  the  sauce  for  a  couple  of  mmutes,  and  serve   it

Toy  hot.  Or,  instead  of  this,  add  a  laige  teaspoanful  of  strong  mada

of  mushroom  cateap  can  he  mixed  irith  this  last  at  pleasure. 

FILLRT8  OF  MACKEREL. 

(Fried  or  Broiled.) 

Take  off  the  flesh  quite  whole  on  either  side,  from  three  fine 
mackerel,  which  have  heen  opened  and  properly  cleaned ;  let  it  be 
entiielj  free  from  bone,  drj  it  well  in  a  cloth,  then  divide  each  part 
in  two,  and  dip  tham  into  the  beaten  yolks  of  a  couple  of  eggs,  sea- 
soned with  salt  and  white  pepper,  or  cayenne ;  cover  them  equdiy  with 
fine  dry  crumbs  of  braid,  and  fry  them  like  soles ;  or  dip  them  into 
clarified  butter,  and  then  again  into  the  crumbs,  and  broU  them  over 
a  rery  dear  fire  of  a  fine  brown.  Dish  them  in  a  circle  one  over  the 
other,  and  send  them  to  table  with  the  Mditre  d*  Hotel  sauce  of 
Chapter  V.,  or  with  the  one  which  follows  it.  The  French  pour  the 
amoe  into  the  centre  of  the  dish ;  but  for  broiled  fillets  this  is  not  so 
wd],  we  think,  as  serving  it  in  a  tureen.  The  roes  of  the  fish,  after 
being  well  washed  and  soaked,  may  be  dressed  with  them,  or  they 
may  be  made  into  patties.  Minced  parsley  can  be  mixed  with  the 
brad  cmmbs  when  it  is  liked. 

BOILED   FILLETS   OF  MACKEREL. 

After  having  taken  off  and  divided  the  flesh  of  the  fish,  as  above, 
place  it  flat  in  one  layer  in  a  wide  stewpan  or  saucepan^  and  just  cover 
the  fillets  with  cold  water ;  throw  in  a  teaspoonf ul  of  adt,  and  two  or 
three  small  sprigs  of  parsley ;  bring  the  mackerel  slowly  to  a  boil, 
dear  off  the  scum  with  care,  and  after  two  or  three  minutes  of  slow 
ammering  try  the  fillets  with  a  fork ;  if  the  thick  part  divides  with  a 
tOQch,  they  are  done.  Lift  them  out  cautiously  with  a  slice ;  drain, 
and  serve  them  very  hot  with  good  parsley  and  butter ;  or  strip  off 
the  akin  qmddy,  and  pour  a  Maiire  a*  Hotel  sauce  over  them. 

M ACiroEFL  BROILED  WHOLE. 

(An  excellent  Receipt.") 

Empty  and  cleanse  perfectly  a  fine  and  very  fresh  mackerel,  but 
without  opening  it  more  than  is  needful ;  dry  it  well,  either  in  a  cloth 
or  by  hanging  it  in  a  cool  air  until  it  is  stiff;  make  with  a  sharp 
knife  a  deep  incision  the  whole  length  of  the  fish  on  either  side  of  the 
back  bone,  and  about  half  an  inch  nom  it,  and  with  a  feather  put  in 
I  little  cayenne  and  fine  salt,  mixed  with  a  few  drops  of  good  salad 
oil  or  danfied  butter.  Lay  the  mackerel  over  a  moderate  fire  upon 
awdl-heated  ^diron  wmch  has  been  rubbed  with  suet ;  loosen  it 
gently  should  it  stick,  which  it  will  do  unless  often  moved ;  and  when 
tt  b  equally  done  on  both  sides,  tun  the  back  to  tiie  fire.    About

MACKEREL  BTEWEP  WITH   WIKB. 

(Verffgood,) 

Work  very  smoothly  together  a  large  teaspoonful  of  flour  with  two 
ounces  of  butter,  put  them  into  a  stewpan,  and  stir  or  shake  them 
round  over  the  fire  until  the  butter  is  dissolved ;  add  a  quarter  of  a 
teaspoonful  of  mace,  twice  as  much  salt,  and  some  cayenne ;  pour  in 
by  slow  degrees  three  glasses  of  claret ;  and  when  the  sauce  boils,  lay 
in  a  couple  of  fine  mackerel  well  cleaned,  and  wiped  quite  dry ;  stew 
them  very  softly  from  fifteen  to  twenty  minutes,  and  turn  them  when 
half  done ;  lift  them  out,  and  dish  Uiem  carefully ;  stir  a  teaspoonful 
of  made  mustard  to  the  sauce,  give  it  a  boil,  and  pour  it  over  the 
fish.  When  more  convenient,  substitute  port  wme  and  a  little 
lemon-juice,  for  the  claret. 

Mackerel,  2 ;  flour,  1  teaspoonful ;  butter,  2  oz. ;  seasoning  of  salt,

spoonful :  15  to  20  minutes.
