OOmiON  TOMATA  BAUOB. 

Tomatas  are  so  juicy  when  ripe  that  they  require  little  or  no  liquid 
to  reduce  them  to  a  proper  consistence  for  sauce ;  and  the^  vary  so 
exceedingly  in  «ze  and  quality  that  it  is  difficult  to  gire  precise  direc- 
tions for  the  exact  quantity  which  in  their  unripe  state  is  needed  for 
them.  Take  off  tbe  stalks,  halve  the  tomatas,  and  gently  squeeze 
out  the  seeds  and  watery  pulp;  then  stew  them  softly  with  a  few 
spoonsful  of  gravy  or  of  strong  broth  until  they  are  quite  melted. 
Press  the  whole  through  a  hair-sieve,  and  heat  it  afresh  with  a  little 
additional  gravy  should  it  be  too  thick,  and  some  cayenne,  and  salt. 
Serve  it  very  hot. 

F^e  ripe  tomatas,  6  or  8 ;  gravy  or  strong  broth,  4  tablespoonsu 
ful:  i  to  )  hour,  or  longer  if  needed.  Salt  and  cayenne  sufficient 
u>  seasoa  the  sauce,  and  two  or  three  spoonsful  more  of  gra?y  if 
reared. 

Obt* — ^For  a  large  tureen  of  this  sance,  increase  the  proportions ; 
and  ahould  it  be  at  first  too  liquid,  reduce  it  by  quick  boiling.  When 
neither  ^avy  nor  broth  is  at  hand,  the  tomatas  may  be  stewed  per*' 
fleet}/  tender,  but  very  gently,  in  a  couple  of  ounces  of  butter,  with 
aome  cayenne  and  salt  only,  or  with  the  addition  of  a  very  little  finely 
misoed  onion ;  then  rubbed  through  a  sieve,  and  heated,  and  served 
vithoat  any  addition,  or  with  only  that  of  a  teaspoonful  of  chili 
naegrr  ;  or,  whai  the  cobur  is  not  a  principal  oonaideration,  with  a 
few  apoonsfol  of  rich  cream,  amoothlj  mixed  with  a  little  flour  to

prevent  its  curdling^.    The  sauce  must  be  stirred  without  ceasixig 
should  the  last  be  added,  and  boiled  for  four  or  five  minutes. 

▲  FINEB  TOMATA  8AUCE. 

Stew  very  g;entlj  a  dosen  fine  red  tomatas,  prepared  as  for  the  pre- 
ceding receipt,  with  two  or  three  sliced  eschalots,  four  or  five  chilies  or 
A  capsicum  or  two  (or  in  lieu  of  either,  with  a  quarter  of  a  teaspoonful 
of  cayenne  pepper),  a  few  small  dice  of  lean  ham,  and  half  a  cupful 
of  rich  fiT^avy.  Stir  these  often,  and  when  the  tomatas  are  reduced 
quite  to  a  smooth  pulp,  rub  them  through  a  sieve ;  put  them  into  a

noltj  add  salt  if  needed,  boil  the  sauce  stirrinp;  it  well  for  ten  minutes,

be  added  with  them.  The  exact  proportion  or  liquid  used  is  imma* 
terial,  for  should  the  sauce  be  too  thin  it  may  be  reduced  by  rapid 
boiling,  and  diluted  with  more  gravy  if  too  thick. 

BOILEO  APPLE  SAUCB. 

Apples  of  a  fine  cooking  sort  require  but  a  very  small  portion  of 
liquid  to  boil  down  well  and  smoothly  for  sauce,  if  placed  orer  a 
gentle  fire  in  a  close-shutting  saucepan,  and  simmered  as  softly  as 
possible  until  they  are  well  broken ;  and  their  flavour  is  injured  by 
the  common  mode  of  adding  so  much  to  them,  that  the  greater  part 
must  be  drained  off  again  before  they  are  sent  to  table.  Pare  the 
fruit  quickly,  quarter  it,  and  be  careful  entirely  to  remove  the  cores ; 
put  one  tablespoonful  of  water  into  a  saucepan  before  the  apples  are 
thrown  in,  and  proceed,  as  we  have  directed,  to  simmer  them  uutil 
they  are  nearly  ready  to  serve :  finish  the  sauce  by  the  receipt  which 
follows. 

Apples,  \  lb. ;  water,  1  tablespoonful ;  stewed  very  softly :  30  to  60 
minutes. 

Oha, — These  proportions  are  sufficient  only  for  a  small  tureen  of 
the  sauce,  and  should  be  doubled  for  a  large  one. 

For  this,  and  all  other  preparations,  apples  will  be  whiter  if  just 
dipped  into  fresh  water  the  instant  before  the^r  are  put  into  the  stew- 
piin.  They  should  be  quickly  lifted  from  it,  and  will  stew  down 
easily  to  sauce  with  only  the  moisture  which  hangs  about  thenu  Tliey 
should  be  watched  and  often  gently  stirred,  that  they  may  be  equally 
done.

WHITB  ONION  SAUCE. 

Strip  the  skin  from  some  large  white  onions,  and  after  baring 
tsken  off  the  tops  and  roots  cut  them  in  two,  throw  them  into  cold 
water  as  they  are  done,  cover  them  plentifully  with  more  water,  and 
boil  them  rery  tender ;  lift  them  out,  drain,  and  then  press  the  water 
thoroughly  from  them ;  chop  them  small,  rub  them  through  a  sieve 
or  strainer,  put  them  into  a  little  rich  melted  butter  mixed  with  a 
spoonful  or  two  of  cream  or  milk,  and  a  seasoning  of  salt,  give  tlie 
sauce  a  boil,  and  serve  it  very  hot.  Portugal  onions  are  superior  to 
aoy  others,  both  for  this  and  for  most  other  purposes  of  cookery. 

For  the  finest  kind  of  onion  sauce^  see  Soubise,  which  follows. 

BBOWlf  OBION  SAUCB. 

Cut  off  both  ends  of  the  onions,  and  slice  them  into  a  saucepan  in 
which  two  ounces  of  butter  hare  been  dissolved ;  keep  them  stewing 
gently  over  a  dear  fire  until  they  are  lightly  Colourea ;  then  pour  to 
them  half  a  pint  of  brown  gravy,  and  when  they  have  boiled  until 
they  are  perfectly  tender,  work  the  sauce  altogether  through  a  strainer, 
season  it  with  a  little  cayenne,  and  serve  it  rery  hot.

SOUBISE. 

(English  Receipt) 

Skin,  slice,  and  mince  quickly  two  pounds'  weight  of  the  white 
part  only  of  some  fine  mild  onions,  and  stew  them  in  from  two  to 
three  ounces  of  good  butter  over  a  very  gentle  fire  until  they  are 
reduced  to  a  pulp,  then  pour  to  them  three-quarters  of  a  pint  of  rich 
Teal  gravy ;  add  a  seasoning  of  salt  and  cayenne,  if  needed ;  skim  off 
the  fat  entirely,  press  the  sauce  through  a  sieve,  heat  it  in  a  clean 
stewpan,  mix  it  with  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  rich  boiling  cream,  and 
serve  it  directly. 

Onions,  2  lbs. ;  butter,  2  to  3  oz. :  30  minutes  to  1  hour.  Teal 
gr^^h  i  P^^^ »  ^^  cayenne :  6  minutes.     Cream,  I  pint. 

S0UBI8E* 

(French  Receipt,) 

Peel  some  fine  white  onions,  and  trim  away  all  tough  and  dis* 
coloured  parts;  mince  them  small,  and  throw  them  into  plenty  of 
boiling  water ;  when  they  have  boiled  quickly  for  five  minutes  drain 
them  well  in  a  sieve,  then  stew  them  very  softly  indeed  in  an  ounce 
or  two  of  fresh  butter  until  they  are  dry  and  perfectly  tender ;  stir 
to  them  as  much  bechamel  as  will  bring  them  to  the  consi^stence  of 
very  thick  pea-soup,  pass  the  whole  through  a  strainer,  pressing  the 
onion  strongly  that  none  maj^  remain  behind,  and  heat  the  sauce 
afresh,  without  allowing  it  to  buiL  A  small  half-teaspoonful  of 
pounded  sugar  is  sometimes  added  to  this  aonbise* 

White  part  of  onions,  2  lbs. :  blanched  5  minutes.  Butter,  2  ox. : 
30  to  50  minutes.    Bechamel,  }  to  I  pint,  or  more. 

Ohs, — These  sauces  are  served  more  frequently  with  lamb  or  mut- 
ton cutlets  than  with  any  other  dishes ;  but  they  would  probably 
find  many  approvers  if  sent  to  table  wilh  roast  mutton,  or  boiled 
veal.  Half  the  quantity  given  above  will  be  sufficient  for  a  moderato- 
fiiaed  dish.

MILD  ESCOALOT  SAUCE. 

Prepare  and  boil  from  half  to  a  whole  pint  of  eschalots  by  the 
preceoiug  receipt;  unless  very  large,  they  will  be  tender  in  about 
fifteen  minnte^s  sometimes  in  less,  in  which  case  the  water  roust  be 
yoared  from  them  shortly  after  it  has  been  changed  for  the  second 
tnne.  When  grown  in  a  suitable  soil,  and  cultivated  with  care,  the 
ttcbalots  are  sometimes  treble  the  size  that  they  are  under  other  cir- 
coDBtances ;  and  thin  difference  must  be  allowed  for  in  boiling  them. 
Drain  them  we//,  and  mix  them  with  white  sauce  or  gravy,  or  with 
good  melted  butter,  and  serve  them  very  hot. 

A  FUTB  SAUCB,  OR  PUBBX  OF  TEOETABLK  UABKOW.

a  fine  and  delicately  clean  hair-sieve ;  heat  it  afresh,  and  stir  to  it 
when  it  boils  about  the  third  of  a  pint  of  rich  cream.  Serve  it  with 
bofled  chickens,  stewed  or  boiled  veal,  lamb  cutlets,  or  any  other 
delicate  meal.  When  to  be  served  as  a  pur6e,  an  additional  half- 
pound  of  the  vegetabte  munt  be  used ;  and  it  should  be  dished  with 
amsll  fried  sippets  round  it.  For  a  maigre  dish,  stew  the  marrow 
and  onion  quite  tender  in  butter,  aud  dilute  them  with  half  boiling 
water  and  half  cream.

gravy,  1  pint :  nearly  or  quite  1  hour.  Pepper  or  cayenne,  and  salt 
aa  needed;  good  cream,  from  i  to  }  of  pint.  For  purle,  i  lb.  more  of 
mtrrow. 

'     SXCELLEWT  TURNIP,  OB  ABTICHOKS  SAUOE  FOR  BOILED  MEAT. 

Pare,  slice,  and  boil  qnite  tender,  some  finely-grained  mild  turnips, 
jvets  tiie  water  from  them  thoroughly,  and  pass  them  through  a 
sieve.  Dissolve  a  slice  of  butter  in  a  clean  saucepan,  and  stir  to  it  a 
large  teaapoonful  of  flour,  or  mix  them  smoothly  together  before  they 
are  pat  in,  and  shake  the  saucepan  round  until  they  boil :  pour  to 


128  MODERN   COOXERY.  fcliAP.  t; 

them  very  gradually  nearly  a  pint  of  thin  cream  (or  of  good  milk 
mixed  with  a  portion  of  crenm),  add  the  turnips  with  a  half-tea- 
spoonful  or  more  of  salt,  and  when  the  whole  is  well  mixed  and  rery 
hot,  pour  it  over  boiled  mutton,  vea],  lamb,  or  poultry.  There  should 
be  sufficient  of  the  sauce  to  cover  the  meat  entirely ;  *  and  when  pro- 
perly made  it  improves  greatly  the  appearance  of  a  joint.  A  liiUe 
cayenne  tied  in  a  muslin  may  be  boiled  in  the  milk  before  it  is  mixed 
with  the  turnips.  Jerusalem  artichokes  make  a  more  delicate  sauce 
of  this  kind  even  than  turnips;  the  weight  of  boih  vegetables  must 
be  taken  after  they  are  pared. 

Pared  turnips  or  artichokes,  1  lb.;  fresh  butter,  1}  oz.;  flour,  1 
large  teaspoonful  (twice  as  much  if  all  milk  be  used) ;  salt,  i  tea- 
spoonful  or  more ;  cream,  or  cream  and  milk  mixed,  from  )  to  1 
pmt. 

OLITE  SAUCB. 

Bemore  the  stones  from  some  fine  French  or  Italian  oHrea  by 
paring  the  fruit  close  to  them,  round  and  round  in  the  form  of  a 
corktKrew:  they  will  then  resume  their  original  shape  when  done. 
Weigh  six  ounces  thus  prepared,  throw  them  into  boiling  water,  let 
them  blanch  for  five  minutes ;  then  drain,  and  throw  them  into  cold 
water,  and  leave  them  in  it  from  half  an  hour  to  an  hoiur,  proper- 
tioning  the  time  to  their  saltness ;  drain  them  well,  and  stew  them 
'  gently  from  fifteen  to  twenty>five  minutes  in  a  pint  of  yeij  rich 
brown  gravy  or  Espagnole  (see  Chapter  IV.) ;  add  the  juioe  of  half 
a  lemon,  and  serve  the  sauce  very  hot.  Half  this  quantity  will  be 
sufficient  for  a  small  party. 

Olives,  stoned,  6  oz. ;  rich  gravy,  1  pint :  15  to  2d  minutes.    Juice, 
i  lemon. 

Obs. — In  France  this  sauce  is  served  rery  commonly  with  ducks, 
and  sometimes  with  beef-steaks,  and  with  stewed  fowl. 

CELERT  SAUCE. 

Slice  the  white  part  of  from  three  to  five  heads  of  young  tender 
celery ;  peel  it  if  not  very  young,  and  boil  it  in  salt  and  water  for 
twenty  minutes.  If  for  white  sauce  put  the  celery,  after  it  has  been 
well  drained,  into  half  a  pint  of  veal  broth  or  grary,  and  let  it  stetr 
until  it  is  quite  Hoft ;  then  add  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  butter,  mixed 
with  a  dertsertspoonful  of  flour,  and  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  thick  cream 
or  the  Tolks  of  three  eggs.  The  French,  after  boiling  the  celery, 
which  they  cut  very  small,  for  about  twenty  minutes,  drain  and  chop 
it ;  then  put  it  with  a  slice  of  butter  into  a  stew-pan,  and  season  i% 
with  pepper,  salt,  and  nutmeg ;  they  keep  these  stirred  over  the  fire

WHITE  CHESTNUT  SAUCE. 

Strip  the  outer  rind  from  six  ounces  of  sound  sweet  chestnuts,  then 
throw  them  into  boiling  water,  and  let  them  simmer  for  two  or  three 
luinutes,  when  the  second  skin  will  easily  peel  off.  Add  to  them  three 
quarters  of  a  pint  of  good  cold  veal  gravy,  and  a  few  strips  of  lemon 
rind,  and  let  them  stew  gently  for  an  nour  and  a  quarter.  Press 
them,  with  the  gravy,  through  a  hair -sieve  reversed  and  placed  over 
a  deep  dish  or  pan,  as  they  are  much  more  easily  rubbed  through  thus 
than  in  the  usual  way :  a  wooden  spoon  should  be  used  in  preference  . 
to  any  other  for  the  process.  Add  a  little  cayenne  and  mace,  some 
salt  if  needed,  and  about  six  tablespoonsful  of  rich  cream.  Keep  the 
nuee  stirred  until  it  boils,  and  serve  it  immediately. 

Chestnuts  without  their  rinds,  6  oz. ;  veal  gravy,  f  pint ;  rind  of 
J  kmon :  1 1  hour.     Salt ;  spice ;  cream,  6  tablespoonsful,

SHOWN   CHESTNUT   SAUCE. 

Substitute  rich  brown  gravy  for  the  veal  stock,  omit  the  lemon- 
nnd  and  cream,  heighten  3ie  seasonings,  and  mix  the  chestnuts  with 
s  few  spoonsful  of  Mtpagnole  or  highly  flavoured  gravy,  after  they 
Ure  been  passed  through  the  sieve. 

PARSLEY-GREEN,   FOR  COLOURING  SAUCES, 

Gather  a  quantity  ef  young  parsley,  strip  it  from  the  stalks,  wash 
it  very  dean,  shake  it  as  dry  as  possible  m  a  cloth,  pound  it  in  a 
Bortar,  press  all  the  juice  closely  from  it  through  a  hair-sieve 
levened,  and  put  it  into  a  clean  jar ;  set  it  into  a  pan  of  boiling 
^ter,  and  in  about  three  minutes,  if  gevJdy  simmered,  the  juice  will 
Ve  poached  sufficiently ;  lay  it  then  upon  a  dea^  sieve  to  dirain,  and 
it  will  be  ready  for  use.

TO  CRISP  PARflLEY, 


Wash  some  branches  of  young  parsley  well,  drain  them  from  the 
water,  and  swing  them  in  a  clean  cloth  nntil  they  are  quite  dry; 
place  them  on  a  sheet  of  vrriting  paper  in  a  Dutch  oren,  before  a 
brisk  fire,  and  keep  them  finequently  turned  until  they  are  quite 
crisp.    They  will  become  so  in  from  six  to  eight  minutes. 

FRIED   PARSLKT. 

When  the  yarsley  has  been  prepared  as  for  crisping,  and  is  qmk 
dry,  throw  it  into  plenty  of  lard  or  butter,  which  is  on  the  point  of 
boiling ;  take  it  up  with  a  skimmer  the  instant  it  is  crisp,  and  drain 
it  on  a  cloth  spiead  upon  a  deve  reversed,  and  placed  before  the 
fire. 

MILD   MUSTARD. 

Mustard  for  instant  use  should  be  mixed  with  milk,  to  which  a 
spoonful  or  two  of  very  thin  cream  may  be  added. 

MUSTARD  THE  COMMON  WAT. 

The  great  art  of  mixing  mustard  is  to  have  it  perfectly  smooth,  and 
of  a  proper  consistency.  The  liquid  with  wnich  it  is  moistened 
ahould  be  added  to  it  in  small  quantities,  and  the  mustard  should  be 
well  rubbed,  and  beaten  with  a  spoon.  Mix  half  a  teaspoonfol  of 
salt  with  two  ounces  of  the  flour  of  mustard,  and  stir  to  them  by 
degrees  sufficient  boiling  water  to  reduce  it  to  the  appearance  of  % 
thick  batter :  do  not  put  it  into  the  mustard-glass  until  it  ia  cold. 
*  Some  persons  like  a  half-teaspoonful  of  sugar  in  the  finest  powder 
mixed  with  it.  It  ouffht  to  be  sufficiently  diluted  always  to  drop  ea«ly 
from  the  spoon ;  and  to  brmg  it  to  this  state  more  than  a  quarter  of

ounces  of  the  best  Durham  mustard.

TO   PREPARE  BREAD   FOR   FRYING  FISH. 

Cot  thick  slices  from  the  middle  of  a  loaf  of  light  stale  bread,  pare 
flie  crust  entirely  frY>m  them,  and  dry  them  gradually  in  a  cool  oven 
until  th^  are  crisp  quite  through ;  let  them  become  cold,  then  roll 
or  belt  them  into  fine  crumbs,  and  keep  them  in  a  dry  place  for  use. 
To  streir  oyer  hams  or  cheeks  of  bacon,  the  bread  should  be  left  all 
night  in  the  oven,  which  should  be  sufficiently  heated  to  brown,  as 
veil  tt  to  harden  it :  it  ought  indeed  to  be  entirely  conyerted  into 
equally-coloured  crust.  It  may  be  sifted  through  a  dredging-boz 
on  to  the  hams  after  it  has  been  reduced  almost  to  powder. 

BROWNED  FLOUR  FOR  THICKEMNO  BOUPS  AND   GRAYIES. 

Spread  it  on  a  tin  or  dish  and  colour  it,  without  burning,  in  a  gentle 
OTCD  or  before  the  fire  in  a  Dutch  or  American  oven :  turn  it  often, 
or  the  edges  will  be  too  much  browned  before  the  middle  is  enough 
»'  This,  blended  with  butter,  makes  a  convenient  thickening  for 
foopc  or  gravies  of  which  it  is  desirable  to  deepen  the  colour ;  and 
it  requires  less  time  and  attention  than  the  Frencn  rvux  of  page  10 

PRIED   BREAD-CRUMBS*

tboQ  with  the  hands.  Dissolve  two  ounces  of  fresh  butter  in  a 
l^ing-pan,  throw  in  the  crumbs,  and  stir  them  constantly  over  a 
iBoderate  fire,  until  they  are  all  of  a  clear  golden  colour ;  mt  them 
oat  with  a  skinomer,  spread  them  3n  a  soft  cloth,  or  upon  white 
bbttmg  paper,  laid  upon  a  sieve  reversed,  and  dry  them  before  the 
fire.  They  may  be  more  delicately  prepared  by  browning  them  in  a 
gentle  oven  without  the  addition  of  butter. 

Bread,  4  oz. ;  butter,  2  oz.

CHAPTER  TI. 

SUPERIOR   MINT-SAUCE. 

{To  serve  with  lamb,) 

The  mint  for  this  saiioe  should  be  fresh  and  young,  for  when  old 
it  is  tough  and  indigestible.  Strip  the  leaves  from  the  stems,  wash 
them  with  great  nicety,  and  drain  them  on  a  sieve,  or  dry  them  in  a 
cloth ;  chop  them  very  fine,  put  them  into  a  sauce-tureen,  and  to 
three  heaped  tablespoonsful  of  the  mint  add  two  of  pounded  sugir; 
let  them  remain  a  short  time  well  mixed  together,  then  pour  to  l&em 
gradually  six  tablespoonsful  of  good  vinegar.  The  sauce  thus  made 
IS  excellent,  and  far  more  wholesome  than  when  a  larger  proportioa 
of  vinegar  and  a  smaller  one  of  sugar  is  used  for  it ;  but,  after  the 
first  trial,  the  proportions  can  easily  be  adapted  to  the  taste  of  the 
eaters. 

COMMON   MINT- SAUCE. 

Two  tablespoonsful  of  mint,  one  large  tablespoonful  of  pale  brown 
snirar,  well  mixed  together,  and  a  Quarter  of  a  pint  of  vinegar,  stirred 
until  the  sugar  is  entirely  dissolved. 

STRAINED  MINT  SAUCE. 

Persons  with  whom  the  mint  in  substance  disagrees  can  have  the 
flavour  of  the  herb  without  it,  by  mixing  the  ingredients  of  either  of 
the  preceding  receipts,  and  strainine  the  sauce  after  it  has  stood  for 
two  or  three  hours ;  the  mint  shouM  be  well  pressed  when  this  is 
done.  The  flavour  will  be  the  more  readily  extracted  if  the  mint 
and  sugar  are  well  mixed,  and  left  for  a  time  before  the  vinegar  ii 
added.

FINB   HORSERADISH   SAUCE. 

(To  serve  with  cold,  rouet^  gtetoed,  or  boiled  bee/.^

been  washed,  wiped,  and  seraph  free  from  the  outer  skin.  We  have

GOLD  DUTCH   OR   AMERICAN  SAUCE,   FOR   SALADS   OF   DRESSED 
TEOBTABLES,   SALT   FISH,   OR   HARD   EGGS. 

Pat  into  a  saucepan  three  ounces  of  good  butter  very  smoothly 
blended  with  a  quite  small  teaspoonful  of  flour,  and  add  to  them  a 
hu]ge  win^lasafui  of  cold  water,  half  as  much  sharp  vinegar  (or  very 
teh,  strained,  lemon-juice)  a  saltspoonful  of  salt,  and  Iwlf  as  much 
eajenne  in  fine  powder.  Keep  these  shaken  briskly  round,  or  stirred 
Of er  a  dear  fire,  until  they  rorm  a  smooth  sauce  and  boil  rapidly ; 
then  stir  them  ver^  quickly  to  the  beaten  yolks  of  four  fresh  eggs, 
vhich  will  immediately  give  the  sauce^lthe  consistence  of  custard; 
pour  it  hot  over  the  salad,  and  place  it  on  ice,  or  in  a  very  cool  larder 
■util  it  k  quite  cold :  if  properly  made,  it  will  be  very  thick  and 
smooth,  and  slightly  eety  as  if  it  contained  a  small  portion  of  isinglass. 
A  dessertspoonnil  of  parsley, — or  of  tarragon,— can  be  mingled  with 
it  at  pleasure,  or  any  flavour  given  to  it  with  store-sauces  which 
B  liknL  It  converts  flakes  of  salt-fish,  sliced  potatoes  (new  or  old), 
aad  hard  «gg8,  into  excellent  salads.

ENGLISH  8AUCB  FOR  SALAD,   COLD  MEAT,  OR  COLD  FISH. 

The  first  essential  for  a  smooth,  well-made  English  salad  dresBiog 
|8  to  have  the  yolks  of  the  eggs  used  for  it  sufficiently  hard  to 
be  reduced  easily  to  a  perfect  paste.  They  should  be  boiled  at  leait 
fifteen  minutes^  and  should  have  become  quUe  cold  before  they  ait 
taken  from  the  shells;  they  should  also  be  well  covered  with  water 
when  they  are  cooked,  or  some  parts  of  them  will  be  tough,  and  wiD 
spoil  the  appearance  of  the  sauce  by  rendering  it  lumpy,  unless  they 
be  worked  through  a  sieve,  a  process  which  is  always  better  avoided 
if  possible.  To  a  couple  of  yolks  broken  up  and  mashed  to  a  paste 
with  the  back  of  a  wooden  spoon,  add  a  small  saltspoonful  of  nit,  a 
large  one  of  pounded  sugar,  a  few  grains  of  fine  cavenne,  and  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  cold  water ;  mix  these  well,  and  stir  to  them  by  degrees  a 
quarter  of  a  pint  of  sweet  cream;  throw  in  next,  stirring  the  sauce 
briskly,  a  tablespoonful  of  strong  chili  vinegar,  and  add  as  much 
common  or  French  vinegar  as  wul  acidulate  the  mixture  agreeably. 
A  tablespoonful  of  either  will  be  sufficient  for  many  tastes,  but  it  is 
easy  to  increase  the  proportion  when  more  is  liked.  Six  tablespoons- 
ful  of  (^ive  oil,  of  the  purest  quality,  may  be  substituted  for  the 
cream :  it  should  be  added  in  very  small  portions  to  the  other  inne- 
dients,  and  stirred  briskly  as  each  is  added  until  the  sauoe  resemUet 
custard.  When  this  is  used,  the  water  should  be  omitted.  The 
piquancy  of  this  preparation — which  is  very  delicate,  made  by  the 
directions  just  given — ^may  be  heightened  by  the  addition  of  a  Uttie 
eschalot  vin^^,  Harvey*8  sauce,  essence  of  .anchovies,  French  mus- 
tard, or  tarraffon  vinegar;  or  by  bruising  with  the  eggs  a  morBel  of 
garlic,  half  me  size  of  a  hazel-nut:  it  should  always,  however, 
be  rendered  as  appropriate  as  may  be  to  the  dish  with  which  it  is  to 
be  served. 

Ohs,  1. — As  we  have  before  had  occasion  to  remark,  garlic,  when 
very  sparingly  and  judiciouslv  used,  imparts  a  remarkably  fine  savour 
to  a  sauce  or  gravy,  and  neither  a  strong  nor  a  coarse  one,  as  it  does 
when  used  in  larger  quantities.  The  venest  morsel  (or,  as  the  French 
call  it,  a  mere  ioup^on)  of  the  root,  is  sufficient  to  give  this  syneeahk 

Siquancy,  but  unless  the  proportion  be  extremely  small,  the  effect  will 
e  quite  different.  The  Italians  dress  their  salads  upon  a  round  of 
delicately  toasted  bread,  which  is  rubbed  with  ^Uc,  saturated  with 
oil,  and  sprinkled  with  cayenne,  before  it  is  laid  mto  the  bowl :  tbey 
also  eat  the  bread  thus  prepared,  but  with  less  of  oil,  and  iintfiaBtfd 
often,  before  their  meals,  as  a  digester. 

Ob$,  2. — ^French  vinegar  is  so  infinitely  superior  to  "R-^glwh  ia 
strength,  purity,  and  flavour,  that  we  cannot  forbear  to  recommend

RED   OR   GREEN   MAYONNAISE   SAUCE. 

Colour  may  be  given  either  to  the  preceding  or  to  the  following 
Sauce  Mayonnaise  by  mingling  with  it  some  hard  lobster-coral  reduced 
to  powder  by  rubbing  it  through  a  very  fine  hair -sieve :  the  red  hue 
of  this  is  one  of  the  most  brilliant  and  beautiful  that  can  be  seen,  but 
the  sauce  for  which  it  is  used  can  only  be  appropriately  served  with 
fish  or  fish-salads.  Spinach-green  will  impart  a  fine  tint  to  any  pre- 
paration, but  its  flavour  is  objectionable :  that  of  parsley -green  is 
more  agreeable.  Directions  ibr  both  of  these  are  contained  in  the 
previous  chapter. 

IMPERIAL  MAYONNAISE. 

{An  elegant  jellied  AKCtf,  or  salad^dresnng.) 

Put  into  a  bowl  half  a  pint  of  aspic^  or  of  any  very  clear  pale 
jellied  stock  (that  made  usually  for  good  white  soup  will  serve  for  the 
purpose  excellently) ;  add  to  it  a  couple  of  spoonsful  of  the  purest 
olive-oil,  one  of  sharp  vinegar,  and  a  little  fine  salt  and  cayenne.  Break 
up  the  jelly  quite  small  with  the  points  of  a  whisk-  of  osier-twigs 
stir  the  mgredients  well  together,  and  then  whisk  them  gently  Uiitil 
they  are  converted  into  a  smooth  white  sauce.  This  receipt  was  de- 
rived originally  from  an  admirable  French  cook,*  who  st<x)d  quite  nt 
the  head  of  his  profession :  but  as  he  was  accustomed  to  purvey  for 
the  tables  of  kings  and  emperors,  his  directions  require  some  curtail- 
ment and  simplifying  to  adapt  them  to  the  resources  of  commoa 
English  life.  He  directs  the  preparation  to  be  mixed  and  worked— 
to  use  a  technical  expression — over  ice^  which  cannot  always  be  com- 
manded, except  in  opulent  establishments,  and  in  large  towns,    li  it

lished  thirty  jears  or  more  sincei  all  modem  ouuka  appear  to  be  specially  indebted-

not,  howereTf  esKntial  to  the  racceas  of  this  satice,  which  will  prove

REMODLADE. 

This  differs  little  from  an  ordinary  English  salad-dressing.  Pound 
veiy  smoothly  indeed  the  yolks  of  two  or  three  hard-boiled  eggs  with 
a  tesspoonful  of  mustard,  half  as  much  salt,  and  some  cayenne,  or 
white  pepper.  Mix  gradually  with  them,  working  the  wnole  well 
together,  two  or  three  tablespoonsful  of  oil  and  two  of  vinegar. 
Shonld  the  sauce  be  curdled,  pour  it  by  degrees  to  the  yolk  of  a  raw 
^,  stirring  it  well  round  as  directed  for  the  Mayonnaise.  A  spoon- 
fol  of  tarrai^on,  cucumber,  or  eschalot- vinegar,  may  be  added  with 
very  good  effect ;  and  to  give  it  increased  relish,  a  teaspoonful  of 
carice,  or  a  little  of  Harvey*s  sauce,  and  a  dessertspoonful  of  chili 
▼in^ar  may  be  thrown  into  it.  This  last  is  an  excellent  addition  to 
all  cold  sauces,  or  salad-dressings. 

Hard  yolks  of  2  or  of  3  eggs ;  mustard,  1  teasi)oonful  (more  when 
Kked)  ;  salt,  \  teaspoonful :  pepper  or  cayenne ;  oil,  3  tablespoonsful ; 
vinegar,  2.  If  curdled,  yolk  of  1  raw  ^g.  Good  additions :  tarragon 
or  eschalot,  or  cucumber-vinegar,  1  tablespoonful ;  chili  vinegar,  1 
dessertspoonful ;  cavice  or  Harvey*s  sauce  at  pleasure. 

Oh9. — A  dessertspoonful  of  eschalots,  or  a  morsel  of  garlic,  verjr 
finely  minced,  are  sometimes  pounded  with  the  yolks  of  eggs  for  this 
sance. 

OXFORD   BRAWN   SAUCE. 

Mingle  thoroughly  a  tablespoonful  of  brown  sugar  with  a  tea- 
rpoonful  of  made  mustard,  a  third  as  much  of  salt,  some  pepper, 
from  three  to  four  tablespoonsful  of  very  fine  salad-oil,  and  two  of 
strong  vinegar ;  or  apportion  the  same  ingredients  otherwise  to  the 
tute. 

FORCED   EGOS  FOR  OARNISHIKO  SALAD. 

Pound  and  press  through  the  back  of  a  hair-sieve  the  flesh  of  throe 
very  fine,  or  of  four  moderate-sized  anchovies,  freed  from  the  bones 
Aul  skin.    BoU  six  fi^sh  eggs  for  twelve  minutes,  and  when  they  are

perfectly  cold,  halre  them  lenffthwiae,  take  out  the  yolks,  pound 
them  to  a  paste  with  a  third  of  toeir  volume  of  fresh  hucter,  then  add 
the  anchovies,  a  quarter  of  a  teaspoonful  of  mace,  and  as  mudi

ANCHOVy   BUTTER. 

(Excellent,) 

Scrape  the  skin  quite  clear  from  a  dozen  fine  mellow  anchovies, 
free  the  flesh  entirely  from  the  bones,  and  pound  it  as  smooth  as  pos- 
sible in  a  mortar;  rub  it  through  the  back  of  a  hair-sieve  with  a 
wooden  spoon;  wipe  out  the  mortar,  and  put  back  the  anchovies 
with  three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  very  fresh  butter,  a  small  half- 
saltspoonful  of  cayenne,  and  more  than  twice  as  much  of  finely  grated 
nutmeg,  and  freshly  pounded  mace;  and  beat  them  together  until 
they  are  thoroughly  blended.  If  to  serve  cold  at  table,  mould  the 
butter  in  small  shapes,  and  turn  it  out.  A  little  rose  pink  (which  is 
sold  at  the  chemists*)  is  sometimes  used  to  give  it  a  fine  colour,  but  it 
must  be  sparingly  used,  or  it  will  impart  an  unpleasant  flavour,  and 
we  cannot  much  recommend  its  use :  it  should  be  well  pounds  and 
very  equally  mixed  with  it.  For  kitchen  use,  press  the  butter  down 
into  jars  or  pattypans,  and  keep  it  in  a  cool  place. 

Fine  anchovies,  12 ;  butter,  |  lb. ;  cayenne,  small  )  saltspoonful ; 
nutmeg  and  maoe,  each  more  than  twice  as  much ;  rose  pink  (if  used), 
i  teaspoonful. 

This  proportion  differs  from  potted  anchovies  only  ill  the  larger  pro- 
portion of  butter  mixed  with  the  fish,  and  the  milder  seasoning  of 
spice.  It  will  assist  to  form  an  elegant  dish  if  made  into  pats,  and 
stamped  with  a  tasteful  impression,  then  placed  alternately  with  pats 
of  lobster-butter,  and  decorated  with  lignt  foliage.  It  is  generally 
eaten  with  much  relish  when  carefully  compounded,  and  makes  ex- 
cellent sandwiches.  To  convert  it  into  a  go<K[  fish  sauce,  mix  two  or 
three  ounces  of  it  with  a  teaspoonful  of  flour  and  a  few  spoonsftii  of 
cold  water,  or  of  pale  veal  stock,  and  keep  them  constantly  stirred 
until  they  boil.  The  butter  should  not  be  moulded  directly  it  is 
taken  from  the  mortar,  as  it  is  then  very  soft  from  the  beating.  It 
should  be  placed  until  it  is  firm  in  a  very  cool  place  or  over  ice,  whcft 
it  can  be  done  conveniently. 

LOBSTER   BUTTER. 

Pound  to  the  smoothest  po«ble  paste  the  coral  of  one  or  two  fresh 
hen  lobsters,  mix  with  it  about  an  equal  proportion  of  fresh  fina  iMit- 


CiiF.  tlJ  cold  BAUCSSy  SAXABSy  ETC.  139 

ter,  and  a  moderate  seaaoniiig  of  mace  and  cajeane,  iritl  a  little  salt 
if  needed.  Let  the  whole  Ik  thoroughly  blended,  and  set  it  aside  in 
ft  cool  larder,  or  place  it  over  ice  until  it  is  lufficiently  firm  to  be 
nude  into  pats.  Serve  it  garnished  with  curled  parsley,  or  with  any 
light  foUage  which  will  contrast  well  with  its  brilliant  colour.  The 
eoral  may  be  nibbed  through  a  fine  saeve  before  it  is  put  into  the 
mortar,  and  will  then  require  but  little  pounding. 

An  excellent  preparation  is  produced  by  mingling  equal  propor- 
tions of  lobster  uid  of  anchovy  butter  in  the  mortar,  or  one-third  of 
the  ftochovy  with  two  of  lobster :  to  this  some  of  the  white  flesh  of 
the  latter  can  be  added  to  give  another  variety,  after  it  has  been 
prepared  by  the  receipt  for  ^mdirettes,  Chapter  ill. 

TBI7FFLED   BUTTBR   (aVD  TRUFFLES  POTTED   IN   BUTTER.) 

{For  the  breakfast  or  Umeheon  table.) 

Cat  up  a  pound  of  sweet  fresh  butter,  and  dissolve  it  gently  over  a 
dear  fire ;  take  off  the  scum  which  will  gather  thickly  upon  it,  and 
when  it  has  simmered  for  three  or  four  minutes,  draw  it  from  the  fire, 
and  let  it  stand  until  all  Uie  butter-milk  has  subsided ;  pour  it  sofUy 
from  this  upon  six  ounces  of  ready-pared  sound  French  truffles,  cut 
into  small,  but  rather  thick,  slices,  and  laid  into  a  delicately  clean 
enamelled  saucepan ;  add  a  full  seasoning  of  freshly  pounded  mace 
tad  fine  cayenne,  a  small  saltspoonf ul  of  salt,  and  naif  a  not  large 
notmeg.  Wlien  the  butter  has  become  quite  cold,  proceed  to  heat  the 
tmffies  slowly,  shaking  the  saucepan  onen  briskly  round,  and  stew 
them  as  gently  as  possible  for  twenty  minutes,  or  longer  should  they 
not  then  be  very  tender.  If  allowed  to  heat,  and  to  boil  quickly, 
tbey  will  become  hardy  and  the  preparation,  as  regards  the  truffles^ 
vill  be  a  comparative  failure.  Lift  them  with  a  spoon  into  quite 
dry  earthen  or  china  pans,  and  pour  the  butter  on  them ;  or  add  to 
them  sufficient  of  it  <mly  to  cover  them  well  and  to  exclude  the  air, 
aad  pot  the  remainder  of  the  butter  apart :  it  will  be  finely  flavoured, 
and  may  be  eaten  by  delicate  persons  to  whom  the  truffle  itself  would 
he  injurious.  It  may  also  be  used  in  compounding  savoury  sauces, 
tod  for  moistening  small  croustadee  before  they  are  fried  or  baked. 
The  truffles  themselves  will  remain  good  for  months  when  thus  pre- 
pared, if  kept  free  from,  damp ;  and  in  flavour  they  vdli  be  found  ex- 
eelleitf.  The  parings  taken  rrom  them  will  also  impart  a  very  agree- 
able savour  to  the  butter,  and  will  serve  extremely  well  for  it  for  im- 
mediate use.  They  will  also  be  valuable  as  additions  to  gravies  or  to 
soups. 

We  should  observe,  that  the  iuice  which  will  have  exuded  firom 
the  tmfiSes  in  the  stewing  will  cause  the  preparation  to  become 
mouldy,  or  otherwise  injure  it,  if  it  be  put  into  the  pans  either  with 
them  or  with  the  butter.  The  truffles  must  be  well  drained  from  it 
vben  they  are  taken  from  the  saucepan,  and  the  butter  must  remain 


140  MODERN  COOKERY.  [cHAP.  Tl. 

undisturbed  for  a  few  minutes,  when  it  can  be  poured  dear  from  the 
juice,  which  will  have  subsided  to  the  bottom  of  the  pan.  We  have 
^ven  here  the  result  of  our  first  experiment,  which  we  found  on 
further  trial  to  answer  perfectly. 

ENGLISH  SALADS. 

The  herbs  and  vegetables  for  a  salad  cannot  be  too  freshly  gathered « 
they  should  be  carefully  cleared  from  insects  and  washed  with  scrupu- 
lous nicety ;  they  are  better  when  not  prepared  until  near  the  time  of 
sending  them  to  table,  and  should  not  be  sauced  until  the  instant 
before  they  are  served.  Tender  lettuces,  of  which  the  stems  should 
be  cut  off,  and  the  outer  leaves  be  stripped  a>vav,  mustard  and  cress, 
young  radishes,  and  occasionally  chives  or  small  sreen  onions  (when 
the  taste  of  a  party  is  in  favour  of  these  last)  are  the  usual  ingredients 
of  summer  salads.  (In  early  spring,  as  we  have  stated  in  another 
chapter,  the  young  white  leaves  of  the  dandelion  vrill  supply  a  very 
wholesome  and  excellent  salad,  of  which  the  slight  bittemesss  is  to 
many  persons  as  agreeable  as  that  of  the  endive.)  Half-grown  cu- 
cumbers sliced  thin,  and  mixed  with  them,  are  a  favourite  addition 
with  many  persons.  In  England  it  is  customary  to  cut  the  lettuces 
extremely  fine ;  the  French,  who  object  to  the  flavour  of  the  hiife, 
which  they  fancy  this  mode  imparts,  break  them  small  inst^uL 
Young  celery  alone,  sliced  and  dressed  with  a  rich  salad  mixture,  is 
excellent :  it  is  still  in  some  families  served  thus  always  with  roast 
pheasants. 

Beet-root,  baked  or  boiled,  blanched  endive,  small  salad-herbs 
which  are  easily  raised  at  any  time  of  the  year,  celery,  and  hardy  let- 
tuces, with  any  ready-dressed  vegetable,  will  supply  salads  through 
the  winter.    Cucumber  vinegar  b  an  agreeable  addition  to  these. 

FRENCH   SALAD. 

In  winter  this  is  made  principally  of  beautifully-blanched  endive, 
washed  delicately  clean  and  broken  into  small  branches  with  the 
fingers,  then  taken  from  the  water  and  shaken  dry  in  a  basket  of 
peculiar  form,  appropriated  to  the  purpose,*  or  in  a  fine  cloth ;  then 
arranged  in  the  salad  bowl,  and  strewed  with  herbs  (tarragon 
generally,  when  in  season)  minced  small :  the  dressing  is  not  added 
until  just  before  the  salad  is  eaten.  In  summer,  youn^  lettuces  are 
substituted  for  the  endive,  and  intermixed  with  a  vanety  of  herbs, 
some  of  which  are  not  generally  cultivated  in  England. 

FRENCH   SALAD   DRESSING. 

Stir  a  saltspoonful  of  salt  and  half  as  much  pepper  into  a  laiy^e 
spoonful  of  oil,  and  when  the  salt  is  dissolved,  mix  with  them  four 

•  Salad-baskets  are  also  to  be  found  in  many  good  English  kitchens,  but  they 
are  not  in  such  general  use  here  as  on  the  cuutiuenL 


CHAP.  ▼!.]  COLD  SAUCES,  SALADS,  ETC.  141 

additional  spoonsfiil  of  oil,  and  ponr  the  whole  over  the  salad ;  let  it 
be  teefftomed,  and  then  add  a  couple  of  spoonsful  of  tarragon  vinegar  ^ 
mix  the  whole  thoroughly,  and  serve  it  without  delay.  The  ndad 
should  not  be  dressed  in  this  way  until  the  instant  before  it  is  wanted 
for  table :  the  proportions  of  «dt  and  pepper  can  be  increased  at 
pleasure,  and  common  or  cucumber  vinegar  may  be  substituted  for  the 
tBrragon,  which,  however,  is  more  frequently  used  in  France  than 
any  other. 

Salt,  1  spoonful :  pepper,  i  as  much ;  oil,  6  saladspoonsful ;  tar- 
ragon, or  other  vinegar,  2  spoonsfuL 

DBS  CERNGAirX,   OR   WALNUT  SALAD. 

This  IS  a  common  summer  salad  in  France,  where  the  growth  of 
^nuts  is  generall  V  abundant,  but  is  not  much  served  in  England ; 
tboagh  the  sweet  flavour  of  the  just-formed  nut  is  very  agreeable. 
Take  the  walnuts  wheti  a  pin  will  pierce  them  easily,  pare  them  down 
to  the  kernels,  and  toss  them  gently,  just  before  they  are  served,  in  a 
French  or  English  salad-dressing  (the  former  would  generally  be 
preferred  we  tmnk),  and  turn  them  into  the  salad-bowl  for  table. 

SUFFOLK  SALAD. 

Fill  a  salad-bowl  from  half  to  three  parts  full  with  very  tender 
lettuces  shred  small,  minced  lean  of  ham,  and  hard-boiled  eggs,  or 
tiuir  yolks  only  also  minced,  placed  in  alternate  layers ;  dress  the 
mixture  with  English  salad  sauce,  but  do  not  pour  it  into  the  bowl 
until  the  instant  of  serving.  A  portion  of  cold  chicken  (or  veal), 
cat  in  thin  slices  about  the  size  of  a  shilling,  may  be  added  when 
convenient;  the  ham  and  eggs  also  may  be  sliced  instead  of  being 
minced,  and  the  whole  neatly  arranged  in  a  chain  or  otherwise  round 
the  inside  of  the  bowL

SORREL  SALAD* 

(To  serve  with  Lamb^cudets^  Veal-cudeU^  or  Boast  Lamh)» 

This,  though  a  yery  agreeable  and  refreshing  salad,  is  not  to  be  re-

system ;  for  the  powerful  acid  of  the  uncooked  sorrel  might  in  that 
case  produce  serious  consequences.* 

Take  from  the  stems  some  yery  younff  tender  sorrel,  wash  it  deli- 
cately clean,  drain  it  well,  and  shake  it  dry  in  a  salad-basket,  or  in  a 
soft  cloth  held  by  the  four  comers ;  arrange  it  lightly  in  the  bowl, 
and  at  the  instant  of  serying,  sauce  it  simply  with  the  preceding 
French  dressing  of  oil  with  a  small  portion  of  yinegar,  or  with  a 
Mayonnaise  mixed  with  chili  instead  or  a  milder  yinegar.  The  sorrel 
may  be  divided  with  the  fingers  and  mii^led  with  an  equal  proper* 
tion  of  yery  tender  lettuces ;  and,  when  it  is  not  objected  to,f  mixed 
tarragon  may  be  strewed  thickly  upon  them.  To  some  tastes  a  smaU 
quantity  of  green  onions  or  of  eschalots  would  be  more  agreeable.

AN  EXCELLENT  HEREIIfO  SALAD. 

(Swedish  Receipt,} 

Soak,  skin,  split,  and  bone  a  large  Norway  herring ;  lay  the  two  sides 
along  a  dish,  and  slice  them  slopingly  (or  substitute  for  this  one  or 
two  fine  Dutch  herrings).  Arrange  in  symmetrical  order  over  the 
fish  slices  of  cooked  beet-root,  cold  boiled  potatoes,  and  pickled  gher- 
kins ;  then  add  one  or  two  sharp  apples  chopped  small,  and  the  yolks 
and  whites,  separately  minced,  of  some  hard-boiled  eggs,  with  any 
thinff  else  which  may  be  at  hand,  and  may  serve  to  vary  tastefully 
the  decoration  of  the  dish.  Place  these  ingredients  in  small  heaps  of 
^>ell-contrasting  colours  on  the  surface  of  Uie  salad,  and  lay  a  border 
of  curled  celery  leaves  or  parsley  round  the  bowl. 

For  sauce,  rub  the  yolk  of  one  hard-boiled  egg  quite  smooth  with 
me  salt;  to  tJhis  add  oil  and  vin^ar  as  for  an  ordinary  salad,  and 
dilate  the  whole  with  some  thick  sour  cream. 

Obs,^^^  Sour  cream**  is  an  ingredient  not  much  approved  by  Eng- 
lish taste,  but  it  enters  largely  into  German  cookery,  and  into  that  of 
Sweden,  and  of  other  norSiem  countries  also.  About  half  a  pound 
of  cold  beef  cut  into  small  thin  shavings  or  collops,  is  often  added  to 
a  herring-salad  abroad :  it  may  be  either  of  simply  roasted  or  boiled, 
ar  of  salted  and  smoked  meat. 

TARTAR  SAUCE. 

{Sauce  a  la  Tartare). 

Add  to  the  preceding  removlade^  or  to  any  other  sauce  of  the  same 
nature,  a  teaspoonful  or  more  of  made  mustard,  one  of  finely-minced 
ahalots,  one  of  parsley  or  tarragon,  and  one  of  capers  or  of  pickled 
gherkins,  with  a  rather  hien  seasoning  of  cayenne,  and  some  salt  if 
needed.  The  tartar-mustard  of  the  previous  chapter,  or  good  French 
Bastard,  is  to  be  preferred  to  English  for  this  sauce,  whieh  is  usually 


144  MODERN  COOKERY.  [chap,  n 

made  very  pungent,  and  for  which  any  ingredients  can  be  used  to  the 
taste  which  will  serve  to  render  it  so.  Tarragon  vinegar,  mmceii 
tarragon  and  eschalots,  and  plenty  of  oil,  are  used  for  it  in  France,  in 
conjunction  with  the  yolks  of  one  or  two  eggs,  and  chopped  capers, 
or  gherkins,  to  which  olives  are  sometimes  added. 

SHRIMP   CHATNEY. 

(^Mauritian  Receipt,^ 

Shell  with  care  a  quart  of  fresh  shrimps  (for  the  mode  of  doing 
this  see  Chapter  III.),  mince  them  quickly  upon  a  dish  with  a  lai^ 
sharp  knife,  then  turn  them  into  a  mortar  and  pound  them  to  a  per- 
fectly smooth  paste.  Next,  mix  with  them  very  gradually  two  or 
three  spoonsful  of  salad  oil  of  the  best  quality,  some  young  green 
chilies  chopped  small  (or  when  these  cannot  be  procured,  some  good 
cayenne  pepper  as  a  substitute),  some  young  onions  finely  minced,  a 
little  salt  ii  required,  and  as  much  vinegar  or  strained  lemon  juice  as 
will  render  the  sauce  pleasantly  acid.  Half  a  saltspoonful  or  more 
of  powdered  ginger  is  sometimes  used  in  addition  to  the  above  ingre- 
dients. 

^Vhen  they  are  preferred,  two  or  three  small  shalots  minced  and  well 
bruised  with  the  shrimps  may  be  substituted  for  the  onions.*  The 
proportion  of  oil  should  be  double  that  of  the  vinegar  used ;  but  in 
this  preparation,  as  in  all  others  of  the  same  nature,  individual  taste 
must  regulate  the  proportion  of  the  most  powerful  condiments  which 
enter  into  its  composition.  All  chatneys  should  be  quUe  thick,  almost 
of  the  consistence  of  mashed  turnips,  or  stewed  tomatas,  or  stiff  bread 
sauce.  They  are  served  with  curries ;  and  also  with  steaks,  cutlets, 
cold  meat,  and  fish.  In  the  East  the  native  cooks  crush  to  a  pulp 
upon  a  stone  slab,  and  with  a  stone  roller,  the  ingredients  whicn  we 
direct  to  be  pounded.  On  occasion  the  fish  might  be  merely  minced. 
When  beaten  to  a  paste,  they  should  be  well  separated  with  a  fork  as 
the  chilies,  &c.,  are  added.

hour  or  two ;  then  drain,  and  dr^  with  oil,  vinegar,  and  salt.

FINE  MUBBROOIC  CATSUP.

**  bitter  sweetness**  which  the  burnt  sugar  used  as  ^browning**  In 
elunuy  cookery  so  often  does.  The  catsup  ought,  in  fact,  to  be  rather 
the  pore  esience  qf  muskro^ms^  made  irith  so  much  salt  and  spice  only 
la  are  required  to  preserve  it  for  a  year  or  longer,  than  the  com- 
pound of  mushroom-juice,  anchovies,  riialots,  allspice,  and  other  con- 
difflents  of  which  it  is  conmionly  composed,  especially  for  sale. 

JHrtctiom  to  he  c^erved  in  making  and  for  keeping  the  catnip. — 
Let  the  mushrooms  be  collected  when  the  weather  is  diy,  for  if 
gathered  during,  or  immediately  after  rain,  the  catsup  made  with 
them  will  not  keep  well. 

Cat  off  the  stalK-endq  to  which  the  earth  adheres,  before  the  mush- 
noms  are  broken  up,  and  throw  them  aside,  as  they  should  never  be 
used  for  the  catsup.  Reject  also  such  of  the  flaps  as  are  worm-eaten 
or  decayed.  Those  which  are  too  stale  for  use  may  be  detected  by  the 
Bmell,  which  is  very  ofieosive. 

When  the  mushroom  first  opens,  the  under-side  is  of  a  fine  pale 
■linon  colour;  this  changes  soon  to  a  sort  of  ashy-brown,  which 
deepens  almost  to  black  as  the  mushroom  passes  from  its  maturity  to 
a  state  of  decay.  As  it  yields  a  greater  abundance  of  juice  when,  it  is 
/ally  rvff^  it  is  usually  taken  in  that  state  for  these  sauces ;  but  cat- 
np  of  tme  and  delicate  flavour,  though  somewhat  pale  in  colour,  can 
be  Blade  even  of  mushroom-bttttons  if  they  be  sliced  up  small  and 
toned  often  in  the  liquid  which  will  be  speedily  drawn  from  them 
b}r  the  application  oT  salt ;  a  rather  smaller  proportion  of  which 
ihoakl  be  mingled  with  them  than  is  directed  ior  the  following 
leeeipL 

Eveiy  thing  used  in  preparing  the  catsup  should  be  delicately 
dean  and  very  dry.  The  bottles  in  which  it  is  stored,  after  being  dried 
m  the  usual  vray,  should  be  laid  into  a  oool  oven  for  an  hour  or  two 
before  they  are  filled,  to  ensure  their  being  free  from  the  slightest 
<^^  of  moisture,  but  they  must  be  qmite  eoid  before  the  catsup  is 
pooled  into  them.  If  the  corks  be  sealed  so  as  to  exclude  the  air 
elFectually,  or  if  well-cleansed  bits  of  bladder  first  dried,  and  then 
feodered  flexible  with  a  little  ^irit  of  any  kind  (spirits  of  wine  is 
coQTenient  for  such  purposes),  be  tied  closely  over  them,  and  the 
bottles  can  be  kept  in  a  cool  place  free  from  damp,  the  catsup  will 
Kmain  good  for  a  long  time. 


MUSHROOM  CATSUP. 

Siceipt: — ^Break  up  small  into  a  deep  earthen  pan,  two  gallons  of 
hige  ripe  mushroom-flaps,  and  strew  amongst  them  three  quarters  of 
A  pound  of  salt,  reserving  the  larser  portion  of  it  for  the  top.  Let 
them  remain  two  days,  and  stir  them  gently  with  a  wooden  spoon 
often  during  the  time;  then  turn  them  into  a  large  stewpan  or 
enamelled  saucepan,  heat  them  slowly,  and  simmer  them  for  fifteen  or 
twenty  m'mntAm.    Stnuu  the  liquor  closely  from  them  without  fMreff* 


148  MODERN  COOKERY.  [chap,  vu, 

.sure ;  strain  and  measure  it ;  put  it  into  a  veir  clean  stcwpan,  and 
boil  it  quickly  until  it  is  reduced  nearly  half.  For  every  quart  allow 
half  an  ounce  of  black  peppercorns  and  a  drachm  of  mace ;  or,  instead 
of  the  pepper,  a  quarter  of  a  teaspoonful  (ten  grains)  of  good  cayenne; 
pour  the  catsup  into  a  clean  jug  or  jar,  lay  a  folded  cloth  over  it,  and 
keep  it  in  a  cool  place  until  the  following  day ;  pour  it  gently  from 
the  sediment,  put  into  small  bottles,  cork  them  vrell,  and  rosin  them 
down.  A  teaspoonful  of  salad  oil  may  be  poured  into  each  bottle  before 
it  is  corked,  the  better  to  exclude  the  air  from  the  catsup. 

Mushrooms,  2  sallons :  salt,  i  lb. ;  to  macerate  three  or  four  days. 
To  each  quart  of  liquor, }  oz.  black  pepper,  or  quarter  of  a  teaspoonful 
of  cayenne ;  and  1  drachm  of  mace :  to  \^  reduced  nearly  hali. 

Obs,  1. — Catsup  made  thus  will  '»nt  hr  too  salt,  nor  will  the  fiaronr 
of  the  mushrooms  be  overpowered  oy  tnat  of  the  spices ;  of  which  a 
larger  quantity,  and  a  greater  variety,  can  be  used  at  will. 

We  can,  however,  answer  for  the  excellence  of  the  present  receipt 
from  long  experience  of  it.  When  the  catsup  is  boiled  down  quite 
early  in  the  day,  it  may  be  bottled  the  same  night :  it  is  necessaij 
only,  that  it  should  be  perfectly  cold  before  this  is  done. 

Obs,  2. — When  the  mushrooms  are  crushed,  or  mashed,  as  some 
authors  direct,  the  liquor  will  necessarily  be  very  thick ;  it  is  better 
to  proceed  as  above,  and  then  to  boil  the  liquor  which  may  after- 
>vards  be  extracted  from  the  mushrooms  by  pressure,  with  the  sedi- 
ment of  the  catsup,  and  sufficient  cloves,  pepper,  allspice,  and  gineer, 
to  flavour  it  highly :  this  second  catsup  will  be  found  very  useful  to 
mix  with  common  thickened  sauces,  hashes,  and  stews. 

MUSHROOM   CATSUP. 

(^Another  Receipt) 

Break  a  peck  of  large  mushrooms  into  a  deep  earthenpan ;  strew 
three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  salt  amongst  them,  and  set  tnem  into  % 
very  cool  oven  for  one  night,  with  a  fold  of  cloth  or  paper  over  them. 
The  following  day  strain  off  the  liquor,  measure,  and  boil  it  for  fifteen 
minutes ;  then,  for  each  quart,  add  an  ounce  of  black  pepper,  a 
quarter  of  an  ounce  of  allspice,  half  an  ounce  of  ginger,  and  two  large 
blades  of  mace,  and  let  it  boil  fast  for  twenty  minutes  loncjer.  When 
thoroughly  cold,  put  it  into  bottles,  cork  them  well,  and  dip  the  necks 
into  melted  bottle -cement,  or  seal  them  so  as  to  secure  the  catsup  from 
the  air. 

Mushrooms,  1  peck ;  salt,  f  lb.  Liquor  to  boil,  15  minntcs.  To 
each  quart,  {  oz.  black  pepper ;  ^  oz.  allspice ;  )  oz.  ginger ;  2  blades 
mace :  20  minutes.

to  them  a  quart  of  ready-made  catsup  (that  which  k  a  3*ear  old  wiH

COMPOUND^   OR  cook's  CATSUP. 

Take  a  pint  and  a  half  of  mushroom  catsup  when  it  is  first  made,

WALNUT  CATSUP. 

The  vinegar  in  which  walnuts  have  been  pickled,  when  they  have 
remained  in  it  a  year,  will  generally  answer  all  the  purposes  for 
which  this  catsup  is  required,  particularly  if  it  be  drained  from  them 
and  boiled  for  a  few  minutes,  with  a  little  additional  spice,  and  a  few 
cachalots ;  but  where  the  vinegar  is  objected  to,  it  may  be  made  either 
by  boilinff  the  expressed  juice  of  young  walnuts  for  an  hour,  with  six 
oonces  of  fine  anchovies,  four  ounces  of  eschalots,  half  an  ounce  of 
Uack  pepper,  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of  doves,  and  a  drachm  of  mace, 
to  every  quart ;  or  as  follows : — 

Found  in  a  mortar  a  hundred  young  walnuts,  strewing  amongst 
them  88  they  are  done  half  a  pound  of  salt ;  then  pour  to  them  a 
quart  of  strong  vinegar,  and  let  them  stand  until  they  have  become 
quite  black,  keeping  them  stirred  three  or  four  times  a  day ;  next  add 
a  quart  of  strong  old  beer,  and  boil  the  whole  together  for  ten 
minntes ;  strain  it,  and  let  it  remain  until  the  next  day ;  then  pour  it 
off  clear  from  the  sediment,  add  to  it  half  a  pound  of  anchovies,  one 
large  head  of  garlic  bruised,  half  an  ounce  of  nutmegs  bruised,  the 
flune  quantity  of  cloves  and  black  pepper,  and  two  drachms  of  mace : 
boQ  these  together  for  half  an  hour,  and  the  following  day  bottle  and 
cork  the  catsup  well.  It  will  keep  for  a  dozen  years.  Many  persons 
add  to  it,  before  it  is  boiled,  a  bottle  of  port  wine ;  and  others  recom- 
xnend  a  large  bunch  of  sweet  herbs  to  be  put  in  with  the  spice. 

Ist  Recipe.  Enressed  juice  of  widnuts,  1  quart ;  anchovies,  6  oz. ; 
csdialotSy  4  oz.;  black  pepper,  }  oz. ;  cloves,  i  oz. ;  mace,  1  drachm  . 
Ihour.

LEMON   PICKLE   OR   CATSUP. 

Either  divide  six  small  lemons  into  quarters,  remove  all  the  pipi 
that  are  in  si^ht,  and  streW  three  ounces  of  salt  upon  them,  and  keep 
them  turned  m  it  for  a  week,  or^  merely  make  deep  incisions  in  tbem, 
and  proceed  as  directed  for  pickled  lemons.  When  they  have  stood 
in  a  warm  place  for  eight  days,  wit  into  a  stone  jar  two  ounces  and 
a  half  of  finely-scraned  horseradish,  and  two  ounces  of  eschalots,  or 
one  and  a  half  of  garlic ;  to  these  add  the  lemons  with  all  their  liquor, 
and  pour  on  tbem  a  pint  and  a  half  of  boiling  vinegar  in  which  half 
an  ounce  of  bruised  gin^r,  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of  whole  white 
pepper,  and  two  blades  or  mace  have  been  simmered  for  two  or  three 
minutes.  The  pickle  will  be  fit  for  use  in  two  or  three  months,  bat 
nuiy  stand  four  or  five  before  it  is  strained  off.

TARRAOOX  VnVEOAR. 

Gather  the  tarragon  just  before  it  blossoms,  which  will  be  late  in 
July,  or  early  in  August;  strip  it  from  the  larger  stalks,  and  put  it 
into  small  stone  jars  or  wide-necked  bottles,  and  in  doins  this  twist 
aome  of  the  branches  so  as  to  bruise  the  leaves  and  wring  tnem  asun- 
der; then  pour  in  sufficient  distilled  or  very  pale  vinegar  to  cover  the 
tarragon;  let  it  infuse  for  two  months,  or  more :  it  will  take  no  harm 
even  by  ^im'*^*^  all  the  winter.  When  it  is  poured  off,  strain  it  very 
dear,  put  it  into  small  dry  bottles,  and  cork  uiem  well.  Sweet  basil 
wnr  is  made  in  exactly  the  same  way,  but  it  should  not  be  left  on 
the  leaves  more  than  three  weeks.  The  jars  or  bottles  should  be 
filled  to  the  neck  with  the  tarragon  before  the  vinegar  is  added :  its 
fisTour  is  strong  and  peculiar,  but  to  many  tastes  ver^  agreeable.  It 
imparts  quite  a  foreign  character  to  the  dishe?  for  which  it  is  used.

GREEN   MIKt   YINKGAR. 

Slightly  chop,  or  bruise,  freshly-gathered  mint,  and  put  it  into 
bottles ;  till  them  nearly  to  the  necks,  and  add  vinegar  as  for  tarra- 
gon :  in  forty  days,  strain  it  off,  and  bottle  it  for  use. 

The  mint  itself,  ready  minced  for  sauce,  will  keep  well  in  yinegar, 
though  the  colour  will  not  be  yery  sood.  The  young  leaves  stripped 
from  the  stems,  should  be  used  for  Uils  preparation. 

CUCUMBER   YINEOAR. 

First  wipe,  and  then,  without  paring,  slice  into  a  stone  iar  some 
young  and  quickly-grown  cucumbers ;  pour  on  them  as  much  boiling 
vinegar  as  will  cover  them  well,  with  a  teaspoonf ul  of  salt,  and  two- 
thirds  as  much  of  peppercorns  to  the  pint  and  a  half  of  vinegar:  it 
may  renuun  on  them  for  a  month,  or  even  for  two,  if  well  defended 
from  the  air :  it  should  then  be  strained,  allowed  to  settle,  and  poured 
quite  clear  into  small  dxj  bottles,  which  should  be  well  corked.  A 
mild  onion  can  be  intermixed  with  the  cucumbers,  when  its  ilavccr  is 
considered  an  improvement 

CELERY   VINEGAR. 

Throw  into  a  pint  and  a  half  of  ready  boiling  vinegar  a  few  grains 
of  cayenne,  or  half  an  ounce  of  peppercorns,  a  large  saltspooni'ul  uf 
salt,  and  a  pint  of  the  white  part  of  the  roots  and  stems  of  some  fine 
fresh  celery  sliced  up  thin :  let  it  boil  for  two  or  three  minutes,  tam 
it  into  a  stone  jar,  and  secure  it  well  from  the  air  as  soon  as  it  is  cold 
It  may  be  strained  off  and  bottled  in  three  or  four  weeks,  but  may 
remain  as  many  months  in  the  jar  without  ii\)ury. 

ESCHALOT,   OR   GARUG   VINEGAR, 

On  from  four  to  six  ounces  of  eschalots  or  on  two  of  fulic  peeled 
and  bruised,  pour  a  quart  of  the  best  vinegar ;  stop  the  jar  or  bottle 
close,  and  in  a  fortnight  or  three  weeks  the  vinegar  may  be  strained 
off  for  use :  a  few  drops  will  give  a  sufficient  flavour  to  a  sauce,  or  to 
a  tureen  of  gravy. 

Eschalots,  4  to  6  oz.;  or,  garlic,  2  to  4  oz.;  vinegar,  1  quart:  15 
to  21  days. 

Obs. — These  roots  may  be  used  in  smaller  or  in  larger  proportion, 
as  a  slighter  or  a  stronger  flavour  of  them  is  desired,  and  may  remain 
longer  in  the  vinegar  without  any  detriment  to  it 

ESCHALOT   WINE, 

This  is  a  far  more  useful  preparation  even  than  the  preceding  one, 
since  it  can  be  used  to  impart  the  flavour  of  the  eschalot  to  dishes  fol

vbich  acid  is  not  required.  Feel  and  slice,  or  bruise,  four  ounces  of

HORSRUADISH    VINEGAR. 

On  four  ounces  of  young  and  freshly-scraped  horseradish  pour  a 
quart  of  boiling  vinegar,  and  cover  it  down  closely :  it  will  be  ready 
for  use  in  three  or  four  days,  but  may  remain  for  weeks,  or  months, 
before  the  vinegar  is  poured  off.  An  ounce  of  minced  eschalot  may 
be  substituted  for  one  of  the  horseradish,  if  the  flavour  be  liked* 

CAY£14NE   VINEGAR. 

Put  from  a  quarter  to  half  an  ounce  of  the  best  cayenne  pepper 
into  a  bottle,  and  pour  on  it  a  pint  of  pale  vinegar.  Cork  it  closelvi 
^d  shake  it  well  every  two  or  three  days.  It  may  remain  any  length 
of  time  before  it  is  poured  off,  but  will  very  soon  be  ready  for  use. 

Good  cayenne  pepper,  ^  to  ^  oz. ;  vinegar,  1  pint:  inl'use  from 
2  weeks  to  12  months. 

LE.MON   BRANDY. 

{For  flavouring  sweet  dishes.) 

Fill  any  sized  wide-necked  bottle  lightly  T^ith  the  very  thin  rinds 
of  finesh  lemons,  and  cover  them  with  good  brandy ;  let  them  remain 
for  a  fortnight  or  three  weeks  only,  then  strain  off  the  spirit  and  keej) 
it  weU  corked  for  use :  a  few  apricot-kernels  blanched  and  infused 
with  the  lemon-rind  vrill  give  it  an  agreeable  fiavoui*. 

DRIED   MUSHROOMS. 

Peel  small,  sound,  freshly-gathered  flaps,  cut  off  the  stems,  and 
scrape  out  the  fur  entirely ;  then  arrange  the  mushrooms  singly  en 
tii»  or  dishes,  and  dry  them  as  gradually  as  possible  in  a  gentle  oven. 
Pat  them,  when  they  are  done,  into  tin  canisters,  and  store  them 
where  they  will  be  secure  from  damp.  French  cooks  give  them  a 
KDgie  boil  in  water,  from  which  they  then  are  well  drained,  and  dried, 
•8  usual.  When  wanted  for  table,  they  should  be  put  into  cold  gravy, 
•bwly  heated,  and  gently  simmered,  imtil  they  arc  tender. 


154  MODERN  COOKEBT.  [chap.  vn. 


HtrSHROOM   POWDER. 

When  the  mushrooms  have  heen  prepared  with  great  nicety,  snd 
dried,  as  in  the  forgoing  receipt,  pound  them  to  a  rerr  fine  powder; 
sift  it,  and  put  it  immSliately  into  small  and  perfecUy  dry  bottles ; 
cork  and  seal  them  without  delay,  for  if  the  powder  be  lone  exposed 
to  the  air,  so  as  to  imbibe  any  humidity,  or  if  it  be  not  well  secured 
from  it  in  the  bottles,  it  will  be  likely  to  become  putrid :  mucfa  of 
that  which  is  purchased,  eyen  at  the  best  Italian  warehouses,  is  found 
to  be  so,  and,  as  it  is  sold  at  a  very  high  price,  it  is  a  great  ecanomy, 
as  well  as  a  surer  plan,  to  have  it  carefully  prepared  at  home.  It  is 
an  exceedingly  useful  store,  and  an  excellent  addition  to  many  dishes 
and  sauces.  To  insure  its  being  good,  the  mushrooms  should  be 
gathered  in  dry  weather,  and  if  any  addition  of  spices  be  made  to  the 
powder  (some  persons  mix  with  it  a  seasoning  of  mace  and  cayenne), 
they  should  be  put  into  the  oven  for  a  while  before  they  are  used: 
but  even  these  precautions  will  not  be  sufficient,  unless  the  powder  be 
stored  in  a  very  dry  place  after  it  is  bottled.  A  teaspoonful  of  it, 
with  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  strongr  veal  gravy,  as  much  cream,  and  a 
small  dessertspoonful  of  flour,  will  make  a  good  hichamel  or  white 
sauce. 

BXCELLENT   POTATO   PLOUR,   OR   ARROWROOT. 

{FecvUe  de  Pamme$  de  ierre.) 

Grate  into  a  large  vessel  full  of  cold  water,  six  pounds  of  sound 
tnealy  potatoes,  and  stir  them  well  together.  In  six  hours  nour  off 
the  water,  and  add  fresh,  stirring  the  mixture  well ;  repeat  tnis  pro- 
cess every  three  or  four  hours  during  the  day,  change  the  water  at 
night,  and  the  next  morning  pour  it  off;  put  two  or  three  quarts 
more  to  the  potatoes,  and  turn  them  directly  into  a  hair- sieve,  set 
over  a  pan  to  receive  the  flour,  which  may  then  be  washed  through 
the  sieve,  by  pouring  water  to  it.  Let  it  settle  in  the  pan,  drain  off 
the  water,  spread  the  potato-sediment  on  dishes,  dry  it  in  a  sJow  oven, 
sift  it,  and  put  it  into  bottles  or  jars,  and  cork  or  cover  them  closely. 
The  flour  thus  made  will  be  beautifully  white,  and  perfectly  flavour- 
less.   It  will  remain  good  for  years. 

Obs. — This  admirable  farina,  or  starch  of  potatoes,  is  now  much 
more  widely  known  and  vended  in  England  than  it  was  eome  years 
since.  It  can  at  present  be  procured  at  most  foreign  warehouses  and 
general  grocers' ;  but  we  would  recommend  its  being  home-made  by 
the  directions  given  above,  which  we  have  had  closely  followed  for 
many  years  wim  the  best  possible  success. 

TO   MAKE   FLOUR   OF   RICE. 

Take  any  quantity  of  whole  rice,  wash  it  thoroughly,  changing  tha 
water  several  times ;  drain  and  press  it  in  a  cloth,  then  spread  it  on  i^ 


CHA?  m.J  STORE    SAUCES.  155 

disli,  and  dry  it  perfectly ;  beat  it  in  a  mortar  to  a  smooth  powder, 
and  sifl  it  tnrough  a  fine  sieTe.  When  used  to  thicken  soup  or 
saaces,  mix  it  with  a  small  quantity  of  cold  water  or  of  broth,  and 
poor  it  to  them  while  they  are  boiling. 

This  flour,  when  newly  made,  is  of  much  purer  flavour  than  any 
usually  prepared  for  sale. 

POWDER   OP   SAVOURY   HERBS. 

All  herbs  which  are  to  be  dried  for  storing  should  be  gathered  in 
fine  weather;  cleared  from  dirt  and  decayed  leaves;  and  dried  quickly, 
bot  withoat  scorching,  in  a  Dutch  oven  before  the  fire,  or  in  any 
other  that  is  not  too  much  heated.  The  leaves  should  then  be 
stripped  from  the  stalks,  pounded,  sifted,  and  closely  corked  in  sepa- 
rate bottles ;  or  several  kinds  may  be  mixed  and  pounded  together  for 
the  convenience  of  seasoning  in  an  instant  gravies,  soups,  forcemeats, 
md  made  dishes :  appropriate  spices,  celery-seed,  and  dried  lemon- 
peel,  all  in  fine  powder,  can  be  added  to  the  herbs. 

TARTAR  HUSTARB. 

Knb  four  ounces  of  the  best  Durham  mustard  very  smooth  with  a 
foil  teaspoonf ul  of  salt,  and  wet  it  by  degrees  with  strong  horseradish 
vinegar,  a  dessertspoonful  of  cayenne,  or  of  chili  vinegar,  and  one  or 
two  of  tarragon  vinegar  when  its  flavour  is  not  disliked.  A  quarter 
ci  a  pint  of  vin^;ar  poured  boiling  upon  an  ounce  of  scraped  horse- 
radish, and  left  for  one  night,  closely  covered,  will  be  ready  to  use 
for  this  mustard,  but  it  willbe  better  for  standing  two  or  three  days 

Durham  mustard,  4  oz. ;  salt,  larse  teaspoonf ul ;  cayenne,  or  chili 
Tinegar,  1  dessertspoonful ;  horseraaish  vinegar,  third  of  pint. 

O&s.^This  is  an  exceedingly  pungent  compound,  but  has  many 
tpprovers. 

AKOTHBR  TARTAR  MUSTARD. 

Mix  the  salt  and  mustard  smoothly,  with  equal  parts  of  horseradish 
▼inegar,  and  of  chili  vinegar.  Mustard  made  by  these  receipts  will 
keep  long,  if  put  into  jars  or  bottles  and  closely  corked.  Cucumber, 
eschalot,  or  any  other  of  the  flavoured  vinegars  for  which  we  have 
given  receipts,  may  in  turn  be  used  for  it,  and  mushroom,  gherkin,  or 
Ldia  p  ckle- liquor,  likewise.

GENERAL  REMARKS. 

The  coarse  and  unpalatable  com- 
pounds so  constantly  met  with 
under  the  denomination  of  force- 
meat, even  at  tables  otherwise 
tolerably  well  served,  show  'vith 
how  little  attention  they  are  com- 
monly prepared. 
Many   very    indifferent  coolss 

pique  themselves  on  never  doing 
Weighing  Machine.  ^^^  ^j^j^^^  ^^  ^^j^^  ^^^  ^^^  ^^, 

quence  of  their  throwing  together  at  random  (or  **  by  guess"  as  they 
call  it)  the  ingredients  vmich  ought  to  be  proportioned  with  exceeding.' 
exactness  is  repeated  failure  in  all  they  attempt  to  do.  Long  expe- 
rience, and  a  very  correct  eye  may,  it  is  true,  enable  a  persoa  to 
dispense  with  weights  and  measures  w^ithout  hazarding  the  success  of 
their  operations;  but  it  is  an  experiment  which  the  learner  will  do 
better  to  avoid. 

A  large  marble  or  Wedgwood  mortar  is  indispensable  in  making  all 
the  finer  kinds  of  forcemeat ;  and  equally  so  indeed  for  many  other 
purposes  in  cookery;  no  kitchen,  therefore,  should  be  without  one;* 
and  for  whatever  preparation  it  may  be  used,  the  pounding  should  be 
continued  with  patience  and  perseverance  until  not  a  single  lumpor 
fibre  be  perceptible  in  the  mass  of  the  articles  beaten  together.  Tbis 
particularly  applies  to  potted  meats,  which  should  resemble  the 
smoothest  paste;  as  well  as  to  several  varieties  of  forcemeat.  Of 
these  last  it  should  be  observed,  that  such  as  are  made  by  the  French 
method  (see  quenelles)  are  the  most  appropriate  for  an  elegant  dinner, 

*  Two  or  three  mortars,  varying  in  size,  shonld  be  in  every  household  where  it 
is  expected  that  the  cookery  should  be  well  conducted :  Uiey  are  often  re^jnired 
idso  for  many  other  domesUo  purposes,  yet  it  is  not  unusual  to  find  both  these 
and  Hcalea,  weights,  and  measures  of  every  kind,  altogether  wanting  in  Englbh 
kitchsaa.

cither  to  serve  in  soups  or  to  fill  boned  poultry  of  any  kind ;  but 
when  their  exceeding  lightness,  which  to  foreigners  constitutes  one  of 
their  great  excellences,  is  objected  to,  it  may  be  remedied  by  substi- 
tatisg  diy  crumbs  of  bread  for  the  panada,  and  pounding  a  small 
qaantit}'  of  the  lean  of  a  boiled  ham,  with  the  other  ingredients:  how- 
ever, this  should  be  done  only  for  the  balls. 

No  particular  herb  or  spice  should  be  allowed  to  predominate 
powerfully  in  these  compositions ;  but  the  whole  of  the  seasonings 
should  be  taken  in  such  quantity  only  as  will  produce  an  agreeable 
OTour  when  they  are  blended  together. 

KO.  1.   GOOD  COMMON  FORCEMEAT^  FOR  ROAST  TEAL, 

TURKEYS,  &C. 

Grate  very  lightly  into  exceedingly  fine  crumbs,  four  ounces  of  the 
hiade  of  a  stale  loaf,  and  mix  thoroughly  with  it,  a  quarter  of  an 
ounce  of  lemon-rind  pared  as  thin  as  possible,  and  minced  extremely 
nnall;  the  same  quantity  of  savoury  herbs,  of  which  two- thirds 
Fhould  be  parsley,  and  one-third  thyme,  likewise  finely  minced,  a  little 
grated  nutmeg,  a  half  teaspoonfal  of  salt,  and  as  much  common 
pepper  or  cayenne  as  will  season  the  forcemeat  sufiiciently.  Break 
into  these,  two  ounces  of  good  butter  in  very  small  bits,  add  the 
onbeaten  yolk  of  one  egff,  and  with  the  fingers  work  the  whole  well 
together  until  it  «<  smoothly  mixed.  It  is  usual  to  chop  the  lemon - 
riid,  but  we  prefer  it  lightly  grated  on  a  fine  grater.  It  should 
always  be  fresh  for  the  purpose,  or  it  will  be  likely  to  impart  a  very 
unpleasant  flavour  to  the  forcemeat.  Half  the  rind  of  a  moderate- 
nzed  lemon  will  be  sufficient  for  this  quantity ;  which  for  a  large 
turkey  must  be  increased  one-half. 

BrKd-crumbs,  4  oz. ;  lemon-rind,  \  oz.  (or  grated  rind  of  ]  lemon) ; 
nnxed  savoury  herbs,  minced,  \  oz. ;  salt,  \  teaspoonful ;  pepper,  \  to 
\  of  teaspoonful ;  butter,  2  oz. ;  yolk,  1  egg. 

Oh%. — This,  to  our  taste,  is  a  much  nicer  and  more  delicate  force- 
aieat  than  that  which  is  made  with  suet,  and  we  would  recommend  it 
for  trial  in  preference.  Any  variety  of  herb  or  spice  may  be  used  to 
pre  it  flavour,  and  a  little  minced  onion  or  eschalot  can  be  added  to 
ft  also;  but  these  last  do  not  appear  to  us  suited  to  the  meats  for 
which  the  forcemeat  is  more  particularly  intended.  Half  an  ounce  of 
the  butter  may  be  omitted  on  ordinary  occasions :  and  a  portion  of 
narjoram  or  of  sweet  basil  may  take  the  place  of  part  of  the  thyme 
sod  parsley  when  preferred  to  them.

hutter,  a  dessertspoonful  of  herbs,  chopped  small,  some  lemon-grate, 
Aouneg,  a  little  salt,  a  good  seasoning  of  pepper  or  cayenne  and  one

>'0.   3.       SUPERIOR   SUET   FORCEMEAT,  FOR   TEAL,   TURKEYS,  &C. 

Mix  well  together  six  ounces  of  fine  stale  crumbs,  with  an  eqoa' 
weight  of  beef- Kidney  suet,  chopped  extremely  small,  a  large  dessert- 
spoonful of  parsley,  mixed  with  a  little  lemon-thyme,  a  teaspoonful 
of  salt,  a  quarter  one  of  cayenne,  and  a  saltspoonful  or  rather  more 
of  mace  and  nutmeg  together ;  work  these  up  with  three  unbeaten 
egg-yolks,  and  three  teaspoonsfVil  of  milk ;  then  put  the  fbroemeat 
into  a  large  mortar,  and  pound  it  perfectly  smooth.  Take  it  out,  and 
let  it  remain  in  a  cool  place  for  hsdf  an  hour  at  least  before  it  is  used; 
then  roll  it  into  balls,  if  it  be  wanted  to  serve  in  that  form ;  flour  and 
fry  them  gently  from  seven  to  eight  minutes,  and  dry  them  well 
before  they  ar^  dished. 

Beef  suet  finely  minced,  6  oz. ;  bread-crumbs,  6  oz.;  parder, 
mixed  with  little  thyme,  1  large  dessertspoonlul ;  salt,  1  teaspoonfoi;

egg-yolks,  3 ;  milk,  3  teaspoonsful :  well  pounded.  Fried  in  balls,  7 
to  8  minutes,  or  poached,  6  to  7. 

Obs. — The  finely  grated  rind  of  half  a  lemon  can  be  added  to  this 
forcemeat  at  pleasure ;  and  for  some  purposes  a  morsel  of  garlic,  or 
three  or  four  minced  eschalots,  may  be  mixed  with  it  before  it  is  pot 
into  the  mortar. 

XO.   4.      COMMON   SUET  FORCEMEAT. 

Beef  suet  is  commonly  used  in  the  composition  of  this  kind  of 
forcemeat,  but  we  think  that  veal-kidney  suet,  when  it  could  be 
obtained,  would  have  a  better  efiect ;  though  the  reader  will  easily 
comprehend  that  it  is  scarcely  possible  for  us  to  have  every  variety  of 
eveiy  receipt  which  we  insert  put  to  the  test;  in  some  caaes  we  are 
compelled  merely  to  suggest  what  appear  to  us  likely  to  be  improve- 
ments. ^  Strip  carefully  every  morsel  of  skin  from  the  snet,  and 
mince  it  small ;  to  six  ounces  add  eight  of  bread-crumbs,  with  the 
same  proportion  of  herbs,  spice,  salt,  and  lemon-peel,  as  in  the  fore- 
going receipt,  and  a  couple  of  whole  eggs,  which  should  be  very 
slightly  beaten,  after  the  specks  have  been  taken  out  with  the  point 
of  a  small  fork.  Should  more  liquid  be  required,  the  yolk  of  another 
egg,  or  a  rooonful  or  two  of  milk,  may  be  used.  Half  thia  quantity 
¥rill  be  sufficient  for  a  small  joint  of  veal,  or  for  a  dozen  balls,  which, 
when  it  is  more  convenient  to  serve  it  in  that  form,  may  be  fried  or

Inowned  beneatli  the  roast,  and  then  dished  round  it,  though  this  last 
is  not  a  Tery  refined  mode  of  dressing  them.  From  eight  to  ten 
minutes  will  fry  them  well.

MO.   6.      A   FINER  OY8TEB   FORCEMEAT. 

Pound  the  preceding  forcemeat  to  the  smoothest  paste,  with  the

may  be  poached  or  fried  in  balls  for  soups  or  made  dishes,  or  used  to 
^  boned  fowls,  or  the  breasts  of  boiled  turkeys  with  equally  good

NO.    8.      FORCEMEAT  FOR   HARE. 

The  first  receipt  of  this  chapter  will  be  found  very  good  for  hare 
without  any  variation ;  but  the  liver  boiled  for  three  minutes  and 
finelv  minced,  may  be  added  to  it  when  it  is  thought  an  improTeroent: 
another  half  ounce  of  butter,  and  a  small  portion  more  of  egg  will 
then  be  required.  A  couple  of  ounces  of  rasped  bacon,  and  a  glas  of 
port-wine,  are  sometimes  recommended  for  this  forcemeat,  but  we 
think  it  is  better  without  them,  especially  when  slices  of  bacon  are 
used  to  line  the  hare.  A  flavouring  of  minced  onion  or  eschalot  can 
be  added  when  the  taste  is  in  its  favour ;  or  the  forcemeat  Ko.  3  may 
be  substituted  for  this  altoprether 


NO.    9.      ONION   AND   SAGE  STUFFING,    FOR   PORK,   GEESE, 

OR   DUCKS. 

Boil  three  large  onions  from  ten  to  fifteen  minutes,  press  the  water 
from  them,  chop  them  small,  and  mix  with  them  an  equal  quantity  of 
bread-crumbs,  a  heaped  tablespoonful  of  minced  sage,  an  ounce  of 
butter,  a  half  saltspoonful  of  pepper,  and  twice  as  much  of  salt,  and 
put  them  into  the  body  of  the  goose ;  part  of  the  liver  boiled  for  two 
or  three  minutes  and  shred  fine,  is  sometimes  added  to  these,  and  the 
whole  is  bound  together  with  the  yolk  of  one  egg  or  two ;  but  they 
are  quite  as  frequently  served  without.  The  onions  can  be  used  raw, 
when  their  very  strong  flavour  is  not  objected  to,  but  the  odour  of 
the  whole  dish  will  then  be  somewhat  overpowering. 

Large  onions,  3 ;  boiled  20  to  30  minutes.  Sage,  2  to  3  dessert- 
spoonsful (  or  i  to  {  oz.) ;  butter,  1  oz. :  pepper,  ^  teaspoonful;  salt, 
1  teaspoonful

NO.   10.      MR.  COOKe's  FORCEMBAT  FOR  DUCKS   OR  GEESE. 

Two  parts  of  chopped  onion,  two  parts  of  bread-crumbsi,  three  of 
better,  one  of  pounded  sage,  and  a  seasoning  of  pepper  and  salt. 
This  receipt  we  have  not  proved. 

NO.  11.     FORCEHBAT   BALJ^S  FOR   HOCK   TURTLE  SOUPS. 

The  French  forcemeat.  No.  17  of  the  present  Chapter,  is  the  most 
nfined  and  appropriate  forcemeat  to  serve  in  mock  turtle,  but  a  more 
solid  and  highly  seasoned  one  is  usually  added  to  it  in  this  country. 
In  very  common  cookery  the  ingredients  are  merely  chopped  small 
and  mixed  together  with  a  moistening  of  egss ;  but  when  the  trouble 
of  poouding  and  blending  them  properly  is  objected  to,  we  would 
Tecommend  the  common  veal  forcemeat  No.  1,  in  preference;  as  the 
undressed  veal  and  suet,  when  merely  minced,  do  not  produce  a  good 
effect  Four  ounces  each  of  these,  with  an  ounce  or  so  of  the  lean  of 
a  boiled  ham,  and  three  ounces  of  bread-crumbs,  a  large  dessert- 
spoonful of  minced  parsley,  a  small  portion  of  thyme  or  marjoram,  a 
altspoonful  of  white  pepper,  twice  as  much  or  more  of  salt,  a  little 
cayenne,  half  a  small  nutmeg,  and  a  couple  of  eggs,  well  mixed  with 
a  fork  first  to  separate  the  meat,  and  atter  the  moistening  is  added, 
vith  the  fingers,  then  rolled  into  balls,  and  boiled  in  a  little  soup  for 
twelre  minutes,  is  the  manner  in  which  it  is  prepared ;  but  the  reader 
will  find  the  following  receipt  very  superior  to  it :— Rasp,  that  is  to 
nj,  scrape  with  a  knife  clear  from  the  fibre,  four  ounces  of  veal, 
▼hieb  should  be  cut  into  thick  slices,  and  taken  quite  free  from  skin 
and  fat ;  chop  it  fine,  and  then  pound  it  as  smoothly  as  possible  in  a 
large  mortar,  with  three  ounces  of  the  rasped  fat  of  an  unboiled  ham 
of  good  flavour  or  of  the  finest  bacon,  and  one  of  butter,  two  ounces 
of  bread-crumbs,  a  tablespoonful  of  the  lean  of  a  boiled  ham,  should 
it  be  at  band,  a  good  seasoning  of  cayenne,  nutmeg,  and  mace,  mixed

Masoning  it  may  require ;  and  when  it  is  of  good  flavour,  roll  it  into 

Us  of  moderate  size,  and  boil  them  twelve  minutes ;  then  drain  and 

Sthem  into  the  soup.    No  forcemeat  should  be  boUcd  in  the  soup
