little,  sprinkle  over  it  a  small  quantity  of  salt  and  cayen^  dip  it  mto 
clarified  butter  and  then  into  some  fine  bread-crumbs,  with  whidi 
it  should  be  thickly  covered,  lay  it  on  the  gridiron,  and  whea 
equally  browned  all  over  serve  it  immediately.  If  more  convenient 
the  ox-tail  may  be  set  into  the  oven  or  before  the  fire,  until  properly 
coloured :  it  may  likewise  be  sent  to  table  without  broiling,  dished 
upon  stewed  cabbage  or  in  its  own  gravy  thickened,  and  wiui  tomaU 
sauce,  in  a  tureen. 

TO   SALT   AND   PICKLE   BEEF,   IN   VARIOUS   WATS. 

Let  the  meat  hang  a  couple  of  days  in  mild  weather,  and  four  or 
five  in  winter,  before  it  is  salted  or  pickled.  During  the  heat  of 
summer  it  is  better  to  immerse  it  entirelv  in  brine,  that  it  may  be 
secured  alike  from  the  flies,  and  from  the  danger  of  becoming  putrid. 
Trim  it,  and  take  out  the  kernels  from  the  fat ;  then  rub  a  little  fine 
dry  salt  over  it,  and  leave  it  until  the  following  day ;  drain  it  wdl 
from  the  blood,  which  will  be  found  to  have  flowed  from  it,  and  it 
will  be  ready  for  any  of  the  following  modes  of  curing,  which  are  all 
•excellent  of  their  kind,  and  have  been  well  proved. 

In  very  cold  weather,  the  salt  may  be  applied  quite  warm  to  the 
meat :  it  should  always  be  perfectly  dry,  and  reduced  to  powder. 

Saltpetre  hardens  and  renders  the  meat  indi^tible ;  sugar,  on  tbe 
contrary,  mellows  and  improves  it  much  ;  and  it  is  more  tender  when 
jured  with  bay  salt  than  when  common  salt  is  used  for  it. 

TO  SALT  AND  BOIL  A  ROUND  OF  BEEF. 

Mix  an  ounce  of  saltpetre,  finely  powdered,  with  half  a  pound  of 
very  coarse  sugar,  and  rub  the  beef  thoroughly  with  them ;  in  tvo 
days  add  three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  common  salt,  well  dried  and 
beaten ;  turn  and  rub  the  meat  well  in  every  part  with  the  pickle 
for  three  weeks,  when  it  will  be  fit  to  dress.  Just  wash  off  the  salt, 
and  skewer  the  beef  as  round  and  ajs  even  as  possible ;  bind  it  tightly 
with  broad  tape,  cover  it  with  cold  water,  place  it  over  a  rather 
brisk  fire,  and  after  it  boils  draw  it  to  the  side  of  the  stove  and  let  it 
simmer  gently  for  at  least  five  hours.  Carrots,  mashed  turnips,  or 
cabbages,  are  usually  served  with  boiled  beef;  and  borse-radbb 
stewed  for  ten  minutes  in  equal  parts  of  vinegar  and  water,  then 
prised  well  from  them,  and  mixed  with  some  rich  melted  butter,  is 
a  ffood  sauce  for  it. 

iBeef,  20  lbs. ;  coarse  sugar,  i  lb. ;  saltpetre,  1  oz. :  2  days.    Salt, 
I  lb. :  21  days.    Boil  5  hours,  or  more. 

Obs. — Beef  cured  by  this  receipt  if  properly  boiled,  is  tender,  of 
good  colour  and  flavour,  and  not  over  salt.  The  rump,  edge-bone, 
a^d  brisket  may  be  raited,  or  pickled  in  the  same  way  as  the 
round

HAMBURGH  PICELB  FOR  BEEP^   HAMS,   AND  TONQUB. 

Boil  together,  for  twenty  minutes,  two  gallons  of  water,  three 
pounds  of  bay  salt,  two  pounds  of  coarce  sugar,  two  ounces  of  salt- 
petre, and  two  of  black  pepper,  bruised,  and  tied  in  a  fold  of  muslin ; 
dear  off  the  scum  thoroughly,  as  it  rises,  pour  the  pickle  into  a  deep 
earthen  pan,  and  when  it  is  quite  cold  lay  in  the  meat,  of  whicn 
ereiy  part  must  be  perfectly  covered  with  it.  A  moderate-sized 
round  of  beef  will  be  ready  for  table  in  a  fortnight ;  it  should  be 
tamed  occasionally  in  the  brine.  Five  pounds  of  common  salt  may 
be  substituted  for  the  quantity  of  bay  salt  given  above ;  but  the 
neat  will  not  be  so  finely  flavoured. 

Water,  2  gallons ;  hay-salt,  3  lbs. ;  saltpetre,  2  oz. ;  black  pepper, 
2  oz. ;  sugar,  2  lbs. :  20  minutes. 

ANOTHER   FICKLE  FOR  TONGUES,   BEEF,   AND   HAMS. 

To  three  gallons  of  spring  water  add  six  pounds  of  common  salt, 
two  pounds  of  bay-salt,  two  pounds  of  common  loaf  sugar,  and  two 
ocmces  of  saltpetre.  Boil  these  over  a  gentle  fire,  and  be  careful  to 
take  off  all  the  scum  as  it  rises :  when  quite  cold  it  will  be  fit  for 
use.  Bub  the  meat  to  be  cured,  with  fine  salt,  and  let  it  drain  for  a 
day  in  order  to  free  it  from  the  blood ;  then  immerse  it  in  the  brine, . 
taking  care  that  every  part  of  it  shall  be  covered.  Young  pork 
siioald  not  remain  more  than  from  three  to  five  days  in  the  pickle ; 
but  hams  for  drying  may  be  left  in  it  for  a  fortnight  at  least: 
tongues  will  be  ready  in  rather  less  time.  Beef  may  remain  from 
one  week  to  two,  according  to  its  size,  and  the  degree  of  saltness 
desired  for  it.  A  little  experience  will  soon  teach  the  exact  time 
Rquired  for  the  different  kinds  of  meat.  W^en  the  pickle  has  been 
in  nse  for  about  three  months,  boil  it  up  again  sently,  and  take  the 
scum  carefully  off.  Add  to  it  three  pounds  of  common  salt,  four 
oimoes  of  sugar,  and  one  of  saltpetre :  it  will  remain  good  for  many 
months. 

Water,  3  gallons ;  common  salt,  6  lbs. ;  bay  salt,  2  lbs. ;  loaf  sugar^ 
2  lbs. ;  saltpetre,  2  oz. :  boil  20  to  30  minutes. 

DUTCH,  OR  HUNO  BEEF. 

For  fourteen  pounds  weight  of  the  round,  the  rump,  or  the  thick 
flank  of  beef^  mix  two  ounces  of  saltpetre  with  the  same  quantity  of 
eoarae  sagar;  rub  the  meat  with  them  in  every  part,  and  let  it 
lemain  for  two  days,  then  add  one  pound  of  bay  salt,  four  ounces  of 
eommon  salt,  and  one  ounce  of  ground  black  pepper.  Bub  these 
ingredients  thoroughly  into  the  beef,  and  in  four  days  pour  over  it  a 
pound  of  treacle ;  rub  and  turn  it  daily  for  a  fortnight ;  drain,  and 
■end  it  to  be  smoked.     When  wanted  for  table,  put  it  into  plenty  of

boiling  water,  boil  it  slowly,  and  press  it  under  a  heavy  weight  while 
hot  A  slice  of  this  beef,  from  which  the  edges  haye  been  carefhlly 
trimmed,  will  serve  to  flavour  soups  or  gravies  as  well  as  ham. 

Beef,  14  lbs. ;  saltpetre  and  coarse  sugar,  each  2  oz. :  2  dajs. 
Bay  salt,  1  lb. ;  common  salt,  4  oa. :  pepper,  1  os. :  4  days.  Treacle, 
1  lb. :  14  days. 

Ob9, — Three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  coarse  suear  may  be  rubbed 
into  the  meat  at  first,  and  the  treacle  may  be  altogether  omitted; 
cloves  and  mace,  too,  may  be  added  in  the  same  proportion  as  ixa 
spiced  beef. 

COLLARED  BEEF* 

Only  the  thinnest  part  of  the  flank,  or  the  ribs,  which  are  not  so 
genendly  used  for  it,  vrill  serve  conveniently  for  collaring.  The  fint 
of  these  should  be  hung  in  a  damp  place  for  a  day  or  two,  to  soften 
the  outer  skin ;  then  rubbed  with  coarse  sugar,  and  left  for  a  couple 
of  days ;  when,  for  eight  pounds  of  the  meat,  one  ounce  of  saltpetre 
and  half  a  pound  of  mlt  should  be  added.  Li  ten  days  it  will  be  fit 
to  dress.  Tne  bones  and  tough  inner  skin  must  be  removed,  and 
the  beef  sprinkled  thickly  on  the  under  side  with  parsley  and  other 
savoury  herbs  shred  small,  before  it  is  rolled,  which  should  be  done 
very  tightly :  it  must  then  be  secured  with  a  doth,  and  bound  as 
closely  as  possible  with  broad  tape.  It  will  require  nearly  or  quite 
five  hours  of  gentle  boiling,  and  should  be  placed  while  hot  under  a 
weight,  or  in  a  press,  without  having  the  tape  and  cloth  removed. 

£ef,  8  lbs. ;  sugar,  8  oz. ;  salt,  8  oz. :  10  days.    Boil  5  hours. 

COLLARED  BEEF. 

{Another  toay.) 

Mix  half  an  ounce  of  saltpetre  with  the  same  quantity  of  pepper, 
four  ounces  of  bay  salt,  and  four  of  common  salt ;  with  th^  mb 
well  from  six  to  seven  pounds  of  the  thin  flank,  and  in  four  days  add 
seven  ounces  of  treacle ;  turn  the  beef  daily  in  the  pickle  for  a  week 
or  more ;  dip  it  into  water,  bone  it  and  skin  the  inside^  roll  and  bind 
it  up  very  tightlv,  lay  it  into  cold  water,  and  boil  it  for  three  hours 
and  a  half.  We  have  found  beef  dressed  by  this  receipt  extremely 
pood :  herbs  can,  of  course,  be  added  to  it  as  usual.  Spices  lund 
juniper  beries  would  to  many  tastes  improve  it,  but  we  give  the 
receipt  simply  as  we  have  been  accustomed  to  have  it  used. 

Thin  flank,  6  to  7  lbs. ;  bay  salt,  and  common  salt,  each  4  oz.; 
saltpetre,  \  oz.;  pepper,  ^  oz. :  4  days.  Treacle,  7  oz.:  8  to  10 
days.    Boiled  8^  hours. 

A  COMMON  RECEIPT  FOR  SALTIMO  BEEF. 

One  ounce  of  saltpetre,  and  a  pound  of  common  salt,  will  be 
sufficient  for  sixteen  pounds  of  beef.    Both  should  be  well  dried,  and

Andy  powdered ;  tbe  saltpetre  nibbed  first  eqnally  over  tbe  meati 
mnd  the  salt  next  applied  in  every  part.  It  should  be  rubbed 
thoroughly  widi  the  pickle  and  turned  daily,  from  a  week  to  ten 
days.  An  ounce  or  two  of  sugar  mixed  with  the  saltpetre  will 
render  the  beef  more  tender  and  palatable. 
Beet,  16 lbs.;  saltpetre,  1  os.;  salt,  1  lb. :  7  to  10  days. 

BPTCBD   ROUND   OF  BEEF. 

(^Tery  highly  floaxmred.) 

Rub  the  beef  well  in  every  part  with  half  a  pound  of  coarse  brown 
sugar,  and  let  it  remain  two  days ;  then  reduce  to  powder,  and  mix 
thoroughly  before  they  are  appued  to  the  meat,  two  ounces  of  salt- 
petre, three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  common  salt,  a  quarter  of  a  pound 
of  black  pepper,  three  ounces  of  allspice,  and  four  of  bruised  juniper- 
berries.  Kub  these  ingredients  strongly  and  equally  over  the  joint, 
and  do  so  daily  for  three  weeks,  turning  it  at  the  same  time.  Just 
wash  off  the  spice,  and  put  the  beef  into  a  tin,  or  covered  earthen 
pan  as  nearly  of  its  size  as  possible,  with  a  cup  of  water  or  gravy ; 
cover  the  top  thickly  with  cnopped  beef-suet,  and  lay  a  coarse  thick 
crust  over  tne  pan ;  place  the  cover  on  it,  and  bake  the  meat  from 
five  to  six  hours  in  a  moderate  oven,  which  should  not,  however,  be 
sufficiently  fierce  to  harden  the  outside  of  the  joint,  which,  if  pro- 
perly managed  will  be  exceedingly  tender.  Let  it  cool  in  the  pan ; 
and  dear  off  the  suet  before  it  is  dished.  It  is  to  be  served  cold,  and 
will  remain  good  for  a  fortnight. 

Beef^  20  to  25  lbs.  weight ;  sugar,  3  oz. :  2  days.  Saltpetre,  2  oz.; 
common  salt,  |  lb. ;  black  pepper,  4  oz. ;  allspioe,  3  oz. ;  juniper- 
berries,  4  oz. :  21  days.    Baked  5  to  6  hours. 

Ohs. — We  have  not  ourselves  tested  this  receipt,  but  the  meat 
cured  by  it  has  reodved  such  hi^h  commendation  from  several  of  our 
friends  who  have  partaken  of  it  frequently,  that  we  think  we  may 
safely  insert  it  vrithout.  The  proportion  of  allspice  appears  to  us 
more  than  would  be  agreeable  to  many  tastes,  and  we  would  rather 
recommend  that  part  of  it  should  be  omitted,  and  that  a  portion  of 
nutmeg,  mace,  and  cloves,  should  be  substituted  for  it ;  as  we  have 
fboad  these  spices  to  answer  well  in  the  following  receipt. 

fiPTCED  BEEF. 

(jOood  and  wkoUfome,) 

For  twelve  pounds  of  the  round,  rump,  or  thick'  flank  of  bee( 
take  a  lai^  teaspoonful  of  freshly-pounded  mace,  and  of  ground 
black  pepper,  twice  as  much  of  doves,  one  small  nutmeg,  and  a 
quarter  of  a  teaspoonful  of  cayenne,  all  in  the  finest  powder.  Mix 
tnem  weU  wrth  seven  ounces  of  brown  sugar,  rub  the  beef  with  them 
sod  lei  it  lie  three  days ;  add  to  it  then  half  a  pound  of  fine  salt,  and 


200  HODEBN  GOOKEBT.  [chap,  z 

rub  and  turn  it  once  in  twenty-four  hours  for  twelve  cUijrs.  Just 
wash,  but  do  not  soak  it ;  skewer,  or  bind  it  into  good  form,  put  it 
into  a  stewpan  or  saucepan  nearly  of  its  size,  pour  to  it  a  pint  and  a 
half  of  good  beef  broth,  and  when  it  begins  to  boil,  take  off  the  scam, 
and  throw  in  one  small  onion,  a  moderate-sized  f^^t  of  thyme  and 
parsley,  and  two  large,  or  four  small  carrots.  Let  it  simmer  quite 
softly  for  four  hours  and  a  half,  and  if  not  wanted  to  serve  hot, 
leave  it  in  its  own  liquor  until  it  is  nearly  cold.  This  is  an  excellent 
and  far  more  wholesome  dish  than  the  hard,  bright-colonied  bei^ 
which  is  cured  with  large  quantities  of  salt  and  saltpetre :  two  or 
three  ounces  of  juniper-berries  may  be  added  to  it  witn  the  spice,  to 
heighten  its  flavour. 

Beef,  12  lbs. ;  sugar,  7  oz. ;  mace  and  black  pepper,  each,  1  large 
teaspoonful ;  cloves,  in  powder,  1  large  dessertspoonful ;  nutmeg,  1 ; 
cayenne,  i  teaspoonful :  3  days.  JPine  salt,  i  \h.:  12  days.  Beef 
broth  (or  bouillon),  H  pint;  onion,  1  small;  bunch  of  herbs;  car- 
rots, 2  large,  or  4  small :  stewed  4|  hours. 

Obs, — We  give  this  receipt  exactly  as  we  have  often  had  it  used, 
but  celery  and  turnips  might  be  added  to  the  gravy;  and  when  the 
apeparance  of  the  meat  is  much  considered,  three-quarters  of  an 
ounce  of  saltpetre  may  be  mixed  with  the  spices ;  the  beef  may  abo 
be  plainly  boiled  in  water  only,  with  a  few  vegetables,  or  baked  in  a 
deep  pan  with  a  little  gravy.  No  meat  must  ever  be  left  to  cool  in 
the  stewpan  or  saucepan  in  which  it  is  cooked ;  it  must  be  lifted  into 
a  pan  of  its  own  deptn,  and  the  liquor  poured  upon  it. 

▲  MINIATURE   ROUND   OF   BEEF. 

'*  Select  a  fine  rib  of  beef,  and  have  it  cut  small  or  laige  in  width, 
according  to  your  taste ;  it  may  thus  be  made  to  weigh  from  five  to 
twelve  pounds,  or  more.  Take  out  the  bone,  and  \Trap  the  meat 
round  like  a  fillet  of  veal,  securing  it  with  two  or  three  woodoi 
skewers ;  place  it  in  a  strong  pickle  for  four  or  five  days,  and  then 
cook  it,  taking  care  that  it  does  not  boil,  but  only  simmers,  from 
forty  minutes,  or  more»  according  to  its  size.  It  is  best  to  put  it  on 
in  hot  water,  as  it  will  not  draw  the  gra,yj  so  much  as  cold.  Many 
persons  adjust  a  rib  of  beef  in  this  way  for  roasting :  let  them  tiy  it 
salted,  and  they  need  not  envy  the  possessor  of  the  finest  round  of 
beef."  We  give  the  receipt  to  our  readers  in  its  original  form,  and 
we  can  assure  them,  from  our  own  experience,  that  it  is  a  good  cue; 
but  we  would  recommend  that,  in  dressing  the  meat,  quite  the  usual 
tiipe  for  each  pound  of  it  should  be  allowed.  When  boned  and 
rolled  at  the  butcher*s,  the  skewers  should  be  removed  when  it  is 
first  brought  in ;  it  should  be  well  wiped  with  a  dry  cloth,  or  washed 
with  a  little  fresh  brine,  and  a  small  quantity  of  salt  and  saltpetre 
should  be  rubbed  over  the  inside,  it  may  then  be  firmly  bound 
with  tape,  and  will  be  quite  ready  to  boil  when  taken  from  the 
pickle.    The  sirloin,  after  the  inside  fillet  is  removed,  may  be  cured 


CBAP.  X.J  BEEP*  201 

md  drened  in  the  same  way,  and  will  be  fonnd  super-excellent  it 
the  beef  be  well  fatted  and  properly  kept.  The  Hamburgh  pickle 
(see  page  197)  is  perhaps  tne  best  for  these  joints.  Part  of  the 
nunp,  taken  clear  of  bone,  answers  admirably  when  prepared  by  this 
receipt. 

BEEF   ROLL,   OR   CANRLLON   DB   BCEUF.      (jSNTRiB.) 

Chop  and  mix  thoroughly  two  pounds  of  lean  and  very  tender 
be^with  one  pound  of  slightly  striped  bacon ;  season  them  with  a 
large  teaspoonful  of  pepper,  a  little  salt,  a  small  nutmeg,  or  two- 
thinls  as  much  mace,  the  grated  rind  of  a  lemon,  or  a  teaspoonful  of 
thjme  and  parsley  finely  minced.  Form  the  whole  into  a  thick 
rouleao,  wrap  a  buttered  paper  round  it,  enclose  it  in  a  paste  made 
of  floor  and  water,  and  send  it  to  a  moderate  oven  for  a  couple  of 
hoars.  Remoye  the  paper  and  the  crust,  and  serve  the  meat  with  a 
little  brown  gniny.  Lamb  and  veal  are  excellent  dressed  in  this 
my,  particularly  when  mixed  with  plenty  of  mushrooms.  Brown 
eoeomber  sauce  should  be  served  with  the  lamb;  and  currie,  or 
ojBter  sauce,  when  there  are  no  mushrooms,  with  the  veal.  A 
la?oaring  of  onion  or  of  eschalot,  where  it  is  liked,  can  be  added  at

un*  the  bacon. 

Beef,  2  lbs. ;  bacon,  1  lb. ;  pepper,  i  oz. ;  little  salt ;  small  nutmeg ; 
nnd  of  1  lemon,  or  savoury  herbs,  1  tablespoonful :  baked  2  hours. 

MINCED   COLLOPS   AU  NATUREL. 

Mince  finely  a  pound  of  very  tender  rump  steak,  free  from  fat  or 
din;  season  it  with  a  moderate  quantity  of  pepper  and  salt,  set  it 
over  a  gentle  fire»  and  keep  it  stiritHl  with  a  fork  until  it  is  quite  hot 
that  it  may  not  gather  into  lumps.  Simmer  it  very  slowly  in  its 
own  gravy  from  ten  to  twelve  minutes,  and  then,  should  it  be  too 
drjr,  add  a  little  boiling  water,  broth,  or  gravy;  stew  it  for  two 
iniiiiites  longer,  and  serve  it  directly. 

These  o^ops  are  particularly  suited  to  persons  in  delicate  health, 
or  of  weak  digestion ;  and  when  an  extra  dish  is  required  at  a  short 
notice,  from  the  expedition  with  which  they  may  be  dressed,  they  are 
a  convenient  resource. 

10  to  12  minutes. 

BAYOURT   MINCED   COLLOPS.

bely-shred  parsley  or  mixed  savoury  herbs,  two-t£rds  as  much  of 
lalt,  and  half  the  quantity  of  pepper.    Keep  these  stirred  over  a

BEEF   TONGUES. 

These  may  be  cured  by  any  of  the  receipts  which  we  have  already 
ffiven  for  pickling  beef,  or  for  those  which  will  be  found  further  oa 
for  hams  and  baicon.  Some  persons  prefer  them  cured  with  salt  sod 
saltpetre  only,  and  dried  naturally  in  a  cool  and  airy  roouL  Far 
such  of  our  readers  as  like  them  highly  and  richly  flavoured  we  giTe 
our  own  method  of  having  them  prepared,  which  is  this: — ^Rvb 
over  the  tongue  a  handful  of  fine  nilt,  and  let  it  drain  until  the  fol- 
lowing day ;  then,  should  it  weigh  from  seven  to  eight  poandi,  mix 
thoroughly  an  ounce  of  saltpetre,  two  ounces  of  the  coarsest  sugar, 
and  hidf  an  ounce  of  black  pepper ;  when  the  ton^e  has  been  weU 
rubbed  with  these,  add  three  ounces  of  bruised  juniper- berries ;  and 
when  it  has  laid  two  days,  eight  ounces  of  bay  salt,  dried  and 
pounded ;  at  the  end  of  three  days  more,  pour  on  it  half  a  pcMind  d 
treacle,  and  let  it  remain  in  the  pickle  a  fortnight  after  Uus ;  thei 
hang  it  to  drain,  fold  it  in  brown  paper,  and  send  it  to  be  amokd 
over  a  wood  fire  for  two  or  three  weeks.    Should  the  peculiar  flaToa

rubbed  into  the  tongue  in  the  int  instance  when  it  is  liked  better

BEEF  TONGUES. 

(A  Suffolk  Beeeipt,) 

For  each  yery  large  tongue,  mix  with  half  a  pound  of  salt  two 
onnoes  of  saltpetre  and  three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  the  coarsest 
sugar;  rub  the  tonnes  daily,  and  turn  them  in  the  pickle  for  five 
v^ks,  when  they  will  be  fit  to  be  dressed,  or  to  be  smoxed. 

1  large  tongue ;  salt,  |  lb. ;  sugar,  |  lb. ;  saltpetre,  2  oz. :  5  weeks. 

TO   DRE88  BEEF   TONGUES. 

When  taken  fresh  from  the  pickle  they  require  no  soaking,  unless 
tbey  should  have  remained  in  it  much  beyond  the  usual  time,  or  have 
been  cured  with  a  more  than  common  proportion  of  salt ;  but  when 
they  have  been  smoked  and  highly  dried,  they  should  be  laid  for  two 
or  three  hours  into  cold,  and  as  much  longer  into  tepid  water,  before 
they  are  dressed :  if  extremely  dry,  ten  or  twelve  hours  must  be 
aUowed  to  soften  them,  and  they  should  always  be  brought  very 
slowly  to  boil.  Two  or  three  carrots  and  a  large  bunch  of  savoury 
herbs,  added  after  the  scum  is  cleared  off,  will  improve  them.  They 
should  be  sinunered  until  they  are  extremely  tender,  when  the  skin 
^  peel  from  them  easily.  A  highly  dried  tongue  of  moderate  size 
wiU  usually  require  from  three  and  a  half  to  four  hours*  boiling ;  an 
unsmoked  one  about  an  hour  less ;  and  for  one  which  has  not  been 
salted  at  all  a  shorter  time  will  suffice. 

BOBDTKB  BECEIPT  FOR  BTEWINO  A  TONGUE. 

After  the  tongue  has  been  soaked,  trimmed,  and  washed  with 
csctreme  nicety,  &y  it  into  a  vessel  of  fitting  size,  and  place  round  it 
tfaiee  or  four  pounds  of  the  neck,  or  of  any  other  lean  cuttings  of 
bee^  with  some  bones  of  undressed  veal,  and  pour  in  sufficient  cold 
Viter  to  keep  it  covered  until  it  is  done ;  or,  instead  of  this,  use 
9^niog  Unseasoned  beef  broth  made  with  the  shin,  and  any  odd  bits  or 
boBes  of  veal  which  may  be  at  hand.  Let  the  tongue  be  brought  to 
^^|A  yenr  gradually,  that  it  may  be  plump  and  tender.  Remove  the 
96001  when  it  first  rises,  and  when  it  is  quite  cleared  off  add  a  large 


204  MODERN  COOKEBY.  [chap.  X. 

faggot  of  parsley,  thyme,  and  winter  sayoury,  three  d  rrots,  a  mdl 
onion,  and  one  mild  turnip.  After  three  hours  and  a  half  of  gentle 
simmering,  prohe  the  tongue,  and  if  sufficiently  done  peel  off  the 
skin  and  serve  it  quickly.  If  not  wanted  hot  for  table,  lay  it  upon 
a  very  clean  board  or  trencher,  and  fasten  it  down  to  it  by  passing  a 
carving  fork  through  the  root,  and  a  smaller  one  through  the  tip, 
drawing  the  tongue  straight  with  the  latter  before  it  is  fixed  in  the 
board;  let  it  remain  thus  until  it  is  quite  cold.  It  is  much  the 
fashion  at  present  to  elaze  hams  and  tongues,  but  this  should  never 
be  attempted  by  a  cook  not  well  acquainted  with  the  manner  of  doing 
it,  and  tne  proper  flavour  and  appearance  of  the  glaze.  For  direc- 
tions to  make  it,  see  pa^  104.  Wnere  expense  is  not  regarded,  three 
or  four  pounds  of  veal  may  be  added  to  the  beef  in  this  receipt,  or 
the  tongue  may  be  stewed  in  a  prepared  gravy  made  with  equal  parts 
of  beef  and  veal,  and  veeetables  as  above,  but  without  salt :  this  may 
afterwards  be  converted  into  excellent  soup.  A  fresh  or  an  nn- 
smoked  tongue  may  be  dressed  in  this  way,  but  will  require  lea 
time :  for  the  former  salt  must  be  added  to  the  gravy. 

TO   ROAST   A   BEEF   HEART. 

Wash  and  soak  the  heart  very  thoroughly,  cut  away  the  lobes, 
fill  the  cavities  with  a  veal  forcemeat  (No.  1,  Chapter  Vtll.),  secure 
it  well  with  a  needle  and  twine,  or  very  coarse  thread,  and  roast 
it  at  a  good  fire  for  an  hour  and  a  half,  keeping  it  basted  plenti- 
fully with  butter.  Pour  melted  butter  over  it,  after  it  is  dished,  and 
send  it  to  table  as  hot  as  possible.  Many  persons  boil  the  heart  for 
three  quarters  of  an  hour  before  it  is  put  to  the  fire,  and  this  is  said 
to  render  it  more  delicate  eating ;  the  time  of  roasting  must  of  course 
be  proportionately  diminished.  Grood  brown  gravy  may  be  sub- 
stituted'for  the  melted  butter,  and  currant  jelly  also  may  be  served 
with  it. 

1^  hour,  or  more. 

BEEF  KIDNEY. 

Slice  the  kidney  rather  thin,  ailer  having  stripped  off  the  skin  and 
removed  the  fat ;  season  it  with  pepper,  salt,  and  grated  nutmeg,  and 
sprinkle  over  it  plenty  of  minced  parsley,  or  equal  parts  of  jwrsley 
and  eschalots  chopped  very  small.  Fry  the  slices  over  a  bruk  fire, 
and  when  nicely  browned  on  both  sides,  stir  amongst  them  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  flour,  and  pour  in  by  degrees  a  cup  of  gravy  and  a  glass 
of  white  wine ;  bring  the  sauce  to  the  point  of  boiling,  add  a  morsel 
of  i^*esh  butter  and  a  tablespoonful  of  lemon-juice,  and  poor  the 
whole  into  a  hot  dish  garnished  with  fried  bread.  This  is  a  French 
receipt,  and  a  very  excellent  one.

AN   EXCELLENT   HASH   OF   COLD   BEEF. 

^  Pot  a  slice  of  butter  into  a  thick  saucepan,  and  when  it  boils  throw 
in  a  dessertspoonful  of  minced  herbs,  and  an  onion  (or  two  or  three 
eschalots)  shred  small:  shake  them  over  the  fire  until  they  are 
lightly  browned,  then  stir  in  a  tablespoonful  of  flour,  a  little  cayenne, 
gome  mace  or  nutmeg,  and  half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt.  When  the 
whole  is  well  coloured,  pour  to  it  three-quarters  of  a  pint  or  more  of 
broth  or  gravy,  according  to  the  quantity  of  meat  to  be  served  in  it. 
Let  this  boil  gently  for  fifteen  minutes ;  then  strain  it,  add  half  a 
wino^lassful  of  mushroom  or  of  compound  catsup,  lay  in  the  meat, 
and  keep  it  by  the  side  of  the  fire  until  it  is  heated  through  and  is  on 
&e  point  of  simmering,  but  be  sure  not  to  let  it  boil.  Serve  it  up  in 
avoy  hot  dish,  and  garnish  it  with  fried  or  toasted  sippets  of  bread. 

A  COMMON   HASH   OF   COLD   BEEF   OR   MUTTON. 

Take  the  meat  from  the  bones,  slice  it  small,  trim  off  the  brown 
edges,  and  stew  down  the  trimmings  with  the  bones  well  broken,  an 
onion,  a  bunch  of  thyme  and  parsley,  a  carrot  cut  into  thick  slices,  a 
few  peppercorns,  four  cloves,  some  salt,  and  a  pint  and  a  half  of 
water.  When  this  is  reduced  to  little  more  than  three  quarters  of  a 
pint,  strain  it,  clear  it  from  the  fat,  thicken  it  with  a  large  dessert- 
qminfiil  of  rice  flour,  or  rather  less  of  arrow-root,  add  salt  and 
pepper  if  needed,  boil  the  whole  for  a  few  minutes,  then  lay  in  the 
meat  and  heat  it  well.  Boiled  potatoes  are  sometimes  sliced  hot  into 
ft  veiy  common  hash. 

Oli, — The  cook  should  be  reminded  that  if  the  meat  in  a  hash  or 
ninoe  be  allowed  to  boil,  it  will  immediately  become  hard,  and  can 
ihen  only  be  rendered  eatable  by  very  long  stetoingy  which  is  by  no

KORMAN  HABH* 

Peel  and  fry  two  dosens  of  button  onions  in  butter  until  they  tn 
lightly  browned,  then  stir  to  them  a  tablespoonful  of  flour,  and  when

of  red  wine,  and  a  large  cup  of  boiling  broth  or  water :  add  a  senoa-

tender ;  cut  and  trim  into  small  handsome  slices  the  remains  of  ather

awhile  to  imbibe  the  flavour  of  the  sauce ;  then  place  the  hash  netf 
the  fire,  and  when  it  is  thoroughly  hot  serve  it  immediately,  withoot 
allowing  it  to  boil.

TO  BOIL  HABBOW  BONES. 

Let  the  large  ends  of  the  bones  be  sawed  by  the  butcher,  so  that 
vhen  they  are  dished  they  may  stand  upright ;  and  if  it  can  be 
done  conveniently,  let  them  be  placed  in  the  same  manner  in  the 
YesKl  in  which  tney  are  boUed.  Put  a  bit  of  paste,  made  with  flour 
lod  water,  over  the  ends  where  the  marrow  is  visible,  and  tie  a  cloth 
t^htlv  over  them;  take  the  paste  off  before  the  bones  are  sent 
to  table,  and  serve  them,  placed  upright  in  a  napkin,  with  slices  of 
dxy  toasted  bread  apart.  When  not  wanted  for  immediate  use,  they 
Bay  be  partially  boiled,  and  set  into  a  cool  place,  where  they  will 
Rnain  good  for  many  days. 

Laige  marrow  bones,  2  hours;  moderate  sized,  1^  hour.  To 
ieeep:  boil  them  1^  hour,  and  from  i  to  }  hour  more  when  wanted 
bruble. 


209  aiODEBH  COOKEBT.  [chap.  X. 


BAKED   HARROW  BONES. 

When  the  bones  have  been  sawed  to  the  length  of  a  deep  pie-dnh, 
wash  and  wipe  them  dry,  lay  them  into  it,  and  cover  them  (entirely 
with  a  good  batter.  Send  them  to  a  moderate  oven  for  an  hour 
or  more,  and  serve  them  in  the  batter. 

CLARIFIED   MARROW   FOR   KEEPINQ. 

Take  the  marrow  from  the  bones  while  it  is  as  fresh  as  possible ;  cat 
it  small,  put  it  into  a  very  clean  jar,  and  melt  it  with  a  gentle  beat, 
either  in  a  pan  of  water  placed  over  the  fire,  or  at  the  mouth  of  a 
cool  oven ;  strain  it  through  a  muslin,  let  it  settle  for  a  minute  or 
two,  and  pour  it,  clear  of  sediment,  into  small  jars.  Tie  skins,  or 
double  folds  of  thick  paper,  over  them  as  soon  as  the  marrow  is 
cold,  and  store  it  in  a  cool  place.    It  will  remain  good  for  months. 

OX-CHEEK   STUFFED   AND   BAKED. 

(Ooody  and  not  expentiv^^ 

Cleanse,  with  the  greatest  nicety,  a  fresh  ox-cheek  by  washing, 
scraping  it  lightly  with  a  knife,  and  soaking  out  the  blood ;  then  put 
it  into  plentv  of  warm  water,  and  boil  it  gently  for  about  an  hour. 
Throw  in  a  large  teaspoonful  of  salt,  and  caremlly  remove  all  the 
scum  as  it  rises  to  the  surface.  Let  it  cool  after  it  is  lifted  out,  and 
then  take  away  the  bones,  remembering  always  to  work  the  knife 
close  to  them,  and  to  avoid  piercing  the  skin.  When  the  cheek  bss 
become  cold,  put  into  it  a  good  roll  of  forcemeat,  made  by  the  receipt 
Nos.  1,  2,  or  3,  of  Chapter  IX.,  or  substitute  the  oyster  or  mushroom 
forcemeat  which  follows ;  but  in  any  case  increase  the  quantity  one* 
half  at  least :  then  skewer  or  bind  up  the  cheek  secui^ely,  and  send  it 
to  a  moderate  oven  fur  an  hour  or  an  hour  and  a  half.  It  should  be 
baked  until  it  is  exceedingly  tender  quite  through.  Drain  it  well 
from  fat,  dish  it,  withdraw  the  skewers,  or  unbind  it  gently,  and  either 
sauce  it  with  a  little  good  brown  gravy,  or  send  it  to  table  with  melted 
butter  in  a  tureen,  a  cut  lemon,  and  cayenne,  or  with  any  sauce  ol 
Chapter  Y.,  which  may  be  considered  more  appropriate.

TO   CHOOSE    VEAL, 

Vbaj.  should  be  fat,  finely  grained,  white,  firm,  and  not  overgrown : 
for  when  very  large  it  is  apt  to  be  coarse  and  tough.  It  is  more 
difficult  to  keep  than  any  other  meat  except  pork,  and  should*  never 
be  allowed  to  acquire  the  slightest  taint  before  it  is  dressed,  as  any 
approach  to  putridity  renders  it  equally  unwholesome  and  offensive 
to  the  taste.  The  fillet,  the  loin,  the  shoulder,  and  die  best  end  of 
Ihe  neck,  are  the  parts  generally  selected  for  roasting ;  the  breast  and 
koockle  are  more  usually  stewed  or  boiled,  although  the  former 
k  excellent  roasted.  The  udder  or  firm  white  fat  of  the  fillet, 
|i  much  used  by  French  cooks  instead  of  butter,  in  the  composition 
~ their  forcemeats:  for  these,  it  is  first  well  boiled,  then  left  until 
ihe  cold,  and  afterwards  thoroughly  pounded  before  it  is  nuxed 
the  other  ingredients.     The  head  and  feet  of  the  calf  are 


210  MODERN  GOOK£KT.  [chap.xj. 

valuable  articles  of  food,  both  for  the  nutriment  which  the  gelatinous 
parts  of  them  afford,  and  for  the  great  yariety  of  modes  in  which 
they  may  be  dressed.  The  kidneys,  with  the  rich  fat  that  surrounds 
them,  and  the  sweetbroads,  are  well  known  delicacies ;  the  liver  and 
the  heart  also  are  very  good  eating;  and  no  meat  is  so  generally 
useful  for  rich  soups  and  gravies  as  veaL 

TO   TAKE  THE   HAIR   FROM   A   CALF's  HEAD   WITH   THB 

BKIN   ON. 

It  is  better  to  do  this  before  the  head  is  divided;  but  If  only  the 
half  of  one  with  the  skin  on  can  be  procured,  it  must  be  managed  in 
the  same  way.  Put  it  into  plenty  en  water  which  is  on  the  point  of 
simmering  but  which  does  not  positively  boil,  and  let  it  remain  in 
until  it  does  so,  and  for  five  or  six  minutes  i^rwards,  but  at  the 
first  full  bubble  draw  it  from  the  fire  and  let  it  merely  scald ;  then 
lift  it  out,  and  with  a  knife  that  is  not  sharp  scrape  off  the  hair  ss 
closely  as  possible.  The  butchers  have  an  instrument  on  purpose 
for  the  operation;  but  we  have  had  the  head  look  quite  as  weU 
when  done  in  the  manner  we  have  just  described,  as  when  it  has 
been  sent  in  ready  prepared  by  them.  After  the  hair  is  off^  the 
head  should  be  toeu  washed,  and  if  it  cannot  be  cooked  the  same  day, 
it  must  be  wiped  extremely  dry  before  it  is  hung  up ;  and  when  it 
has  not  been  divided,  it  should  be  left  whole  until  the  time  ap- 
proaches for  dressing  it.  The  brain  must  then  be  taken  out,  and 
both  that  and  the  head  well  soaked  and  waslled  with  the  greatest 
nicety.  When  the  half  head  only  is  scalded,  the  brain  shonid  first 
be  removed.  Calves*  feet  are  freed  from  the  hair  easily  in  the  same 
manner;  indeed,  we  find  it  a  better  mode  of  having  it  cleared  from 
them  than  the  one  we  have  given  in  Chapter  XXII.,  though  that  is 
practised  by  many  good  butchers. 

BOILED  calf's   HEAD. 

When  the  head  is  dressed  with  the  skin  on,  which  many  personi 
prefer,  the  ear  must  be  cut  off  quite  close  to  it ;  it  will  require  three 
quarters  of  an  hour  or  upwards  of  additional  boiling,  and  should  be 
served  covered  with  fried  crumbs :  the  more  usual  mode,  however, 
is  to  boil  it  without  the  skin.  In  either  case  first  remove  the  brain, 
wash  the  head  delicatelv  clean,  and  soak  it  for  a  quarter  of  an  hour ; 
cover  it  plentifully  with  cold  water,  remove  the  scum  as  it  rises  with 
great  care,  throw  in  a  little  salt,  and  boil  the  head  gently  until  it  is 
perfectly  tender.  In  the  mean  time,  wash  and  soak  the  brains  first  in 
cold  and  then  in  warm  water,  remove  the  skin  or  film,  boil  them  in 
a  small  saucepan  from  fourteen  to  nixteen  minutes,  accordinff  to  their 
sage,  and  when  they  are  done,  chop  and  mix  them  with  eight  or  ten 
size  leaves  boiled  tender  and  finely  minced ;  or,  if  preferred,  with 


CHAP.  XI.]  VEAL.  211 

boiled  parsley  instead ;  warm  them  in  a  spoonful  or  two  of  melted 
batter,  or  white  sauce ;  skin  the  tongue,  trim  off  the  root,  and  serve 
it  in  a  small  dish  with  the  hrains  round  it.  Send  the  head  to  table 
Terj  hot  with  parsley  and  butter  poured  over  it,  and  some  more  in  a 
tnieen.  A  cheek  of  bacon,  or  very  delicate  pickled  pork,  is  the  usual 
accompaniment  to  boiled  calf  s  head. 

We  have  given  here  the  common  English  mode  of  serving  this 
dbb,  b^  some  persons  considered  the  best,  and  by  others,  as  exceed- 
ingly msipid.  On  the  continent,  tomata  sauce  takes  place  of  the 
parsley  and  bntter;  and  rich  oyster  or  Dutch  sauce,  are  varieties 
often  substituted  for  it  in  this  country. 

With  the  skin  on,  from  2^  to  2|  hours ;  without  the  skin,  from  H 
to  If  hour. 

calf's  hbad^  the  wardbr^s  way. 
(iln  excellent  Receipt') 

BoO  the  half- head  nntil  tolerably  tender ;  let  it  cool,  and  bone  it 
entirely ;  replace  the  brain,  lay  the  head  into  a  stewpan,  and  simmer 
it  gently  for  an  hour  in  rich  gravy.  From  five-and-twenty  to  thirty 
minntes  before  it  is  dished,  add  half  a  pint  of  mushroom-buttons. 
Thicken  the  gravy,  if  needful,  with  rice  flour  or  with  flour  and 
hatter,  and  serve  plenty  of  forcemeat-balls  round  the  head.  For 
dishes  of  this  kind,  a  little  sweet-basil  wine,  or  a  few  spriss  of  the 
herb  itself^  impart  a  very  agreeable  flavour.  When  neither  these 
nor  mnshrooms  are  within  reach,  the  very  thin  rind  pf  a  small  but 
fresh  lemon  may  be  boiled  in  the  gravy,  and  the  strained  juice  added 
at  the  instant  of  serving. 

Boiled  from  1  to  2  hours ;  stewed  1  hour. 

Obs. — ^The  skin,  vnth  the  ear,  may  be  left  on  the  head  for  this

an  inch  and  a  half  of  the  base ;  which  will  give  it  a  feathery  and 
omunental  appearance,  the  head  may  then  be  glazed  or  not  at 
pleasure. 

PREPARED   calf's   HEAD. 

(^Tke  CooJCs  Receipt.) 

Take  away  the  brains  and  tongue  from  the  half  of  a  cairs  head, 
md  then  remove  the  bones,  being  careful  in  doin^  so  to  keep  the 
knife  as  close  to  them  as  possible,  and  to  avoid  piercing  the  outer 
ikin :  in  this  consists  the  whole  art  of  boning,  in  which  an  attentive 
»ok  may  easily  render  herself  expert.  Next  wash  the  head  and  dr}*^ 
t  in  a  clean  cloth ;  sprinkle  over  the  inside  a  little  pounded  mace, 
lod  cajenne  or  white  pep^r ;  roll  it  up  tightly,  and  bind  it  round 
rith  tape  or  twine.  Lay  into  a  small  stewpot  three  or  four  pounds 
f  neck  of  veal  or  q?  beef^  twice  or  thrice  divided,  and  place  the 


212  MODERN   COOKERY.  [cBAP.Xi. 

head  upou  it  with  the  bones  well  broken ;  tk  or  in  half  a  gallon 
of  cold  water,  or  as  much  as  will  suffice  to  keep  the  head  covered 
until  it  is  done,  and  simmer  it  very  gently  from  an  hour  and  a 
quarter  to  an  hour  and  three  quarters.  When  it  is  extremely 
tender,  lift  it  out,  and  if  wanted  for  table,  remove  the  binding, 
and  serve  it  very  hot,  with  currie  sauce,  rich  oyster  sauce,  or  e^ 
sauce  and  brown  gravy ;  but  should  the  remains,  or  the  >vhole  of  it 
be  required  for  the  following  receipts,  pour  no  gravy  over  it: 
in  the  latter  case  do  not  take  off  the  tape  for  several  hours.  The 
tongue  may  be  stewed  with  the  head,  Dut  will  require  radier  leas 
time.  We  do  not  think  it  needful  to  repeat  in  every  receipt  our 
directions  for  adding  salt  to,  and  removing  carefully  the  scum  from, 
meat  that  is  stewed  or  boiled,  but  the  cook  must  not  neglect  either. 
When  the  trouble  of  boning  is  objected  to,  it  can  be  dispensed  with 
for  some  of  the  dishes  which  follow,  but  not  for  all.  After  the  head 
is  taken  out,  boil  the  gravy  until  it  is  well  reduced,  and  rich :  it 
should  be  strongly  jellied  wnen  cold.  A  boue  of  ham,  or  a  slice  of 
hung  beef  will  much  improve  its  flavour;  but  vegetables  must  be 
avoided  if  it  be  wanted  to  Keep :  a  little  spice  and  a  laggot  of  parsley 
may  be  added  to  it,  and  a  calf *s  foot  will  be  sure  to  give  it  the 
requisite  degree  of  firmness.  This  receipt  is  for  a  head  without  the 
skin. 

BURLINGTON   WHIMSEY. 

Set  aside  until  quite  cold  half  a  calf  *s  head  dressed  by  the  pre- 
ceding receipt.  If,  on  cutting  it,  the  gelatinous  part  should  not 
appear  perfectly  tender,  pare  it  off  closely  from  the  head,  weigh, 
and  mince  it ;  put  it  into  a  pint  of  good  gravv,  and  stew  it  gently 
from  ten  to  fifteen  minutes.  Mince  as  much  more  of  the  head 
as  will  make  up  a  pound  in  weight  after  the  edges  are  trimmed  off, 
and  part  of  the  fat  is  taken  away ;  add  to  this  three  ounces  of  the 
lean  of  a  boiled  haun  finely  chopped,  the  grated  rind  of  a  lat^e 
lemon,  three  tcaspoonsful  of  parsley  and  one  of  thyme  shred  vexy 
small,  three  quarters  of  a  teaspoonful  of  mace,  half  a  small  nutmeg 
grated,  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  and  a  half-quarter  one  of  cayenne;  stir 
the  whole  well  together,  and  put  it,  with  half  a  pint  more  of  gravy, 
to  the  portion  which  has  been  already  simmered.  When  the 
whimsey  has  boiled  softly  from  four  to  five  minutes,  pour  it  into 
moulds  or  pans,  in  which  slices  of  the  tongue  have  been  evenly 
arranged,  and  when  quite  cold  it  will  turn  out  very  firmly.  It  may 
be  garnished,  before  it  is  sent  to  table,  with  branches  of  parsley, 
which  should,  however,  be  perfectly  dry;  and  when  served  for 
supper  or  luncheon,  it  may  be  accompanied  by  a  salad  dressing. 

Calf's  head,  1  lb.;  lean  of  ham,  3  oz. ;  gravy,  IJ-  pint;  rind  of 
1  large  lemon ;  parsley,  3  tea<«poonsful ;  thjrme  and  salt,  each  1 
teaspoonful;  niacc,  f  teaspoonful;  i  nutmeg;  cayenne,  J  part  of 
teaspoonful :  o  minutcti.

Ohs. — The  remains  of  a  plain  boiled  head  may  he  made  to  serve 
for  this  dish,  provided  the  gravy  used  with  it  be  well  jellied  and  of 
high  flavonr.  Slices  from  the  small  end  of  a  boiled  and  smoked 
ox-tongue,  from  their  bright  colour  improve  greatly  its  appearance. 
It  should  be  tasted  before  it  is  poured  out,  that  salt  or  any  other 
seasoning  may  be  added  if  needful.  After  three  or  four  days* 
keeping,  should  any  mould  appear  upon  the  surface,  take  it  off, 
re-melt  the  whimsey,  and  give  it  two  minutes*  boil.  For  change, 
the  herbs  may  be  omitted,  and  the  quantity  of  ham  increased,  or 
some  minced  tongue  substituted  for  it 

CUTLETS  OF   CALp's  HEAD. 

Prepare,  by  the  Cook's  Receipt,  half  a  calfs  head  with  or  without 
the  skm  on ;  only,  in  the  latter  case,  allow  more  time  for  the  boiling. 
When  it  is  quite  cold,  remove  the  fillets  of  tape,  and  cut  the  head  into 
siices  of  half  an  inch  thick,  brush  them  with  yolk,  of  egg,  and  dip 
them  into  fine  bread-crumbs,  seasoned  with  the  grated  rind  of  half  a 
lemon,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  minced  savoury  herbs,  some  cayenne,  and 
a  little  of  the  lean  of  a  boiled  ham  chopped  very  small,  should  this 
last  be  at  hand.  Fry  the  cutlets  in  butter  of  a  fine  light  brown, 
make  some  gravy  in  the  pan  as  for  veal  cutlets,  and  add  to  it  the 
juice  of  half  a  lemon ;  or  mix  a  large  teaspoonfUl  of  currie-powder, 
and  one  of  floor,  very  smoothly  with  the  butter,  shake  them  over  the 
fire  for  four  or  five  minutes,  and  let  the  gravy  simmer  as  much 
longer,  after  the  water  is  added ;  or  serve  the  cutlets,  covered  with 
good  mushroom  sauce. 

HASHED   calf's   HEAD.      (rEMOVE.) 

When  the  whole  of  this  dish  has  to  be  prepared,  make  for  it  a 
quart  of  stock,  and  proceed  in  all  else  as  directed  for  mock  turtle 
sonp  (page  24) ;  but  after  the  head  has  been  parboiled,  cut  down  a 
fall  pound  and  a  half  of  it  for  the  hash,  and  slice  it  small  and  thick, 
inst^  of  dividing  it  into  dice.  Make  the  brains  into  cakes  (see 
page  162),  and  garnish  the  dish  with  forcemeat  -  balla,.  rolled  in 
egg,  and  in  the  finest  bread-crumbs,  then  fried  a  delicate  brown,  and 
^11  drained,  and  dried  upon  a  warm  sieve  reversed.  The  wine  and 
other  seasonings  should  be  the  same  as  for  the  soup. 

Rich  gravy,  1  quart ;  flesh  of  calPs  head,  full  H  lb. ;  wine,  and 
ether  seasonings,  as  for  mock  turtle  soup.

CHEAP  HASH   OF   CALf's  HEAD. 

Take  the  flesh  from  the  bone  of  a  cold  boiled  head,  and  put  it 
aride  until  wanted ;  take  about  three  pints  of  the  liquor  in  which  it 
VIS  cooked;  break  the  bones>  and  stew  them  down  with  a  small 


214  MODERN  COOKERY,  [chap,  xi 

bunch  of  savonry  herbs,  a  carrot,  or  two  should  they  he  small,  a 
little  carefully  fried  onion,  four  cloves,  a  dozen  coins  of  pepper, 
and  either  a  slice  or  two  of  lean  ham  or  of  smoked  beef.  When 
the  liquid  is  reduced  nearly  half,  strain  it,  take  oiF  the  fat,  thicken  it  ' 
with  a  little  well  made  roux,  or,  if  more  convenient,  with  flour  and 
butter,  stirred  into  it,  when  it  boils,  or  with  rice  flour  or  arrow-root, 
mixed  with  a  little  spice,  mushroom  catsup,  or  Harvey*s  sauce,  and  a 
small  quantity  of  lemon  pickle  or  chili  vinegar,  lleat  the  meat 
slowly  in  the  sauce  when  it  if  ready,  but  do  not  allow  it  to  boil 
The  forcemeat.  No.  1,  of  Chapter  VUI.,  may  be  rolled  into  balls, 
fried,  and  served  round  it  The  gravy  should  be  weli  seasoned.  A 
little  of  Liebeg*s  extract  of  beef  (see  Chapter  L),  or  as  much  good 
beef  broth  as  may  be  required  for  the  hash,  will  convert  this  into  & 
really  good  dish.  For  preparations  which  are  of  themselv^  insipid, 
the  Jewish  beef,  of  which  we  have  of^en  already  spoken,  is  an  ad* 
mirable  addition. 

TO   DRESS   COLD  CALF's   HEAD   OR   TEAIi 
▲   LA   MAITRE   D^HOTEL.     (goOD.) 

{English  Receipt) 

Cut  into  small  delicate  slices,  or  into  scollops  of  equal  size,  sufficieot 
cold  calfs  head  or  veal  for  a  dish.  Next  knead  very  smoothly 
together  with  a  knife  two  ounces  of  butter,  and  a  small  dessertspoon'- 
ful  of  flour ;  put  these  into  a  stewpan  or  well  tinned  saucepan,  and 
keep  them  stured  or  shaken  over  a  gentle  fire  until  they  have 
simmered  for  a  minute  or  two,  but  do  not  let  them  take  the  slightest 
colour;  then  add  to  them  in  very  small  portions  (letting  the  saace 
boil  up  after  each  is  poured  in)  half  a  pint  of  pale  veal  gravy,  or  of 
good  shin-of-beef  stock,  and  when  the  whole  is  very  smoothly 
blended,  and  has  boiled  for  a  couple  of  minutes,  mix  together  and 
stir  to  it  a  tablespoonful  of  common  vinegar,  a  dessertspoonful  of 
chili  vinegar,  a  little  cavenne,  a  tablespoonful  of  good  mushroom 
catsup,  and  a  very  smaU  bit  of  sugar;  and  when  the  sauce  again 
boils,  strew  a  tablespoonful  of  minc^  parsley  over  the  meat,  lay  it  in, 
and  let  it  stand  by  the  fire  until  it  is  ouite  heated  through,  but  do  nA 
allow  it  to  boil :  if  kept  just  at  tne  simmering  point  for  ten  or 
twelve  minutes  it  may  be  served  perfectly  hot  without.  The  additiaa 
of  the  mushroom  catsup  converts  this  into  an  English  sauce,  and 
renders  it  in  colour,  as  well  as  in  flavour,  unlike  the  Frendi  one 
which  bears  the  same  name,  and  which  is  acidulated  generally  with 
lemon -juice  instead  of  vinegar.  Pickled  mushrooms  are  sometiiaes 
added  to  the  dish :  the  parsley  when  it  is  objected  to  may  be  omitted^ 
and  the  yolks  of  two  or  three  eggs  mixed  with  a  little  cream  may  be 
stirred  in,  but  not  allowed  to  boil,  just  before  the  meat  is  served. 
\yhen  veal  is  used  for  this  hash  instead  of  calf  s  head,  it  should  ba

calf's  head  brawn. 

(Author'M  Receipt,) 

The  half  of  a  fine  lanze  calf  s  head  with  the  skin  on,  will  best 

answer  for  this  brawn.    Take  out  the  brains,  and  bone  it  entirely,  or 

get  the  butcher  do  ^s ;  rub  a  little  fine  salt  over,  and  leave  it  to 

dnin  for  ten  or  twelve  hours ;  next  wipe  it  dry,  and  rub  it  well  in 

every  part  with    three  quarters  of  an  ounce  of  saltpetre  finely 

powdered  (or  with  an  ounce  should  the  head  be  verv  large)  and 

mixed  with  four  ounces  of  common  salt,  and  three  of  bay-^t,  aJso 

besten  fine ;  turn  the  head  daily  in  this  pickle  for  four  or  five  days, 

robbing  it  a  little  each  time ;  and  then  pour  over  it  four  ounces  of 

treade,  and  continue  to  turn  it  every  day,  and  baste  it  with  the  brine 

very  frequently  for  a  month.    Hang  it  up  for  a  night  to  drain,  fold 

it  in  brown  paper,  and  send  it  to  be  smoked  where  wood  only  is 

bomed,  from  three  to  four  weeks.    When  wanted  for  table,  wash 

and  scrape  it  very  clean,  but  do  not  soak  it ;  lay  it,  with  the  rind 

downwards,  into  a  saucepan  or  stewpan,  which  will  hold  it  easilv ; 

cover  it  weU  with  cold  water,  as  it  vrill  swell  considerably  in  the 

cooking;  let  it  heat  rather  slowly,  skim  it  thoroughly  when  it  first 

begins  to  fdmmer,  and  boil  it  as  gently  as  possible  irom  an  hour  and 

three  auarters  to  a  couple  of  hours  or  more,  should  it  not  then  be 

perfectly  tender  auite  through;  for  unless  sufficiently  boiled,  the 

skin,  which  greatly  resembles  brawn,  will  be  unpleasantly  touffh 

when  cold     When  the  fleshy  side  of  the  head  is  done,  which  will  be 

twenty  minutes  or  half  an  hour  sooner  than  the  outside,  pour  the 

water  from  it,  leaving  so  much  only  in  the  stewpan  as  will  just  cover 

the  gelatinous  part,  and  simmer  it  until  this  is  thoroughly  tender.   The 

head  thus  cored  is  very  highly  flavoured,  and  most  excellent  eating. 

Hie  receipt  for  it  is  entirely  new,  having  originated  vfith  ourselves.

iaife,  without  the  skin,  pickled  with  three  ounces  of  common  salt,

sugar,  and  half  an  oun^  qfpeppery  left  four  days ;  then  three  oanoa

might  supply  the  place  of  the  pepper.  It  is  an  infinite  improvement 
to  nave  the  skin  oi  the  head  left  on. 

TO   ROAST  A    FILLET   OF   VEAL, 

Take  out  the  bone  and  put  a  ^ood  roll  of  forcemeat  (Xa  1, 
Chapter  YUI.)  under  the  flap,  dividmg  first,  with  a  sharp  knife,  the 
skin  from  the  meat  sufficiently  to  admit  the  quantity  ret^uired; 
secure  it  well,  truss  the  veal  firmly  into  good  shape,  place  it  at  a 
distance  from  the  fire  at  first,  and  baste  it  with  butter.  The  outside 
wUl  have  a  richer  crust  of  browning  if  the  meat  be  washed,  wiped 
tolerably  dry,  and  well  floured  before  it  is  laid  to  the  fire.  It  should 
be  carefully  watched,  and  basted  often,  that  the  fat  may  not  bum. 
Four  melted  butter  over  it  after  it  is  dished,  and  serve  with  it 
a  boiled  cheek  of  bacon  and  a  lemon.  Roast  it  from,  three  hours  and 
a  half,  to  four  hours  and  a  half,  according  to  its  sixe. 

FILLET  OF   TEAL^   AU   BECHAMEL^   WITH  OYSTERS. 

Roast,  in  the  usual  way,  a  delicate  fillet  of  veal,  and  in  preparing 
it  for  the  spit  be  careful  to  bind  it  up  tightly,  so  diat  no  cavity  may 
be  left  where  the  bone  has  been  taken  out.  While  it  is  at  the  fire, 
plump  ffently  in  their  own  strained  liquor,  without  allowing  them  to 
boil,  half  a  pint  of  fine  native  oysters,  and,  after  having  freed  them 
from  the  beards,  set  them  aside ;  then  boil  the  beards  for  fiAeen  or 
twenty  minutes  in  nearly  three  quarters  of  a  pint  of  good  veal  stodc, 
or  in  strong  veal  broth,  made  for  the  purpose ;  strain  them  out,  add 
the  liquor  of  the  oysters,  also  passed  through  a  muslin  or  other  fine 
strainer,  and  convert  the  broth  into  rich  white  sauce,  of  which  there 
should  be  a  full  pint.  When  the  veal  is  ready  to  Ferve,  take  it  firooa 
the  spit,  dish  it  in  a  very  hot  dish,  and  cut  out  quickly  from  the 
centre  in  a  cup-like  form,  about  a  pound  of  the  meat,  leaving  a  wide 
margin  round  the  joint,  to  be  carved  in  the  usual  way.  Mince,  as 
rapidly  as  possible,  the  white  part  of  the  veal  which  has  been  cut 
from  the  fillet,  and  the  plumped  oysters;  put  the  whole  into  the 
white  sauce,  which  should  be  ready  heated,  bring  it  to  the  poiai  of 
boilinff,  pour  it  into  the  fillet,  and  send  it  immediately  to  table.  The 
joint  snould  be  placed  under  a  well-heated  cover,  wnile  the  minee  is 
m  course  *',t'  preparation,  and  be  kept  near  the  fire. 

W^hen  the  knuckle  of  veal  has  been  sent  in  with  the  fillet,  a  few 
thick  slices  from  it  may  be  taken  for  the  sauce ;  but  it  should  fat

neat  ought  to  make,  with  the  addition  of  the  oyster  liquor,  sufficient

ROAST   LOIN   OF   TEAL. 

It  is  not  usual  to  stuff  a  loin  of  veal,  but  we  greatly  recommend 
the  practice,  as  an  infinite  improvement  to  the  joint.  Make  the 
ame  forcemeat  as  for  the  fillet ;  and  insert  it  between  the  skin  and

ttantly  basted,  and  be  especially  careful  not  to  allow  the  kidney  fat  to 
bom:  to  prevent  this,  and  to  ensure  the  good  appearance  of  the 
jomt,  a  buttered  paper  is  often  fastened  round  the  loin,  and  removed 
sboat  half  an  hour  before  it  is  taken  from  the  fire  It  is  the  fashion 
in  some  counties  to  serve  egg-sauce  and  browr  gravy  with  roast 
I(Rn,  or  breast  of  veal.

BOILED  LOIN  OF   TEAL. 

If  dressed  with  care  and  served  with  good  sauces,  this,  when  the

8TEWED   LOIN  OF  TEAL. 

Take  part  of  a  loin  of  veal,  the  chump  end  will  do ;  put  into 
a  larffe,  tnick,  well-tinned  iron  saucepan,  or  into  a  stewpan,  about  a 
couple  of  ounces  of  butter,  and  skake  it  over  a  moderate  fire  until 
it  begins  to  brown;  flour  the  veal  well  all  over,  lay  it  into  the 
saucepan,  and  when  it  is  of  a  fine,  equal,  light  brown,  pour  ffraduallj 
in  veal  broth,  gravy,  or  boiling  water  to  nearly  half  its  depth ;  add  s 
little  sauce,  one  or  two  sliced  carrots,  a  small  onion,  or  more  when 
the  flavour  is  much  liked,  and  a  bunch  of  parslev ;  stew  the  veal 
very  softly  for  an  hour  or  rather  more ;  then  turn  it,  and  let  it  stew 
for  nearly  or  quite  another  hour,  or  longer  should  it  not  be  per- 
fectly tender.  As  none  of  our  receipts  have  been  tried  with  laige, 
coarse  veal,  the  cooking  must  be  regulated  by  that  circumstance,  and 
lonser  time  allowed  should  the  meat  be  of  more  than  moderate  siie. 
Dish  the  joint,  skim  all  the  fat  from  the  gravy,  and  strain  it  over  the 
meat ;  or  keep  the  joint  hot  while  it  is  rapidly  reduced  to  a  ricber 
consistency.    This  is  merely  a  plain  family  stew. 

BOILED   BREAST   OF    VEAL. 

Let  both  the  veal  and  the  sweetbread  be  washed  with  exceeding 
nicety,  cover  them  with  cold  water,  clear  off  the  scum  as  it  rises, 
throw  in  a  little  salt,  add  a  bunch  of  parsley,  a  lai^  blade  of  mace, 
and  twenty  white  peppercorns ;  simmer  the  meat  from  an  hour  to 
an  hour  and  a  quarter,  and  serve  it  covered  with  rich  onion  sanee. 
Send  it  to  table  very  hot  The  sweetbread  may  be  taken  up  when 
half  done,  and  curried,  or  made  into  cutlets,  or  stewed  in  brown 
gravy.  When  onions  are  objected  to,  substitute  white  sauce  and 
a  check  of  bacon  for  them,  or  parsley  and  butter,  if  preferred  to  it 

1  to  li  hour.

TO   AOAST   A   BREAST   OF   TEAL. 

Let  the  caul  remain  skewered  over  the  joint  till  with  within  half 
n  hoar  of  its  being  ready  for  table :  place  it  at  a  moderate  distance 
from  a  brisk  fire,  baste  it  constantly,  and  in  about  an  hour  and  a 
half  remote  the  caul,  flour  the  joint,  and  let  it  brown.  Dish  and 
poor  melted  butter  over  it,  and  serve  it  with  a  cut  lemon,  and  any 
other  of  the  usual  accompaniments  to  veal.  It  may  be  garnished 
with  fried  balls  of  the  forcemeat  (No.  1,  Chapter  Vlil.)  about  the 
SK  of  a  walnut.

TO   BONE   A  SHOULDER   OF   YEAL,   MUTTON,   OR   LAMB. 

Spread  a  clean  cloth  upon  a  table  or 
dieaser,  and  lay  the  joint  flat  upon  it,  with 
the  sldn  downwards;  with  a  sharp  knife 
cat  off  the  flesh  from  the  inner  side  nearly 
down  to  the  blade  bone,  of  which  detach  the 
edges  first,  then  work  the  knife  under  it, 
keeping  it  always  close  to  the  hone^  and  using 
ill  possible  precaution  not  to  pierce  the  outer  shoulder  of  VeU  boned, 
skin;  when  it  is  in  every  part  separated  from  the  flesh,  loosen  it 
from  the  socket  with  the  point  of  the  knife,  and  remove  it ;  or,  vrith- 
out  dividing  the  two  bones,  cut  round  the  joint  until  it  is  freed 
entirely  from  the  meat,  and  proceed  to  detach  the  second  bone. 
That  of  the  Jcnuckle  is  frequently  left  in,  but  for  some  dishes  it  is 
necesEary  to  take  it  out;  in  doing  this,  be  careful  not  to  tear  the 
ddn.  A  most  excellent  grill  may  be  made  by  leaving  sufficient 
meat  for  it  upo^  the  bones  of  a  shoulder  of  mutton,  when  they  are 
lemoved  from  the  joint:  it  will  be  found  very  superior  to  the 
broiled  blade-bone  of  a  rocut  shoulder,  which  is  so  much  liked  by 
many  people. 

STEWED  SHOULDER  OF   VEAL. 

(English  Receipt.) 

Bone  a  shoulder  of  veal,  and  strew  the  inside  thickly  with  satoury 
herbs  minced  small ;  season  it  well  with  salt,  cayenne,  and  pounded 
mace ;  and  place  on  these  a  layer  of  ham  cut  in  thin  slices  and  freed 
from  rind  and  rust.  Roll  up  the  veal,  and  bind  it  tightly  with  a 
fillet;  roast  it  for  an  hour  and  a  half,  then  simmer  it  gently  in  good 
hmwn  gnLYj  for  five  hours ;  add  forcemeat  balls  before  it  is  dished ; 
ikim  the  rat  from  the  gravy,  and  serve  it  with  the  meat.  This 
receipt,  for  which  we  are  mdebted  to  a  correspondent  on  whom  we

NECK  OF   VEAL   A   LA   CRtME. 

(Or  An  Bechamel.) 

Take  the  best  end  of  a  neck  of  white  and  well-fed  veal,  detach 
the  flesh  from  the  ends  of  the  bones,  cut  them  sufficiently  short  to 
give  the  joint  a  good  square  form,  fold  and  skewer  the  skin  over 
them,  wrap  a  buttered  paper  round  the  meat,  lay  it  at  a  moderate 
distance  from  a  clear  fire,  and  keep  it  well  basted  with  butter  for  rb 
hour  and  a  quarter;  then  remove  the  paper  and  continue  the 
basting  with  a  pint,  or  more,  of  bechamel  or  of  rich  white  sauce,  until 
the  veal  is  sumciently  roasted,  and  well  encrusted  with  it.  Serve 
some  bechamel  under  it  in  the  dish,  and  send  it  very  hot  to  table. 
For  variety,  give  the  bechamel  in  making  it  a  high  flavour  of 
mushrooms,  and  add  some  small  buttons  stewed  vpry  white  and 
tender,  to  the  portion  reserved  for  saucing  the  joint 

2  to  2i  hours. 

TEAL  GOOSE. 

(City  of  London  receipt.) 

"  This  is  made  with  the  upper  part  of  the  flank  of  a  loin  of  v«i 
(or  sometimes  that  of  the  fillet)  covered  with  a  stuffing  of  sage  and 
onions,  then  rolled,  and  roasted  or  broiled.  It  is  served  with  brown 
gravy  and  apple  sauce,  is  extremely  savoury,  and  has  many 
admirers.'*    We  transcribe  the  exact  receipt  for  this  dish,  which  was 

Erocured  for  us  from  a  house  in  the  city,  which  is  famed  for  it    We 
ad  it  tested  with  the  skin  of  the  best  end  of  a  fine  neck  of  veal, 
from  which  it  vns  pared  with  something  more  than  an  inch  depA

o{  the  fksk  adhering  to  it  It  was  roasted  one  hour,  and  a  iswered 
extremely  welL  It  is  a  convenient  mode  of  dressing  the  flank  of  the 
veal  for  eaters  who  do  not  object  to  the  somewhat  coarse  savour  of 
the  preparation.  When  the  tendrons  or  gristles  of  a  breast,  or  part 
of  a  breast  of  veal^  are  required  for  a  separate  dish,  the  remaining

KNUCKLE  OF  YEAL  EN  RAGOUT. 

Cnt  in  smaU  thick  slices  the  flesh  of  a  knuckle  of  yeal,  season  it 
with  a  httle  fine  salt  and  white  pepper,  flour  it  lightly,  and  fry  it  in 
butter  to  a  pale  brown,  lay  it  into  a  very  clean  stewpan  or  saucepan, 
and  just  cover  it  with  boiling  water;  skim  it  clean,  and.  add  to  it  a 
laggot  of  thyme  and  parsley,  the  white  part  of  a  head  of  celery,  a 
small  quantity  of  cayenne,  and  a  blade  or  two  of  mace.  Stew  it 
Tery  softly  from  an  hour  and  three  quarters  to  two  hours  and  a 
half.  Thicken  and  enrich  the  gravy  if  needful  with  rice-flour  and 
mnshroom  catsup  or  Harvey*s  sauce,  or  with  a  large  teaspoonful 
of  flour,  mixed  with  a  slice  of  butter,  a  little  sood  store-sauce  and  a 
dass  of  sherry  or  Madeira.  Fried  forcemeat  balls  of  No.  1,  Chapter 
VIIL  may  be  added  at  pleasure.  With  an  additional  quantity  of 
water,  or  of  broth  (made  with  the  bones  of  the  joint),  a  pint  and 
ahalf  of  youuff  green  peas  stewed  with  the  veal  for  an  hour  will 
give  an  agreeable  variety  of  this  dish. 

BOILED   KNUCKLE   OF    YEAL. 

Ailer  the  joint  has  heen  trimmed  and  well  washed,  put  it  into  a 
▼essel  well  adapted  to  it  in  size,  for  if  it  be  very  large,  so  much 
water  will  be  required  that  the  veal  will  be  deprived  of  its  flavour ; 
it  should  be  well  covered  with  it,  and  very  gently  boiled  until  it  is 
perfectly  tender  in  every  part,  but  not  so  much  done  as  to  separate 
irom  the  bone.  Clear  ofl^  the  scum  with  scrupulous  care  when  the 
simmering  first  commences,  and  throw  in  a  small  portion  of  salt; 
tsthis,  if  sparingly  used,  will  not  redden  the  meat,  and  will  other- 
wise much  improve  it.  Parsley  and  butter  is  usually  Uoth  poured 
over,  and  sent  to  table  with  a  knuckle  of  veal,  and  boiled  bacon 
also  should  accompany  it.  From  the  sinewy  nature  of  this  joint, 
it  requires  more  than  the  usual  time  of  cooking,  a  quarter  of  an 
honr  to  the  pound  not  being  sufficient  for  it. 

Veal  6  to  7  lbs. :  2  hours  or  more. 

KNUCKLE   OF   VEAL   WITH   RICE. 

Pour  over  a  small  knuckle  of  veal  rather  more  than  sufficient 
water  to  oov«r  it ;  bring  it  slowly  to  a  boil ;  take  off  all  the  scum 


222  MODERN  COOKEKT.  [chaf.  n 

with  great  care,  throw  in  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  and  when  the  joint 
has  simmered  for  ahout  half  an  hour,  throw  in  from  eight  to  twelve 
ounces  of  well  washed  rice,  and  stew  the  veal  eently  for  an  hour 
and  a  half  longer,  or  until  hoth  the  meat  and  rice  are  perfectly 
tender.  A  seasoning  of  cayenne  and  mace  in  fine  powder  with  more 
salt,  should  it  he  required,  must  be  added  twenty  or  thirty  minutes 
before  they  are  served.  For  a  superior  stew  good  veal  luoth  may 
be  substituted  for  the  water. 

Veal,  6  lbs.;  water,  3  to  4  pints;  salt,  1  teaspoonful:  30  to  40 
minutes.    Bice,  8  to  12  oz :  1^  hour. 

Obi. — A  quart  or  even  more  of  full  crown  green  peas  added  to 
the  veal  as  soon  as  the  scum  hajs  been  cleared  off  will  make  a  most 
excellent  stew.  It  should  be  well  seasoned  with  white  pepper,  and 
the  mace  ^ould  be  omitted.  Two  or  three  cucumbers,  inred  and 
freed  from  the  seeds,  may  be  sliced  into  it  when  it  boils,  or  four 
or  five  younff  lettuces  shred  small  may  be  added  instead.  Green 
onions  also,  i/men  they  are  liked,  may  be  used  to  give  it  flavour. 


SHALL  PAIN  DE  YEAU,  OR,  TEAL  CAKE, 

Chop  separatelv  and  very  fine,  a  pound  and  a  quarter  of  veil 
quite  free  from  uit  and  skin,  and  six  ounces  of  beef  kidney-snet; 
add  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  a  full  third  as  much  of  white  pepper  and 
of  mace  or  nutmeg,  with  the  grated  rind  of  half  a  lemon,  and  turn 
the  whole  well  together  with  the  chopping-knife  until  it  is 
thoroughly  mixed ;  then  press  it  smoothly  into  a  small  round  baJdng 
dish,  and  send  it  to  a  moderate  oven  for  an  hour  and  a  quarter. 
Lift  it  into  a  clean  hot  dish,  and  serve  it  plain,  or  with  a  Httle 
brown  gravy  in  a  tureen.  Three  ounces  of  the  lean  of  a  boiled  ham 
minced  small,  will  very  much  improve  this  cake,  of  which  the 
size  can  pe  increased  at  will,  and  proportionate  time  allowed  for 
dressing  it.    If  baked  in  a  hot  oven,  the  meat  \iill  shrink  to  half  its 

I)roper  size,  and  be  very  dry.    When  done,  it  should  be  of  a  fine 
ight  brown,  and  like  a  cake  in  appearance. 

Veal,  U  lb. ;  beif-suet,  6  oz. ;  salt,  1  teaspoonful ;  pepper  and 
mace,  or  nutmeg,  |  teaspoonful  each ;  rind  of  i  lemon ;  nan^  (when 
added)  3  oz. ;  baked  1^  nour.  • 

BORDYKE  TEAL  CAKE. 

{Good.) 

Take  a  pound  and  a  half  of  veal  perfectly  clear  of  fat  and  ddn, 
and  eight  ounces  of  the  nicest  stripea  bacon;  chop  them  separately, 
then  mix  them  well  together  with  the  gprated  rind  of  a  small  lemon, 
half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  a  fourth  as  much  of  cayenne,  the  third  part 
of  a  nutmeg  grated,  \nd  a  half- teaspoonful  of  freshly  pounded  mace

Wben  it  is  pressed  into  the  dish,  let  it  be  somewhat  higher  in  the 
centre  than  at  the  edge ;  and  whether  to  he  served  hot  or  cold,  liit 
it  out  as  90on  as  it  comes  from  the  oven,  and  place  it  on  a  strainer 
that  the  fat  m^  drain  from  it;  it  will  keep  many  days  if  the  under 
side  be  dry.  The  bacon  should  be  weighed  after  the  rind,  and  any 
rust  it  may  exhibit,  have  been  trimmed  from  it.  This  cake  is  ex- 
oellent  cold,  better  indeed  than  the  preceding  one;  but  slices  of 
either,  if  preferred  hot,  may  be  warmed  through  in  a  Dutch  oven,  or 
on  the  gridiron,  or  in  a  few  spoonsful  of  gravy.  The  same  in- 
gredients made  into  small  cakes,  well  floured,  and  slowly  fried  from 
tvelve  to  fifteen  minutes,  then  served  with  gravy  made  in  the  pan  as 
for  cutlets,  will  be  found  extremely  good. 

Teal,  l\  lb.;  striped  bacon,  8  oz.;  salt  and  mace,  1  teaspoonful 
etch ;  rind  of  lemon,  1 ;  third  of  1  nutmeg ;  cayeime,  4  grains ; 
baked  li  to  14  hour. 

FRICAXDEAU   OF   TEAL.      (eNTr£b). 

French  cooks  always  prefer  for  this  dish,  which  is  a  common  one 
Q  their  own  country,  that  part  of  the  fillet  to  which  the  fat  or 
ndder  is  attached  ;*  but  the  flesh  of  the  finer  part  of  the  neck  or 
iom,  raised  clear  from  the  bones,  may  be  made  to  answer  the  purpose 
nearly  or  quite  as  well,  and  often  much  more  conveniently,  as  the 
meat  with  us  is  not  divided  for  sale  as  in  France ;  and  to  purchase 
the  entire  fillet  for  the  sake  of  the  fricandeau  would  render  it  ex- 
ceedingly expensive.     Lay  the  veal  flat  upon  a  table  or  dresser, 
with  Uie  skin  uppermost,  and  endeavour,  with  one  stroke  of  an 
exceedingly  sharp  knife,  to  dear  this  ofl^,  and  to  leave  the  surface  of 
the  meat  extremely  smooth ;  next  lard  it  thickly  with  small  lardooru^ 
as  directed  for  a  pheasant  (page  181),  and  make  one  or  two  in- 
cisions in  the  underside  with  the  point  of  a  knife,  that  it  may  the 
better  imbibe  the  flavour  of  the  seasonings.    Take  a  stewpan,  of 
nifficicnt  size  to  hold  the  fricandeau,  and  the  proper  quantity  of 
vegetables  compactly  arranged,  without  much  room  being  left  round 
the  meat    Put  into  it  a  couple  of  large  carrots,  cut  in  thick  slices, 
two  onions  of  moderate  size,  two  or  three  roots  of  parsley,  three  bay 
leaves,  two  small  blades  of  mace,  a  branch  or  two  of  lemon  thyine, 
and  a  little  cayeime,  or  a  saltspoonful  of  white  peppercorns.    Kaise 
these  high  in  the  centre  of  the  stewpan,  so  as  to  support  the  meat, 
and  prevent  its  touching  the  gravy.    Cover  them  witn  slices  of  very 
&t  Incon,  and  place  the  fricandeau  gently  on  them ;  then  pour  in  as 
much  good  veal  broth,  or  stock,  as  will  nearly  cover  the  vegetables 
without  reaching  to  the  veal.    A  calfs  foot,  split  in  two,  may  with 
advantage  be  laid  under  them  in  the  first  instance.      Stew  the 
fiieandeaa  very  gently  for  upwards  of  three  hours,  or  until  it  is 
fomid  to  be  extremely  tender  when  probed  with  a  fine  skewer  or  a 
krding-pin.    Plenty  of  live  embers  must  then  be  put  on  the  lid  of 

*  Called  by  them  the  noix. 


^24  MODERN  COOKERS.  [chap.  U 

the  stewpan  for  ten  minutes  or  a  quarter  of  an  hour,  to  render  the 
lardooiis  iirm.  Lift  out  the  fricandeau  and  keep  it  hot ;  strain  ud 
reduce  the  gravy  very  quickly,  after  having  skimmed  off  every 
particle  of  fat ;  glaze  tlie  veal,  and  serve  it  on  a  ragout  of  aorrel, 
cucumbers,  or  spinach.  This,  though  rather  an  elaborate  receipt 
is  the  best  we  can  offer  to  the  reader  for  a  dish,  which  is  now  almost 
as  fashionable  with  us  as  it  is  common  on  the  Continent.  Some 
English  cooks  have  a  very  summary  method  of  preparing  it;  they 
merely  lard  and  boil  the  veal  until  they  can  "'  cut  it  with  a  spoon," 
then  glaze  and  serve  it  with  "  brown  gravy  in  the  dish."  This 
may  he  very  tolerable  eating,  but  it  will  bear  small  resemblaiMe 
to  the  French  fricandeau.

SPRING -STEW   OP    VEAL. 

Cut  two  pound  of  veal,  free  from  fat,  into  small  half-inch  thick 
cutlets ;  flour  them  well,  and  fry  them  in  butter  with  two  small 
cucumbers  sliced,  sprinkled  with  pepper,  and  floured,  one  modente 
sized  lettuce,  and  twenty-four  green  gooseberries  cut  open  length- 
wise and  seeded.  When  the  whole  is  nicely  browned,  lift  it  into  t 
thick  saucepan,  and  pour  gradually  into  the  pan  half  a  pint,  or 
rather  more,  of  boiling  water,  broth,  or  gravy.  Add  as  much  adt 
and  pepper  as  it  requires.  Give  it  a  minute's  simmer,  and  pour  it 
over  the  meat,  shaking  it  well  round  the  nan  as  this  is  done.  Let 
the  veal  stew  gently  from  three  quarters  or  an  hour  to  an  hour.  A 
bunch  of  green  onions  cut  small  may  be  added  to  the  other  vege- 
tables if  liked ;  and  the  veal  will  eat  better,  if  slightly  seasoned  with 
salt  and  pepper  before  it  is  floured ;  a  portion  of  fat  can  be  left  on  it 
if  preferred. 

Veal  2  lbs.;  cucumbers,  2;  lettuce,  1;  green  gooseberries,  24; 
water  or  broth,  i  pint  or  more :  f  to  1  hour. 

NORMAN   UARRICO. 

Brown  in  a  stewpan  or  fry  lightly,  after  having  sprinkled  them 
with  pepper,  salt,  and  flour,  from  two  to  three  pounds  of  veal 
cutlets.  If  taken  from  the  neck  or  loin,  chop  the  bones  very  short, 
and  trim  away  the  greater  portion  of  the  fat.  Arrange  them  as  flat 
as  they  can  be  in  a  saucepan  ;  give  a  pint  of  water  a  boil  in  the  pan 
in  which  they  have  been  browned,  and  pour  it  on  them ;  add  a  small 
faggot  of  parsley,  and,  should  the  flavour  be  liked,  one  of  green 
onions  also.  Let  the  meat  simmer  softlv  for  half  an  hour ;  then 
cover  it  with  small  new  potatoes  which  nave  had  a  single  boil  in 
water,  give  the  saucepan  a  shake,  and  let  the  harrico  stew  very 
gently  for  another  half  hour,  or  until  the  potatoes  are  quite  done, 
and  the  veal  is  tender.  When  the  cutlets  are  thick  and  the 
potatoes  approaching  their  full  size,  more  time  will  be  required  for 
tlu  meat,  and  the  vegetables  may  be  at  once  divided :  if  extremely 


CSAP.  zi.]  V£AL«  225 

jouiig  they  will  need  the  preyiotis  boil.  Before  the  hanico  is  seryed^ 
skim  the  fat  from  it,  and  add  salt  and  pepper  should  it  not  be 
rafficiently  seasoned.  A  few  bits  of  lean  ham,  or  shoulder  of  bacon 
farowned  with  the  veal,  will  much  improve  this  dish,  and  for  some 
tastes,  a  little  add  will  render  it  more  agreeable.  Very  delicate  pork 
chops  may  be  dressed  in  the  same  way.  A  cutlet  taken  from  the 
fillet  and  freed  from  fat  and  skin,  answers  best  for  this  dish. 
Additional  vegetables,  cooked  apart,  can  be  added  to  it  after  it  is 
dtthed.  Peas  boiled  very  green  and  well  drained,  or  young  carrots 
sliced  and  stewed  tender  in  butter,  are  both  well  suited  to  it. 

Veal,  2  to  3  lbs. ;  water  (or  gravy),  1  pint ;  new  potatoes  1|  to  2 
lbs. ;  ilfeggot,  parsley,  and  green  onions :   1  hour  or  more. 

PLAIN  VEAL  CUTLETS.

uniperfecdtj  done.    Lift  them  into  a  hot  dish,  pour  the  fat  from  the 

pan,  throw  m  a  slice  of  fresh  butter,  and  when  it  is  melted,  stir  or 

dredge  in  a  dessertspoonful  of  flour ;  keep  these  shaken  until  they 

aie  well-coloured,  tnen  pour  ffradually  to  them  a  cup  of  gravy  or  of 

holing  water ;  add  peppier,  siut,  a  little  lemon-pickle  or  juice,  give 

the  whole  a  boil,  and  pour  it  over  the  cutlets :  a  few  forcemeat  balls 

fried  and  served  with  them,  is  usuall}  a  very  acceptable  addition  to 

thb  dish,  even  when  it  is  garnished  or  accompanied  with  rashers  of 

ham  or  bacon.  A  morsel  mgUize^  or  of  the  jelly  of  roast  meat,  should 

when  at  hand  be  added  to  the  sauce,  which  a  little  mushroom  powder 

would  further  improve :  mushroom  sauce,  indeed,  is  considered  by 

many  mcures,  as  mdispensable  with  veal  cutlets.    We  have  recom- 

mendeci  in  this  one  instance  that  the  meat  should  be  thoroughly 

heateuy  because  we  find  that  the  veal  is  wonderfully  improved  by  the 

pTDoeas,  which,  however,  we  still  deprecate  for  other  meat. 

12  to  15  minutes.

TEAL  CUTLETS,  OR  C0LL0P8,  A  LA  FRAN9AISE.      (bNTr£e.) 

Cut  the  veal  into  small,  thin,  round  coUops  of  equal  size,  anaoge

Set  the  pan  aside  until  the  dinner-hour,  then  fry  the  collops  over  a 
clear  fire,  and  when  they  are  lightly  browned,  which  will  be  in  frooi 
four  to  five  minutes,  lift  them  into  a  hot  dish,  and  sauce  them  with  a 
little  JEfipagTiole,  or  with  a  gravy  made  quickly  in  the  pan,  and  fla- 
voured with  lemon-juice  and  cayenne.  They  are  excellent  evea 
without  any  sauce.

8WEETBRBAD8  SIMPLY  DRESSED.      (h:NTr£b.) 

In  whatever  way  sweetbreads  are  dressed,  they  should  -first  be  well 
soaked  in  lukewarm  water,  then  thrown  into  boiling  water  to  blanch 
them,  as  it  is  called,  and  to  render  them  firm.  K  lilted  out  after  they 
have  boiled  from  five  to  ten  minutes  according  to  their  size,  and  laid 
immediately  into  fresh  spring  water  to  cool,  tneir  colour  will  be  the 
better  preserved.  They  may  then  be  gently  stewed  for  three  quarters 
of  an  hour  in  veal  gravy,  wnich  with  the  usual  additions  of  cream, 
lemon,  and  egg-yolks,  may  be  converted  into  a  fricassee  sauce  for 
them  when  they  are  done ;  or  they  may  be  Ufted  from  it,  glazed,  and 
served  with  good  Spanish  gravy ;  or,  the  glazing  being  omitted,  they 
may  be  sauced  with  the  sharp  MaUre  (t Hotel  sauce  of  page  117. 
They  may  also  be  simply  floured,  and  roasted  in  a  Dutch  oven,  being 
often  basted  with  butter,  and  frequently  turned.  A  full  sized  sweet- 
bread, after  having  been  blanched,  will  require  quite  three  quarters 
of  an  hour  to  dress  it. 

Blanched  5  to  10  minutes.    Stewed  }  hour  or  more. 

SWEETBREAD  CUTLETS.      (eNTrIe.) 

BoQ  the  sweetbreads  for  half  an  hour  in  water  or  veal  broth,  and 
when  they  are  perfectly  cold,  cut  them  into  slices  of  equal  thickness, 
brnsh  them  witn  yolk  of  egg,  and  dip  them  into  very  fine  bread- 
cmmbs  seasoned  with  salt,  cayenne,  grated  lemon-rind,  and  mace ; 
fry  them  in  butter  of  a  fine  light  brown,  arrange  them  in  a  dish 
|»Mciiig  them  high  in  the  centre,  and  pour  under  them  a  gravy  made 
m  the  pan,  thickened  with  mushroom  powder  and  flavoured  with. 
lemoa-juiee ;  or,  in  lieu  of  this,  sauce  them  with  some  rich  brown 
f"^Vf^  to  which  a  glass  of  sherry  or  Madeira  has  been  added.  When 
It  can  be  done  conveniently,  take  as  many  slices  of  a  cold  boiled 
toc^ue  as  there  are  sweetbread  cutlets;  pare  the  rind  from  them, 
tnm  them  into  good  shape,  and  dress  them  with  the  sweetbreads, 
they  have  been  egged  and  seasoned  in  the  same  way ;  and  place 
cutlet  upon  a  slice  of  tongue  when  they  are  dished.  For  variety, 
betitute  croutons  of  fried  hxGBA  stamped  out  to  the  size  of  the  cutletn 
a  round  or  fluted  paste  or  cake  cutter.  The  crumb  of  a  8tal« 
\aadf  very  erenly  sliced,  is  best  for  the  purpose.
