selim's  cttrries. 
{Captain  White's.) 

These  curries  are  made  with  a  sort  of  paste,  which  is  labelled  with 
the  above  names,  and  as  it  has  attracted  some  attention  of  late,  and 
the  curries  made  with  it  are  verv  good,  and  quickly  and  easily 
prepared,  we  give  the  directions  mr  them.  **  Cut  a  pound  and  s 
half  of  chicken,  fowl,  veal,  rabbit,  or  mutton,  into  pieces  an  inch  and 
a  half  square.  Put  from  two  to  three  ounces  of  fresh  butter  in  ■ 
stewpan,  and  when  it  is  melted  put  in  the  meat,  and  give  it  a  good 
stir  with  a  wooden  spoon ;  add  from  two  to  three  dessertspoonsful  at 
the  currie-paste ;  mix  the  whole  up  well  together,  and  continue  the 
stirring  over  a  brisk  fire  from  five  to  ten  minutes,  and  the  currie  will 
be  done.  This  is  a  dry  currie.  For  a  gravy  currie,  add  two  or 
three  tablespoonsful  of  boiling  water  after  the  paste  is  well  mixed  in, 
and  continue  the  stewing  and  stirring  from  ten  to  twelve  minutes 
longer,  keeping  the  sauce  of  the  consistency  of  cream.  Prepare 
salmon  and  lobster  in  the  same  way,  but  very  quickly,  that  they 
may  come  up  firm.  The  paste  may  be  rubbea  over  steaks,  or 
cutlets,  when  they  are  nearly  broiled;  three  or  four  minutes  will 
finish  them."* 

CURRIBD   MACCARONI.

beef  or  veal  stock  or  gravy,  previously  mixed  and  boiled  for  twenty 
minutes,  with  a  smiSl  tablespoonful  of  fine  currie-powder,  a  tea* 
spoonful  of  arrowroot,  and  a  little  lemon-juice.    Heat  and  toes  tbs

CURRIED   EGGS. 

Boil  six  or  eight  treih  eggs  quite  hard,  as  for  salad,  and  put  them 
aade  until  they  are  cold.  Mix  well  together  from  two  to  three 
canoes  of  good  butter,  and  from  three  to  four  dessertspoonsful  of 
eurrie-powder ;  shake  them  in  a  stew-pan  or  thick  saucepan,  over  a 
dear  but  moderate  fire  for  some  minutes,  then  throw  in  a  couple  of 
mild  onions  finely  minced,  and  fry  them  gently  until  they  are 
tolerably  soft :  pour  to  them,  by  degrees,  from  half  to  three  quarters 
of  a  pint  of  broth  or  gravy,  and  stew  them  slowly  until  tnev  are 
reduced  to  pulp ;  mix  smoothly  a  small  cup  of  thicK  cream  with  two 
teaspoonsful  ot  wheaten  or  of  rice-flour,  stur  them  to  the  currie,  and 
rinuner  the  whole  until  the  raw  taste  of  the  thickening  is  gone. 
Cat  the  eggs  into  half  inch  slices,  heat  them  quite  through  in  the 
sauce  without  boiling  them,  and  serve  them  as  hot  as  possible.

CURRIED   OYSTERS. 

"  Let  a  hundred  of  large  sea-oysters  be  opened  Into  a  basin 
without  losing  one  drop  of  their  liquor.  Put  a  lump  of  fresh  buttei 
into  a  ^^ood-sized  saucepan,  and  when  it  boils,  add  a  large  onion,  cut 
into  thin  slices,  and  let  it  fry  in  the  uncovered  stewpan  until  it  is  of 
a  rich  brown :  now  add  a  bit  more  butter,  and  two  or  three  table- 
spoonsful  of  currie-powder.  When  these  ingredients  are  well  miied 
over  the  fire  with  a  wooden  spoon,  add  gradually  either  hot  wiler, 
or  broth  from  the  stock- pot;  cover  the  stewpan,  and  let  the  whole 
boil  up.  Meanwhile,  have  ready  the  meat  or  a  cocoa-nut,  grated  or 
rasped  fine,  put  this  into  the  stewpan  with  a  few  sour  tamarindi 
(if  they  are  to  be  obtained,  if  not,  a  sour  apple,  chopped).  Let  the 
whole  simmer  over  the  fire  until  the  apple  is  dissolved,  and  the 
cocoa-nut  very  tender ;  then  add  a  cupful  of  strong  thickening  made 
of  flour  and  water,  and  sufficient  salt,  as  a  currie  >vill  not  bear  being 
salted  at  table.  Let  this  boil  up  for  five  minutes.  Have  ready 
also,  a  vegetable  marrow,  or  part  of  one,  cut  into  bits,  and  sufficiently 
boiled  to  require  little  or  no  further  cooking.  Put  this  in  with  a 
tomata  or  two ;  either  of  these  vegetables  may  be  omitted.  Now 
put  into  the  stewpan  the  oysters  with  their  kquor,  and  the  nulk 
of  the  cocoa-nut,  if  it  be  perfectly  sweet ;  stir  them  well  with  the 
former  ingredients ;  let  the  currie  stew  gently  for  a  lew  minutes,  tbeo 
throw  in  the  strained  juice  of  half  a  lemon.  Stir  the  currie  frooi 
time  to  time  with  a  wooden  spoon,  and  as  soon  as  the  oj^ters  are 
done  enough  serve  it  up  with  a  corresponding  dish  of  rice  on  the 
opposite  Hide  of  the  table.  The  dish  is  considered  at  Madras  the 
ne  plus  ultra  of  Indian  cookery."* 

We  have  extracted  this  receipt,  as  it  stands,  from  the  Maganne  of 
Domestic  Economy,  the  season  in  which  we  have  met  with  it  not 
permitting  us  to  have  it  tested.  Such  of  our  readers  as  may  hav% 
partaken  of  the  true  Oriental  preparation,  vnll  be  able  to  judge  of 
its  correctness ;  and  others  may  consider  it  worthy  of  a  tnaL  Wt 
should  suppose  it  necessary  to  beard  the  oysters.

very  lai^ge  proportions  of  them  which  are  acceptable  to  some  eaters^ 
preventing  others  altogether  from  partaking  of  the  dish.     Slice,  and 
ny  gently  in  a  little  good  hutter,  from  two  to  six  large  onions  (with

tbey  are  coloured  equally  of  a  fine  yellow-hrown,  lift  them  on  to  a

nut,  or  of  any  of  the  other  condiments  we  have  already  specified, 
which  may  require  as  much  stewins  as  the  onions  (an  apple  or  two, 
for  instance),  and  simmer  them  softfy  from  half  to  three  quarters  of 
an  hour,  or  until  the  onion  is  sufficiently  tender  to  be  pressed 
throogh  a  strainer.     We   would  recommend  that  for  a  delicate 
corrie  this  should  always  be  done:  for  a  conmion  one  it  is  not 
necessary;  and  many  persons  prefer  to  have  the  whole  of  it  left  in 
this  last.    After  the  gravy  has  been  worked  through  the  strainer, 
and  again  boHs,  add  to  it  from  three  to  four  dessertspoonsful  of 
currie-powder,  and  one  of  flour,  with  as  much  salt  as  the  gravy  may 
require,  the  whole  mixed  to  a  smooth  batter  with  a  small  cupful  of 
eood  cream.*    Simmer  it  from  fifteen  to  twenty  minutes,  and  it  will 
be  ready  for  use.    Lobster,  prawns,  shrimps,  maccaroni,  hard-boiled 
eggs,  cold  calTs  he&d,  and  various  other  meats  may  be  heated  and 
served  in  it  with  advantage.    For  all  of  these,  and  indeed  for  every 
kind  of  cnrrie,  acid  of  somie  sort  should  be  added.    Chili  vinegar 
answers  well  when  no  fresh  lemon-juice  is  at  hand. 

Onions,  2  to  6  (garlic,  1  clove,  or  eschalots,  4  to  5,  or  neilker) ; 
fried  a  light  brown.  Gravy,  IJ  pint ;  cocoa-nut,  2  oz.  (3,  if  very 
joung)  :  I  to  f  hour.  Gurrie-powder,  3  to  4  dessertspoonsful ;  flour, 
1  dessertspoonful;  salt,  as  needed;  cream,  1  small  cupful:  15  to  20 
minates. 

Obs. — In  India,  curds  are  frequently  added  to  curries,  but  that  may 
poflnblj  be  from  their  abounding  much  more  than  sweet  cream  in 
so  hot  a  climate. 

POTTED  MEATS. 

Any  tender  and  well-roasted  meat,  taken  free  of  fat,  skin,  and 
gnstle,  as  well  as  from  the  drv  outsides,  will  answer  for  potting  ad- 
mirably, better  indeed  than  that  which  is  generally  baked  for  the 
purpose,  and  which  is  usually  quite  deprived  of  its  juices  by  the 
pirocess.  Spiced  or  corned  beef  also  is  excellent  when  thus  prepared ; 
ind  any  of  these  vrill  remain  good  a  long  time  if  mixed  with  cold 
^esh  butter,  instead  of  that  which  is  clarified ;  but  no  addition  that 
an  be  made  to  it  will  render  the  meat  eatable,  unless  it  be  thoroughly 
';  reduced,  in  fact,  to  the  smoothest  possible  paste,  free  from  a

single  lump  or  a  morsel  of  unbroken  fibre.  If  rent  into  fragmenti, 
instead  of  being  quite  cut  through  the  grain  in  being  minced,  before  it 
is  put  into  the  mortar,  no  beatmg  will  bring  it  to  the  proper  state. 
Unless  it  be  very  dry,  it  is  better  to  pound  it  for  some  time  before  anv 
butter  is  added,  and  it  must  be  long  and  patiently  beaten  after  ail  the 
ingredients  are  mixed,  that  the  whole  may  be  equally  blended  md 
well  mellowed  in  flavour. 

The  quantity  of  butter  required  will  depend  upon  the  nature  of 
the  meat ;  ham  and  salted  beef  will  need  a  larger  proportion  than 
roast  meat,  or  than  the  breasts  of  poultry  and  game ;  white  fish,  from 
being  less  dry,  will  require  comparatively  little.    Salmon,  lobsten, 

?rawns,  and  shrimps  are  all  extremely  good,  prepared  in  this  way. 
'hey  should,  however,  be  perfectly  freui  when  they  are  pounded, 
and  be  set  inmiediately  afterwards  into  a  yery  cool  place.  For  these, 
and  for  white  meats  in  ^neral,  mace,  nutmeg,  and  cayenne  or  while 
pepper,  are  the  appropnate  spices.  A  small  quantity  of  doves  may 
be  added  to  hare  and  other  brown  meat,  but  allspioe  we  would  not  ^^ 
commend  unless  the  taste  is  known  to  be  in  favour  of  it.  The  fol- 
lowing receipt  for  pounding  ham  will  serve  as  a  general  one  for  the 
particular  manner  of  proceeding. 

POTTED   HAH. 

{An  excellent  Receipt.') 

To  be  eaten  in  perfection  this  should  be  made  with  a  fineahly  cored 
ham,  which,  after  having  been  soaked  for  twelve  hours,  thoold  be 
.wiped  dry,  nicely  trimmed,  closely  wrapped  in  coarse  paste,  and  baked 
very  tender.*  When  it  comes  from  the  oven,  remove  the  crust  and 
rind,  and  when  the  ham  is  perfectly  cold,  take  for  each  pound  of 
the  lean,  which  should  be  weighed  after  every  morsel  of  skin  and 
fibre  has  been  carefully  removed,  six  ounces  of  cold  roast  veal,  pre* 
pared  with  equal  nicety.  Mince  these  quite  fine  with  an  exceeding^ 
sharp  knife,  taking  care  to  cut  through  the  meat,  and  not  to  tear  tka 
fibre,  as  on  this  much  of  the  excellence  of  the  preparation  depends 
Next  put  it  into  a  large  stone  or  marble  mortar,  and  xxmnd  it  to  tk9 
smootnest  paste  with  eight  ounces  of  fresh  butter,  which  must  be  added 
by  degrees.  When  three  parts  beaten,  strew  over  it  a  teaqxtonful  ol 
freshly-pounded  mace,  half  a  large,  or  the  whole  of  a  stnail  nutm^ 
grated,  and  the  third  of  a  teaspoorJul  of  cayenne  well  mixed  togethcb 
it  is  better  to  limit  the  spice  to  this  quantity  in  the  first  ?"«tanrf^  aal 
to  increase  afterwards  either  of  the  three  kinds  to  the  taste  of  thl 
parties  to  whom  the  meat  is  to  be  served.f  We  do  not  find  half  a 
teaspoonful  of  cayenne,  and  nearly  two  teaspoonsful  of  maoe,

Obi, — The  roast  veal  is  ordered  in  this  receipt  because  the  ham  alone 
is  generally  too  salt ;  for  the  same  reason  butter,  firesh  taken  from  the 
chum,  or  that  which  is  but  slightly  salted  and  quite  new,  should  be 
used  for  it  in  preference  to  its  own  fat.  When  there  is  no  ready- 
dressed  veal  in  the  house,  the  best  part  of  the  neck,  roasted  or  stewed, 
will  supply  the  requisite  quantity.  The  remains  of  a  cold  boiled  ham 
will  answer  quite  well  for  potting,  even  when  a  little  dry. 

POTTED  CHICKEN,   PARTRIDGE,   OR  PHEASANT. 

Roast  the  birds  as  for  table,  but  let  them  be  thoroughly  done,  for 
if  the  gravy  be  left  in,  the  meat  will  not  keep  half  so  well.  Kaise  the 
flesh  of  the  breast,  wings,  and  merrythought,  quite  clear  from  the 
bones,  take  off  the  skin,  mince,  and  then  pound  it  very  smoothly  with 
about  one  third  of  its  weight  of  fresh  butter,  or  something  less,  if  the 
meat  should  appear  of  a  proper  consistence  without  the  full  quantity ; 
season  it  with  mlt,  mace,  and  cayenne  only,  and  add  these  in  small 
portions  until  the  meat  is  rather  highly  flavoured  with  both  the  last : 
proceed  with  it  as  with  other  potted  meats. 

POTTED   OX-TONQOB. 

Boil  tender  an  unsmoked  tongue  of  good  flavour,  and  the  following 
day  cut  from  it  the  quantity  desired  for  potting,  or  take  for  this 
piurpose  the  remains  of  one  which  has  already  been  served  at  table. 
Trim  off  the  skin  and  rind,  weigh  the  meat,  mince  it  very  small,  then 
poand  it  as  fine  as  possible  with  four  ounces  of  butter  to  each  pound 
of  tongue,  a  small  teaspoonfiil  of  mace,  hidf  as  much  of  nutmeg  and 
doves,  and  a  tolerably  high  seasoning  of  cayenne.  After  the  spices 
are  well  beaten  with  the  meat,  taste  it,  and  add  more  if  required.  A 
few  ounces  of  any  weU-roasted  meat  mixed  with  the  tongue  will  give 
it  firmness,  in  which  it  is  apt  to  be  deficient.  The  breasts  of  turkeys, 
fowls,  partridges,  or  pheasants,  may  be  used  for  the  purpose  with  good 
effect. 

Tongue,  1  lb.;  butter,  4  oz. ;  maoe,  1  teaspoonful;  nutmeg  and 
doves  each,  J  teaspoonful ;  cayenne,  5  to  10  grains.

POTTED   ANCHOVIES. 

Scrape  the  ancboies  very  dean,  raise  the  flesh  from  the  hooes, 
and  pound  it  to  a  perfect  paste  in  a  Wedgwood  or  marble  morta? ; 
then  with  the  back  of  a  wooden  spoon  press  it  through  a  hair-siex^ 
reversed.  Next,  weigh  the  anchoyies,  and  pound  them  again  with 
double  their  weight  of  the  freshest  butter  that  can  be  procured,  a  hig^ 
suisoning  of  mace  and  cayenne,  and  a  small  quantity  of  finely-gnted 
nutmeg ;  set  ihe  mixture  by  in  a  cool  place  for  three  or  four  hours  to 
harden  it  before  it  is  put  into  the  pottmg  pans.  If  butter  be  ponred 
over,  it  must  be  only  lukewarm ;  but  the  anchovies  will  keep  well  for 
two  or  three  weeks  without.  A  very  small  portion  of  rose-innk  may 
be  added  to  improve  the  colour,  but  unless  it  be  sparingly  used,  it 
^▼ill  impart  a  bitter  flavour  to  the  preparation.  The  quantity  of 
butter  can  be  increased  or  diminishdl  in  proportion  as  it  is  wefaed 
that  the  flavour  of  the  anchovies  should  prevail.

ful ;  half  as  much  cayenne ;  little  nutmeg.

after  they  are  quite  done,  or  both  their  nutritiye  properties  and  their

TO   CLEAR   TEOBTABLES   FROM   INSECTS. 

Lay  them  for  half  an  hour  or  more  into  a  pan  of  strong  brine,  with 
the  stalk  ends  uppermost ;  this  will  destroy  the  small  snads  and  other 
insects  which  cluster  in  the  leaves,  and  they  wiU  fall  out  and  sink  to 
the  bottom.  A  pound  and  a  half  of  salt  to  the  gallon  of  water  will 
answer  for  this  purpose,  and  if  strained  daily  it  will  last  for  some 
time. 

TO   BOIL   TEGETABLBS  GREEN. 

Afler  they  have  been  properly  prepared  and  washed,  throw  them 
into  plenty  of  boiling  water  which  has  been  salted  and  well  skimmed ; 
and  keep  them  uncovered  and  boiling  fast  until  they  are  done,  taking 
eveiypreeautionaffainst  their  being  smoked.  Should  the  water  be 
Teiy  hard,  a  smdU  half-teaspoonral  of  carbonate  of  soda,  may  be 
added  with  tihe  salt,  for  every  two  quarts,  and  will  greatly  improve 
the  colour  of  the  vegetables ;  but  if  used  in  undue  proportion  it  will 
hguze  them ;  green  peas  especially  will  be  quickly  reduced  to  a  mash 
if  boiled  with  too  huge  a  quantity. 

Water,  1  gallon ;  nit,  2  oz. ;  soda,  i  oz. ;  or  carbonate  of  soda,  1

POTATOES* 

(^Remarks  on  their  properties  and  importance.^ 

lliere  is  no  vegetable  commonlv  cultivated  in  this  country,  we 
venture  to  assert,  which  is  comparable  in  value  to  the  potato  when  it 
is  ctf  a  good  sort,  has  been  nown  in  a  suitable  soil,  and  is  properly 
cooked  and  served.  It  must  he  very  nutritious,  or  it  would  not  sus- 
tain the  strength  of  thousands  of  people  whose  almost  sole  food  it 
DOBStitutes,  and  who,  when  they  can  procure  a  sufficient  supply  of  it 
bo  satisfy  fully  the  demands  of  hunger,  are  capable  of  accomplishing 
the  heaviest  daily  labour.  It  may  not  be  wise  to  depend  for  sub- 
istenee  on  a  root  of  which  the  crop  unhappily  is  so  frequently  in 
liese  days  destroyed  or  greatly  iniured  by  disease,  and  for  whidi  it 
•  so  diMcult  to  find  a  substitute  that  is  equally  cheap,  wholesome, 
nd  satisfying ;  but  we  can  easily  comprehend  the  preduection  of  an 
ntire  people  for  a  tober  which  combines,  like  the  potato,  the  so- 


310  MODERN  COOKERT.  [rBAi.XTO. 

lidity  almost  of  bread,  with  the  healthful  properties^  of  Tarions  otber 
fresh  vegetables,  without  their  acidity ;  and  which  can  also  be  cooked 
and  served  in  so  many  different  forms.  The  wretched  manner  ia 
which  it  is  dressed  in  man^  English  houses  renders  it  comiNiratiTelT 
valueless,  and  accounts  in  a  measure  for  the  prodigaJitj  with 
which  it  is  thrown  away  when  cold,  even  in  seasons  when  its  price 
b  highest.! 

TO   BOIL   POTATOES. 

(Am  in  Ireland,) 

Potatoes,  to  boil  well  together,  should  be  all  of  the  same  sort,  and 
as  nearly  equal  in  size  as  may  be.  Wash  off  the  mould,  and  scrub 
them  very  clean  with  a  hard  brush,  but  neither  scoop  nor  apply  t 
knife  to  them  in  any  way,  even  to  clear  the  eye8.|  Rinse  them  well, 
and  arrange  them  compactly  in  a  saucepan,  so  that  they  may  not  lie 
loose  in  the  water,  ana  that  a  small  quantity  may  suffice  to  cover 
them.  Pour  this  in  cold,  and  when  it  boils,  throw  in  about  a  large 
teaspooniul  of  salt  to  the  quart,  and  simmer  the  potatoes  until  tbrf 
are  nearly  done,  but  for  the  last  two  or  three  nunutes  let  them  bml 
rapidly.  When  they  are  tender  quite  through,  which  may  be  known 
b^  probing  them  with  a  fork,  pour  all  the  water  from  them  imme- 
diately, liu  the  lid  of  the  saucepan  to  allow  the  steam  to  escape,  and 
place  them  on  a  trivet,  high  over  the  fire,  or  by  the  side  of  it,  until 
the  moisture  has  entirely  evaporated ;  then  peel,  and  send  them  to 
table  as  quickly  as  possible,  either  in  a  hot  napkin,  or  in  a  dish,  oi 
which  the  cover  is  so  placed  that  the  steam  can  pass  off.  There 
should  be  no  delay  in  serving  them  after  they  are  once  taken  from 
the  fire.  Irish  families  always  prefer  them  served  in  their  sldnK. 
Some  kinds  will  be  sufficiently  lx>iled  in  twenty  minutes,  others  ia 
not  less  than  three  quarters  of  an  hour. 

20  minutes  to  1  hour,  or  more. 

•  The  late  Dr.  Pereira  has  stated  in  hie  excellent  work  on  diet,  page  970,  tlMt

TO   BOIL   POTATOES. 

{The  Lancashire  way.) 

Pare  the  potatoes,  cover  them  with  cold  water,  and  boil  them  slowly 
Batil  they  are  quite  tender,  but  watch  them  carefully,  that  they  may 
not  be  overdone ;  drain  off  the  water  entirely,  strew  some  salt  over 
them,  leave  the  saucepan  uncovered  by  the  side  of  the  fire,  and  shake 
it  forcibly  every  minute  or  two,  until  the  whole  of  the  potatoes  appear 
diy  and  floury.  Lancashire  cooks  dress  the  veeetable  in  this  way  to 
perfection,  but  it  is  far  from  an  economical  mode,  as  a  large  portion 
of  the  potato  adheres  to  the  saucepan ;  it  has,  however,  many 
admirers. 

TO  BOIL  NEW  POTATOES, 

These  are  never  good  unless  freshly  dug.  Take  them  of  equal 
Bze,  and  mb  off  the  skins  with  a  brusn  or  a  very  coarse  cloth,  wash 
them  dean,  and  put  them  without  salt  into  boiling,  or  at  least,  auite 
hot  water;  boil  them  softly,  and  when  they  are  tender  enough  to 
lerre,  pour  off  the  water  entirely,  strew  some  fine  salt  over  them,  give 
them  a  shake,  and  let  them  stand  by  the  fire  in  the  saucepan  for  a 
minute ;  then  dish  and  serve  them  immediately.  Some  cooks  throw 
n  a  flmall  slice  of  f^h  butter,  with  the  salt,  and  toss  them  gently  in 
ft  tSUa  it  is  ^ssolved.  This  is  a  good  mode,  but  the  more  usual  one 
a  to  send  melted  butter  to  table  with  them,  or  to  pour  white  sauce 
ever  than  when  they  are  very  young,  and  served  early  in  the 
fleason. 

Very  small,  10  to  15  minutes :  moderate  sized,  15  to  20  minutes. 

06f.~.We  always,  for  our  own  eating,  have  new  potatoes  steamed 
for  ten  minutes  or  longer  after  the  water  is  poured  from  them,  and 
think  they  are  much  unproved  by  the  process.  They  should  be  tho* 
longhly  boiled  before  tms  is  done.

KEW   POTATOES  IN   BUTTER. 

Rub  off  the  skins,  wash  the  potatoes  well  and  wipe  them  dry;  pot 
them  with  three  ounces  of  good  butter,  for  a  small  dish,  and  ivith 
four  ounces  or  more  for  a  large  one,  into  a  well-tinned  stewpan  or 
saucepan,  and  simmer  them  over  a  gentle  fire  for  about  half  an  Iranr. 
Keep  them  well  shaken  or  tossed,  that  they  may  be  equally  done,  and 
throw  in  some  salt  when  they  begin  to  stew.  This  is  a  good  mode  of 
dressing  them  when  they  are  yery  young  and  watery. 

TO   BOIL  POTATOES. 

{Captain  Kater^M  Receipt.) 

Wash,  wipe,  and  pare  the  potatoes,  cover  them  with  cold  water, 
and  boil  them  gently  until  they  are  done,  pour  off  the  water,  and 
sprinkle  a  little  fine  salt  over  them;  then  take  each  potato  separatelj 
with  a  spoon,  and  lay  it  into  a  clean  warm  cloth,  twist  this  so  as  to 
press  all  the  moisture  from  the  vegetable,  and  render  it  quite  round; 
turn  it  carefully  into  a  dish  placed  before  the  fire,  throw  a  doth  over, 
and  when  all  are  done,  send  them  to  table  quickly.  Potatoes  diesBed 
in  this  way  are  mashed  without  the  slightest  trouble ;  it  is  also  by  fiff 
the  best  method  of  preparing  them  for  puddings  or  for  cakes. 

TO  ROAST   OR   BAKE  POTATOES. 

Scrub  and  wash  exceedingly  clean  some  potatoes  nearly  assorted  in 
size ;  wipe  them  very  dry,  ana  roast  them  in  a  Dutch  oven  before  the 
fire,  placing  them  at  a  distance  firom  it,  and  keeping  them  offea 
turned ;  or  arrange  them  in  a  coarse  dish,  and  bake  them  in  a  mo- 
derate oven.  Disn  them  neatly  in  a  napkin,  and  send  them  yery  hot 
to  table ;  serve  cold  butter  with  them. 

If  to  upwards  of  2  hours.

CRI8PBD   POTATOES,   OR   POTATO-RTBBOMS.      (ENTREMETS.) 

(Or  to  lerve  with  Cheeie,) 

Wash  weD,  and  wipe,  some  potatoes  of  good  flavour ;  cut  them  up 
into  slices  of  firom  half  to  a  whole  inch  thick,  free  them  from  the 
skins,  and  then  pare  them  round  and  round  in  very  thin,  and  very 
loQg  ribbons.  Lay  them  into  a  pan  of  cold  water,  and  half  an  hour 
before  they  are  wanted  for  table  lift  them  on  to  a  sieve  that  they 
may  be  well  drained.  Fry  them  in  good  butter,  which  should  be 
very  hot  when  they  are  throvm  in,  until  thev  are  quite  crisp,  and 
Gghtly  browned ;  drain  and  dr^  them  on  a  soft  cloth,  pile  them  in  a 
hot  dish,  strew  over  them  a  nuzed  feasoning  of  salt  and  cayenne  in 
fine  powder,  and  serve  them  without  delay.  For  the  second  course, 
dress  them  in  the  same  manner,  but  omit  the  cayenne.  Five  or  six 
minutes  will  fry  them. 

PRIED   POTATOES.      (eNTREMETS.) 

(A  Plainer  Receipt) 

After  having  washed  them,  wipe  and  pare  some  raw  potatoes,  cut 
ihem  in  slices  of  equal  thickness,  or  into  thin  shaving,  and  throw 
them  into  plentv  ot  boiling  butter,  or  very  pure  clarified  dripping. 
Fry  them  of  a  nne  light  brown,  and  very  crisp ;  lift  them  out  with  a 
•kdmnier,  drain  them  on  a  soft  warm  cloth,  cush  them  very  hot,  and 
qKinkle  fine  salt  over  them.  This  is  an  admirable  way  of  dressing 
potatoes,  very  common  on  the  Continent,  but  less  so  in  England  than 
It  deserves  to  be.  Fared  in  ribbons  or  shavings  of  eoual  width,  as  in 
ibe  reeeipt  above,  and  served  dry  and  well  fried,  lightly  piled  in 
a  dish,  they  make  a  handsome  appearance,  and  are  excellent  eating.^ 
K  sliced  tney  should  be  something  less  than  a  quarter  of  an  inch* 
thick.

page  810,  peel  them  quickly,  take  out  every  speck,  and  while  tfacy  are

never  pound  them  in  a  mortar,  as  that  will  reduce  them  to  a  dose 
heavy  paste.  Let  them  he  entirely  free  from  lumps,  for  nothing  can 
be  more  indicative  of  carelessness  or  want  of  skiU  on  the  part  of  the 
cook,  than  mashed  potatoes  sent  to  table  full  of  these.  Mdt  in  t 
clean  saucepan  a  slice  of  good  butter  with  a  few  spoonsful  of  milk, 
or,  better  still,  of  cre^tm ;  put  in  the  potatoes  after  naving  sprinkled 
some  fine  salt  upon  them,  and  stir  the  whole  over  a  gentle  fire  with 
a  wooden  spoon,  until  the  ingredients  are  well-mixed,  and  the  whole 
is  very  hot.  It  may  then  1^  served  directly ;  or  heaped  hi^  in  a 
dish,  left  rough  on  the  surface,  and  browned  before  the  fire ;  or  it 
may  be  pressei  into  a  well  buttered  mould  of  handsome  fonn,  whidi 
has  been  strewed  with  the  finest  bread  crumbs,  and  shaken  free  from 
the  loose  ones,  then  turned  out,  and  browned  in  a  Dutch  or  commoa 
oven.  More  or  less  liquid  will  be  required  to  moisten  sufficiently 
potatoes  of  various  kinds. 

Potatoes  mashed,  2  lbs.;  salt,  1  teaspoonful;  butter,  1  to  2  ox. ; 
milk  or  cream,  }  pint. 

Obs, — Mashed  potatoes  are  often  moulded  with  a  cup,  and  then 
equally  browned :  any  other  shape  will  answer  the  purpose  as  weD, 
and  many  are  of  better  appearance. 

ENGLISH   POTATO   BALLS^   OR   CROQUETTES. 

Boil  some  floury  potatoes  very  dry,  mash  them  as  smoothly  as 
possible,  season  them  well  with  salt  and  white  pepper,  warm  them 
with  about  an  ounce  of  butter  to  the  pound,  or  rather  more  if  it  will 
not  render  them  too  moist,  and  a  few  spoonsful  of  good  cream.  Boil 
them  very  dry ;  let  them  cool  a  little,  roll  them  into  balls,  sprinkle 
over  them  vermicelli  crushed  slightly  with  the  hand,  and  firy  them  a 
fine  light  brown.  They  may  be  dished  round  a  shape  of  plain 
mashed  potatoes,  or  piled  on  a  napkin  by  themselves.  They  may 
likewise  be  rolled  in  egg  and  fine  bread-crumbs  instead  of  in  the 
vermicelli,  or  in  ground  rice,  which  answers  very  well  for  them.

{Good.)

small  pan  of  boiling  butter,  or  of  yery  pure  lard,  and  fry  the 
boulettes  for  five  minutes  over  a  moderate  fire :  they  should  be  of  a

POTATO   RISSOLES. 

(^French,) 

Mash  and  season  the  potatoes  with  salt,  and  white  pepper  or 
cayenne,  and  mix  with  them  plenty  of  minced  parsley,  and  a  small 
quantity  of  green  onions,  or  eschalots ;  add  sufficient  yolks  of  eggs  to 
bind  the  mixture  together,  roll  it  into  small  balls,  and  fry  them  in 
plenty  of  lard  or  butter  over  a  moderate  fire,  or  they  will  be  too 
mach  browned  before  they  are  done  through.  Ham,  or  any  other 
kind  of  meat  finely  minced,  may  be  substituted  for  the  herbs,  or 
added  to  them. 

POTATOES   A   LA   MaItRB   d'hOTEL. 

Boil  in  the  usual  manner  some  potatoes  of  a  firm  kind,  peel,  and 
let  them  cool ;  then  cut  them  equally  into  quarter-inch  slices.  Dis- 
solve in  a  very  clean  stewpan  or  saucepan  from  two  to  four  ounces  of 
good  butter,  stir  to  it  a  small  dessertspoonful  of  flour,  and  shake  the 
pan  over  the  fire  for  two  or  three  mmutes ;  add  by  slow  degrees  a 
anall  cupful  of  boiling  water,  some  pepper,  salt,  and  a  tablespoonful 
of  minced  parsley;  put  in  the  potatoes,  and  toss  them  eently  over  a 
dear  fire  untU  they  are  quite  hot,  and  the  sauce  adBeres  well  to 
them :  at  the  instant  of  serving  add  a  dessertspoonful  of  strained 
lemon-juice.  Pale  veal  gravy  may  be  substituted  for  the  water ;  and 
the  potatoes  after  being  thickly  sliced,  may  be  quickly  cut  of  the 
ttme  size  with  a  small  round  cutter. 

POTATOES   A   LA   CREME. 

Prepare  the  potatoes  as  above,  and  toss  them  gently  in  a  quarter 
of  a  pint  or  more  of  thick  white  sauce  or  of  common  bechamel^  with 
or  without  the  addition  of  the  minced  parsley.

TO   BOIL  SEA-KALE. 

Wash,  trim,  and  tie  the  kale  in  bunches,  and  throw  it  into  plenty 
of  boiling  water  with  some  salt  in  it.  When  it  is  perfectly  tender, 
lift  it  out,  drain  it  well  from  the  water,  and  send  it  to  table  with 
good  melted  butter.  When  fashion  is  not  particularly  r^arded  ne 
would  recommend  its  being  served  upon  a  \t»s^  like  aBpaniga& 
About  twenty  minutes  will  boil  it,  rather  less  f&/  persons  who  like  it 
crisp. 

18  to  20  minutes. 

8EA-KALE   STEWED   IN   GRAyY.      (eNTRRMETS.) 

Boil  the  kale  for  ten  minutes  in  salt  and  water ;  drain  it  well,  and 
put  it  into  a  saucepan  with  as  much  good  brown  gravy  as  will  near^ 
cover  it ;  stew  it  gently  for  ten  minutes  or  until  it  is  tender,  and  seni 
it  to  table  in  the  gravy  very  hot.  Another  excellent  mode  of  aerrinf 
this  vegetable  is,  to  boil  it  in  salt  and  water,  and  to  poor  over  ft 
plenty  of  rich  white  sauce  after  it  is  dished. 

SPINACH.       (entremets.) 

(JFrench  Receipt) 

Pick  the  spinach  leaf  by  leaf  from  the  stems,  and  wash  it  in  aban- 
dance  of  spring  water,  changing  it  several  times ;  then  shake  it  in  i 
dry  cloth  held  by  the  four  comers,  or  drain  it  on  a  large  aieTd 
Throw  it  into  sumdent  well-salted  boiling  water  to  allow  it  to  ficil 
Treely,  and  keep  it  pressed  down  with  a  skimmer  that  it  may  be 
equally  done.  When  quite  young  it  will  be  tender  in  ttom  eignt  li 
ten  minutes,  but  to  ascertain  if  it  be  so,  take  a  leaf  and  sqneeae  il 
between  the  fingers.    li'to  be  dressid  in  the  French  mode,  drain,  aai 


CHAP.  X vn."  VEGETABLES.  317 

then  throw  it  direcUy  into  plenty  of  firesh  water,  and  when  it  is  cool 
form  it  into  balls  and  press  the  moisture  thoroughly  from  it  with  the 
hands.  Next,  chop  it  extremely  fine  upon  a  clean  trencher ;  put  two 
ounces  (for  a  large  dish)  of  butter  into  a  stewpan  or  bright  thick 
saucepan,  lay  the  spinach  on  it,  and  keep  it  stirred  over  a  gentle  fire 
for  ten  minutes,  or  until  it  appears  dry ;  dredge  in  a  spoonful  of 
flour,  and  turn  the  sploach  as  it  is  added ;  pour  to  it  gradually,  a 
few  spoonsful  of  very  rich  veal  gravy,  or,  if  preferred,  of  good 
boilii^  cream  (with  the  last  of  these  a  dessertspoon^  or  more  of 
pounded  sugar  may  be  added  for  a  second-course  dish,  when  the 
true  French  mode  of  dressing  the  vegetable  is  liked.)  Stew  the 
whole  briskly  until  the  liquid  is  entirely  absorbed ;  dish,  and  serve 
the  spinach  very  hot,  with  small,  pale  fined  sippets  round  it,  or  with 
leaves  of  puffpaste  fresh  firom  the  oven,  or  well  dried  after  bavins 
been  frie^  Yor  ornament,  the  sippets  may  be  fancifully  shaped  with 
a  tin  cutter.  A  proper  seasoning  of  salt  must  not  be  omitted  in  this, 
or  any  other  preparation  of  the  spinach. 

SPINACH   A   l'aNOLAISE.      (eNTREMETS.) 

(pTy  JSnglish/ashiori) 

Boil  the  spinach  as  already  directed,  and  after  it  has  been  well 
squeezed  and  chopped,  stir  it  over  a  moderate  fire  until  it  is  very  dry ; 
moisten  it  with  as  much  thick  rich  gravy  as  will  flavour  it  well,  and 
turn  and  stew  it  quite  fast  until  it  is  again  very  dry ;  then  press  it 
into  a  hot  mould  of  handsome  form,  turn  it  into  a  dish  and  serve  it 
quickly.  Two  or  three  ounces  of  fresh  butter  may  be  laid  into  the 
sanoepan  with  the  spinach  at  first,  as  a  substitute  for  the  ^ravy. 
When  a  perforated  tm  shape,  ordinarily  used  for  moulding  spinach, 
is  not  at  hand,  one  of  earthenware,  slightly  buttered,  will  serve 
nearly  as  well. 

SPINACH. 

(^Common  English  mode,') 

Boil  the  spinach  very  green  in  plenty  of  water,  drain,  and  then 
press  the  moisture  from  it  between  two  trenchers ;  chop  it  small,  put 
It  into  a  clean  saucepan,  with  a  slice  of  fresh  butter,  and  stir  the 
whole  until  well  mixed  and  very  hot  Smooth  it  in  a  dish,  mark  it 
Ji  dice,  and  send  it  quickly  to  table. 

ANOTHER  COMMON   ENGLISH   RECEIPT   FOR   SPINACH. 

Take  it  leaf  by  leaf  from  the  stalks,  and  be  very  careful  to  clear  it 
from  any  weeds  that  may  be  amongst  it,  and  to  free  it  by  copious 
Knd  repeated  washings  from  every  particle  of  sand,  or  earth.    Put 


318  MODERN  COOKERY.  [chai.xtii. 

it  into  a  large  well-tinned  stewpan  or  saucepan,  with  the  water  onlj 
which  hangs  about  it ;  throw  in  a  small  spoonful  of  salt,  and  keep  it 
constantly  pressed  down  with  a  wooden  spoon,  and  turned  oiten  for 
about  a  quarter  of  an  hour,  or  untU  it  is  perfectly  tend?r.  Drain  off 
the  superfluous  moisture,  chop  the  spinach  quickly  on  a  hot  trencher; 
dish  and  serve  it  immediately.  Fried  sippets  of  bread  should  alviys 
be  served  round  this  vegetable,  unless  it  be  prepared  for  an  invalid. 

TO   DRESS   DANDELIONS   LIKE   SPINACU^   OR   AS   A   SALAD. 

{Very  toJiolesomeJ) 

This  common  weed  of  the  fields  and  hiizhwajs  is  an  exoelleDt 
vegetable,  the  young  leaves  forming  an  admirable  adjunct  to  a  salad, 
and  much  resembling  endive  when  boiled  and  prenu-ed  in  the  same 
way,  or  in  any  of  the  modes  directed  for  spinach.  The  slight  bitter- 
ne^  of  its  flavour  is  to  manv  persons  very  agreeable ;  and  it  is  often 
served  at  well-appointed  tables.  It  has  also,  we  believe,  the  advan- 
tage of  possessing  valuable  medicinal  qualities.  Take  the  roots  before 
the  blossom  is  at  all  advanced,  if  they  can  readily  be  found  in  that 
state ;  if  not,  pluck  off  and  use  the  youn||;  leaves  only.  Wash  them 
as  clean  as  possible,  and  boil  them  tender  in  a  large  quantity  of  i»Yter 
salted  as  for  sprouts  or  spinach.  Drain  them  well,  press  them  dry 
with  a  wooden  spoon,  and  serve  them  quite  plain  with  melted  butter 
in  a  tureen ;  or,  squeeze,  chop,  and  heat  them  afresh,  with  a  season- 
ing of  salt  and  pepper,  a  morsel  of  butter  rolled  in  flonr,  and  a 
s|)oonful  or  two  of  gravy  or  ci'eam.  A  very  large  portion  of  the 
leaves  will  be  required  for  a  dish,  as  they  shrink  exceedingly  in  the 
cooking.  For  a  salad,  take  them  very  young  and  serve  them  entire, 
or  break  them  quite  small  vrith  the  fingers ;  then  wash  and  dnin 
them.  Dress  them  with  oil  and  vinegar,  or  with  any  other  sauce 
which  may  be  preferred  with  them. 


BOILED   TURNIP-RADISHES. 

These  should  be  freshly  drawn,  young  and  white.  Wash  and 
trim  them  neatly,  leaving  on  two  or  three  of  the  small  inner  leaves 
of  the  top.  Boil  them  in  plentv  of  salted  water  from  twenty  to 
thirty  minutes,  and  as  soon  as  they  are  tender  send  them  to  tabk 
well  drained,  with  melted  butter  or  white  sauce.  Common  radisbet 
when  young,  tied  in  bunches,  and  lioiled  from  eighteen  to  twenty* 
five  minutes,  then  served  on  a  toast  like  asparagus,  are  very  good. 


BOILED  LEEKS. 

Trim  off  the  coarser  leaves  from  some  younff  leeks,  cut  them  into 
equal  lengths,  tie  them  into  small  bunches,  and  boil  them  in  pleii^ 


CVAP.  xvu.]  VEGETABLES.  319 

of  water  which  has  heen  previouslj  salted  and  skimmed ;  serve  them 
on  a  toast,  and  send  melted  butter  to  table  with  them. 
20  to  25  minutes. 

STEWED   LETTUCES. 

Strip  off  the  outer  leaves,  and  cut  awav  the  stalks;  wash  the 
lettuces  with  exceeding  nicety,  and  throw  them  into  water  salted  as 
for  all  green  vegetables.  When  they  are  quite  tender,  which  will  be 
in  from  twenty  to  thirty  minutes,  according  to  their  age,  lift  them 
out  and  press  the  water  thoroughly  from  them ;  chop  them  a  little, 
and  heat  them  in  a  clean  saucepan  with  a  seasoning  of  pepper  and 
salt,  and  a  small  slice  of  butter ;  then  dredge  in  a  little  flour  and  stir 
them  well ;  add  next  a  small  cup  of  broth  or  gravy,  boil  them 
quickly  until  they  are  tolerably  dry,  then  stir  in  a  little  pale  vinegar 
or  lemon-juice,  and  serve  them  as  hot  as  possible,  with  fried  sippets 
round  them. 

TO   BOIL   ASPARAGUS. 

With  a  sharp  knife  scrape  the  stems  of  the  asparagus  lightly  but 
very  clean,  from  within  one  to  two  inches  of  the  green  tender  points ; 
throw  them  into  cold  water  as  they  are  done,  and  when  all  are  ready, 
tie  them  in  bunches  of  equal  size,  cut  the  large  ends  evenly,  that  the 
asparagus  may  be  all  of  the  same  length,  and  put  it  into  plenty  of 
boiling  water  prepared  bj  the  directions  of  page  309.  Cut  a  round 
of  bread  quite  half  an  mch  thick,  and  after  having  pared  off  the 
crust,  toast  it  a  delicate  brown  on  both  sides.  When  the  stalks  of 
the  asparagus  are  tender,  lift  it  out  directly,  or  it  will  lose  both  its 
colour  and  its  flavour,  and  will  also  be  liable  to  break ;  dip  the  toast 
quickly  into  the  water  in  which  it  was  boiled,  and  dish  the  vegetable 
upon  It,  with  the  points  meeting  in  the  centre.  Send  rich  melted 
batter  to  table  witn  it.  In  France,  a  small  quantity  of  vinegar  is 
stirred  into  the  sauce  before  it  is  served ;  and  many  persons  like  the 
addition.  Asparagus  may  be  preserved  for  a  day  or  two  sufiiciently 
fresh  for  use,  by  keeping  the  stalks  immersed  in  an  inch -depth  of 
eold  water ;  but  it  is  never  so  good  as  when  dressed  directly  it  is  cut, 
or  within  a  few  hours  after. 

20  to  25  minutes. 

Ob$. — Abroad,  boiled  asparagus  is  very  frequently  served  cold, 
tnd  eaten  with  oil  and  vinegar,  or  a  sauce  Mayonnaise. 

ASPARAGUS  POn«TS  DRESSED   LIKE   PEAS.      (eNTREMETS. 

This  is  a  convenient  mode  of  dressing  asparagus,  when  it  is  too 
email  and  green  to  make  a  good  appearance  plainly  boiled.  Cut  the 
points  so  far  only  as  they  are  perfectly  tender,  in  bits  of  equal  size, 
Dot  more  than  the  third  of  an  inch  in  length    wash  them  v^  dev\ 


320  MODERN  COOKEBT.  [chap,  zrn 

and  throw  them  into  plenty  of  hoiling  water,  with  the  usual  auantitj 
of  salt  and  a  few  grains  of  carbonate  of  soda.  When  they  are 
tolerably  tender,  which  will  be  in  from  ten  to  twelve  minutes,  drun 
them  well,  and  spread  them  on  a  clean  cloth  \  fold  it  oyer  them, 
wipe  them  gently,  and  when  they  are  quite  dry  put  them  into  a  dean 
stewpan  witn  a  good  slice  of  butter,  wnich  should  be  just  dissolved 
before  the  asparagus  is  added ;  stew  them  in  this  over  a  brisk  fire, 
shaking  them  o&n,  for  eight  or  ten  minutes ;  dredge  in  about  \ 
small  teaspoonful  of  flour,  and  add  half  that  quantity  of  white  su^; 
then  pour  in  boiling  water  to  nearly  cover  the  asparagus,  and  boil  it 
rapidly  until  but  little  liquid  remains :  stir  in  the  beaten  yolks  of 
two  eggs,  heap  the  asparagus  high  in  a  dish,  and  serve  it  very  hot 
The  sauce  should  adhere  entirely  to  the  vegetable  as  in  green  pets 
a  la  Frangaise. 

TO   BOIL   GREEN   PEAS. 

To  be  eaten  in  perfection  these  should  be  youn?,  very  ireshlr 
gathered,  and  shelled  just  before  they  are  boiled ;  snould  there  be 
great  inequality  in  their  size,  the  smaller  ones  may  be  separated  from 
the  others,  and  thrown  into  the  saucepan  four  or  five  minutes  later. 
Wash,  and  drain  the  peas  in  a  cullender,  put  them  into  plenty  of 
fast-boiling  water,  salted  by  the  directions  of  page  309 ;  keep  the  pan 
uncovered,  and  let  them  boil  rapidly  until  they  are  tender;  dnia 
them  well,  dish  them  quickly,  and  serve  them  very  not,  with  good 
melted  butter  in  a  tureen ;  or  put  a  slice  of  fresh  butter  into  the 
midst  of  the  peas,  heap  them  well  over  it  in  the  centre  of  the  dish, 
and  let  it  dissolve  before  they  are  disturbed.  Kever,  on  any  account, 
boil  or  mix  mint  with  them  unless  it  be  expressly  ordered,  as  it  is 
particularly  distasteful  to  many  persons.  It  should  be  served  in 
small  heaps  round  them,  if  at  all. 

15  to  2^  minutes,  or  more  if  old. 

green  peas  a  la  franqatsk,  or  french  fashion. 

(entremets).

their  first  beginning  to  simmer ;  then  pour  to  them  as  much  boaluir

OREBK   PEAB   WITH   CREAM.      (enTIIBMETS.) 

Boil  a  quart  of  young  peas  perfectly  tender  in  salt  and  water,  and 
drain  them  as  dry  as  possible.  Dissolve  an  ounce  and  a  half  of 
butter  in  a  clean  stewpan,  stir  smoothly  to  it  when  it  boils  a  dessert- 
spoonful of  jflour,  and  shake  these  over  the  lire  for  three  or  four 
minutes,  but  without  allowing  them  to  take  the  slightest  colour; 
pour  gradually  to  them  a  cup  of  rich  cream,  add  a  small  lump  of 
sugar  pounded,  let  the  sauce  boil,  then  put  in  the  peas  and  toss  them 
gently  in  it  until  they  are  very  hot :  disn,  and  serve  them  quickly.

TO   BOIL   FRENCH   BBANS. 

When  the  beans  are  very  small  and  young,  merely  take  off  the 
ends  and  stalks,  and  drop  them  into  plenty  of  spring  water  as  they 
are  done ;  when  all  are  ready  wash  and  drain  them  well,  throw  them 
into  a  large  saucepan  of  fast-boiling  water,  salted  as  usual  (see  page 
309),  and  when  tney  are  quite  tender,  which  will  be  in  from  twelve 
to  eighteen  minutes,  pour  them  into  a  cullender,  shake  the  water 
from  them,  dish,  and  send  them  quickly  to  table  with  good  melted 
batter  in  a  tureen.  When  from  half  to  two  parts  grown,  cut  the 
beans  obliquely  into  a  lozenge  form,  or,  when  a  less  modem  fashion 
is  preferred,  split  them  lengthwise  into  delicate  strips,  and  then  cut 
them  once  across :  the  strings  should  be  drawn  off  with  the  tops  and 
stalks.  No  mode  of  dressing  it  can  render  this  vegetable  good  when 
it  is  old,  but  if  the  sides  be  pared  off,  the  beans  cut  thin,  and  boiled 
tender  with  rather  more  than  the  ordinary  proportion  of  soda,  they 
will  be  of  exceUent  colour,  and  tolerably  eatable. 

FRENCH  BEANS  ▲  LA  FBANQAISE.   (eNTREMETB.) 

Boil,  and  drain  them  thoroughly ;  then  put  them  into  a  clean 
ttewnan,  or  well-tinned  iron  saucepan,  and  snake  them  over  the  fire 
until  they  are  very  dry  and  hot;  add  to  them  from  two  to  four 
ounces  of  ffesh  butter  cut  into  small  bits,  some  white  pepper,  a  little 
flUt,  and  the  juice  of  half  a  lemon ;  toss  them  gently  for  a  few 
minutes  over  a  dear  fire,  and  serve  them  very  hot.  Should  the 
batter  turn  to  oil,  a  spoonful  or  two  of  veal  gravy  or  boiling  water 
mnst  be  added.

MANDKANQ,   OR   MAKDRAM. 

,  (  West  Indian  Receipt,)

the  quantity  of  mild  onion ;  add  the  juice  of  a  lemon,  a  saltspoonful 
or  more  of  salt,  a  third  as  much  of  cayenne,  and  one  or  two  glasses  of 
l^IadeiFa,  or  of  any  other  dry  white  wine.  This  preparation  is  to  be 
served  with  any  land  of  roast  meat

DRESSED   CUCUMBERS. 

(Author's  Receipt.)

thered cucumbers,  take  off  the  rind,  and  then  pare  them  round  and

be  thrown  aside.  When  all  are  done,  sprinkle  them  with  cayenne  and 
&ie  salt,  and  leave  them  to  drain  a  little ;  then  arrange  them' lightly

chili  vinegar,  or  with  equal  parts  of  chili  and  of  common  vinegar.

CUCUMBERS  A   LA   POULETTE. 

The  cucumbers  for  this  dish  may  be  pared  and  sliced  veiy  thin; 
or  quartered,  freed  from  the  seeds,  and  cut  into  half-inch  lengths ;  in 
either  case  they  should  be  steeped  in  a  little  vinegar  and  sprinkled 
with  salt  for  half  an  hour  before  they  are  dressed.  Drain,  and  then 
press  them  dry  in  a  soft  cloth ;  flour  them  well,  put  a  slice  of  butter 
mto  a  stewpan  or  saucepan  bright  in  the  inside,  and  when  it  b^ins  to 
boil  throw  m  the  cucumbers,  and  shake  them  over  a  gentle  nre  for 
ten  minutes,  but  be  careful  to  prevent  their  taking  the  slightest 
colour ;  pour  to  them  gradually  as  much  strong,  but  very  pale  veal 
stock  or  gravy  as  will  nearly  cover  them ;  when  it  boils  skim  oflT  the 
fat  entirely,  add  salt  and  white  pepper  if  needed,  and  when  the 
cucumbers  are  quite  tender,  strew  in  a  large  teaspoonfol  of  finehr- 
minced  parsley,  and  thicken  the  sauce  with  tne  yolks  of  two  or  three 
eggs.  French  cooks  add  the  flour  when  the  vegetable  has  stewed  in 
the  butter,  instead  of  dredging  it  upon  them  at  first,  and  this  is 
perhaps  the  better  method.

MELON. 

This  in  France  and  in  other  parts  of  the  Continent  is  served  and 
eaten  with  the  houiUi  (or  beef  boiled  tender  in  tlie  soup-pot),  with  a 
Kasoning  of  salt  and  pepper  only ;  but  the  fruit  is  there  far  more 
abondant,  and  of  infinitely  finer  ^wth  than  with  us,  and  requires  so 
little  care,  compaiatively,  that  it  is  planted  in  many  places  in  the 
open  fields,  where  it  flourishes  admiraoly. 

TO    BOIL   CAULIFLOWERS. 

Trim  off  the  outside  leaves,  and  cut  the  stems  quite  close  to  the 
cauliflowers ;  let  them  lie  for  an  hour  in  plenty  of  cold  water  with  a 
handful  of  salt  in  it,  to  draw  out  any  insects  that  may  be  amongst 
them ;  then  wash  them  very  thoroughly,  and  examine  them  well,  to 
be  assured  that  none  remain  in  any  part  of  them ;  throw  them  into  a 
large  pan  of  boiling  water  salted  as  for  asparagus,  and  quite  cleared 
&Dm  scum ;  for  this,  if  not  removed,  will  adhere  to  the  cauliflowers 
and  spoil  their  appearance.  When  the  stalks  arc  tender  lifl  them 
out,  wsh.  them  neatly,  and  send  good  melted  butter  to  table  with 
them. 

20  to  30  minutes. 

CAULIFLOWERS. 

(French  Receipt.} 

Cut  the  cauliflowers  into  small  handsome  tufts,  and  boil  them  until 
three  parts  done,  drain  them  well,  toss  them  for  a  moment  in  some 
tkidt  melted  butter  or  white  sauce,  and  set  them  by  to  cool.  When 
they  are  quite  cold,  dip  them  separately  into  the  batter  of  Chapter  Y., 
fry  them  a  light  brovm,  arrange  them  neatly  in  a  dish,  and  serve 
them  very  hot. 

CAULIFLOWERS   WITH   PARMESAN   CHEESE. 

Take  all  the  sreen  leaves  from  two  or  three  fine  white  cauliflowers, 
and  cat  the  stalks  off  very  closely,  so  that  they  will  stand  upright  in 
the  dish  in  which  they  are  served ;  boil  them  tolerably  tender,  but 
not  sufficiently  so  as  to  hazard  their  breaking ;  drain  them  well,  and 
dish  them,  so  as  to  give  the  whole  the  appearance  of  one  cauliflower ; 
pour  a  little  good  vrnite  sauce  equally  over  the  tops,  and  on  this  strew 
grated  Parmesan  cheese,  drop  over  it  a  little  clarified  butter,  ada 
■aother  layer  of  cheese,  and  cover  the  whole  with  the  finest  bread- 
crumbs ;  moisten  these  with  more  clarified  butter,  and  brown  thcia

with  a  salamaiider,  or  set  the  dish  into  the  oven,  to  give  them  oolonr;

12  to  18  minutes.

TO   BOIL   ARTICHOKES. 

After  they  have  been  soaked  and  well  vrashed,  cut  oflT  the  stems 
quite  close,  trim  away  a  few  of  the  lower  leaves,  and  clip  the  points 
of  all ;  throw  the  artichokes  into  plenty  of  fast-boiling  water,  readv 
salted  and  skimmed,  with  the  audition  of  the  proportion  of  soda 
directed  in  page  309,  as  this  will  greatly  improve  the  colour  of  the 
vegetable.  When  extremely  voting,  the  artichokes  will  be  tender  ia 
from  half  to  three  quarters  or  an  hour,  but  they  will  require  more 
than  double  that  time  when  at  their  fUll  growth  :  when  the  leaves 
can  be  drawn  out  easily  they  are  done.  Send  good  melted  butter  to 
table  with  them.  They  should  be  boiled  always  with  the  stalk-ends 
uppermost. 

Very  young,  i  to  J  hour ;  full-grown,  1|  to  2  hours.

VEGETABLE   MARROW* 

It  18  customary  to  gather  this  when  not  larger  than  a  turkey's  egg, 
bat  we  should  say  that  the  v^etable  is  not  then  in  its  penection. 
The  flesh  is  whiter  and  of  better  flavour  when  the  ffourd  is  about  six 
inches  lone ;  at  least  we  have  found  it  so  vdth  the  Kinds  which  have 
Men  uncter  our  observation.  It  may  either  be  boiled  in  the  skin, 
then  pared,  halved,  and  served  upon  a  toast ;  or  quartered,  fi^eed  from 
the  seed,  and  left  imtil  cold,  then  dipped  into  egg  and  fine  crumbs  of 
bread,  and  fried ;  or  it  may  be  cut  into  dice,  and  re-heated  in  a  little 
good  white  sauce ;  or  stewed  tender  in  butter,  and  served  in  well- 
uickcned  veal  gravy,  flavoured  with  a  little  lemon-juice.  It  may 
likewise  be  mashed  by  the  receipt  which  we  have  given  for  turnips, 
and  in  that  form  will  be  found  excellent.  The  French  make  a  fan- 
aM  dish  of  the  marrows  thus :  they  boil  them  tender  in  water,  and 
halve  them  lengthwise  as  is  usual,  tney  then  slice  a  small  bit  ofl*  each 
to  make  them  stand  evenly  in  the  dish,  and  afler  having  hollowed  the 
insides,  so  as  to  leave  a  mere  shell,  about  half  an  inch  thick,  they  fill 
them  with  a  thick  rich  mince  of  white  meat,  and  pour  white  sauce 
roand  them ;  or  they  heap  fried  bread-crumbs  over  the  tops,  place 
the  dish  in  the  oven  for  a  few  minutes,  and  serve  them  without  sauce. 

Sue  of  turkey's  egg,  10  to  15  minutes ;  moderate-sized,  20  to  30 ; 
hrge,  I  to  1  hour. 

ROAST   TOMATAS* 

(To  serve  wilh  roast  legy  loin^  or  sJundder  of  mutton,) 

Select  them  nearly  of  the  same  size,  take  off  the  stalks,  and  roast 
them  gently  in  a  Dutch  oven,  or  if  more  convenient,  place  them  at 
the  edge  of  the  dripping-pan,  taking  care  that  no  fat  from  the  joint 
shall  fall  upon  them,  and  keeping  them  turned  that  they  nmy  be 
equality  done.    From  ten  to  fourteen  minutes  will  roast  them. 

STEWED   TOMATAS« 

Arrange  them  m  a  single  layer,  and  pour  to  them  as  much  gravy 
as  will  reach  to  half  theu*  height ;  stew  them  very  softly  untu  the 
under  sides  are  done,  then  turn,  and  fim'sh  stewing  them.  Thicken 
the  gravy  with  a  little  arrow-root  and  cream,  or  with  flour  and  butter, 
and  serve  it  round  them.

FORCED   TOUATAS. 

{French  Receipt.) 

Let  the  tomatas  be  well  shaped  and  of  equal  size ;  divide  tbem 
neHrly  in  the  middle  leaving  the  blossom-side  the  largest,  as  this  oaiY 
is  to  be  used ;  empty  them  carefully  of  their  seeds  and  juice,  and  fill 
them  with  the  following  ingredients,  which  must  previously  be  stewed 
tender  in  butter  but  without  being  allowed  to  brown :  minced  mush- 
rooms and  shalots,  with  a  moderate  proportion  of  parsley,  some  lean 
of  ham  chopped  small,  a  seasoning  of  cayenne,  and  a  little  fine  salt,  if 
needed ;  let  them  cool,  then  mix  with  them  about  a  third  as  much  of 
fine  crumbs  of  bread,  and  two  yolks  of  eggs ;  fill  the  tomatas,  eover 
them  with  fine  crumbs,  moisten  them  with  clarified  butter,  and  bake 
them  in  a  brisk  oven  until  they  are  well  coloured.  Serve  them  as  t 
garnish  to  stewed  rump  or  sirloin  of  bcef^  or  to  a  boned  and  forced 
leg  of  mutton. 

Minced  lean  of  ham,  2  oz. ;  mushrooms,  2  oz. ;  bread-crumbs,  2 
oz. ;  shalots,  4  to  8 ;  parsley,  full  teaspoonful ;  cavenne,  quarter  salt- 
spoonful  ;  little  salt,  if  needed ;  butter,  2  oz. ;  yolks  of  eggs,  2  to  3: 
baked  10  to  20  minutes. 

Obs,— The  French  pound  the  whole  of  these  ingredients  with  a  bit 
of  garlic,  before  they  fill  the  tomatas  with  them,  but  this  is  not  abso- 
lutely necessary,  and  the  garlic,  if  added  at  all,  should  be  parboiled 
first,  as  its  strong  flavour,  combined  with  that  of  the  eschalots,  would 
scarcely  suit  the  general  taste.  When  the  lean  of  a  dressed  ham  is  it 
hand,  only  the  herbs  and  vegetables  will  need  to  be  stewed  in  the 
butter ;  this  should  be  mixed  with  them  into  the  forcemeat,  which  an 
intelligent  cook  will  vary  in  many  ways. 

PUREE  OF   TOAIATAS. 

■

cream,  previously  mixed  and  boiled  for  five  minutes  with  a  tcaspooufuU

of  flour.  Tliis  pur^e  is  to  be  served  with  cairs  head,  veal  cutlets,

TO   BOIL  GREEN   INDIAN   CORN, 

When  still  quite  green  and  tender,  the  ears  of  maize  or  Indian  com 
tre  very  sood  ooiled  and  served  as  a  vegetable ;  and  as  tbey  will  not 
ripen  well  in  this  country  unless  the  summer  be  unusually  warm  and 
&vourable,  it  is  an  advantageous  mode  of  tumine  them  to  account. 
Strip  away  the  sheath  which  encloses  them,  ana  take  off  the  long 
silken  fibres  from  the  tops ;  put  the  corn  into  boiling  water  salted  as 
for  asparagus,  and  boil  it  for  about  half  an  hour.  Drain  it  well,  disb 
it  on  a  toiSt,  and  send  it  to  table  with  nxelted  butter.  The  Americans, 
who  have  it  served  commonly  at  their  tables,  use  it  when  more  fully 
grown  than  we  have  recommended,  and  boil  it  without  removing  the 
inner  leaves  of  the  sheath ;  but  it  is  sweeter  and  more  delicate  before 
it  has  reached  so  advanced  a  state.  The  grains  may  be  freed  from  the 
corn-stalks  with  a  knife,  and  tossed  up  with  a  slice  of  fresh  butter 
and  some  pepper  and  salt,  or  served  simply  like  green  peas.  Other 
modes  of  dressing  the  young  maize  will  readily  siiggest  toemselves  to 
an  intelligent  cook,  and  our  space  will  not  permit  us  to  enumerate 
them. 

25  to  30  minutes. 

MUSHROOMS   AU   BEURBE. 

(^Delicious.) 

Cut  the  stems  from  some  fine  meadow  mushroom-buttons,  and  clean 
them  with  a  bit  of  new  flannel,  and  some  fine  salt ;  then  either  wipe 
them  dry  with  a  soft  cloth,  or  rinse  them  in  fresh  water,  drain  them 
qoicklv,  spread  them  in  a  clean  cloth,  fold  it  over  them,  and  leave 
them  for  ten  minutes,  or  more,  to  drv  For  every  pint  of  them  thus 
prepared,  put  an  ounce  and  a  half  oi  fresh  butter  mto  a  thick  iron 
saucepan,  shake  it  over  the  fire  until  it  jiut  begins  to  brown,  throw  in 
the  mushrooms,  continue  to  shake  the  saucepan  over  a  clear  fire  that 
they  may  not  stick  to  it  nor  bum,  and  when  they  have  simmered 
three  or  four  minutes,  strew  over  them  a  little  salt,  some  cayenne, 
and  pounded  mace ;  stew  them  until  they  are  perfectly  tender,  heap 
them  in  a  dish,  and  serve  them  with  their  own  sauce  only,  for  break- 
fast, supper,  or  luncheon.  Nothing  can  be  finer  than  the  flavour  of 
the  mushrooms  thus  prepared ;  and  the  addition  of  any  liquid  is  far 
from  an  improvement  to  it.  The;^  are  very  G^ood  when  drained  from 
the  butter,  and  served  cold,  and  in  a  cool  larder  may  be  kept  for 
several  days.  The  butter  in  which  they  are  stewed  is  admirable  for 
flavouring  gravies,  sauces,  or  potted  meats.    Small  flaps,  freed  firom 


330  MODEBN  COOKERY,  [chap.  xtd. 

the  fur  and  skin,  may  be  stewed  in  the  same  way ;  and  either  these, 
or  the  buttons,  served  under  roast  poultry  or  partridges,  will  give  a 
dish  of  very  superior  relish. 

Meadow  mushrooms,  3  pints,  firesh  butter  4|  oz. :  3  to  5  minutei. 
Salt,  1  small  teaspoonful ;  mace,  half  as  much ;  cayenne,  third  of 
saltspoonf^ :  10  to  15  minutes.  More  spices  to  be  added  if  required 
— much  depending  on  their  quality;  but  they  should  not  OTerpower 
the  flavour  of  the  mushrooms. 

O^*.— Persons  inhabiting  parts  of  the  country  where  mushrooms 
are  abundant,  may  send  them  easily,  when  thus  prepared  (or  when 
potted  by  the  following  receipt),  to  their  friends  m  cities,  or  in  ks 
productive  counties.  If  poured  into  jars,  with  sufficient  butter  to 
cover  them,  they  will  travel  any  distance,  and  can  be  re-warm^  for 
use. 

POTTED   MUSBROOMS. 

Prepare  either  small  flaps  or  buttons  with  great  nicety,  without 
wetting  them,  and  wipe  the  former  very  diy,  alter  the  application  of 
the  salt  and  flannel.  Stew  them  quite  tender,  with  the  same  propor- 
tion of  butter  as  the  mushrooms  an  beurre^  but  increase  a  little  the 
quantity  of  spice ;  when  they  are  done  turn  them  into  a  lai^  dtfb, 
spread  them  over  one  end  of  it,  and  raise  it  two  or  three  inches  that 
they  may  be  well  drained  from  the  butter.  As  soon  as  they  are 
quite  cold,  press  them  very  closely  into  small  potting-pans;  poar 
lukewarm  clarified  butter  thickly  over  them,  and  store  them  in  a 
cool  dry  place.  If  intended  for  present  use,  merely  turn  them  down 
upon  a  clean  shelf ;  but  for  longer  keeping  cover  the  tops  first  with 
very  dry  paper,  and  then  with  melted  mutton-suet.  We  have  our- 
selves had  the  mushrooms,  after  being  simply  spread  upon  a  dish 
while  hot,  remain  perfectly  good  in  that  state  for  seven  or  d|rht 
weeks :  they  were  prepared  late  in  the  season,  and  the  weather  was 
consequently  cool  during  the  interval.

TRUFFLES   AND   THEIR   USES. 

The  truffle,  or  underground  mushroom,  as  it  has  sometimes  been 
called,  is  held  in  almost  extravagant  estimation  by  epicures,*^  and 
enters  largely  into  what  may  be  termed  first-class  cookery,  both  in 
England  and  abroad ;  though  it  is  much  less  generally  known  and 
used  here  than  in  France,  Germany,  and  other  parts  of  the  Continent, 
where  it  is  far  more  abundant,  and  of  very  superior  quality. 

As  it  is  in  constant  demand  for  luxuriously-served  tables,  and  has 
hitherto,  we  believe,  baffled  all  attempts  to  increase  it  by  cultivation, 
it  bears  usually  a  high  price  in  the  English  market,-)*  and  is  seldom 
to  be  had  cheap  in  any ;  but  although  tqo  costly  for  common  con- 
sumption, where  the  expenditure  is  regulated  by  rational  economy, 
it  may  at  times  be  made  to  supply,  at  a  reasonable  expense,  some 
excellent  store-preparations  for  the  breakfast  and  luncheon-table ;  as 
a  small  portion  will  impart  its  peculiar  flavour  to  them. 

The  blackest  truffles  are  considered  the  best.  All  are  in  their 
perfection  during  the  latter  part  of  November,  December,  and 
January ;  though  they  may  be  procured  usually  from  October  to 
March;  yet  as  they  are  peculiarly  subject  to  decay— or,  properly 
speaking,  become  really  putrid — from  exposure  to  the  air,  it  is  an 
advantage  to  have  them  as  early  in  their  season  as  may  be. 

In  sumptuous  households  the  very  finest  foreign  truffles  are  often 
lerved  as  a  vegetable  in  the  second  course. 

•  It  hw  been  named  by  a  celebrated  gastronomer  of  past  days,  "  Le  diatnant 
Ir  ia  cuinne." 

•f  Vaiying  from  eight  to  sixteen  slullings  the  ponnd  at  the  best  foreign  ware* 
lOQBes.  The  truffles  which  are  pared,  bottled  and  steamed  like  fhiit,  are  more 
■peasiTe  sdll ;  bat  they  can  be  kept  after  the  season  of  the  fresh  ones  is  entirely 
Mst.  English  tmffies — ^which  are  found  in  Hampshire  (in  the  New  Forest) — 
tad  in  some  few  other  of  our  counties,  are  rery  good,  tiiough  seldom  or  eyer 
aajd  in  quality  to  those  of  France,  Oermany,  and  of  different  parts  of  lUdv, 
ixMt  most  esteemed  of  the  French  ones  are  from  PeriffortU

TO   PREPARE   TRUFFLES  FOR   USE. 

First  soak  them  for  an  hour  or  two  in  fresh  water,  to  loosen  tk 
earth  which  adheres  to  them ;  then  rinse  them  well  from  it,  and  with 
a  hard  hrush  scrub  them  until  not  a  particle  of  the  mould  in  vhich 
they  have  been  embedded  can  be  seen  upon  them.  This  part  of  the 
operation  should  be  especially  attended  to,  because  the  parings  are  as 
useful  as  the  truffles  themselves.  It  is  often  needful  to  leave  them 
longer  in  the  water  after  it  has  been  changed ;  and  even  to  soak  them 
sometimes  in  lukewarm  water  also :  when  they  are  perfectly  cleaned, 
wipe  them  gently  with  a  soft  cloth,  or  fold  them  in  to  di>%  should 
they  be  wanted  for  any  preparation  to  which  moisture  would  be 
injurious. 

TRUFFLES   A    LA   SERVIETTE. 

Select  the  finest  truffles  for  this  dish,  be  particular  in  smelliK 
them,  and  reject  any  that  have  a  musty  smell.  Wash  and  \snm 
them  well  with  cold  water  only,  change  it  several  times,  and  wbea 
they  are  perfectly  clean  line  a  stewpan  with  slices  of  bacon ;  put  in 
the  truffles  with  a  bunch  of  parsley,  green  onions,  and  thyme,  two  or 
three  bay- leaves,  half  a  dozen  cloves,  and  a  little  sweet  basil ;  pour  in 
sufficient  rich  veal  gravy  to  cover  them,  ¥rith  the  addition  of  firoa 
half  a  pint  to  half  a  bottle  of  champagne ;  boil  them  very  softly  for 
an  hour,  then  draw  them  aside  and  let  them  cool  in  the  gTavy. 
Heat  them  afresh  in  it  when  they  are  wanted  for  table ;  lift  them  out 
and  drain  them  in  a  very  clean  cloth,  and  dish  them  neatly  in  a  fine 
and  beautifully  white  napkin,  which  will  contrast  as  strongly  a9  pos- 
sible with  the  dark  hue  of  the  truiiies. 

TRUFFLES   A  l'iTALIENNE. 

Wash  perfectly  clean,  wipe,  and  pare  some  truffles  extreoKiy 
thin ;  slice  them  about  the  size  of  a  penny ;  put  them  into  a  saute* 
pan  (or  small  frying-pan),  with  a  slice  of  fresh  butter,  some  mincei 
parsley  and  eschalot,  salt  and  pepper ;  put  them  on  the  fire  and  stir 
them,  that  they  may  fry  equally ;  when  they  are  done,  which  will  bt 
in  about  ten  minutes,  drain  of!'  part  of  the  butter,  and  throw  in  a  bil 
of  fresh  butter,  a  small  ladleful  of  Spanish  sauce  (see  page  101),  the 

i'uice  of  one  lemon,  and  a  little  cayenne  pepper.    This  is  a  dish  cl 
ligh  relish. 

TO   BOIL   SPROUTS,   CABBAaES,   BATOYS,   LETTUCES,   OB    KNDITE. 

All  green  vegetables  should  be  thrown  into  abundance  of  £h4 
boiling  water  ready  salted  and  skmmied,  with  the  addition  of  thQ 
small  quantity  of  carbonate  of  soda  which  we  have  recommended,  in  a 


CHAP.  XVII.]  VEGETABLES*  333 

previous  page  of  this  chapter ;  the  pan  shoula  be  left  uncovered,  and 
every  precaution  taken  to  prevent  the  smoke  from  reaching  its  con- 
tents. Endive,  sprouts,  and  spring  greens,  will  only  require  copious 
washing  before  they  are  boiled ;  but  savoys,  large  lettuces,  and  close- 
leaved  cabbages  should  be  thrown  into  soft  and  water  for  half  an  hour 
or  more  before  they  are  dressed,  with  the  tops  downwards  to  draw  out 
the  insects.  The  stems  of  these  last  should  be  cut  off,  the  decayed 
leaves  stripped  away,  and  the  vegetable  halved  or  quartered,  or  ^lit 
deeply  across  the  stalk-end,  and  divided  entirely  beK)re  it  is  dishea. 

Very  young  greens,  15  to  20  minutes ;  lettuces,  20  to  30  minutes ; 
large  savoyn,  or  cabbages,  1  to  1^  hour,  or  more. 

06«. — When  the  stSk  of  anv  kind  of  cabbage  is  tender  it  is  ready 
to  serve.  Turnip-greens  shoula  be  well  washed  in  several  waters,  and 
^joiled  in  a  very  large  quantity  to  deprive  them  of  their  bitterness. 

STKWED  CABBAGE, 

Cut  out  the  stalk  entirely,  and  slice  a  fine  firm  cabbage  or  two  in 
very  thin  strips ;  throw  them  after  they  have  been  well  washed  and 
drsmed,  into  a  large  pan  of  boiling  water  ready  salted  and  skimmed, 
and  when  they  are  tender,  whicn  will  be  in  from  ten  to  fifteen 
minutes,  pour  tnem  into  a  sieve  or  strainer,  press  the  water  thoroughly 
from  them,  and  chop  them  slightly.  Put  into  a  very  clean  saucepan 
about  a  couple  of  ounces  of  butter,  and  when  it  is  dissolved  add  the 
cabbage  with  sufficient  pepper  and  salt  to  season  it,  and  stir  it  over  a 
clear  fire  until  it  appears  tolerably  dry;  then  shake  lightly  in  a 
tablespoonful  of  flour,  turn  the  whole  well,  and  add  by  slow  degrees  a 
cup  of  thick  cream :  yeal  gravy  or  good  white  sauce  may  be  sub- 
stituted for  this,  when  preferred  to  it. 

TO   BOIL  TDRNIPS. 

Pare  entirely  from  them  the  fibrous  rind,  and  either  split  the 
turnips  once  or  leave  them'  whole ;  throw  them  into  boiling  water 
slightly  salted,  and  keep  them  closely  covered  from  smoke  and  dust 
until  they  are  tender.  When  small  and  young  they  will  be  done  in 
fit>m  fift^n  to  twenty  minutes ;  at  theur  full  growth  they  will  require 
fh)m  three  quarters  to  a  full  hour,  or  more,  of  gentle  boiling.  After 
they  become  old  and  woolly  they  are  not  worth  dressing  in  any  way. 
When  boiled  in  their  skins  and  pared  afterwards,  they  are  said  to  be 
of  better  flavour  and  much  less  watery  than  when  cooked  in  the  usual 
way. 

loung  turnips,  15  to  20  minutes:  full  gro^vn,  f  to  1  hour,  or 
more.

TUUNIPS  IN  GRAVY. 

To  a  pound  of  turnips  sliced  and  cut  into  dice,  pour  a  quarter  of  a 
pint  of  boiling  veal  gravy,  add  a  small  lump  of  sugar,  some  salt  and 
cayenne,  or  white  pepper,  and  boil  them  quickly  from  fifty  to  sixty 
minutes.    Serve  them  very  hot. 

TO   BOIL   CARROTS. 

Wash  the  mould  from  them,  and  scrape  the  skin  off  lightly  with 
the  edge  of  a  sharp  knife,  or,  should  this  be  objected  to,  pare  them  as 
thin  and  as  equally  as  possible;  in  either  case  free  them  from  all 
blemishes,  and  should  they  be  very  large,  divide  them,  and  cut  the 
thick  parts  into  quarters ;  rinse  them  well,  and  throw  them  into  plenty 
of  boiling  water  with  some  salt  in  it.  The  skin  of  very  young  carrots 
may  be  rubbed  off  like  that  of  new  potatoes,  and  from  twenty  to 
thirty  minutes:  will  then  be  sufficient  to  boil  them ;  but  at  their  full 
growth  they  will  require  from  an  hour  and  a  half  to  two  hours.  It 
was  formerly  the  custom  to  tie  them  in  a  doth,  and  to  wipe  the  skin 
from  them  with  it  after  they  were  dressed ;  and  old-fashioned  cooks 
still  use  one  to  remove  it ;  but  all  vegetables  should,  we  think,  be 
dished  and  served  vdth  the  least  possible  delay  after  they  are  ready  for 
table.    Melted  butter  should  accompany  boiled  carrots. 

Veiy  young  carrots,  20  to  30  minutes.  Full-grown  ones,  1^  to  2 
hours. 

CARROTS*      (EKTRiE.) 

(^The  Windsor  Receipt.) 

Select  some  good  carrots  of  equal  size,  and  cut  the  upper  parts  into 
even  lengths  of  about  two  inches  and  a  half,  then  trim  one  end  of 
each  into  a  point,  so  as  to  give  the  carrot  the  form  of  a  sugar-loaf.* 
When  all  are  ready,  throw  them  into  plenty  of  ready-salted  boiling 
water,  and  boil  them  three  quarters  of  an  hour.  Lift  them  out,  and 
drain  them  well,  then  arrange  them  upright,  and  all  on  a  level  in  a 
broad  stewpan  or  saucepan,  and  pour  m  good  hot  beef-broth  or  veal- 
gravv  to  half  their  height ;  add  as  much  salt  as  may  be  needed,  and  a 
small  teaspoonful  of  sugar,  and  boil  them  brisklv  for  half  an  hour,  or 
longer,  should  thev  require  it.  Place  them  again  upright  in  dishing 
them,  and  keep  them  hot  while  a  little  good  brown  gravy  is  thick- 
ened to  pour  over  them,  and  mixed  with  a  large  teaspoonful  of  pars- 
ley and  a  little  lemon-juice ;  or  sauce  them  with  common  bechamel 
(see  Chapter  V.),  or  white  sauce,  with  or  without  the  addition  of 
parslejr.

SWEET   CARROTS.      (ENTHEMKTS.) 

Boil  quite  tender  some  fine  highly-flavoured  carrots,  press  the

MASHED  (or  buttered)  CARROTS. 

{A  Dutch  Receipt.) 

Prepare  some  finely  flavoured  carrots  as  above,  and  dry  them  owr 
a  ^ntle  fire  like  mashed  turnips ;  then  for  a  dish  of  moderate  siss 
mix  well  with  them  from  two  to  three  ounces  of  good  butler,  cut 
into  small  bits,  keeping  them  well  stirred.  Add  a  seasoning  of  salt 
and  cayenne,  and  serve  them  very  hot,  garnished  or  not  at  pleasure, 
with  small  sippets  (croutotu)  of  fried  br^d. 

CARROTS   AU   BEURRE,   OR   BUTTERED   CARROTS. 

(French.) 

Either  boil  suffldent  carrots  for  a  dish  quite  tender,  and  then 
cut  them  into  slices  a  quarter  of  an  inch  thick,  or  first  slice,  and  then 
l)oil  them :  the  latter  method  is  the  most  expeditious,  but  the  other 
liest  preserves  the  flavour  of  the  vegetable.  Drain  them  well,  and 
while  this  is  being  done  just  dissolve  from  two  to  three  ounces  of 
butter  in  a  saucepan,  and  strew  in  some  minced  pardey,  some  salt, 
and  white  pepper  )r  cayenne ;  then  add  the  carrots,  and  toss  then

very  eently  until  they  are  equally  covered  with  the  sauce,  which 
should  not  he  allow«l  to  hoil :  the  parsley  may  he  omitted  at 
pleasure.    Cold  carrots  may  he  rewarmed  in  tnis  way. 

CARROTS   IK   THEIR  OWN  JUICE. 

(ii  stmple  hut  excellent  Beceipt) 

By  the  following  mode  of  dressing  carrots,  whether  young  or  old, 
their  full  flavour  and  all  the  nutnment  they  contain  are  entirelj^ 
preserved ;  and  they  are  at  the  same  time  rendered  so  palatahle  hy  it 
that  they  furnish  at  once  an  admirahle  dish  to  eat  without  meat,  as 
well  as  with  it.  Wash  the  roots  very  clean,  and  scrape  or  lightly 
pare  them,  cutting  out  any  discoloured  parts.  Have  ready  hoiline  and 
adted,  as  much  water  as  will  cover  them ;  slice  them  rather  uiick, 
throw  them  into  it,  and  should  there  he  more  than  sufficient  to  just 
float  them  (and  harely  that),  pour  it  away.  Boil  them  gently  until 
they  are  tolerahly  tender,  ana  then  very  quickly,  to  evaporate  the 
water,  of  which  only  a  spoonful  or  so  should  he  left  in  the  saucepan. 
Dust  a  seasoning  of  pepper  on  them,  throw  in  a  morsel  of  hutter  rolled 
in  flour,  and  turn  and  toss  them  gently  until  their  juice  is  thickened  hy 
them  and  adheres  to  the  roots.  Send  them  immediately  to  tahle.  They 
are  excellent  without  cmy  addition  hut  the  pepper ;  though  they  may 
be  in  many  ways  improved.  A  dessert-spoonful  of  minced  parsley 
may  he  strewed  over  them  when  the  hutter  is  added,  and  a  little  thick 
cream  mixed  with  a  small  proportion  of  flour  to  prevent  its  curdling, 
may  he  strewed  amongst  them,  or  a  spoonAil  or  two  of  good  gravy. 

TO   BOIL  PARSNKPS. 

These  are  dressed  in  precisely  the  same  manner  as  carrots,  hut 
require  much  less  hoiling.  According  to  their  quality  and  the  time 
of  year,  they  will  take  from  twenty  minutes  to  nearly  an  hour. 
Every  speck  or  hlemish  should  he  cut  from  them  after  they  are 
scraped,  and  the  water  in  which  they  are  hoiled  should  he  well 
skimmecL  They  are  a  favourite  accompaniment  to  salt  fish  and 
boiled  pork,  and  may  he  served  either  mashed  or  plain. 

20  to  25  minutes. 

FRIED  PARSNEP8. 

Boil  them  until  they  are  about  half  done,  lift  them  out,  and  let 
them  cool;  slice  them  rather  thickly,  sprinkle  them  with  fine  salt 
and  white  pepper,  and  fry  them  a  pale  brown  in  good  butter.  Serve 
them  with  roast  meat,  or  dish  them  under  it.

MASHED   JERUSALEM   ARTICHOKES. 

Boil  them  tender,  press  the  water  well  from  them,  and

tichokes  well,  both  before  and  after  the  milk  or  cream  is  added 
them ;  they  will  be  excellent  if  good  white  sauce  be  substituted 
either  of  these. 

HARICOTS   BLANCS. 

The  haricot  hlanc  is  the  seed  of  a  particular  kind  of  French 
of  which  we  find  some  difficulty  in  ascertaining  the  English 
for  though  we  have  tried  several  which  resembled  it  in  ap]^ 
we  have  found  their  flavour,  after  they  were  dressed,  veiy  diffe 
and  far  from  agreeable.    The  larse  white  Dutoh  runner,  is, 
believe,  the  proper  variety  for  cookmff ;  at  least  we  have  obta' 
small  quantity  under  that  name,  wnich  approach^  much 

nearly  than  any  others  we  had  tried  to  those  which  we  had 

abroad.    The  haricots,  when  fresh  may  be  thrown  into  plenty 


CH4r.  ZTH.]  y£a£TABLE5.  339 

bofling  water,  with  some  salt  and  a  small  bit  of  butter;  if  dry,  they 
must  be  previously  soaked  for  an  hour  or  two,  put  into  cold  water, 
brought  to  boil  gently,  and  simmered  until  they  are  tender,  for  if 
boiled  fast  the  skins  will  burst  before  the  beans  are  done.  Drain 
them  thoroughly  from  the  water  when  they  are  ready,  and  lay  them 
into  a  clean  saucepan  over  two  or  three  ounces  of  fresh  butter,  a 
small  dessertspoonful  of  chopped  parsley,  and  sufficient  salt  and 
pepper  to  season  the  whole;  then  gently  shake  or  toss  the  beans 
until  they  are  quite  hot  and  equally  covered  with  the  sauce ;  add  the 
strained  juice  of  half  a  lemon,  and  serve  them  quickly.  The  vege- 
table thus  dressed,  is  excellent ;  and  it  affords  a  convenient  resource 
in  the  season  when  the  supply  of  other  kinds  is  scantiest.  In  some 
inantries  the  dried  beans  are  placed  in  water,  over-night,  upon  a 
stove,  and  by  a  very  gentle  degree  of  warmth  are  sufficiently  soft- 
ened bv  the  following  day  to  be  served  as  follows  :--they  are  drained 
from  iiie  water,  spr^id  on  a  clean  cloth  and  wiped  quite  dry,  then 
lightly  floured  and  fried  in  oil  or  butter,  with  a  seasoning  of  pepper 
and  salt,  lifted  into  a  hot  dish,  and  served  under  roast  beei,  or 
mutton. 

TO   BOIL   BEET   ROOT. 

Wash  the  roots  delicately  clean,  but  neither  scrape  nor  cut  them, 
ibr  should  even  the  small  fibres  be  taken  off  before  they  arc  cooked, 
their  beautiful  colour  would  be  much  iiyured.  Throw  them  into 
boiling  water,  and,  according  to  their  size,  which  varies  greatly,  as 
they  arc  sometimes  of  enormous  growth,  boil  them  from  one  hour 
and  a  half  to  two  and  a  half,  or  longer  if  requisite.  Fare  and  serve 
them  whole,  or  cut  into  thick  slices  and  neatly  dished  in  a  close 
circle :  send  melted  butter  to  table  with  them.  Cold  red  beet  root  io 
often  intermingled  with  other  vegetables  for  winter  salads ;  and  it 
makes  a  pickle  of  remarkably  brilliant  hue.  A  common  mode  of 
serving  it  at  the  present  day  is  in  the  last  course  of  a  dinner  with  the 
cheese:  it  is  merely  pared  and  sliced  after  having  been  baked  or 
boiled  tender. 

1}  to  2}  hours,  or  longer. 

TO   BAKE  BEET   BOOT. 

Beet  root  if  slowly  and  carefully  baked  until  it  is  tender  quite 
through,  is  very  rich  and  sweet  in  flavour,  although  less  bright  in 
colour  than  'when  it  is  boiled :  it  is  also,  we  believe,  remarkably 
nutritious  and  wholesome.  Wash  and  wipe  it  verj^  dry,  but  neither 
cut  nor  break  any  part  of  it ;  then  lay  it  into  a  coarse  earthen  dish, 
and  bake  it  in  a  gentle  oven  for  four  or  five  hours :  it  will  sometimes 
require  even  a  longer  time  than  this.  Fare  it  quickly  if  it  be  seryeU 
hot ;  but  leave  it  to  cool  first,  when  it  is  to  be  sent  to  table  cold. 

In  slow  oyen  from  4  to  6  hours.
