8TEW£D   BEET  ROOT. 

Bake  or  boil  it  tolerably  tender,  and  let  it  remain  until  it  is  cold, 
then  pare  and  cut  it  into  slices ;  beat  and  stew  it  for  a  abort  time  in 
some  good  pale  veal  gravy  (or  in  strons  veal  broth  for  ordinsiy 
occasions),  tnicken  this  with  a  teaspoonfuT  of  arrowroot,  and  half  a 
cupful  or  more  of  good  cream,  and  stir  in,  as  it  is  taken  from  the 
fire,  from  a  tea  to  a  tablespoonful  of  chili  vinegar.  The  beet  root 
may  be  served  likewise  in  tnick  white  sauce,  to  which,  just  before  it 
is  mshed,  the  mild  eschalots  of  page  128  may  be  added. 


TO   8TBW   RED   CABBAGE. 

(^Flemish  Receipt) 

Strip  the  outer  leaves  from  a  fine  and  fresh  red  cabbage ;  wash  it 
well,  and  cut  it  into  the  thinnest  possible  slices,  be&pnning  at  tbe 
top ;  put  it  into  a  thick  saucepan  m  which  two  or  Uiree  ouneei  of 
good  butter  have  been  just  dissolved ;  add  some  pepper  and  salt,  and 
stew  it  very  slowly  indeed  for  three  or  four  hours  in  its  own  juke, 
keeping  it  often  stirred,  and  well  pressed  down.  When  it  b  per- 
fectly tender  add  a  tablespoonful  of  vinegar  ;  mix  the  whole  up 
thoroughly,  heap  the  cabbage  in  a  hot  dish,  and  serve  broika 
sausages  round  it;  or  omit  these  last,  and  substitute  lemon-joict, 
cayenne  pepper,  and  a  half-cupful  of  good  ^rayy. 

The  stalk  of  the  cabbage  should  he  split  in  quarters  and  takcc 
entirely  out  in  the  first  instance. 

3  to  4  hours. 

BRUSSELS  SPROUTS. 

These  delicate  little  sprouts,  or  miniature  cabbages,  which  at 
fullest  growth  scarcely  exceed  a  large  walnut  in  size,  should  be  qi 
freshly  gathered.    Free  them  from  all  discoloured  leaves,  cut 
stems  even,  and  wash  the  sprouts  thoroughly.     Throw  them  ^ 
a  pan  of  water  properly  salted,  and  boil  them  quickly  from  dghti 
ten  minutes ;  drain  them  welly  and  serve  them  upon  a  rather  th' 
round  of  toasted  bread  buttered  on  both  sides.    Send  G;ood  mei 
butter  to  table  with  them.    This  is  the  Belgian  mode  of  dressing 
excellent  vegetable,  which  is  served  in  France  with  the  sauce  pot 
over  it,  or  it  is  tossed  in  a  stcwpan  with  a  slice  of  butter  and  wi 
pepper  and  salt :  a  spoonful  or  two  of  veal  gravy  (and  soroetii 
little  lemon-juice)  is  added  when  these  arc  perfectly  mixed.

BOILED  CELERY. 

This  T^etable  is  extremely  good  dressed  like  sea-kale,  and  served 
on  a  toast  with  rich  melted  butter.  Let  it  be  freshly  dug,  wash  it

the  roots  of  equal  len^h,  tie  them  in  bunches,  and  boil  them  in 
plenty  of  water,  with  tne  usual  proportion  of  salt,  from  twenty  to 
thirty  minutes.

hot,  then  cleared,  and  closely  shut  from  half  to  a  whole  hour  hefore  it

In  mixing  paste,  the  water  should  he  added  gradually,  and  the 
whole  gently  drawn  together  with  the  fingers,  until  sufficient  has 
been  added,  when  it  should  he  lightly  kneaded  until  it  is  as  smooth 
as  possible.  When  carelessly  made,  the  surface  is  often  left  covered 
with  small  dry  crumbs  or  lumps ;  or  the  water  is  poureci  in  heed- 
lessly in  so  large  a  proportion  that  it  becomes  necessary  to  add  more 
flour  to  render  it  workable  in  any  way ;  and  this  ought  particularly 
to  be  avoided  when  a  certain  weight  of  all  the  ingn^ents  has  been 
tfikjn. 

TO   GLAZE   OR   ICE   PASTRY.* 

The  fine  yellow  glaze  appropriate  to  meat  pies  is  given  with  beaten 
rolk  of  ^n^,  which  should  be  laid  on  with  a  paste  hrush,  or  a  small 
Imnch  of  leathers :  if  a  lighter  colour  be  wished  for,  whisk  the  whole 
of  the  egg  together,  or  mix  a  little  mUk  with  the  yolk. 

The  best  mode  of  icing  fruit-tarts  before  they  are  sent  to  the  oven 
is,  to  moisten  the  paste  with  cold  water,  to  sift  sugar  thickly  upon  it, 
and  to  press  it  lightly  on  with  the  hand ;  but  when  a  whiter  icing  is 
preferred,  the  pastry  must  be  drawn  from  the  oven  when  nearly 
baked,  and  brushed  with  white  of  egg,  wisked  to  a  froth  ;  then  well 
covered  with  the  sifted  sugar,  and  sprinkled  with  a  few  drops  of 
water  before  it  is  put  in  again :  this  glazing  answers  also  very  well, 
though  it  takes  a  slight  ccnour,  if  used  before  the  pastry  is  baked. 

FEUILLETAOE,   OR   FINE  FRENCH   PUFF   PASTE. 

^  This,  when  made  by  a  good  French  cook,  is  the  perfection  of  rich 
l^t  paste,  and  will  nse  in  the  oven  from  one  to  six  inches  in  height ; 
but  some  practice  is,  without  doubt,  necessary  to  accomplish  this.  In 
summer  it  is  a  great  advantage  to  have  ice  at  hand,  and  to  harden 
the  butter  over  it  before  it  is  used ;  the  paste  also  between  the  in- 
tervals of  rolling  is  improved  by  being  laid  on  an  oven-leaf  over  a 
vessel  containing  it.  Take  an  equal  weight  of  good  butter  free  from 
the  coarse  salt  which  is  found  in  some,  and  which  is  disadvantageous 
for  this  paste,  and  of  fine  dry,  sifted  flour ;  to  each  pound  of  these 
illow  the  volks  of  a  couple  of  eggs,  and  a  small  tcaspoonful  of  salt. 
Break  a  lew  small  bits  of  the  butter  very  lightly  into  the  flour,  put 
the  salt  into  the  centre,  and  pour  on  it  sufficient  water  to  dissolve  it 
(we  do  not  understand  why  the  doing  this  should  be  better  than 
mixing  it  with  the  flour,  as  in  other  pastes,  but  such  is  the  method 
always  pursued  for  it) ;  add  a  little  more  water  to  the  eggs,  moisten 
the  floor  gradually,  and  make  it  into  a  very  smooth  paste,  rather 

•  For  other  pastry  icings  see  chapter  of  *'  cakes.**

lithe  in  summer,  and  never  exceedingly  stiff,  though  the  opposite 
fault,  in  the  extreme,  would  render  the  crust  unmanageahle.  Pro, 
in  a  soft  thin  cloth,  all  the  moisture  from  the  remainder  of  the  hotter, 
and  form  it  into  a  ball,  but  in  doing  this  be  careful  not  to  soften  it  too 
much.  Should  it  be  in  an  unfit  state  for  pastry  from  the  heat  of  the 
weather,  put  it  into  a  basin,  and  set  the  basin  into  a  pan  of  water 
mixed  with  plenty^  of  salt  and  saltpetre,  and  let  it  remain  in  a  eool 
place  for  an  hour  if  possible  before  it  is  used.  When  it  is  ready  (and 
the  paste  should  never  be  commenced  until  it  is  so),  roll  the  crust  ont 
square,*  and  of  sufficient  size  to  enclose  the  butter,  flatten  this  a  little 
upon  it  in  the  centre,  and  then  fold  the  crust  well  over  it,  and  roH  it 
out  thin  as  lightly  as  possible,  after  having  dredged  the  board  and 

?aste  roller  with  a  little  flour:  this  is  c^led  giving  it  one  ten. 
'hen  fold  it  in  three,  give  it  another  turn,  and  set  it  aside  where  it 
will  be  very  cool,  for  a  few  minutes ;  give  it  two  more  turns  in  the 
same  way,  rolling  it  each  time  very  ushtly  but  of  equal  thicknen, 
and  to  the  full  length  that  it  will  reach,  taking  always  especial  cm 
that  the  butter  shaB  not  break  through  the  paste.  Let  it  asaia  be 
set  aside  to  become  cold ;  and  after  it  has  been  twice  more  rolled  and 
folded  in  three,  give  it  a  half  turn,  by  folding  it  once  only,  and  a 
will  be  ready  for  use. 

Equal  weight  of  the  finest  flour  and  good  butter ;  to  each  pound  of 
these,  the  yolks  of  two  eggs,  and  a  small  saltspoonful  of  salt :  6|

VERY   GOOD   LIGHT   PASTE. 

Mix  with  a  pound  of  sifted  flour  six  ounces  of  fresh,  pure  lard, 
and  make  them  into  a  smooth  paste  with  cold  water ;  press  the  but- 
termilk from  ten  ounces  of  butter,  and  form  it  into  a  ball,  by  twistii^ 
it  in  a  clean  cloth.  Roll  out  the  paste,  put  the  ball  of  butter  in  the 
middle,  close  it  like  an  apple-dumpling,  and  roll  it  very  lightly  until 
it  is  less  than  an  inch  thick ;  fold  the  ends  into  the  middle,  dust  a 
little  flour  over  the  board  and  paste-roller,  and  roll  the  paste  thin  a 
second  time,  then  set  it  aside  for  three  or  four  minutes  in  a  very  cool 
place ;  give  it  two  more  ftinu,  after  it  has  again  been  left  for  a  few 
minutes,  roll  it  out  twice  more,  folding  it  each  time  in  three.  Thif 
ought  to  render  it  fit  for  use.  The  sooner  this  paste  is  sent  to  the 
oven  after  it  is  made,  the  lighter  it  will  be:  if  allowed  to  remaia 
before  it  is  baked,  it  will  be  tough  and  heavy. 

flour,  1  lb. ;  lard,  6  oz. ;  butter,  10  oz. ;  little  salt.

eight  ounces  of  butter ;  add  a  pinch  of  salt,  and  suflicient  cold  water

i^aia  six  ounces  of  butter,  and  roll  the  paste  out  thin  three  or  four 
times,  folding  the  ends  into  the  middle. 

Flour,  2  lbs. ;  little  salt;  butter,  lib.  10  oz. 

If  rery  rich  paste  be  required,  equal  portions  of  flour  and  butter 
must  be  used ;  and  the  latter  may  be  divided  into  two,  instead  of 
three  parts,  when  it  is  to  be  rolled  in. 

CREAM   CRUST. 

{Auihor*s  Receipt,     Very  good,) 

Stir  a  little  fine  salt  into  a  pound  of  dry  flour,  and  mix  gradually 
^h  it  suflicient  very  thick,  sweet  cream  to  form  a  smooth  paste ;  it 
▼ill  be  found  sufficiently  good  for  common  family  dinners,  without 
the  addition  of  butter ;  but  to  make  an  excellent  crust,  roll  in  four 
oonces  in  the  usual  way,  after  having  given  the  paste  a  couple  of 
Amu.  Handle  it  as  lightly  as  possible  in  making  it,  and  send  it  to 
the  oven  as  soon  as  it  is  ready :  it  may  be  used  for  fruit  tarts,  canne* 
Ions,  puffs,  and  other  varieties  of  small  pastry,  or  for  good  meat  pies. 
Six  ounces  of  butter  to  the  pound  of  flour  will  give  a  very  rich 
crust. 

Flour,  1  lb. ;  salt,  1  small  saltspoonful  (more  for  meat  pies) ;  rich 
cream,  ^  to  f  pint ;  butter,  4  oz. ;  for  richest  crust,  6  oz. 

PATB   BRIsiE,   OR   FRENCH   CRUST   FOR   HOT   OR   COLD 

MEAT   PIES. 

Sift  two  pounds  and  a  quarter  of  fine  dry  flour,  and  break  into  it 
one  pound  of  butter,  work  them  together  with  the  fingers  until  they 
resemble  fine  crumbs  of  bread,  then  add  a  small  teaspoonful  of  salt, 
and  make  them  into  a  firm  paste,  with  the  yolks  of  four  eggs,  well 
beaten,  mixed  with  hal/  a  pint  of  cold  water,  and  strained ;  or  for  a 
somewhat  richer  crust  of  the  same  kind,  take  two  pounds  of  flour, 
one  of  butter,  the  yolks  of  four  eggs,  half  an  ounce  of  salt,  and  less 
ibaai  the  half  pint  of  water,  and  work  the  whole  well  until  the  paste 
is  perfectly  smooth. 

Floar,  2i  lbs. ;  butter,  1  lb. ;  salt,  1  small  teaspoonful ;  yolks  of 
eggs,  4;  water,  )  pint.  Or:  flour,  2  lbs.;  butter,  1  lb.;  yolks  of 
cggB^  4 ;  water,  less  than  |  pint. 

PLEAD  CRUST. 

PUad  is  the-provindal  name  for  the  leaf,  or  inside  fat  of  a  pig, 
vhich  makes  excellent  crust  when  fresh,  much  finer,  indeed,  tnan 


348  MODERN  COOKERY.  [cbaf  inn. 

after  it  is  melted  into  lard.  Clear  it  quite  from  skin,  and  slloe  it  very 
thin  into  tl^  fiour,  add  sufficient  salt  to  give  flavour  to  the  ptsle. 
and  make  the  whole  up  smooth  and  firm  with  cold  water ;  laj  it  on 
a  clean  dresser,  and  beat  it  forcibly  with  a  rolling-pin  until  the  fletd 
is  blended  perfectly  with  the  flour.  It  may  then  be  made  into  ctka 
with  a  paste-cutter,  or  used  for  pies,  round  the  edges  of  which  a  knife 
should  be  passed,  as  the  crust  rises  better  when  cut  than  if  merely 
rolled  to  the  proper  size.  With  the  addition  of  a  small  quantity  <h 
butter,  which  may  either  be  broken  into  the  flour  before  the  flead  is 
mixed  with  it,  or  rolled  into  the  paste  after  it  is  beaten,  it  will  be 
found  equal  to  fine  puff  crust,  with  the  advantage  of  being  more  easy 
of  diction. 

Quite  common  crust :  flour,  1|  lb. ;  flead,  8  oz. ;  salt,  1  small  tea- 
spoonful.  Gk)od  common  crust :  flour,  1  lb. ;  flead,  6  oz. ;  butter,  2 
oz.  Rich  crust :  flead, )  lb. ;  butter,  2  oz. ;  flour,  1  lb.  Tlie  crost  b 
very  good  when  made  without  any  butter. 

COMMON   SUET-CRUST   FOR   PIES. 

In  many  families  this  is  preferred  both  for  nies  and  tarts,  to  crust 
made  with  butter,  as  being  much  more  wholesome;  but  it  shoold 
never  be  served  unless  especially  ordered,  as  it  is  to  some  penoos 
peculiarly  distasteful.  Chop  the  suet  extremely  small,  and  aad  from 
six  to  eight  oimces  of  it  to  a  pound  of  flour,  with  a  few  grains  of  salt; 
mix  these  with  cold  water  into  a  firm  paste,  and  work  it  very  smootL 
Some  cooks  beat  it  with  a  paste-roller,  until  the  suet  is  perfectly 
blended  with  flour ;  but  the  crust  is  lighter  without  this.  In  exceed- 
ingly sultry  weather  the  suet,  not  being  firm  enough  to  chop,  may  be 
sliced  as  thin  as  possible,  and  well  beaten  into  the  paste  after  it  ii 
worked  up. 

Flour,  2  lbs. ;  beef  or  veal  kidney-suet,  12  to  16  oz. ;  salt  (for 
fruit-pies),  \  teaspoonful,  for  meat- pies,  1  teaspoonful. 

. 

TERY   SUPERIOR   SUET-CRUST. 

Strip  the  skin  entirely  from  some  fresh  veal  or  beef  kidney-soet ; 
chop,  and  then  put  it  into  the  mortar,  with  a  small  quantit^^  of  pore- 
flavoured  lard,  oil,  or  butter,  and  pound  it  perfectly  smooth :  it  may 
then  be  used  for  crust  in  the  same  way  tnat  butter  is,  in  maldiig 
puff-paste,  and  in  this  form  will  be  found  a  most  excellent  snbstitnte 
for  it,  for  hot  pies  or  tarts.  It  is  not  quite  so  good  for  those  which 
are  to  be  served  cold.  Eight  ounces  of  suet  poimded  with  two  of 
butter,  and  worked  with  the  fingers  into  a  pound  of  flour,  will  make 
%n  exceedingly  good  short  crust ;  but  for  a  very  rich  one  the  pro- 
portion must  be  increased. 

Good  short  crust :  flour,  1  lb. ;  suet,  8  oz. ;  butter,  2  oz. ;  salt,  4 
teaspoonful.  Richer  crust :  suet,  16  oz. ;  butter,  4  oz. ;  flour,  1|  ilk ; 
salt,  1  small  teaspoonful

YEKT    RICH  SHORT  CRU6T  FOR   TARTS. 

Break  lightly,  vriih  the  least  poesihle  handling,  six  ounces  of 
batter  into  eisht  of  flour ;  add  a  aessertspoonful  of  pounded  sugar, 
aud  two  or  tnree  of  water ;  roll  the  paste,  for  several  minutes,  to 
blend  the  ingredients  well,  folding  it  together  like  puff-crust,  and 
touch  it  as  little  as  possible. 

Flour,  8  oz. ;  butter,  6  oz. ;  pounded  sugar,  1  dessertspoonful ; 
water,  1  to  2  spoonsful. 

EXCELLENT   SHORT   CRUST   FOR  SWEET   PASTRY. 

Crumble  down  very  lightly  half  a  pound  of  hutter  into  a  pound  of 
flour,  breaking  it  quite  small.  Mix  well  \rith  these  a  slight  pinch  of 
salt  and  two  ounces  of  silled  sugar,  and  add  sufficient  milk  to  make 
them  up  into  a  very  smooth  and  somewhat  firm  paste.  Bake  this 
slowly,  and  keep  it  pale.  It  will  be  found  an  admirable  crust  if  well 
made  and  lightly  handled,  and  will  answer  for  manv  dishes  much 
better  than  puff-paste.  It  will  rise  in  the  oven  too,  and  be  extremely 
light.  Ten  ounces  of  butter  will  render  it  very  rich,  but  we  find 
eight  quite  sufficient. 

BRIOCHE   PASTE. 

The  brioche  is  a  rich,  light  kind  of  unsweetened  bun  or  cake,  very 
eommonly  sold,  and  served  to  all  classes  of  people  in  France,  where 
it  is  made  in  great  perfection  bv  good  cooks  and  pastrycooks.  It  is 
iaahionable  now  at  English  tables,  though  in  a  different  form,  serving 
principally  as  a  crust  to  enclose  rissoUs,  or  to  make  canneUnu  and 
fritters.  We  have  seen  it  recommended  for  a  vol-au'vent,  for  which 
we  should  say  it  does  not  answer  by  any  means  so  well  as  the  fine 
puflT-paste  called  feuiUetage.  The  large  proportion  of  butter  and 
e^s  which  it  contains  render  it  to  many  persons  highly  indigestible ; 
and  we  mention  this  to  warn  invalids  agiunst  it,  as  we  have  known 
it  to  cause  great  suffering  to  persons  out  of  health.  To  make  it,  take 
a  couple  of  pounds*  of  fine  dry  fieur,  sifted  as  for  cakes,  and  separate 
ei^ht  ounces  of  this  from  the  remainder  to  make  the  leaven.  Put 
it  mto  a  small  pan,  and  mix  it  lightly  into  a  lithe  paste,  with  half  an 
ounce  of  yeast,  and  a  spoonful  or  two  of  warm  water ;  make  two  or 
three  slight  incisions  across  the  top,  throw  a  cloth  over  the  pan,  and 
place  it  near  the  fire  for  about  twenty  minutes  to  rise.  In  the  in- 
terval make  a  hollow  space  in  the  centre  of  the  remainder  of  the 

*It  flhonld  be  remarlced,  that  the  directions  for  brioche-making  are  principally 
cleri?ed  from  the  French,  and  that  the  pound  in  their  coonti^  ireighs  two  ounco's 
more  than  with  ub:  this  differeDce  will  acconnt  for  the  dimcolty  of  working  in 
the  number  of  eggs  which  they  generally  specify,  and  which  render  the  paste  u»q 
iftoist.

flour,  axid  put  into  it  half  an  ounce  of  salt,  as  mucn  fine  sifted  sngir, 
and  half  a  gill  of  cream,  or  a  dessertspoonful  of  water ;  add  a  pound 
of  butter  as  free  from  moisture  as  it  can  be,  and  ouite  so  from  Uigt 
grains  of  salt ;  cut  it  into  small  bits,  put  it  into  Uie  flour,  and  poor

Place  it  in  summer  in  a  cool  place,  in  winter  in  a  warm  one.  It  is 
usually  made  over-night,  and  baked  in  the  early  part  of  the  follow- 
ing day.  It  should  then  be  kneaded  up  afresh  the  first  thing  in  the 
morning.  To  mould  it  in  the  usual  form,  make  it  into  balls  of 
uniform  size,  hollow  these  a  little  at  the  top  by  pressing  the  thumb 
round  them,  brush  them  over  with  yolk  of  egg,  and  put  a  seoood 
much  smaller  ball  into  the  hollow  part  of  each ;  glaze  them  entirely 
w^ith  3'olk  of  egg,  and  send  them  to  a  quick  oven  for  half  an  hour  or 
more.  The  paste  may  also  be  made  into  the  form  of  a  large  cike, 
then  placed  on  a  tin,  or  copper  oven-leaf,  and  supported  with  a 
pasteboard  in  the  baking ;  for  the  form  of  which  see  introductory 
page  of  Chapter  XXVII.

eggs,  6  to  8 


MODERN  POTATO   PASTT. 

{An  excellent  f canity  dish,) 

A  tin  mould  of  the  construction  shown  in  the  plate,  with  a  per- 
forated moveable  top,  and  a  small  valve  to  allow  the  escape  of^ 
fteam,  must  be  had  for  this  pasty,  which  is  a  good  family  dish,  and 
which  may  be  varied  in  numberless  ways.  Arrange  at  the  bottom  of 
the  mould  from  two  to  three  pounds  of  mutton  cutlets,  freed,  ac- 
cording to  the  taste,  from  all,  or  from  the  greater  portion  of  the  lit, 
then  washed,  lightly  dredged  on  both  sides  with  flour,  and  seaaoocd 
Avith  salt  and  pepper,  or  cayenne.  Pour  to  them  sufiScient  broth  or 
water  to  make  the  gravy,  and  add  to  it  at  pleasure,  a  tablespoonfol  of 
mushroom  catsup  or  of  Harvey^s  sauce.  Have  ready  boiled,  and 
jery  smoothly  mashed,  with  about  an  ounce  of  butter,  and  a  spoon- 
ful or  two  of^^milk  or  cream  to  each  pound,  as  many  good  potatoes  tt 
will  form  a  crust  to  the  pasty  of  quite  three  inches  thick ;  put  the 


CBAT  x\-m.]  PASTRY.  351 


eoTer  on  the  mould  and  arrange  these  equally  upon  it,  leaying  them 
a  little  rough  on  the  surfaced  Bake  the  pasty  in  a  moderate  oven 
from  three-quarters  of  an  hour  to  an  hour  and  a  quarter,  according  to 
its  ioie  and  its  contents.  Pin  a  folded  napkin  neatly  round  the 
mould,  before  it  is  served,  and  have  ready  a  hot  dish  to  receive  the 
cover,  which  must  not  be  lifted  off  until  after  the  pasty  is  on  the 
table. 

Chicken,  or  veal  and  03rsters ;  delicate  pork  chops  with  a  seasoning 
oi'  sage  and  a  little  parboiled  onion,  or  an  eschalot  or  two  finely 
minc^ ;    partridges  or   rabbits  neatly  carved,  mixed  with  small 
mushrooms,  and  moistened  with  a  little  good  stock,  will  all  give 
excellent  varieties  of  this  dish^  which  may  be  made  likewise  with 
highly  seasoned  slices  of  salmon  freed  from  the  skin,  sprinkled  with 
fine  herbs  or  intermixed  with  shrimps:  clarified  butter,  rich  veal 
stocky  or  good  white  wine,  may  be  poured  to  them  to  form  the  gravy. 
To  thicken  this,  a  little  flour  should  be  dredged  upon  the  fish  oefore 
it  is  laid  into  the  mould.    Other  kinds,  such  as  cod,  mullet,  mackerel 
in  fillets,  salt  fish  (previously  kept  at  the  point  of  boiling  until  three 
parts  done,  then  pulled  into  flakes,  and  put  into  the  mould  with  hard 
^gs  sliced,  a  little  cream,  flour,  butter,  cayenne,  and  anchovy- 
essence,  and  baked  with  mashed  parsneps  on  the  top),  will  all  answer 
well  for  this  pasty.    Veal,  when  used  for  it,  should  be  well  beaten 
first :  sweetbreads,  sliced,  may  be  laid  in  with  it. 

For  a  pasty  of  moderate  size,  two  pounds,  or  two  and  a  half  of 
meat,  and  from  three  to  four  of  potatoes,  will  be  sufiicient ;  a  quarter 
of  a  pint  of  milk  or  cream,  two  small  teaspoonsful  of  salt,  and  from 
one  to  two  ounces  of  butter  must  be  mixed  up  with  these  last.*

A   GOOD   COMMON   ENGLISH   GABfE   PIE.

(see  page  337),  lay  the  venison  in  the  centre,  and  the  hare  closcily

well  heated  oven.  The  remnants  and  bones  oi^  the  hare  and  veniaon 
may  be  stewed  down  into  a  small  quantity  of  excellent  soup,  or  with 
a  less  proportion  of  water  into  an  admirable  gravy,  part  of  which, 
after  bavins  been  cleared  from  fat,  may  be  poured  into  the  pie.  The 
jelly,  added  to  its  contents  at  first,  can  be  made,  when  no  such  stock 
IS  at  hand,  of  a  couple  of  pounds  of  shin  of  beef,  boiled  down  in  a 
quart  of  water,  which  must  be  reduced  quite  half,  and  seasoned  only 
with  a  good  slice  of  lean  ham,  a  few  peppercorns,  seven  or  eisht 
cloves,  a  blade  of  mace,  and  a  little  salt.  One  ^und  and  a  ludf  of

MODERN   CHICKEN   PIE. 

Skin,  and  cut  down  into  joints  a  couple  of  fowls,  take  out  all  the 
bones,  and  season  the  flesh  highly  with  salt,  cayenne,  pounded  mace, 
and  nutmeg ;  line  a  dish  with  a  thin  paste,  and  spread  over  it  a 
hjer  of  the  finest  sausage-meat,  which  has  previously  been  moistened 
with  a  spoonful  or  two  of  cold  water ;  over  this  place  closely  together 
some  of  the  boned  chicken  joints,  then  more  sausage-meat,  and  con- 
tinue thus  with  alternate  layers  of  each,  until  the  dish  is  full ;  roll 
out,  and  iasten  securely  at  the  edges,  a  cover  half  an  inch  thick,  trim 
off  the  superfluous  paste,  make  an  incision  in  the  top,  lay  some  paste 
leaves  round  it,  glaze  the  whole  with  yoUc  of  egff,  and  bake  the  pie 
from  an  hour  and  a  half  to  two  hours  in  a  well  heated  oven.  Lay 
a  sheet  or  two  of  writing-paper  over  the  crust,  should  it  brown  too 
quickly.  Minced  herbs  can  be  mixed  with  the  sausage-meat  at 
pleasure,  and  a  small  quantity  of  eschalot  also,  when  its  flavour  is 
much  liked :  it  should  oe  well  moistened  with  water,  or  the  whole 
will  be  unpalatably  dry.  The  pie  may  be  served  hot  or  cold,  but  we 
would  rather  recommend  the  latter. 

A  eouple  of  very  young  tender  rabbits  will  answer  exceedingly 
well  for  it  instead  of  fowls,  and  a  border,  or  half  paste  in  the  dish 
will  generally  be  preferred  to  an  entire  lining  of  the  crust,  which  is 
now  but  rarely  served,  unless  for  pastry,  which  is  to  be  taken  out  of 
the  dish  or  mould  in  which  it  is  b^ed  before  it  ia  sent  to  table. 

A  COMMON  CHICKEN  FIB. 

Prepare  the  fowls  as  for  boiling,  cut  them  down  into  joints,  and 
Koaon  them  with  salt,  white  pepper,  and, nutmeg  or  pounded  mace; 
irnmge  them  neatly  in  a  dish  bordered  with  paste,  lay  amongst  them 
ihiee  or  four  fresh  eggs  boiled  hard,  and  cut  in  halves,  pour  in  some 
lold  water,  put  on  a  thick  cover,  pare  the  edge,  and  ornament  it,

make  a  hole  in  the  centre,  lay  a  roll  of  paste,  or  a  few  leaves  roood

PiaEON   PIB. 

Lay  a  border  of  fine  puff  paste  round  a  large  dish,  and  cover  tbc 
bottom  with  a  veal  cutlet  or  tender  rump  steak,  free  from  fat  and 
bone,  and  seasoned  with  salt,  cayenne,  and  nutmee  or  pounded  msoe; 
prepare  with  great  nicety  as  many  freshly-killed  young  pigeons  ai 
the  dish  will  contain  in  one  layer ;  put  into  each  a  slice  or  ball  of 
butter,  seasoned  with  a  little  cayenne  and  mace,  lay  them  into  tbe

quarters  of  an  inch  thick,  secure  it  wdl  round  the  edge,  omaocDtit 
highly,  and  bake  the  pie  for  an  hour  or  more  in  a  weU-heated  o^cb 
It  is  a  great  improvement  to  fill  the  birds  with  small  muahrooni- 
buttons,  prepared  as  for  partridges  (see  Chapter  XY.) :  their  liven 
also  may  be  put  into  them.

paste.  It  should  be  seasoned  with  salt  and  pepper,  or  cayenne,  ani 
sufficient  water  poured  in  to  make  the  gravy,  and  keep  the  rae 
moist.  Lay  on  the  cover,  and  be  careful  always  to  brush  the  edge 
every  part  with  egg  or  cold  water,  then  join  it  securely  to  the  p« 
which  IS  round  the  rim,  trim  both  off  close  to  the  dish,  pass  the  pool 
of  the  knife  through  the  middle  of  the  cover,  lay  some  slight  rai  oi 
ornament  of  paste  round  it,  and  decorate  the  border  of  the  pie  in  as) 
of  the  usual  modes,  which  are  too  common  to  require  deacriptiaa 
Send  the  pie  to  a  well-heated,  but  not  fierce  oven  for  about  an  Yum 
and  twenty  minutes.  To  ;nake  a  richer  beef-steak  pie  put  beardaj

COMUON  MUTTON   PIE. 

A  pound  and  a  quarter  of  flour  will  make  sufficient  paste  for 
a  moderate-sized  pie,  and  two  pounds  of  mutton  freed  from  the 
greater  portion  of  the  fat  will  fill  it.  Butter  a  dish  and  line  it  with 
about  half  the  paste  rolled  thin ;  lay  in  the  mutton  evenly,  and 
sprinkle  over  it  three-quarters  of  an  ounce  of  salt,  and  from  half  to 
a  whole  teaspoonful  of  pepper  according  to  the  taste ;  pour  in  cold 
water  to  within  an  inch  of  tne  brim.  Roll  the  cover,  wnich  should 
be  quite  half  an  inch  thick,  to  the  size  of  the  dish ;  wet  the  edges  of 
the  paste  with  cold  water  or  white  of  egg,  be  careful  to  close  them 
securely,  cut  them  off  close  to  the  rim  of  the  dish,  stick  the  point  of 
the  knife  through  the  centre,  and  bake  the  pie  an  hour  and  a  quarter 
in  a  well-heated  oven. 

Flour,  1^  lb. ;  minced  suet  rather  less  than  i  lb. ;  or,  butter,  4  oz., 
and  very  pure  lard,  2  or  3  oz. ;  mutton,  2  lbs. ;  salt,  }  oz. ;  pepper, 
half  to  a  whole  teaspoonful ;  water,  i  pint :  1^  hour. 


A  GOOD  MUTTON  PIE. 

Lay  a  half-paste  of  short  or  of  puff  crust  round  a  buttered  dish ; 
take  the  whole  or  part  of  a  loin  of  mutton,  strip  off  the  fat  entirely, 
and  raise  the  flesh  clear  from  the  bones  without  dividing  it,  then  slice 
it  into  cutlets  of  equal  thickness,  season  them  well  with  salt  and 
pepper,  or  cayenne,  and  strew  between  the  layers  some  finely-minced 
herbs  mixed  with  two  or  three  eschalots,  when  the  flavour  of  these 
kttt  is  liked ;  or  omit  them,  and  roll  quite  thin  some  good  forcemeat 
(which  can  be  flavoured  with  a  little  minced  eschalot  at  pleasure), 
and  lay  it  between  the  cutlets :  two  or  three  mutton  kidneys  inter- 
mingled with  the  meat  will  greatly  enribh  the  gravy;  pour  in  a 
little  cold  water,  roll  the  cover  haLT  an  inch  thick,  or  more  should 
the  crust  be  short,  as  it  will  not  rise  like  puff  paste,  close  the  pie 
very  securely,  trim  the  edges  even  with  the  dish,  ornament  the  pie 
according  to  the  taste,  make  a  hole  in  the  centre,  and  bake  it  from 
an  hour  and  a  half  to  a  couple  of  hours.  The  proportions  of  paste 
and  meat  may  be  ascertained  by  consulting  the  last  receipt.  Gravy 
made  with  part  of  the  bones,  quite  cleared  from  fat,  and  left  to 
become  cold,  may  be  used  to  fill  the  pie  instead  of  water.

OYSTER   PATTIES.*      (BNTRiE). 

Line  some  small  patty-pans  with  fine  puff-paste,  rolled  thin  and  to 
preserve  their  form  when  baked,  put  a  hit  of  bread  into  each ;  lay  on 
the  covers,  pinch  and  trim  the  edges,  and  send  the  patties  to  a  brisk 
oven.  Plump  and  beard  from  two  to  three  dozens  of  small  oysters ; 
mix  very  smoothly  a  teaspoonful  of  flour  with  an  ounce  of  butter, 
put  them  into  a  clean  saucepan,  shake  them  round  over  a  gentle  fire, 
and  let  them  simmer  for  two  or  three  minutes ;  throw  in  a  little  salt, 
pounded  mace,  and  cayenne,  then  add,  by  slow  degrees,  two  or  three 
spoonsful  of  rich  cream,  give  these  a  boil,  and  pour  in  the  strained 
liquor  of  the  oysters ;  next,  lay  in  the  fish,  and  keep  at  the  point  of 
boiling  for  a  couple  of  minutes.  Raise  the  covers  from  the  patties, 
take  out  the  bread,  fill  them  with  the  oysters  and  their  sauce,  and  re- 
place the  covers.  We  have  found  it  an  improvement  to  stew  the 
Deu^  of  the  fish  with  a  strip  or  two  of  lemon-peel,  in  a  little  good 
veal  stock  for  a  quarter  of  an  hour,  then  to  strain  and  add  it  to  the 
sauce.  The  oysters,  unless  very  small,  should  be  once  or  twice 
divided. 

COMMON   LOBSTER   PATTIES. 

Prepare  the  fish  for  these  as  directed  for  fricasseed  lobster.  Chapter 
n.,  increasing  a  little  the  proportion  of  sauce.  Fill  the  patty-cases 
widi  the  mixture  quite  hot,  and  serve  immediately. 

SUPERLATIVE  LOBSTER-PATTIES. 

(Author's  Receipt,) 

Form  into  balls  about  half  the  size  of  a 

filbert  either   the   cutlet-mixture  or  the 

pounded  lobster  of  Chapter  111.,  roll  them

pole  a  larger  one  in  the  centre,  and  serve  the  whole  very  quickly. 

The  Dresden  patties  of  page  387  may  be  thus  filled. 

GOOD  CHICKEN  PATTIES.      (ENTRic.) 

Baise  the  white  flesh  entirely  from  a  young  undressed  fowl,  divide 
it  once  or  twice,  and  lay  it  into  a  small  dean  saucepan,  in  which 

*  These  patties  shonld  be  made  small,  with  a  thin  crust,  and  id«U  fllUd  with

about  an  ounce  of  butter  has  been  dissolved,  and  just  begins  to

sharp  knife ;  heat  it,  without  allowing  it  to  boU,  in  a  little  good

fifteen  to  eighteen  minutes.  It  is  a  great  improvement  to  stew  md 
mince  a  few  mushrooms  with  the  chicken. 

The  breasts  of  cold  turkeys,  fowls,  partridges,  or  pheasants,  or  the

id  lay  them  on  the  remainder  of  the  rounds  of  paste,  so  as  to  form 
te  rims  of  the  tartlets.  Bake  them  from  ten  to  twelve  minutes,  fill

A   SEFTON,   OR   VEAL  CU8TARD. 

Pour  boiling,  a  pint  of  rich,  clear,  pale  veal  gravy  on  six  ihsh 
^gs,  which  have  been  well  beaten  and  strained :  sprinkle  in  directly 
the  grated  rind  of  a  fine  lemon,  a  little  cayenne,  some  salt  if  needed, 
and  a  quarter- teaspoonful  of  mace.  Put  a  paste  border  round  a  dish, 
pour  in,  first  two  ounces  of  clarified  butter,  and  then  the  other  ingre- 
dients ;  bake  the  Sefton  in  a  very  slow  oven  from  twenty-five  to 
thirty  minutes,  or  until  it  is  quite  firm  in  the  middle,  and  send  it  to 
table  with  a  little  food  gravy.  Very  highly  flavoured  game  stock, 
in  which  a  few  mushrooms  have  been  stewed,  may  be  u^  for  tim 
dish  with  great  advantage  in  lieu  of  veal  gravy ;  and  a  sauce  made  of 
the  smallest  mushroom  buttons,  may  be  served  with  it  in  either  cue. 
The  mixture  can  be  baked  in  a  whole  paste,  if  preferred  so,  or  in  well 
buttered  cups ;  then  turned  out  and  covered  with  the  sauce  before  it 
u  sent  to  table. 

Rich  veal  or  game  stock,  1  pint ;  fresh  ^gs,  6 ;  rind,  1  lemon ; 
little  salt  and  cayenne ;  pounded  mace,  i  teaspoonful ;  butler,  2  oa. : 
baked,  25  to  30  minutes,  slow  oven« 

APPLE  CAKBy   OR  GERMAN  TART. 

Work  together  with  the  finffers,  ten  ounces  of  butter  and  a  ponnd 
of  flour,  until  they  resemble  fine  crumbs  of  bread ;  throw  in  a  mdl 
pinch  of  salt,  and  make  them  into  a  firm  smooth  paste  with  the  ydks 
of  two  eggs  and  a  spoonful  or  two  of  water.  Butter  thickly,  a  plain 
tin  cake,  or  pie  mould  (those  which  open  at  the  sides,  see  pWte,  page 
344,  are  best  adapted  for  the  purpose)  ;  roll  out  the  paste  thin,  pla» 
the  mould  upon  it,  trim  a  bit  to  its  exact  size,  cover  the  bottom  of  the 
mould  with  this,  then  cut  a  band  the  height  of  the  sides,  and  preas 
it  smoothly  round  them,  joining  the  edge,  which  must  be  moistened 
with  egg  or  water,  to  the  bottom  crust ;  and  fasten  upon  them,  Ui 
prevent  their  separation,  a  narrow  and  thin  band  of  paste,  also 
moistened.  Next,  fill  the  mould  nearly  from  the  brim  with  the  fol- 
lowing marmalade,  which  must  be  quite  cold  when  it  is  put  in. 
Boil  together,  over  a  gentle  fire  at  first,  but  more  quickly  aftenraida» 
three  pounds  of  ffood  apples  with  fourteen  ounces  of  pounded  i 
or  of  the  finest  Lisbon,  the  strained  juice  of  a  large  lemon, 
ounces  of  fresh  butter,  and  a  teaspoonful  of  pounded  cinnamon, 
the  lightly  grated  rind  of  a  couple  of  lemons :  when  the  whole 
perfectly  smooth  and  dry,  turn  it  into  a  pan  to  cool,  and  let  it 
quite  cold  before  it  is  put  into  the  paste.  In  early  autumn,  a  lar 
proportion  of  sugar  may  be  required,  but  this  can  be  regulated  by 
iaste      When  the  mould  is  filled,  roll  out  the  coyer,  lay  it  carefixUy 


ciri?.  xtielJ  PASTRT*  363 

orer  the  marmalade  tbat  it  may  not  touch  it ;  and  when  the  cake  is 
Mcnrely  dosed,  trim  otf  the  superfluous  paste,  add  a  little  pounded 
sugar  to  the  parings,  spread  them  out  yery  thin,  and  cut  them  into 
leaves  to  ornament  the  top  of  the  cake,  round  which  they  may  be 
placed  as  a  sort  of  wreath.*  Bake  it  .for  an  hour  in  a  moderately 
Driskoven;  take  it  from  the  mould,  and  should  the  sides  not  be 
mfficiently  coloured  put  it  back  for  a  few  minutes  into  the  oven  upon 
a  baking  tin.  Lay  a  paper  over  the  top,  when  it'  is  of  a  fine  light 
brown,  to  prerent  its  bemg  too  deeply  coloured.  This  cake  should 
be  served  hot. 

Paste :  flour,  1  lb. ;  butter,  10  oz. ;  yolks  of  eggs,  2 ;  little  water. 
Marmalade :  apples,  3  lbs. ;  sugar,  14  oz.  (more  if  needed) ;  juice  of 
kmon,  1 ;  linds  of  lemons,  2 ;  butter,  8  oz. :  baked,  1  hour. 

TOURTB  UERINGViE,   OR   TART  WITH   ROYAL   ICINQ.t 

Lay  a  band  of  fine  paste  round  the  rim  of  a  tart-dish,  fill  it  with 
any  kind  of  fruit  mixed  with  a  moderate  proportion  of  sugar,  roll 
oizt  the  cover  very  evenly,  moisten  the  edges  oi  the  paste,  press  them 
together  carefully,  and  trim  them  off  close  to  the  dish ;  spread 
eifnally  over  the  top,  to  within  rather  more  than  an  inch  of  the  edge 
alJ  round,  the  whites  of  three  fresh  eggs  beaten  to  a  quite  solid  froth 
and  mixed  quickly  at  the  moment  ol  using  them  with  three  table- 
spoonsful  of  dry  sifted  susar.  Put  the  tart  into  a  moderately  brisk 
OTen,  and  when  the  crust  nas  risen  well  and  the  icin^  is  set,  either 
lay  a  sheet  of  writing-paper  lightly  over  it,  or  draw  it  to  a  part  of 
the  oven  where  it  will  not  take  too  much  colour.  This  is  now  a 
iasfaionable  mode  of  icing  tarts,  and  greatly  improves  their  appear- 
ance. 

Bake  half  an  hour. 

A.   GOOD   APPLE   TART. 

A  pound  and  a  quarter  of  apples  weighed  after  they,  are  pared  and 
cored,  will  be  sufncient  for  a  small  tart,  and  four  ounces  more  for 
one  of  moderate  size.  Lay  a  border  of  £nglish  puff-paste,  or  of 
eream-cmst  round  the  dish,  just  dip  the  apples  into  water,  arrange 
them  very  compactly  in  it,  higher  m  the  centre  than  at  the  sides, 
and  strew  amongst  them  from  three  to  four  ounces  of  pounded 
sugar,  or  more  qhould  they  be  very  acid :  the  grated  rind  and  the 
atntned  juice  of  naif  a  lemon  will  much  improve  their  flavour.    Lay

piAin  or  ooloored.

TART  OF  TBRY  TOUNO  OREBN  APPLES.   (gOOD.) 

Take  very  young  apples  from  the  tree  before  the  cores  ore  formed, 
clear  off  the  buds  and  stalks,  wash  them  well,  and  fill  a  tart-^sh 
with  them  after  having  rolled  them  in  plenty  of  sugar,  or  strew 
layers  of  sugar  between  them ;  add  a  very  small  quantity  of  water, 
and  bake  the  tart  rather  slowly,  that  the  fruit  may  be  tender  quite 
through.  It  will  resemble  a  green  apricot-tart  if  carefully  nade. 
We  give  this  receipt  from  recollection,  having  had  the  dish  served 
often  formerly,  and  having  found  it  very  good. 

BARBERRY  TART. 

Barberries,  with  half  their  weight  of  fine  brown  sugar,  when  thef 
are  thoroughly  ripe,  and  with  two  ounces  more  when  they  are  ml 
quite  so,  make  an  admirable  tart.  For  one  of  moderate  siae,  put  into 
a  dish  bordered  with  paste  three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  barberna 
stripped  from  their  stalks,  and  six  ounces  of  sugar  in  altemite 
layers;  pour  over  them  three  tablespoonsful  of  water,  put  on  the 
coyer,  and  bake  the  tart  for  half  an  hour.  Another  way  of  making 
it  is,  to  line  a  shallow  tin  pan  with  very  thin  crust,  to  mix  the  finni 
and  sugar  well  together  with  a  spoon  before  they  are  laid  in,  and  fo 
put  bars  of  paste  across  instead  of  a  cover;  or  it  may  be  baked 
without  either.*

finest  flonr,  and  break  lightly  into  it  four  ounces  of  fresh  butter, 
whkh  should  be  firm.    I^iuke  these  up  smoothly  with  cold  milk  or 
viter,  of  which  nearly  a  quarter  of  a  pint  will  be  sufficient,  unless 
the  batter  should  be  very  hard,  when  a  spoonful  or  two  more  must 
be  added.    Roll  the  paste  out  as  lightly  as  jwesible  twice  or  thrice 
if  needful,  to  blend  the  butter  thoroughly  with  it,  and  each  time 
either  fold  it  in  three  by  wrapping  the  ends  over  each  other,  or  fold 
it  over  and  over  like  a  roll  pudding.     An  additional  ounce,  or  even 
two,  of  butter  can  be  used  for  it  when  very  rich  pastiy  is  liked,  but 
the  taurte  will  not  then  retain  its  form  so  well.    Roll  it  out  evenly  to, 
something  more  than  three-quarters  of  an  inch  in  thickness,  and 
preas  the  large  cutter  firmly  tnrough  it ;  draw  away  the  superfluous 
paste,  and  lav  the  tourte  on  a  liehuy  floured  baking-tin.    Iloll  the 
lemainder  of  the  paste  until  it  is  less  thui  a  ouarter  of  an  inch  thick, 
and  stamp  out  with  the  smaller  cutter — of  wnich  the  edge  should  be 
dipped  into  hot  water,  or  slightly  encrusted  with  flour— as  many 
A>ands  as  will  form  the  border  of  the  taurte.    In  placing  them  upon 
it,  lay  the  edge  of  one  over  the  other  just  sufficiently  to  give  a  shell- 
like appearance  to  the  whole ;  and  with  the  finger  press  lightly  on 
the  opposite  part  of  the  round  to  make  it  adhere  to  the  under  paste. 
Next,  with  a  sharp -pointed  knife,  make  an  incision  very  evenly 
round  the  inside  of  the  taurte  nearly  close  to  the  border,  but  tie 
extremely  careful  not  to  cut  too  deeply  into  the  paste.     Bake  it  in  a 
gentle  oven,  from  twenty  to   thirty  minutes.     When  it  is  done, 
detach  the  crust  from  the  centre,  where  it  has  been  marked  with 
the  knife,  take  out  part  of  the  crumb,  fill  the  space  high  with 
aprioot-jam,  or  with  any  other  choice  preserve,  set  it  again  for  an 
instant  into  the  oven,  and  serve  it  hot  or  cold.    Spikes  of  blanched 
almonds,  filberts,  or  pistachio-nuts,  may  be  strewed  over  the  pre- 
aerve,  when  they  are  considered  an  improvement ;  and  the  border  of 
the  pastry  may  be  glazed  or  ornamented  to  the  fancy ;  but  if  well 
made,  it  will  genendly  please  in  its  quite  sunple  form.     It  may  be 
Donverted  into  a  delicious  entrSe^  by  filling  it  either  with  oysters, 
>r   sliced  sweetbreads,   stewed,  and   served  in  thick,  rich,  white 
laace,  pr  bSehameL    Lobster  ailso  prepared  and  moulded  as  for  the

GENOISBS  A  LA  RBINE,  OR  HER  HAJBSTY*8  PASTRY. 

Make  some  nouiUes  (see  page  5),  with  the  yolks  of  four  tnA 
eggs,  and  when  they  are  all  cut  as  directed  there,  drop  them  lightlj 
into  a  pint  and  a  half  of  boiling  cream  (new  milk  will  answer  quite 
as  well,  or  a  portion  of  each  may  be  used),  in  which  six  ounces  of 
fresh  butter  have  been  dissolved.    When  these  have  boiled  qukkly 
for  a  minute  or  two,  during  which  time  they  must  be  stirred  to  pre- 
vent their  gathering  into  lumps,  add  a  small  pinch  of  salt,  and  six 
ounces  of  sugar  on  which  the  rinds  of  two  lemons  have  been  rasped; 
place  the  saucepan  over  a  clear  and  very  gentle  fire,  and  when  the 
mixture  has  simmered  from  thirty  to  forty  minutes  take  it  ofi^  sdr 
brisklv  in  the  ^olks  of  six  eggs,  and  pour  it  out  upon  a  delicately 
clean  baking-tm  which  has  been  slightly  rubbed  in  every  part  with 
butter;    level  the  notUUes  with  a  knue  to  something  less  than  a 
quarter  of  an  inch  of  thickness,  and  let  thera  be  verv  evenly  spread; 
put  them  into  a  moderate  oven,  and  bake  them  of  a  fine  equal  brown : 
should  any  air-bladders  appear,  pierce  them  with  the  point  of  a  knife. 
On  taking  the  paste  from  tne  oven,  divide  it  into  two  equal  parts: 
turn  one  of  these,  the  unde«-*side  uppermost,  on  to  a  clean  tin  or  a 
large  dish,  and  spread  qu  ckly  over  it  ajar  of  fine  apricot-jam,  place 
the  other  half  upon  it,  the  brown  side  outwards,  and  leave  the  pasle 
to  become  cold ;  then  stamp  it  out  with  a  round  or  diamond-shaped 
cutter,  and  arrange  the  genoises  tastefully  in  a  dish.    This  pastry  wiU 
be  found  deliciout  the  day  it  is  baked,  but  its  excellence  is  destroyed 
by  keeping     Peach,  green-gage,  or  magnum  bonum  jam,  will  aeive 
for  it  quite  a8  well  as  apricot.     We  strongly  recommend  to  our 
readers  this  preparation,  baked  in  patty -pans,  and  served  hot ;  or  the 
whole  quantity  made  into  a  pudding.      From  the  smaller  ones  a 
little  may  be  taken  out  with  a  teaspoon,  and  replaced  with  some 
preserve  just  before  they  are  sent  to  table;  or  they  may  thus  he 
eaten  cold.

ALMOND   PASTE, 

For  a  single  dish  of  pastry,  blanch  seven  ounces  of  fine  Jordan 
almonds  and  one  of  bitter ;  *  throw  them  into  cold  water  as  they  are 
done,  and  let  them  remain  in  it  for  an  hour  or  two ;  then  wipe,  and 
pound  them  to  the  finest  paste,  moistening  them  occasionally  with  a 
few  drops  of  cold  water,  to  prevent  their  oiling ;  next,  add  to,  and 
mix  thoroughly  with  tiiem,  seven  ounces  of  highly-refined,  dried* 
and  rifled  sugar ;  put  them  into  a  small  preserving-pan,  or  enamelled 
8tewpan»  and  stir  them  over  a  clear  and  very  gentle  fire  until  they 
are  so  dry  as  not  to  adhere  to  the  finger  when  touched ;  turn  the 
paste  immediately  into  an  earthen  pan  or  jar,  and  when  cold  it  will 
be  ready  for  use. 

Jordan  almonds,  7  oz. ;  bitter  almonds,  1  oz. ;  cold  water,  1  table- 
spoonful  ;  sugar,  7  oz. 

Obs. — The  pan  in  which  the  paste  is  dried,  should  by  no  means  be 
placed  upon  the  fire,  but  high  above  it  on  a  bar  or  trevet :  should  it 
be  allowed  by  accident  to  harden  too  much,  it  must  be  sprinkled 
plentifully  with  water,  broken  up  quite  small,  and  worked,  as  it 
warms,  with  a  strong  wooden  spoon  to  a  smooth  paste  a^n.  We 
have  found  this  method  perfectly  successful ;  but,  if  time  will  permit, 
it  should  be  moistened  some  hours  before  it  is  again  set  over  the 
fire. 

TARTLETS  OF  ALMOND   PASTE, 

Butter  slightly  the  smallest-sized  patty-pans,  and  line  them  with 
the  almond-paste  rolled  as  thin  as  possible ;  cut  it  with  a  sharp  knife 
dose  to  their  edges,  and  bake  or  rather  dry  the  tartlets  slowly  at  the 
mouth  of  a  very  cool  oven.  If  at  all  coloured,  they  should  be  only 
of  the  palest  brown ;  but  they  will  become  perfectly  crisp  without 
losing  their  whiteness  if  left  for  some  hours  m  a  very  gently- heated 
stove  or  oven.  They  should  be  taken  from  the  pans  when  two-thirds 
done,  and  laid,  reversed,  upon  a  sheet  of  paper  placed  on  a  dish  or 
board,  before  they  are  put  back  into  the  oven.  At  the  instant  of 
serving,  fill  them  with  bright-coloured  whipped  cream,  or  with  peach 
or  apricot  jam ;  if  the  preserve  be  used,  lay  over  it  a  small  star  or 
other  ornament  cut  from  the  same  paste,  and  dried  with  the  tartlets. 
Sifted  sugar,  instead  of  flour,  must  be  dredged  upon  the  board  and 
toiler  in  usins  almond  paste.  Leaves  and  lowers  formed  of  it,  and 
diied  graduaUy  until  perfectly  crisp,  will  keep  for  a  long  time  in  a 

*  When  these  are  objected  to,  use  half  a  pound  of  the  sweet  almonds.

SUPERLATIYE   MINCEMEAT. 

Take  four  large  lemons,  with  their  weight  of  golden  pippins  pared 
and  cored,  of  jar-raisins,  currants,  candied  citron  and  orange-rind, 
and  the  finest  suet,  and  a  fourth  part  more  of  pounded  sugar.  Boil 
the  lemons  tender,  chop  them  small,  but  be  careful  first  to  extract 
all  the  pips ;  add  them  to  the  other  ingredients,  after  all  have  been 
prepared  with  great  nicety,  and  mix  the  whole  well  with  from  three 
to  tour  glasses  of  good  brandy.  Apportion  salt  and  spice  by  the 
preceding  receipt.  We  think  that  the  weight  of  one  lemon,  in  meat, 
impioves  this  mixture ;  or,  in  lieu  of  it,  a  small  quantity  of  crushed 
macaroons  added  just  before  it  is  baked. 

MINCE  PIES,      (entremets.) 

Butter  some  tin  pattypans  well,  and  line  them  evenly  with  fine 
puff  paste  rolled  thin ;  nil  them  with  mincemeat,  moisten  the  edges 
of  the  covers,  which  should  be  nearly  a  quarter  of  an  inch  thick, 
close  the  pies  carefully,  trim  off  the  superfluous  paste,  make  a  small 
aperture  in  the  centre  of  the  crust  with  a  fork  or  tne  point  of  a  knife, 
ice  the  pies  or  not,  at  pleasure,  and  bake  them  half  an  hour  in  a  well- 
beated  but  not  fierce  oven :  lay  a  paper  over  them  when  they  are 
partially  done,  ^ould  they  appear  likely  to  take  too  much  colour.

they  are  pared  and  cored ;  add  to  them  from  three  to  four  ounces  of 
pounded  sugar,  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  fresh  butter  cut  small,  and 
naif  a  teaspoonful  of  pounded  cinnamon,  or  the  lightly  grated  rind  of 
a  small  lemon.    Let  them  stand  oyer,  or  by  the  side  of  a  gentle  fire 
until  they  begin  to  soften,  and  toss  them  now  and  then  to  mingle  the 
whole  well,  but  do  not  stir  them  with  a  spoon ;  they  should  all  R- 
main  unbroken  and  rather  firm.    Turn  them  into  a  dish,  and  kC 
them  become  cold.    Divide  three-quarters  of  a  poimd  of  good  ^ht 
paste  into  two  eoual  portions ;  roll  out  one  quite  thin  and  roond,

after  it  is  made ;  place  the  apples  upon  it  in  the  form  of  a  dome,  hcl 
leave  a  clear  space  of  an  inch  or  more  round  the  edge ;  m<nsten  tfaii 
with  white  of  egg^  and  press  the  remaining  half  of  the  paste  (wUdi 
should  be  rolled  out  to  the  same  size,  and  laid  sarefully  over  tbe 
apples)  closely  upon  it :  they  should  be  well  secured,  that  the  synf 
from  the  fruit  may  not  burst  through.     Whisk  the  white  of  an  es 
to  a  froth,  brush  it  over  the  tart  with  a  paste  brush  or  a  small  bonS 
of  feathers,  sift  sugar  thickly  over,  and  then  strew  upcm  it  snae

moderate  oven  from  thirty-five  to  forty-five  minutes.     It  may  be

PUDDING  PIES,      (entremets.) 

This  form  of  pastry  (or  its  name  at  least)  is,  we  believe,  peculiar 
to  the  county  of  Kent,  where  it  is  made  in  abundance,  and  eaten  by 
all  classes  of  people  during  Lent.  Boil  for  fifteen  minutes  three 
ounces  of  ground  rice*  in  a  pint  and  a  half  of  new  milk,  and  when 
taken  from  the  fire  stir  into  it  three  ounces  of  butter  and  four  of 
sujzar ;  add  to  these  six  well-beaten  eggs,  a  grain  or  two  of  salt,  and 
a  n&YouriDg  of  nutm^  or  lemon-rind  at  pleasure.  When  the  mix- 
ture is  nearly  cold,  line  some  lar^e  pattypans  or  some  saucers  with 
thin  puff  pi^te,  fill  them  with  it  three  parts  full,  strew  the  tops 
thickly  with  currants  which  have  been  cleaned  and  dried,  and  base 
the  pudding-pdes  from  fifteen  to  twenty  minutes  in  a  gentle  oven. 

Milk,  14  pint;  ground  rice,  3  oz.:  16  minutes.  Butter,  3  oz.; 
sugar,  i  lb. ;  nutn^g  or  lemon-rind;  ^gs,  6 ;  currants,  4  to  6  os.: 
15  to  30  minutes. 


PUDDING  PIES. 

(A  commoner  kind,') 

One  quart  of  new  milk,  five  ounces  of  ground  rice,  butter,  one 
ounce  and  a  half  (or  more),  four  ounces  of  sugar,  half  a  small 
nutmeg  grated,  a  pinch  of  ssit,  four  large  eggs,  and  three  ounces  of 
currants. 

COCOA-NUT  CHEESE-CAKES.      (eNTREMETS.) 

(Jamaica  Receipt) 

Break  carefully  the  shell  of  the  nut,  that  the  liquid  it  contains 
may  not  escape.f  Take  out  the  kernel,  pare  thinly  off  the  dark 
skin,  and  grate  the  nut  on  a  delicately  clean  grater;  put  it,  with  its 
weight  of  pounded  sugar,  and  its  own  milk,  or  a  couple  of  spoonsful 
or  rather  more  of  water,  into  a  silver  or  block-tin  saucepan,  or  a  very 
small  copper  stewpan  perfectly  tinned,  and  keep  it  gently  stirred  over 
a  quite  clear  fire  until  it  is  tender:  it  will  sometimes  require  an 
hour's  stewing  to  make  it  so.  When  a  little  cooled,  add  to  the  nut, 
and  beat  well  with  it,  some  eggs  pronerly  whisked  and  strained,  and 
the  grated  rind  of  half  a  lemon.  Line  some  pattypans  with  fine 
paste,  put  in  the  mixture,  and  bake  the  cheese-cakes  from  thirteen 
to  fifteen  minutes. 

Grated  cocoa-nut,  6  oz. ;  sugar,  6  oz. ;  the  sulk  of  the  nut,  or  of

U  is  broken.    The  milk  of  the  nat  shoold  never  be  need  anlees  it  be  Mry  fresh.

COMMON    LEMON    TARTLETS. 

Beat  four  eggs  until  they  are  exceedingly  light,  add  to  tbcm 
gradually  four  ounces  of  pounded  sugar,  and  whisk  these  tc^ether 
for  five  minutes;  strew  lightly  in,  if  it  be  at  hand,  a  dessertspooofol 
of  potato  flour,  if  not,  of  common  flour  well  dried  and  sifted,*  then 
throw  into  the  mixture  by  slow  degrees,  three  ounces  of  good 
butter,  which  should  be  dissolved,  but  only  just  luke-warm:  beat 
the  whole  well,  then  stir  briskly  in,  the  strained  juice  and  the 
grated  rind  of  one  lemon  and  a  half.  Line  some  pattypans  with  fine 
puff-paste  rolled  very  thin,  fill  them  two-thirds  full,  and  bake  the 
tartlets  about  twenty  minutes,  in  a  moderate  oven.

spoonful;  butter,  3  oz. ;  juice  and  rind  of  1^^  full-sixed  lemon: 
baked  15  to  20  minutes. 

MADAME  Werner's  rosenyik  cheese-cakes. 

Blanch  and  pound  to  the  finest  possible  paste,  four  ounces  of  fine 
fresh  Jordan  almonds,  with  a  few  drops  of  lemon-juice  or  water, 
then  mix  with  them,  very  gradually  indeed,  six  fresh,  and  thoroughly 
well-whisked  eggs ;  throw  in  by  degrees  twelve  ounces  of  pounded 
sugar,  and  beat  the  mixture  without  intermission  all  the  time ;  add 
then  the  finely  grated  rinds  of  four  small,  or  of  three  large  lemons, 
and  afterwards,  by  very  slow  degrees,  the  strained  juice  of  all 
When  these  ingredients  are  perfectly  blended,  pour  to  them  in  smill 
portions,  four  ounces  of  just  liquefied  butter  (six  of  clarified  if 
exceedingly  rich  cheese-cakes  are  wished  for),  and  again  whiak  the 
mixture  lightly  for  several  minutes ;  thicken  it  over  the  fire  like 
boiled  custard,  and  either  put  it  into  small  pans  or  jars  for  storingtf 
or  fin  with  it,  one-third  full,  some  pattypans  lined  with  the  final 
paste;  place  lightly  on  it  a  layer  of  apricot,  orange,  or  Ictmd* 
marmalade,  and  on  this  pour  as  much  more  of  the  mixture,  fiike 
the  cheese-cakes  from  fifteen  to  twenty  minutes  iu  a  moderate  ove& 
They  are  very  good  loithotU  the  layer  of  preserve.

APPEL   ERAPFEN. 

•  

(^German  Receipt*) 

Boil  down  tbree-quarters  of  a  pound  of  good  apples  with  four 
ounces  of  pounded  sugar,  and  a  small  glass  of  white  wine,  or  the 
stnuBed  juice  of  a  lemon ;  when  they  are  stewed  quite  to  a  pulp, 
keep  them  stirred  until  they  are  thick  and  dry;  then  mix  them 
gradually  with  four  ounces  of  almonds,  beaten  to  a  paste,  or  very 
finely  chopped,  two  ounces  of  candied  orange  or  lemon-rind  shred 
extremely  small,  and  six  ounces  of  jar  raisins  stoned  and  quartered : 
to  these  the  Germans  add  a  rather  high  flavouring  of  cinnamon, 
which  is  a  very  favourite  spice  with  them,  but  a  grating  of  nutmeg, 
and  some  fresn  lemon-peel,  are,  we  think,  preferable  for  this  com- 
position. Mix  all  the  ingredients  well  together;  roll  out  some 
butter-crust  a  full  back-of-knife  thickness,  cut  it  into  four-inch 
squares,  brush  the  edges  to  the  depth  of  an  inch  round  with  beaten 
€f^,  fill  them  with  the  mixture,  lay  another  square  of  paste  on  each, 
press  them  very  securely  together,  make,  with  th^  point  of  a  knife,  a 
small  incision  in  the  top  of  each,  glaze  them  or  not  at  pleasure,  and 
bake  them  rather  slowly,  that  the  raisins  may  have  time  to  become 
tender.  They  are  very  good.  The  proportion  of  sugar  must  be 
r^ulated  by  the  nature  of  the  fruit ;  and  that  of  the  almonds  can  be 
diminished  when  it  is  thought  too  much.  A  delicious  tart  of  the 
kind  18  made  by  substituting  for  the  raisins  and  candied  orange-rind, 
two  heaped  tablespoonsful  of  very  fine  apricot  jauL 

CRiME   PATISSI^RE^   OR  PASTRY  CREAM. 

To  one  ounce  of  fine  flour  add,  very  gradually,  the  beaten  yolks  of 
three  fresh  eggs ;  stir  to  them  briskly,  and  in  sinall  portions  at  first, 
three-quarters  of  a  pint  of  boiling  cream,  or  of  cream  and  new  milk 
mixed ;  then  turn  the  whole  into  a  clean  stewpan,  and  stir  it  over  a 
very  gentle  fire  until  it  is  quite  thick,  take  it  ofl*,  and  stir  it  well  up 
and  round ;  replace  it  over  the  fire,  and  let  it  just  simmer  from  six 
to  eight  minutes ;  pour  it  into  a  basin,  and  add  to  it  immediately  a 
couple  of  ounces  of  pounded  sugar,  one  and  a  half  of  fresh  butter, 
cut  small,  or  clarified,  and  a  spoonful  of  the  store  mixture  of  page 
153,  or  a  little  sugar  which  has  been  rubbed  on  the  rind  of  a  lemon. 
The  cream  is  rich  enough  for  common  use  without  further  addition ; 
but  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  ratifias,  crushed  almost  to  powder  with  a 
paste-roller  improves  it  much,  and  they  should  be  mixed  with  it  for 
the  receipt  which  follows.

SOUFFLis. 

Thb  admirable  lightnen*  and  delicacy  of  a  well-made  soufflS  render 
it  generaUj  a  Tery  favourite  dish,  and  it  is  now  a  fashionable  one 
aho.  It  may  be  greatly  varied  in  its  composition,  but  in  all  cases 
must  be  served  the  very  instant  it  is  taken  from  the  oven ;  and  even 
in  peasing  to  the  dining-room  it  should,  if  possible,  be  prevented 
from  sinking  by  a  heated  iron  or  salamander  held  above  it.  A 
rommon  soum^-pen  may  be  purchased  for  four  or  five  shillings,  but 
those  of  silver  or  plated  metal,  which  are  of  the  form  shown  at  the 
eonunenoement  of  this  chapter,  are  of  course  expensive ;  the  part  in 
nriijch  the  soujffli  is  baked  is  placed  within  the  more  ornamental  dish 
irhen  it  is  drawn  firom  the  oven.    A  plain,  round,  cake-mould,  with 

*  Tliis  is  giTen  to  ereiy  description  of  i(n{ffU  in  the  flame  manner  as  to  Sayoy 
r  Kponge-cakes,  hj  mingling  gently  with  the  other  ingredients  the  whites  of 
«B  wluaked  to  a  solid  mass  or  mow  frothy — that  is  to  say,  that  no  portion  of 
Scta  moat  remain  in  a  Uqnid  state.  For  the  proper  mode  of  prepanng  them, 
ee  eommencement  of  the  chapter  of  Cakes :  «o«(/^-pudding8  are  rendered  light 
B  the  same  maimer,  and  steamed  instead  of  being  boiled. 


378  HODEBK  COOKEBT.  [chap.xii. 

a  strip  of  writing  paper  six  inches  high,  placed  inside  the  rim,  will 
answer  on  an  emergency  to  bake  a  souffle  in.  The  following  receipt 
will  serve  as  a  guide  for  the  proper  mode  of  making  it :  the  process 
is  always  the  same  whether  the  principd  ingredient  be  whole  rice 
boiled  very  tender  in  milk  and  pressed  tnrough  a  sieve,  bread-crumbs 
soaked  as  for  a  pudding  and  worked  through  a  sieve  also,  arrow-root, 
potato-flour,  or  aught  else  of  which  light  puddings  in  general  aie 
made. 

Take  from  a  pint  and  a  half  of  new  milk  or  of  cream  sufficient  to 
mix  four  ounces  of  flour  of  rice  to  a  perfectly  smooth  hatter ;  pot 
the  remainder  into  a  very  clean,  well-tinned  saucepan  or  stewpan, 
and  when  it  boils,  stir  the  rice  briskly  to  it ;  let  it  simmer,  keeping 
it  stirred  all  the  time,  for  ten  minutes,  or  more  should  it  not  be  veir 
thick ;  then  mix  well  with  it  two  ounces  of  fi^h  butter,  one  and  a 
half  of  pounded  sugar,  and  the  grated  rind  of  a  fine  lemon  (or  let 
the  sugar  which  is  used  for  it  be  well  rubbed  on  the  lemon  before  it 
is  crushed  to  powder)  ;  in  two  or  three  minutes  take  it  from  the  fire, 
and  beat  ouickly  and  carefully  to  it  by  degrees  the  yolks  of  six 
eggs ;  whisk  the  whites  to  a  very  firm  solid  froth,  and  when  the  pta 
is  buttered,  and  all  else  quite  ready  for  the  oven,  stir  them  gently  to 
the  other  ingredients ;  pour  the  souffli  immediately  into  the  pan  and 
place  it  in  a  moderate  oven,  of  which  keep  the  door  closed  for  a 
quarter  of  an  hour  at  least.    When  the  wuffle  has  risen  very  h^ 
is  of  a  fine  colour,  and  quite  done  in  the  centre,  which  it  will  be 
in  from  half  to  three-quarters  of  an  hour,  send  it  instantly  to  table. 
The  exact  time  for  baking  it  depends  so  much  on  the  oven  that  it 
cannot  be  precisely  specified.     We  have  known  quite  a  small  one  not 
too  much  baked  in  forty-five  minutes  in  an  trtm  oven ;  but  generaily 
less  time  will  suffice  for  them :  the  heat,  however,  should  always  be 
moderate. 

New  milk  or  cream,  1^  pint ;  flour  of  rice,  4  oz. ;  fresh  butter, 
2  oz. ;  pounded  sugar,  I^  oz. ;  eggs,  6 ;  grain  of  salt ;  xind,  1  lemoii: 
do  to  45  minutes. 

Ohs.  I. — The  soujfie  may  be  flavoured  with  vanilla,  onmge-floweis, 
or  aught  else  that  is  liked.  Chocolate  and  cofiee  also  may  be  used 
for  it  with  soaked  bread :  a  very  strong  infusion  of  the  last,  and  aa 
ounce  or  two  of  the  other,  melted  with  a  little  water,  are  to  be  added 
to  the  milk  and  bread. 

Obs,  2. — A  Bouffle  is  commonly  served  in  a  dinner  of  cerenMmy  as 
a  remove  of  the  second-course  roast ;  but  a  good  plan  for  this,  ai 
for  a  foridtL,  is  to  have  it  quickly  handed  round,  instead  of 
placed  upon  the  table. 

LOUISE   franks'  citron  SOUFFLi. 

To  obtain  the  flavour  of  the  citron-rind  for  this  celebrated 
•ouffllt  take  a  lump  of  sugar  which  weighs  two  ounces  and  a  faal( 
and  rub  it  on  the  fruit  to  extract  the  essence,  or  should  the  citrao 


CEA?.  XIX.]  SOUFFLte,  OHLETS,  &C.  379 

not  be  sufficiently  fresh  to  yield  it  by  this  means,  pare  it  off  in  the 
tbjonest  possible  strips  and  infuse  it  by  the  side  of  the  fire  in  the 
cream  of  which  the  souffle  is  to  be  made.  Should  the  first  method 
be  pursued,  crush  the  sugar  to  powder  and  dry  it  a  little  before  it  is 
added  to  the  other  ingredients.  Blend  yeiry  smoothly  two  ounces 
of  potato-flour  with  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  milk,  and  pour  boiling  to 
them  a  pint  of  good  cream ;  stir  the  mixture  in  a  large  basin  or  bowl 
until  it  thickens,  then  throw  in  a  grain  of  salt,  two  ounces  of  firesh 
butterjust  dissolved  in  a  small  saucepan,  and  the  sugar  which  has 
been  rubbed  on  the  citron ;  or  should  the  rind  have  l^n  pared,  the 
aame  weight  some  of  which  is  merely  pounded.  Add  next,  by 
degrees,  Uie  thoroughly  whisked  yolks  or  six  fresh  eggs,  or  seven 
should  they  be  very  small.  Beat  the  whites  lightly  and  quickly 
until  they  are  sufficiently  firm  to  remain  standing  in  points  when 
dropped  from  the  whisk;  mix  them  with  the  other  ingredients  at 
the  mouth  of  the  oven,  but  without  beating  them ;  fill  the  souffle^ 
pan  less  than  half  full ;  set  it  instantly  into  the  oven,  which  should 
be  gentle,  but  not  exceedingly  slow,  dose  the  door  immediately,  and 
do  not  open  it  for  fifteen  or  twenty  minutes:  in  from  thirty  to 
forty  the  souffle  will  be  ready  for  table  unless  the  oven  should  be 
very  cool :  a  fierce  degree  oi  heat  will  have  a  most  unfavourable 
effect  upon  it 

Bind  of  half  citron  (that  of  a  Seville  orange  may  be  substituted 
an  occasions);  sugar,  2^  oz. ;  cream,  1  pint;  potato-flour,  2  oz.; 
milk,  ^  pint ;  butter,  2  oz. ;  yolks  and  white  of  6  large  or  of  7  small 
eggs :  30  to  40  minutes,  or  more  in  very  slow  oven. 

Obs. — The  fresh  citron  would  appear  to  be  brought  as  yet  but 
very  sparingly  into  the  English  market,  though  it  may  sometimes 
be  procured  of  first-rate  fruiterers.  NoUiinff  can  well  be  finer  than 
fts  hishly  aromatic  flavour,  which  is  infimtely  superior  to  that  of 
any  otner  fruit  of  its  species  that  we  have  ever  tasted.  We  have 
had  delicious  preparations  made  too  from  the  young  green  citron 
when  extremely  small,  of  which  we  may  have  occasion  to  speak 
elsewhere. 

A   FONDVy   OR   CHEESE   SOUFFLi. 

Mix  to  a  smooth  batter,  with  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  new  milk,  two 
oanees  of  potato-flour,  arrow-root,  or  tous  les  mots ;  pour  boiling  to 
tliem  three-quarters  of  a  pint  more  of  milk,  or  of  cream  in  pre- 
(erance :  stir  them  well  together,  and  then  throw  in  two  ounces  of 
tetter  cat  smalL  When  this  is  melted,  and  well-beaten  into  the 
nixCnie,  add  the  well- whisked  yolks  of  four  lai>ge  or  of  five  small 
iggs,  half  a  tcaspoonful  of  salt,  something  less  of  cayenne,  and  three 
mnoea  of  lightly-grated  cheese,  Parmesan  or  English,  or  equal  parts 
if  both.  Whisk  the  whites  of  the  eggs  to  a  quite  firm  and  solid 
roth ;  then  proceed,  as  for  a  souffle^  to  mix  and  hake  thtfondu,

OBSERVATIONS   OX  OMLETS^   FRITTERS,   &C. 

The  composition  and  nature  of  a  souffli^  as  we  have  shown,  ue 
altogether  different,  but  there  is  no  difficulty  in  making  good  oxnlets, 
pancakes,  or  fritters ;  and  as  they  may  be  expeditiously  prepared  and 
served,  they  are  oftien  a  very  convenient  resource  when,  on  short 
notice,  an  addition  is  required  to  a  dinner.    The  eggs  for  all  of  them 
should  be  well  and  lightly  whisked;  the  lard  for  frying  batter 
should  be  extremely  pure  in  flavour,  and  quite  hot  when  the  iritten 
are  dropped  in ;  the  batter  itself  should  be  smooth  as  cream,  and  it 
should  be  briskly  beaten  the  instant  before  it  is  used.    All  fried 
pastes  should  be  perfectly  drained  from  the  fat  before  they  are 
served,  and  sent  to  table  promptly  when  they  are  ready.    Eggs 
may  be  dressed  in  a  multiplicity  of  wa3's,  but  are  seldom  more 
relished  in  any  form  than  in  a  well-made  and  expeditiously  sen-ed 
omlet.    This  may  be  plain,  or  seasoned  with  minced  herbs  and  a 
very  little  eschalot,  wnen  the  last  is  liked,  and  is  then  casUed  ao 
*'  Omlette  aux  fines  kerbes ; "  or  it  may  be  mixed  with  minced  ham, 
or  grated  cheese ;  in  any  case,  it  should  be  light,  thick,  full-tasted, 
And  fried  only  on  one  Me ;  if  turned  in  the  pan,  as  it  frequently  is  in 
England,  it  will  at  once  be  flattened  and  rendered  tough.     Should 
the  slight  rawness  which  is  sometimes  found  in  the  middle  of  the 
inside,  when  the  omlet  is  made  in  the  French  way,  be  objected  to,  a 
heated  shovel,  or  a  salamander,  mav  be  held  over  it  for  an  instaot, 
before  it  is  folded  on  the  dish.    The  pan  for  frying  it  should  be 
quite  small ;  for  if  it  be  composed  of  four  or  five  eggs  only,  and  then 
put  into  a  large  one,  it  will  necessarily  spread  over  it  and  be  thio, 
which  would  render  it  more  like  a  pancake  than  an  omlet ;  the  only 
partial  remedy  for  this,  when  a  pan  of  proper  size  cannot  be  had,  is 
to  raise  the  handle  of  it  high,  and  to  keep  the  opposite  side  dose 
down  to  the  fire,  which  will  confine  the  eggs  into  a  smaller  space. 
No  gravy  should  be  poured  into  the  dish  with  it,  and  indeol,  if 
properly  made,  it  will  require  none.    Lard  is  preferable  to  butter  for 
frymg  batter,  as  it  renders  it  lighter ;  but  it  must  not  be  used  iiar 
omlets. 

A   COMMON  OMLET. 

Six  egffs  are  sufficient  for  an  omlet  of  moderate  sise.  Let  them  be 
very  fresh ;  break  them  singly  and  carefully ;  clear  them  in  the  way 
we  have  already  pointed  out  in  the  introduction  to  boiled  puddings, 
or  when  they  are  sufficiently  whisked  pour  them  through  a  aeve, 
and  resume  the  beating  until  they  are  very  light.  Add  to  them 
from  half  to  a  whole  teaspoonful  of  salt,  and  a  seasoning  of  pepper. 
Dissolve  in  a  small  fr3ring-pan  a  couple  of  ounces  of  butter,  pour  m 
the  eggs,  and  as  soon  as  the  omlet  is  well  risen  and  firm  throughout, 
slide  it  on  to  a  hot  dish,  fold  it  together  like  a  turnover,  and  sonre  it 
immediaiely ;  from  five  to  seven  mmutes  will  fry  it

AN  OMLETTE  SOUFPl£e.      SECOND   COURSE   REMOVE   OF   ROAST.* 

Separate,  as  they  are  hroken,  the  whites  from  the  yolks  of  six  fine 
fresh  egs^ ;  heat  these  last  thoroughly,  first  by  themselves  and  then 
with  four  tablespoonsful  of  dry,  white  sifted  snsar,  and  the  rind  of 
half  a  lemon  grated  on  a  fine  grater.  Whisk  tne  whites  to  a  solid 
froth,  and  jtist  before  the  omlet  is  poured  into  the  pan,  mix  them 
wdl,  but  lightly,  with  the  yolks,  rut  four  ounces  of  fresh  butter 
into  a  very  small  delicately  clean  omlet  or  frying  pan,  and  as  soon  as 
it  is  all  dusolved,  add  the  eggs  and  stir  them  round  that  they  may 
absorb  it  entirely.  When  the  under  side  is  just  set,  turn  the  omlet 
into  a  well-buttered  dish,  and  send  it  to  a  tolerably  brisk  oven. 
From  five  to  ten  minutes  will  bake  it;  and  it  must  be  served  the 
mtOMt  it  is  taken  out ;  carried,  indeed,  as  quickly  as  possible  to  table 
f^om  the  oven!  It  will  have  risen  to  a  great  height,  but  will  sink 
and  become  heavy  in  a  very  short  space  of  time :  if  sugar  be  sifled 
over  it,  let  it  be  done  with  the  utmost  expedition. 

Eggs,  6;  sugar,  4  tablespoonsful;  rind,  ^  lemon;  butter,  4  oz.: 
omlet  baked,  5  to  10  minutes. 

0^. — ^This  omlette  may  be  served  on  a  layer  of  apricot-marmalade, 
which  mnst  be  spread  over  the  dish  in  which  it  is  to  be  baked,  and 

sent  to  table  before  the  omlette  is  turned  into  it.

PLAIN   COMMON   FRITTERS. 

Ifix  with  three  well-whisked  eggs  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  milk,  and 
itrain  them  through  a  fine  sieve ;  add  tnem  gradually  to  three  large 
tablespoonsful  of  flour,  and  thin  the  batter  with  as  much  more  milk 
as  will  bring  it  to  the  consistence  of  cream ;  beat  it  up  thoroughly  at 
the  moment  of  using  it,  that  the  fritters  may  be  fight.  Drop  it 
in  small  portions  from  a  spouted  jug  or  basin  into  boiling  lard ; 
when  lightly  coloured  on  one  side,  turn  the  fritters,  drain  them  well 
from  the  lard  as  they  are  lifted  out,  and  serve  them  very  quickly. 
They  are  eaten  generally  with  fine  sugar,  and  orange  or  lemon  juice  : 
the  first  of  these  may  be  sifted  quickly  over  them  after  they  are 
dished,  and  the  oranges  or  lemons  halved  or  quartered,  and  sent  to 
table  with  theuL  ^  The  lard  used  for  frying  them  should  be  fresh  and 
pore-flavoared :  it  renders  them  more  crisp  and  light  than  butter, 
md  IB,  therefore,  better  suited  to  the  purpose.  These  fritters  may  be 
igreeably  yaried  by  mingling  with  the  batter  just  before  it  is  used, 
two  or  three  ounces  of  well  deaned  and  well  dried  currants,  or  three

PANCAKES. 

These  may  be  made  with  the  same  batter  as  fritters,  if  it  be  suffi- 
ciently thinned  with  an  additional  egg  or  two,  or  a  little  milk  or 
cream,  to  spread  quickly  over  tbe  pan  :  to  fry  them  well,  this  ought 
to  be  small.  When  the  batter  is  ready,  heat  the  pan  over  a  dear 
fire  and  rub  it  with  butter  in  every  part,  then  pour  in  sufficient 
batter  to  spread  over  it  entirely,  and  let  the  pancake  be  very  thin: 
in  this  case  it  will  require  no  turning,  but  otherwise  it  must  be 
tossed  over  with  a  sudden  jerk  of  the  pan,  in  which  the  cook  who  is 
not  somewhat  expert  will  not  always  succeed;  therefore  the  safer 
plan  is  to  make  them  so  thin  that  they  will  not  require  this.  Keep 
them  hot  before  the  fire  or  in  the  stove-oven  until  a  snfficicDt 
number  are  ready  to  send  to  table,  then  proceed  with  a  second 
supply,  as  they  should  always  be  quickly  served.  Bither  pile  them 
one  on  the  other  with  sugar  strewed  between,  or  spread  quickly  over 
them,  as  they  are  done,  some  apricot  or  other  good  preserve,  and  roll 
them  up :  in  the  latter  case,  they  may  be  neatly  divided  and  dished 
in  a  circle.  Clotted  cream  is  sometimes  sent  to  table  with  them.  A 
richer  kind  of  pancake  may  be  made  with  a  pint  of  cream,  or  of 
cream  and  new  milk  mixed,  five  eg^  or  their  yolks  only,  a  couple  of 
ounces  of  flour,  a  little  pounded  cmnamon  or  lemon-nnd  rasped  on 
sugar  and  scraped  into  them,  with  two  ounces  more  of  pounded 
sugar,  and  two  ounces  of  clarified  butter :  a  few  ratifias  rolled  to 
powder  may  be  added  at  pleasure,  or  three  or  four  macaroons. 

From  4  to  5  minutes. 

FBITTER8  OF  CAKZ  AlfP  PUDDING. 

Cut  plain  pound,  or  rice  cake,  or  rich  seed  cake,  into  small  square 
slices  half  an  inch  thick ;  trim  away  the  crust,  fry  them  slowly  a 
light  brown  in  a  small  quantity  of  fresh  butter,  and  spread  over 


they  are  fried :  they 

then  be  slightly  floured.  Cold  plum-pudding  sliced  down  as  thick 
as  the  cake,  and  divided  into  portions  of  equal  size  and  good  formi 
then  dipped  into  French  or  En^li^  batter  and  gently  fried,  will  also 
make  an  agreeable  variety  of  fntter.  Orange  marmalade  and  Devon-

MUfCEMEAT  FRITTERS. 

With  half  a  pound  of  mincemeat  mix  two  ounces  of  fine  bread- 
erombs  (or  a  tablespoonful  of  flour),  two  eggs  well  beaten,  and  the 
stramed  ^uice  of  half  a  small  lemon.  Mix  these  well,  and  drop  the 
fritters  with  a  dessertspoon  into  plenty  of  yeiy  pure  lard  or  fresh 
butter;  firy  them  from  seven  to  eight  minutes,  drain  them  on  a 
napkin  or  on  white  blotting  paper,  and  send  them  very  hot  to  table : 
they  should  be  quite  small. 

Mincemeat,  ( lb. ;  bread-crumbs,  2  oz.  (or  flour,  1  tablespoonful) ; 
eggs,  2 ;  juice  of  i  lemon :  7  to  8  minutes. 

VENETIAN   FRITTERS. 

(Very  good.) 

Wash  and  drain  three  ounces  of  whole  rice,  put  it  into  a  full  pint 
of  cold  milk,  and  bring  it  vei^  slowly  to  boil ;  stir  it  often,  and  let  it 
simmer  gently  until  it  is  quite  thick  and  dry.  When  about  three 
narts  done,  add  to  it  two  ounces  of  pounded  sugar,  and  one  of  fresh 
outter,  a  grain  of  salt,  and  the  grated  rind  of  half  a  small  lemon. 
Let  it  cool  in  the  saucepan,  and  when  only  iust  warm,  mix  with  it 
thoroughly  three  ounces  of  currants,  four  oi^  apples  chopped  fine,  a 
teaspoonfiu  of  flour,  and  three  large  or  four  small  well-beaten  eggs. 
Drop  the  mixture  in  small  fritters,  fry  them  in  butter  from  five  to 
seven  minutes,  and  let  them  become  ouite  firm  on  one  side  before 
they  are  turned :  do  this  with  a  slice.  Drain  them  as  they  are  taken 
up,  and  sift  white  sugar  over  them  after  they  are  dished. 

Whole  rice,  3  oz.;  milk,  1  pint;  sugar,  2  oz. ;  butter,  1  oz. ; 
grated  rind  of  i  lemon ;  currants,  3  oz. ;  minced  apples,  4  oz. ;  flour, 
1  teaspoonful;  a  little  salt;  eggs,  3  large,  or  4  small:  6  to  7 
minutes. 

RHUBARB  FRITTERS* 

The  rhubarb  for  these  shoidd  be  of  a  good  sort,  quickly  grown, 
and  tender.  Fare,  cut  it  into  equal  len^hs,  and  throw  it  into  the 
French  batter  of  page  130 ;  with  a  fork  lift  the  stalks  separately,  and 
put  them  into  a  pan  of  boiling  lard  or  butter :  in  from  five  to  six 
minutes  they  will  be  done.  Drain  them  well  and  dish  them  on  a 
napkin,  or  pile  them  high  without  one,  and  strew  silted  sugar 
plentifully  over  them :  they  should  be  of  a  very  light  brown,  and 
quite  dry  and  crisp.  The  young  stalks  look  well  when  left  the 
length  of  the  dish  in  which  they  are  served,  and  only  slightly 
encrusted  with  the  batteri  through  which  they  should  be  merely 
drawn.
