FINER  CROQUETTES  OF   RICE.      (eNTREMETS.) 

Swell  the  rice  in  thin  cream,  or  in  new  milk  strongly  flavoured 
with  vanilla  or  cocoa-nut ;  add  the  same  ingredients  as  in  the  fore- 
going receipt,  and  when  the  rice  is  cold,  form  it  into  balls,  and  with 
the  tnumb  of  the  right  hand  hollow  them  sufficiently  to  admit  in  the 
centre  a  small  portion  of  peach  jam,  or  of  apricot  marmalade;  dose 
the  rice  well  over  it ;  egg,  crumb,  and  fry  the  croquettes  as  usual. 
Ab,  from  the  difierence  of  quality,  the  same  proportions  of  rice  and 
milk  will  not  always  produce  the  same  effect,  the  cook  must  use  her 
discretion  in  adding,  should  it  be  needed,  sufficient  liquid  to  soften 
the  rice  perfectly  :  but  she  must  bear  in  mind  that  if  not  boiled  ex- 
tremely thick  and  dry,  it  wUl  be  difficult  to  make  it  into  cro- 
quettes.* 

SAVOURY    CROQUETTES   OF   RICE.      (eNTr£e.) 

These  are  made  with  the  same  preparation  as  the  casserole  of  rice 
of  Chapter  XVIII,,  but  it  must  be  boiled  very  dry,  and  left  to 
become  quite  cold  before  it  is  used.  A  few  spoonsful  of  rich  white 
sauce  stirred  into  it  when  it  is  nearly  tender,  will  improve  it  much. 
Form  and  hollow  the  croquettes  as  directed  in  the  last  receipt ;  fill 
them  with  a  small  portion  of  minced  fowl,  partrid^  or  pheasant  in 
a  thick  sauce,  or  with  a  stewed  oyster  or  two  cut  in  quarters ;  dose 
the  rice  perfectly  over  them ;  egg,  and  crumb  the  croquettes,  fry  and 
serve  them  garnished  with  crisped  parsley.  French  cooks  mix 
sometimes  a  little  grated  Parmesan  cheese  with  the  rice  at  the  moment 
it  is  taken  from  the  fire,  and  roll  the  croquettes  in  more  after  they 
are  eaged ;  they  press  this  on  and  dip  them  again  in  ^g,  and  then 
into  the  crumbs.     Raise  the  pan  high  above  the  fire  when  the  cro*

RISSOLES.      (ENTniE.) 

This  is  the  French  name  for  small  fried  pastry  of  various  forms, 
filled  with  meat  or  fish  previously  cooked ;  they  may  be  made  with 
brioche,  or  with  light  puff- paste,  either  of  which  must  be  rolled  ex- 
tremely thin.  Cut  it  with  a  small  round  cutter  fluted  or  plain ;  put 
a  little  rich  mince,  or  sood  pounded  meat,  in  the  centre,  and  moisten 
the  edges,  and  press  them  securely  together  that  they  may  not  burst 
open  in  the  frying.  The  rissoles  may  be  formed  like  small  patties, 
by  laying  a  second  round  of  paste  over  the  meat,  or  like  caTineloru ; 
they  may,  likewise,  be  brushed  with  egg,  and  sprinkled  with  ver- 
micelli, broken  small,  or  with  fine  crumbs.  They  are  sometimes 
made  in  the  form  of  croquettes^  the  paste  being  gathered  round  the 
meat,  which  must  form  a  ball.* 

In  frying  them,  adopt  the  same  plan  as  for  the  croquettes,  raising 
the  pan  as  soon  as  the  paste  is  lightly  coloured,  ^rve  all  these 
fried  dishes  well  drained,  and  on  a  napkin. 

From  5  to  7  minutes,  or  less. 

YERT   SAVOURY   ENGLISH   RISSOLES.      (eNTr£e.) 

Make  the  forcemeat  No.  1,  Chapter  VlLL,  sufficiently  firm  with 
unbeaten  yolk  of  egg,  to  roll  rather  thin  on  a  well-floured  board ; 
cut  it  into  very  small  rounds,  put  a  little  pounded  chicken  in  the 
centre  of  one  half,  moistening  the  edges  with  water,  or  white  of  egg, 
lay  the  remaining  rounds  over  these,  close  them  securely,  and  fry 
them  in  butter  a  fine  light  brown ;  drain  and  dry  them  well,  and 
heap  them  in  the  middle  of  a  hot  dish,  upon  a  napkin  folded  fiat : 
these  rissoles  may  be  egged  and  crumbed  before  they  are  fried. 

SMALL  FRIED  BREAD  PATTIES,   OR  OROUSTADES   OF   VARIOUS 

KINDS. 

These  may  be  either  sweet  or  savoury,  and  many  of  them  may  be 
so  promptly  prepared,  that  they  ofier  a  ready  resource  when  an 
extra  disn  is  unexpectedly  required.  They  should  be  carefully  fried 
very  crisp,  and  of  a  fine  equal  gold  colour,  either  in  clarified  marrow, 
for  whicA  we  give  our  own  receipt,  or  in  really  good  butter* 

DRESDEN   PATTIES,   OR   CROUSTADES. 

(F«ry  delicate,) 

Pare  the  crust  neatly  from  one  or  two  French  rolls,  slice  off  the 
ends,  and  divide  the  remainder  into  as  many  patties  as  the  size  of  the 
rolls  will  allow ;  hollow  them  in  the  centre,  dip  them  into  milk  or

thin  cream,  and  lay  them  on  a  drainer  over  a  dish ;  pour  a  spoonfo] 
or  two  more  of  milk  over  them  at  intervals,  but  not  sufficient  tc 
cause  them  to  break ;  brush  them  with  egg,  rasp  the  crust  of  the

warm  apricot  marmalade,  cherry -jam,  or  other  good  preserve. 

This  receipt  came  to  us  direct  from  DreMen^  and  on  testing

TO   PREPARE   BEEF   MARROW   FOR   FRYING  CROUSTADES, 

SAVOURY    TOASTS,   &C 

At  a  season  when  butter  of  pure  flavour  is  often  procured  with 
difiiculty,  beef-marrow,  carefully  clarified,  is  a  valuable  substitute 
for  it ;  and,  as  it  is  abundantly  contained  in  the  joints  which  are  m 
constant  request  for  soup-making,  it  is  of  slight  comparative  co^ 
in  a  well  managed  kitchen.  It  is  oflen  thrown  into  the  stock- 
pot  by  careless  or  indolent  cooks,  instead  of  being  rendered  available 
for  the  many  purposes  .to  which  it  is  admirably  adapted.  Take  it 
from  the  bones  as  fresh  as  possible,  put  it  into  a  white  jar,  and  melt 
it  with  a  very  gentle  degree  of  heat  at  the  mouth  of  the  oven,  or  by 
the  side  of  the  stove,  taking  all  precaution  to  prevent  its  being 
smoked  or  discoloured;  strain  it  off,  through  a  very  fine  sieve  or 
muslin,  into  a  clean  pan  or  pans,  and  set  it  aside  for  use.  It  will  be 
entirely  flavourless  ir  prepared  with  due  care  and  attention ;  but,  if 
dissolved  with  too  great  a  degree  of  heat,  it  will  acquire  the  taste 
almost  of  dripping.  A  small  quantity  of  fine  salt  may  be  sprinkled 
into  the  pan  with  it  when  it  is  used  for  frying. 

SMALL   CROUSTADBS,   OR   BREAD   PATTIES^   DRESSED  IN    MARROW. 

{Authoir*s  Receipt.) 

Cut  very  evenly,  from  a  firm  stale  loaf,  slices  nearly  an  inch  and 
a  half  thick,  and  with  a  plain  or  fluted  paste-cutter  of  between  two 
and  three  inches  wide  press  out  the  number  of  patties  required, 
loosening  them  gently  from  the  tin,  to  prevent  their  breaking ;  then, 
with  a  plain  cutter,  scarcely  more  than  half  the  size,  mark  out  the 
space  which  is  afterwards  to  be  hollowed  from  it.  Melt  some  clari- 
fied beef-marrow  in  a  small  saucepan  or  frying-pan,  and,  when  it 
begins  to  boil,  put  in  the  patties,  and  fry  them  gently  until  they  are 
equally  coloured  of  a  pale  golden  brown.  In  l^ng  them  from  the 
pan,  let  the  marrow  (or  butter)  drain  well  from  them ;  take  out  the 
rounds  which  have  been  marked  on  the  tops,  and  scoop  out  part  of 
the  inside  crumb,  but  leave  them  thick  enough  to  contain  securely 
the  gravy  of  the  preparation  put  into  them.  Fill  them  with  any 
good  patty-meat,  and  serve  them  very  hot  on  a  napkin. 


3HAP.XXX.]  SOUFFL^S^  OHLETS,  &C.  389 

Ohs. — These  croiutades  are  equally  good  if  dipped  into  cla-ified 
batter  or  marrow,  and  baked  in  a  tolerably  quick  oven.  It  is  well, 
in  either  case,  to  place  them  on  a  warm  sheet  of  double  white  blot- 
ting-paper while  they  are  being  filled,  as  it  will  absorb  the  super- 
fluous ttiX.  A  rich  mince,  with  a  thick,  well-adhering  sauce,  either 
of  mutton  and  mushrooms,  or  oysters,  or  with  fine  herbs  and  an 
eschalot  or  two ;  or  of  venison,  or  hare,  or  partridges,  may  be  ap- 
propriately used  for  them. 

SMALL   CROUSTADES   A   LA   BONNE   MAMAN. 

{The  Grandmama^s  PaitieiJ) 

Prepare  the  croustades  as  above,  or  use  for  them  French  rolls  of 
very  even  shape,  cut  in  thick  equal  slices.  If  quite  round,  the  crust 
may  be  left  on  ;  mark  each  slice  with  a  small  cutter  in  the  centie, 
dip  the  croustades  into  butter  or  marrow,  fry  them  lightly,  or  bake 
them  without  permitting  them  to  become  very  hard ;  empty,  and 
then  fill  them ;  dish  them  without  a  napkin,  and  pour  some  good 
brown  gravy  round,  but  not  over  them. 

Obs. — From  being  cooked  without  butter,  these  and  the  preceding 
patties  are  adapted  to  a  Jewish  table. 

CURRIED   TOASTS   WITH   ANCHOYIES. 

Fry  lightly,  in  good  butter,  clarified  marrow,  or  very  pure  olive 
oD,  some  slices  of  bread,  free  from  crust,  of  about  half  an  inch  thick, 
and  two  inches  and  a  half  square ;  lift  them  on  to  a  dish,  and  spread 
a  not  very  thick  layer  of  Captain  White's  currie-paste  on  the  top ; 
place  them  in  a  gentle  oven  for  three  or  four  minutes,  then  lay  two 
or  three  fillets  oi  anchovies  on  each,  replace  them  in  the  oven  for  a 
couple  of  minutes,  and  send  them  immediately  to  table.  Their  pun- 
gency may  be  heightened  by  the  addition  of  cayenne  pepper,  when  a 
veiT  hot  preparation  is  liked. 

Obs. — We  have  spoken  but  slightly  in  our  chapter  of  curries  of 
Captain  White's  currie-paste,  though  for  many  years  we  have  had  it 
used  in  preference  to  any  other,  and  always  found  it  excellent  Lat- 
terly, however,  it  has  been  obtained  with  rather  less  facility  than 
when  attention  was  first  attracted  to  it.  The  last  which  we  procured 
directed,  on  the  label  of  the  jar,  that  orders  for  it  should  be  sent  per 
post  to  83,  Copenhagen  Street,  Islington.  It  may,  however,  be 
procured  without  doubt  from  any  good  purveyor  of  sauces  and  other 
condiments.  It  is  sold  in  jars  of  all  sizes,  the  price  of  the  smallest 
being  one-and-sixpence.  We  certainly  think  it  much  superior  to 
any  of  the  others  which  we  have  tested,  its  flavour  being  peculiarly 
agreeable. 

TO   FILLET   ANCHOVIES, 

Drain  them  well  from  the  pickle,  take  off  the  heads  and  fins,  lay 
'.hem  separately  on  a  plate,  and  scrape  off  the  skin  entirely ;  then

Slace  them  on  a  clean  dish  and  with  a  sharp^ged  knife  raise  the 
esh  on  either  side  of  the  back-bone,  passing  it  from  the  tail  to  the 
shoulders,  and  keeping  it  nearly  flat  as  it  is  worked  along.  Divide 
each  side  (or  fillet)  in  two,  and  use  them  as  directed  for  the  preceding 
toasts  or  other  purposes.  They  make  excellent  simple  suidwicbei 
with  slices  of  bread  and  butter  only ;  but  very  superior  onea  wiwii 
they  are  potted  or  made  into  anchovy  butter. 

SAVOURY  TOASTS. 

Cut  some  slices  of  bread  free  from  crust,  about  half  an  inch  thick, 
and  two  inches  and  a  half  square ;  butter  the  tops  thickly,  spread  t 
little  mustard  on  them,  and  then  cover  them  with  a  deep  layer  of 
grated  cheese  and  of  ham  seasoned  rather  highly  with  cayenne ;  fir 
them  in  good  butter,  but  do  not  turn  them  in  the  pan ;  lift  them  oat, 
and  place  them  in  a  Dutch  oven  for  three  or  minutes  to  dissolve  the 
cheese :  serve  them  very  hot. 

To  4  tablespoonsful  of  grated  English  cheese,  an  equal  portioa  of 
very  finely  minced,  or  grated  ham ;  but  of  Parmesan,  or  Gruy^  6 
tablespoonsful.    Seasoning  of  mustard  and  cayenne. 

Obs. — These  toasts,  for  which  we  give  the  original  receipt  na- 
altered,  may  be  served  in  the  cheese-course  of  a  dinner.  Sucn  mcie 
**  relMee  "  as  they  are  called,  do  not  seem  to  us  to  demand  much  of 
our  space,  or  many  of  them  which  are  very  easy  of  preparation  might 
be  inserted  here :  a  good  cook,  however,  will  easily  supply  them  it 
slight  expense.  Truffles  minced,  seasoned,  and  stewed  tender  in 
butter  with  an  eschalot  or  two,  may  be  served  on  fried  toasts  or 
croutons  and  will  generally  be  liked. 

TO  CHOOSE  MACX;AR0NI   and   other   ITALIAN  PASTES. 

The  Naples  maccaroni,  of  which  the  i>ipes  are  large,  and  somewhat 
thin,  should  be  selected  for  the  table  in  preference  to  the  Geno^ 
which  is  less  in  size,  but  more  substantial,  and  better  suited  to  the 
formation  of  the  various  fanciful  timbales*  for  which  it  ia  usoally 
chosen.  We  have  inserted  here  no  receipts  for  these,  because  ante 
very  skilfully  prepared  they  are  sure  to  fail,  and  they  are  not  m 
much  request  m  this  country,  unless  it  be  at  the  tables  of  the 
aristocracy,  for  which  they  are  prepared  by  efficient  cooks.  Of  tfat 
ribbon  maccaroni  (or  lazcmges)  we  have  given  particulara  in  the 
pages  which  follow.  The  macaroncini^  though  not  much  larger  tbaa 
a  straw,  requires  much  boiling  for  its  size,  to  render  it  sott.  Ths 
celery-maccaroni  is  made  very  large  and  of  an  ornamental  form,  but 
in  short  lengths.  It  is  used  by  '*  professed**  cooks  as  a  sort  of  cntst 
or  case  for  ^v^n^Se-forcemeat,  or  other  expensive  preparatioiis  of  the

same  nature.  The  ling  or  cut  maccaroni  is  another  foim  giyen  to 
the  Italian  paste :  it  may  be  had  at  ahnost  any  good  foreign  ware-

All  ^ese  pastes  should  he  of  a  yellowish  tint  (by  no  means  white 
88  one  sees  them  when  they  are  of  inferior  quality) ;  they  should 
also  be  quite  fresh,  as  they  contract  a  most  unpleasant  flavour  from 
being  too  long  stored.  The  Naples  vermicelli,  which  is  much  larger 
than  any  other,  may  be  dressed  like  maccaroni :  by  many  persons  it 
is  also  preferred  to  the  smaller  varieties  for  serving  in  soup. 

TO   BOIL   MACCARONI. 

We  have  always  found  the  continental  mode  of  dressing  maccaroni 
the  best.  English  cooks  sometimes  soak  it  in  milk  and  water  for  an 
hour  or  more,  before  it  is  boiled,  that  the  pipes  may  be  swollen  to 
the  utmost,  but  this  is  apt  to  render  it  pulpy,  though  its  appearance 
may  be  improved  by  it.  Drop  it  lightly,  and  by  degrees,  into  a  large 
pan  of  fast-boiling  water,  into  which  a  little  salt,  and  a  bit  of  butter 
the  sue  of  a  walnut,  have  previously  been  thrown,  and  of  which  the 
toiling  should  not  be  stopped  by  the  addition  of  the  maccaroni.  In 
about  three-quarters  of  an  hour  the  Naples  oAaccaroni  will  be  suffi- 
ciently tender :  every  kind  should  always  be  perfectly  cooked,  for 
otherwise  it  will  prove  very  indigestible,  but  the  pipes  of  that 
commonly  served  snould  remain  entire.  Pour  it  into  a  large  cul- 
lender, and  drain  the  water  well  from  it.  It  should  be  very  softly 
boiled  after  the  first  minute  or  two. 

Time  ofhoUing: — Naples  maccaroni,  about  f  hour;  Genoa,  nearly 
•r  quite  1  hour ;  maearoncini,  20  to  25  minutes ;  cut  maccaroni,  10 
Biinutes ;  Naples  vermicelli  (in  water),  about  20  minutes ;  longer  in 
•oup,  or  milk. 

RIBBON   MACCARONI.* 

This  kind  of  maccaroni,  though  more  delicate  in  flavour  and  much 
more  quickly  boiled  than  the  pipe  maccaroni,  is  far  less  frequently 
seen  at  English  tables ;  yet  it  is  extremely  ^ood  in  many  simple  forms 
and  very  wholesome,  therefore  well  suited  to  invalids  and  children 
as  well  as  to  persons  in  health.  Drop  it  gradually  into  plenty  of 
boiling  water,  and  turn  it  over  occasionally  that  it  may  be  equally 
done.  Drain  it  thoroughly  when  it  is  perfectly  tender,  and  serve  it 
qniekly  either  quite  plain,  to  be  eaten  instead  of  vegetables  or  rice ; 
or  with  a  eompote  of  fruit ;  or  with  sugar  and  cinnamon,  or  lemon-

DRESSED  MACCARONI* 

After  earefhl  and  repeated  trial  of  different  modes  of  dresdns 
Tarious  kinds  of  maccaroni,  we  find  that  in  preparing  them  witn 
Parmesan  cheese,  nnmixed  with  an^  of  a  more  mellow  nature,  there 
is  always  a  chance  of  failure,  from  its  tendency  to  gather  into  lumps; 
we  would  therefore  recommend  the  inexperienced  reader  to  substitiite 
for  it  in  part,  at  least,  any  finely  flavoured  English  cheese ;  tnd  tbe 
better  to  ensure  its  blencung  smoothly  with  &e  other  ingredieaa 
(when  neither  white,  nor  any  other  thickened  sauce  is  used  with  it), 
to  dissolve  the  butter,  and  to  stir  to  it  a  small  teaspoonful  of  floor, 
before  any  liquid  is  added,  then  carefully  to  mix  with  it  the  creim 
or  gravy,  as  directed  for  Sauce  Toumiey  Chanter  Y^  and  to  give  tin 
a  boil  before  the  maccaroni  and  cheese  are  added :  if  gently  tosKd  as 
these  become  hot,  the  whole  will  be  smooth,  and  the  cheese  will 
adhere  properly  to  the  paste.    Four  ounces  of  pipe  maocarooi  9 
sufficient  for  a  small  dish,  but  from  six  to  eight  should  be  prepsrcd 
for  a  family  party  where  it  is  liked.    The  common  English  mcde  of 
dressing  it  is  with  grated   cheese,    butter,  and  cream,  or  nolk 
French  cooks  substitute  generally  a  spoonful  or  two  of  very  sbwg 
rich  jellied  gravy  for  the  cream;  and  the  Italians,  amongst  tber 
many  other  modes  of  serving  it,  toss  it  in  rich  brown  gravy,  vidi 
sufficient  grated  cheese  to  flavour  the  whole  strongly;  they  send  it 
to  table  also  simply  laid  into  a  good  JEspagnole  or  brown  gravy  (tbat 
drawn  from  the  stu/ato^*  for  example),  accompanied  by  a  plate  «t 
grated  cheese.    Another,  and  an  easy  mode  of  dressing  it  is  to  boil 
and  drain  it  well,  and  to  put  it  into  a  deep  dish,  strewing  gntsd 
cheese  on  every  layer,  and  adding  bits  of  fresh  butter  to  it.    Tbe 
top,  in  this  case,  should  be  covered  with  a  layer  of  fine  bread-crumbs, 
mixed  with  grated  cheese;  these  should  be  moistened  plentifalW 
with  clarified  butter,  and  colour  given  to  them  in  the  oven,  tf 
before  the  fire ;  the  crumbs  may  be  omitted,  and  a  layer  of  cheese 
substituted  for  them     An  excellent  preparation  of  maccaroni  m^ 
l)e  made  with  any  well- flavoured,  dry  white  cheese,  which  am  be 
„ratcd  easily,  at  much  less  cost  than  with  the  Parmesan,  which  ii 
expensive,  and  in  the  country  not  always  procurable :  and  we  think 
that  the  brown  gravy  and  a  seasoning  of  cayenne  are  great  improve 
ments  to  it.

BEUOULIKA  AND  POLENTA  A  l'itALIENKE,  (ooOD.) 
(_To  teree  intUad  of  Maccaroni.') 
Throw  into  a  qnart  of  milk,  when  it  is  fa.<it  boiling, 
half  a  teaspoonfnl  of  salt,  and  then  shake  lijjhlly  into  it 
five  ounces  of  the  bttt  semonlina ;  stir  the  milk  a.t  this  is 
added,  and  continue  to  do  so  from  eight  to  ten  minutes, 
letting  the  mixture  boil  gently  during  the  time.  It 
should  be  very  thick,  and  great  care  must  be  taken  to 
prevent  its  sticking  to  the  saucepan,  which  should  be 
placed  over  a  clear  fire  on  a  bar  or  trivet,  but  not  upon 
the  coals.  Four  the  semoulina,  when  it  is  done,  into  a 
basin,  or  a  plain  mould  which  it  will  not  £11  by  an  inch 
or  two,  and  let  it  remain  some  hours  in  a  cool  place,  that  i 
it  may  become  perfectly  cold ;  it  will  then  turn  out  ^uile 
■olid,  and  like  a  pudding  in  appearance.  Cut  it  with  a 
large,  sharp  carvmg-knife,  or  a  bit  of  thin  wire,  into 
balf-inch  BLices  ;  wasb  the  basin  into  which  it  was  poured 
at  first,  and  butter  it  well ;  grate  from  six  to  eight  ounces 
of  good  cheese  (Farmesan,  or  any  other),  and  mix  with  it     Wiiic. 


394  MODEBK  OOOKERT.  [cmr.  m 

a  half-teaBpoonful  of  cayenne,  and  twice  as  much  pounded  mace; 
clarify  from  two  to  three  ounces  of  fresh  butter,  and  put  a  snail 
quantity  into  the  basin,  strew  in  a  little  of  the  cheese,  and  then  lay  in 
tne  first  slice  of  the  scmoulina,  on  this  put  a  thick  layer  of  the  cheese, 
moisten  it  with  some  drops  of  butter,  and  place  the  second  slice  upo& 
it ;  then  more  cheese  ana  butter,  and  continue  thus  until  all  the  se- 
moulina  is  replaced  in  the  basin ;  put  plenty  of  cheese  upon  the  top,  add 
the  remainder  of  the  clarified  butter,  and  bake  the  mixture  for  about 
half  an  hour  in  a  gentle  oven.  It  should  be  of  a  fine  golden  coknii 
when  served.  Turn  it  carefully  into  a  dish,  and  send  it  instantly  to 
table.  A  little  rich  brown  gravy  poured  round  might,  to  sooe 
tastes,  improve  it,  but  it  is  excellent  without,  and  may  be  substituted 
for  maccaroni,  which  it  much  resembles  in  flavour.  It  may  be  es' 
nched  by  adding  butter  to  the  milk,  or  by  mixing  with  it  a  portios 
of  cream ;  and  it  may  be  browned  in  a  Dutch  oven,  when  no  other  is 
in  use. 

In  Italy  the  flour  of  Indian  com,  which  is  much  grown  there,  and 
eaten  by  all  ranks  of  people,  is  used  for  this  dish ;  but  the  semonlma 
is  perhaps  rather  better  suited  to  English  taste  and  habits  of  diet, 
from  being  somewhat  lighter  and  more  delicate.  The  maize-floor 
imported  irom  Italy  is  sold  at  the  foreign  warehouses  here  un^  the 
name  of  polenta^*  though  that  properly  speaking  is,  we  believe,  a 
boiled  or  stewed  preparation  of  it,  which  forms  the  most  common  food 
of  the  poorer  classes  of  the  inhabitants  of  many  of  the  Itilias 
states.  It  seems  to  us  superior  in  quality  to  the  Indian  com  floor 
grown  in  America. 

New  milk  (or  milk  mixed  with  cream),  1  quart;  salt,  large  { tea- 
spoonful  ;  semoulina,  6  oz. :  10  minutes.  Grated  cheese,  6  to  8  oi.; 
cayenne,  (  teaspoontul ;  mace,  1  small  teaspoonful ;  butter,  2  to  3  ot: 
baked  )  hour,  j^entle  oven. 

Obs. — A  plam  mould  can  be  used  instead  of  the  basin.

of  the  articles  of  which  they  are  com-

atcMJan  the  loss  of  many  when  this  precaution  is  neglected.  They 
■boold  also  be  cleared  from  the  sp«\k8  with  scrupulous  attention, 
Other  with  the  point  of  a  smalt  tnree-pronged  fork  while  they  are 
in  the  cup,  or  by  straining  the  whole  throngh  a  fine  hair-sieve  after 
Ihey  are  beaten.  The  perfect  sweetness  of  suet  and  milk  should  be 
especially  attended  to  before  they  are  mixed  into  a  pudding,  as 
Krthing  can  be  more  offensive  than  the  first  when  it  is  ovet-kept,  nor 
woToe  in  its  effect  than  the  curdling  of  the  milk,  which  is  the  certain 
mult  c^  Ua  being  ever  so  sli^tly  soured. 


S96  MODEBN  COOKEBT*  CcHAP.iL 

Currants  should  be  cleaned,  and  raisins  stoned  with  exceeding  care; 
almonds  and  spices  very  finely  pounded,  and  the  rinds  of  oranges  or 
lemons  rasped  or  grated  lightly  off,  that  the  bitter  part  of  the  skin 
may  be  avoided,  when  they  are  used  for  this,  or  for  any  other  class  of 
dishes ;  if  pared,  they  should  be  cut  as  thin  as  possible. 

Custard  puddings  to  have  a  ^ood  appearance,  most  be  simmeftd 
only  but  without  ceasing;  for  if  boiled  in  a  quick  and  careless 
manner,  the  surface  instead  of  being  smooth  and  velyety,  will  be  fiill 
of  holes,  or  honey-combed,  as  it  is  called,  and  the  whey  will  flow 
from  it  and  mingle  with  the  sauce.  A  thickly-battered  sheet  of 
writing-paper  should  be  laid  between  the  custard  mixture  and  the 
cloth  before  it  is  tied  over,  or  the  cover  of  the  mould  is  closed  upon 
it ;  and  the  mould  itself  or  the  basin  in  which  it  is  boiled,  and  which 
should  always  be  quite  full,  must  likewise  be  well  buttered;  and 
after  it  is  lifted  from  the  water  the  pudding  should  be  left  in  it  for 
quite  five  minutes  before  it  is  dished,  to  prevent  its  breaking  or 
spreading  about. 

Batter  is  much  lighter  when  boiled  in  a  cloth,  and  allowed  full 
room  to  swell,  than  when  confined  in  a  mould :  it  should  be  well 
beaten  the  instant  before  it  is  poured  into  it,  and  put  into  the  water 
immediately  after  it  is  securely  tied.  The  cloth  should  be  moist  and 
thickly  floured,  and  the  pudding  should  be  sent  to  table  as  expe- 
ditiously as  possible  after  it  is  done,  as  it  will  quickly  become  beavj. 
This  applies  equally  to  all  puddings  made  vrith  paste,  which  are  ren- 
dered uneatable  by  any  delay  in  serving  them  after  they  are  ready: 
they  should  be  opened  a  little  at  the  top  as  soon  as  they  are  talsn 
from  the  boiler  or  stewpan  to  permit  the  escape  of  the  steam  from 
within. 

Plum-puddings,  which  it  is  customary  to  boil  in  moulds,  are  both 
lighter  and  less  diy,  when  closely  tied  in  stout  cloths  well  buttend 
and  floured,  especially  when  they  are  made  in  part  with  bread ;  but 
when  this  is  done,  care  should  be  taken  not  to  allow  them  to  bum  to 
the  bottom  of  the  pan  in  which  they  are  cooked ;  and  it  is  a  good 
plan  to  lay  a  plate  or  dish  under  them,  by  way  of  precaution  against 
this  mischance ;  it  will  not  then  so  much  matter  whether  they  be 
kept  floating  or  not.  It  is  thought  better  to  mix  these  entirdy  (ex- 
cept the  liquid  portion  of  them)  the  day  before  they  are  boiled,  and 
it  IS  perhaps  an  advantage  when  they  are  of  large  sixe  to  do  so,  baf 
it  is  not  really  necessary  for  small  or  common  ones. 

A  very  little  salt  improves  all  sweet  puddings,  by  taking  off  the 
insipidity,  and  bringing  out  the  full  flavour  of  the  other  ingredients, 
but  its  presence  should  not  be  in  the  slightest  degree  perceptihie, 
When  brandy,  wine,  or  lemon-juice  is  added  to  them  it  should  be 
stirred  in  briskly,  and  by  degrees,  quite  at  last,  as  it  would  be  likely 
other^'ise  to  curdle  the  milk  or  egsrg. 

Many  parsons  prefer  their  puddings  steamed ;  but  when  this  n  not 
done,  they  should  be  dropped  into  plenty  of  boiling  water,  and  be 
kept  well  covered  with  it  until  they  are  ready  to  ser^-e ;  lad  Uit 


CXAP.  xz.]  30ILEO  PUDDINGS.  397 


boiling  should  never  be  allowed  to  cease  for  an  instant,  for  they  soon 
become  heavy  if  it  be  interrupted. 

Padding  and  dumpling  clotns  should  not  only  be  laid  into  plenty 
of  water  as  soon  as  the^  are  taken  ofif,  and  weU  washed  afterwards, 
bat  it  is  essential  to  tbeur  perfect  sweetness  that  they  should  be  well 
and  quickly  dried  (in  the  open  air  if  possible),  then  folded  and  kept 
in  a  clean  drawer. 

TO   CLEAN   CURRANTS  FOR  PUDDINGS  OR   CAKES.. 

Pat  them  into  a  cullender,  strew  a  handful  of  flour  over  them,  and 
nib  them  gently  with  the  hands  to  separate  the  lumps,  and  to  detach 
the  stalks ;  work  them  round  in  the  cullender,  and  shake  it  well, 
when  the  small  stalks  and  stones  will  fall  through  it.  Next  pour 
plenty  of  cold  water  over  the  currants,  drain  and  spread  them  on  a 
soft  ciotb,  press  it  over  them  to  absorb  the  moisture,  and  then  lay 
them  on  a  clean  oven-tin,  or  a  large  dish,  and  dry  them  very 
gradually  (or  they  will  become  hard),  either  in  a  cool  oven  or  before 
Ibe  fire,  taking  care  in  the  latter  case  that  they  are  not  placed 
saffidently  near  it  for  the  ashes  to  fall  amongst  them.  When  they 
are  perfectly  dry,  clear  them  entirely  from  the  remaining  stalks,  and 
from  every  stone  that  may  be  amongst  them.  The  best  mode  of  de- 
tecting these  is  to  lay  the  fruit  at  the  far  end  of  a  large  white  dish,  or 
sheet  of  paper, 'and  to  pass  it  lightly,  and  in  very  small  portions, 
with  the  fingers,  towards  oneseli,  examining  it  closely  as  this  is 
done. 

TO  STEAM    A   PUDDING   IN   A   COMMON   8TEWPAN   OR  SAUCEPAN. 

Butter  and  fill  the  mould  or  basin  as  usual ;  tie  over  it,  first,  a 
well-buttered  paper,  and  then  a  thin  floured  cloth  or  musHn,  which 
should  be  quite  small ;  gather  up  and  tie  the  comers,  and  be  careful 
that  no  part  of  it,  or  of  the  paper,  reaches  to  the  water ;  pour  in  from 
two  to  three  inches  depth  of  this,  according  to  the  height  of  the 
Qoold,  and  when  it  boils  put  in  the  pudding,  and  press  the  cover 
of  the  stewpan  closely  on ;  then  boil  it  gently  without  ceasing  until  it 
is  done.  This  is  the  safer  method  of  boiling  all  puddings  made  with 
polenta,  or  with  the  American  flour  of  maize ;  as  well  as  many  others 
of  the  custard  kind,  which  are  easilj^  spoiled  by  the  admission  of 
water  to  them.  As  the  evaporatron  diminishes  that  in  the  saucepan, 
DBore,  leady-boiling,  must  be  added  if  necessary  ;  and  be  poured 
axefully  down  the  side  of  the  pan  without  touching  the  pudding. 

TO  MIX  BATTER  FOR  PUDDINGS, 

Fnt  the  flour  and  salt  into  a  bowl,  and  stir  them  together ;  whisk 
iie  eggs  thoroughly,  strain  them  through  a  fine  hair-sieve,  and  add 
hem  very  gradually  to  the  flour ;  for  if  too  much  liquid  be  poured  to 
t  at  once  it  vrill  be  full  of  lumps,  and  it  is  easy  with  care  to  keep  tha

batter  perfectly  smooth.  Beat  it  well  and  lightly  with  the  hack  of  a 
strong  wooden  spoon,  and  after  the  egss  are  added  thin  it  with  milk 
to  a  proper  consistence.  The  whites  oi  the  eggs  beaten  sepaiatelj  to 
a  solid  froth,  and  stirred  gently  into  the  mixture  the  instant  before  it 
is  tied  up  for  boiling,  or  before  it  is  put  into  the  oyen  to  be  baked, 
will  render  it  remarkably  light.  When  fruit  is  added  to  the  batter, 
it  must  be  made  thicker  than  when  it  is  served  plain,  or  it  will  sink 
to  the  bottom  of  the  pudding.  Batter  should  never  stick  to  the  hmfe 
when  it  is  sent  to  table :  it  wdl  do  this  both  when  a  sufficient  nomlxr 
of  eggs  are  not  mixed  with  it,  and  when  it  is  not  enough  cooked. 
About  four  eggs  to  the  half  pound  of  flour  will  make  it  Arm  enough 
to  cut  smoothly 

SUET-CRUST,   FOR   MEAT   OR   FRUIT   PUDDINGS. 

Clear  off  the  skin  from  some  fresh  beef  kidney-suet,  hold  it  firmil; 
with  a  fork,  and  with  a  sharp  knife  slice  it  thin,  free  it  entirely  from 
fibre,  and  minoe  it  very  fine :  six  ounces  thus  prepared  will  be  found 
quite  sufficient  for  a  pound  of  flour.  Mix  them  well  together,  add 
half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  for  meat  puddings,  and  a  third  as  much  for 
fruit  ones,  and  sufficient  cold  water  to  make  the  whole  into  a  Toy 
Arm  paste ;  work  it  smooth,  and  roll  it  out  of  equal  thickneas  whoi 
it  is  used.  The  weight  of  suet  should  be  taken  after  it  is  roinoed. 
This  crust  is  so  much  lighter,  and  more  wholesome  than  that  which 
is  made  with  butter,  that  we  cannot  refrain  from  recommending  it  in 
preference  to  our  readers.  Some  cooks  merely  slice  the  suet  in  thin 
shaving  mix  it  with  the  flour,  and  beat  the  crust  with  a  paste-rolkr, 
until  the  flour  and  suet  are  perfectly  incorporated ;  but  it  is  better 
minced. 

Flour,  2  lbs. ;  suet,  12  oz.;  salt,  1  teaspoonful;  water,  1  pint. 

BUTTER  CRUST  FOR  PUDDINGS. 

When  suet  is  disliked  for  crust,  butter  must  supply  its  place,  hot 
there  must  be  no  intermixture  of  lard  in  paste  which  is  to  be  boiled. 
Eight  ounces  to  the  pound  of  flour  will  render  it  sufficienty  rich  for 
most  eaters,  and  less  will  generally  be  preferred ;  rich  crust  of  this 
kind  being  more  indigestible  by  far  than  that  which  b  baked.  The 
butter  may  be  lightly  broken  into  the  flour  before  the  water  is  added, 
or  it  may  be  laid  on,  and  rolled  into  the  paste  as  for  puff-cmsL  A 
small  portion  of  salt  must  be  added  to  it  always,  and  for  a  meat 
pudding  the  same  proportion  as  directed  in  the  preceding  receipt 
For  kitchen,  or  ibr  quite  common  family  puddings,  butter  and 
clarified  dripping  are  used  sometimes  in  equal  proportions.  From 
three  to  four  ounces  of  each  will  be  sufficient  for  the  pound  and 
quarter  of  flour. 

Flour,  1  lb.;  butter,  8  oz. ;  salt,  for  fruit  puddings,  |  saltspoonful ; 
for  meat  puddings,  i  teaspoonfuL

SAVOURY    PUDDINGS, 

The  perfect  manner  in  which  the  nutriment  and  flavour  of  an 
mfinite  variety  of  viands  may  be  preserved  by  enclosing  and  boiling 
them  in  paste,  is  a  great  recommendation  of  this  purely  English  class 
of  dishes,  the  advantages  of  which  foreign  cooks  are  beginning  to 
acknowledge.  If  really  well  made,  these  savoury  puddings  are 
worthy  of  a  place  on  any  table ;  though  the  decrees  of  fashion— 
which  in  many  instances  have  so  much  more  influence  with  us  than 
they  deserve — have  hitherto  confined  them  almost  entirely  to  the 
simple  family  dinners  of  the  middle  classes ;  but  we  are  bound  to 
acknowledge  that  even  where  they  are  most  commonly  served  they 
are  seldom  prepared  with  a  creditable  degree  of  skill ;  and  they  are 
equally  umnviting  and  unwholesome  wncn  heavily  and  coarsely 
concocted.  From  the  general  suggestions  which  we  make  here,  and 
the  few  detailed  receipts  which  follow,  a  clever  cook  will  easily  com- 
pound them  to  suit  the  taste  and  means  of  her  employers ;  for  they 
may  be  either  very  rich  and  expensive,  or  quite  the  reverse.  Venison 
(the  neck  is  best  for  the  purpose),  intermingled  or  not  with  truffles ; 
sweetbreads  sliced,  and  ovsters  or  nicely  prepared  button-mushrooms 
in  alternate  layers,  with  good  veal  stock  for  gravy;*  pheasants, 
partridges,  moorfowl,  woodcocks,  snipes,  plovers,  wheatears,  may  all 
be  converted  into  the  first  class  of  these ;  and  veal  kidneys,  seasoned 
with  fine  herbs,  will  supply  another  variety  of  them.  Many  persons 
like  eels  dressed  in  this  way,  but  they  are  unsuited  to  delicate  eaters ; 
and  sausages  are  liable  to  the  same  objection ;  and  so  is  a  harslet 
pudding,  which  is  held  in  much  esteem  in  certain  counties,  and 
which  IS  made  of  the  heart,  liver,  kidneys,  &c.,  of  a  pig.  We  can 
reconunend  as  both  wholesome  and  economical  the  receipts  which 
follow,  for  the  more  simple  kind  of  savoury  puddings,  and  which 
may  serve  as  guides  for  such  others  as  the  intelligence  of  the  cook 
nay  snggest. 

BEEF-STEAK,    OR   JOHN   BULl's   PUDDING. 

All  meat  puddings  are  more  conveniently  made  in  deep  pans, 
moulds,  or  basins  having  a  thick  rim,  below  which  the  cloths  can  be 
tied  without  the  hazard  of  their  slipping  off;  and  as  the  puddings 
should  by  no  means  be  turned  out  before  they  are  sent  to  table,  one 
to  match  the  dinner-service,  at  least  in  colour^  is  desirable-f    BM 

*  The  Uqnor  of  the  OTBten  shoald  be  added  when  they  are  nsed. 

•f  It  is  oow  enstomary  in  some  families  to  have  both  meat  and  fhiit  pnddinga 
boiled  and  aerred  in  pie  or  tar^dishes.  Thej  are  lined  entirely  with  very  thin 
Brost,  or  merely  edged  with  it,  according  to  taste ;  then  filled,  closed,  and  cooked 
in  the  uaiial  manner.  The  plan  is  a  good  and  convenient  one,  where  the  light 
Mfper-eruat  is  preferred  to  the  heavy  and  sodden  part  which  is  under  the  meat. 
In  Kent  and  Sussex,  shallow  pans,  in  form  somewhat  resembling  a  large  deep 
ac**l  expressly  for  boiling  meat  puddings. 


400  MODEBN  COOKEET.  [cBAP.ix 

out  a  suet  crust  to  half  an  inch  in  thickness,  line  evenly  with  it  a 
quart,  or  any  other  sized  basin  that  may  be  preferred,  and  raise  the 
.crust  from  an  inch  and  a  half  to  two  inches  above  the  edge.  Fill  it 
with  layers  of  well-kept  rump-steak,  neatly  trimmed,  and  seasoned 
with  salt  and  pepper,  or  cayenne ;  pour  in  some  cold  water  to  make 
the  gravy ;  roll  out  the  coyer,  moisten  the  edge,  as  well  as  that  ot 
the  pudding;  draw  and  press  them  together  carefully,  fold  than 
over,  shake  out  a  cloth  which  has  been  dipped  into  hot  water,  wiung 
out,  and  well  floured ;  tie  it  over  the  pudding,  gather  the  ooinen 
together,  tie  them  over  the  top  of  the  pudding,  put  it  into  plenty  of 
fast  boiling  water,  and  let  it  remain  in  from  three  to  five  boon, 
according  to  its  size.  The  instant  it  is  lifted  out,  stick  a  fork  qnile 
through  the  middle  of  the  paste  to  prevent  its  bursting ;  remove  die 
cloth  quickly,  and  cut  a  small  round  or  square  in  the  top  to  allow  the 
steam  to  escape,  and  serve  the  pudding  immediately.  Though  not 
considered  very  admissible  to  an  elegantly  served  table,  this  is  a 
favourite  dish  with  man^  persons,  and  is  often  in  great  esteem  with 
sportsmen,  for  whom  it  is  provided  in  preference  to  fare  which  re« 
quires  greater  exactness  in  the  time  of  cooking;  as  an  additioml 
nour*s  boiling,  or  even  more,  will  have  little  effect  on  a  large  puddii^ 
of  this  kind,  beyond  reducing  thie  quantity  of  gravy,  and  rendering  it 
very  thick. 

Some  cooks  flour  the  meat  slightly  before  it  is  laid  into  the  cmst, 
out  we  do  not  think  it  an  improvement :  where  fat  is  liked,  a  portioD 
may  be  added  with  the  lean,  but  all  skin  and  sinew  should  be  care- 
fully rejected.  Beat  the  steak  with  a  paste  roller,  or  cutlet-bat, 
should  it  not  appear  to  be  perfectly  tender,  and  divide  it  into  poitioos 
about  the  width  of  two  fingers.  Two  or  three  dozens  of  oysfcen. 
bearded  and  washed  free  firom  grit  in  their  own  li(^uor  (which  shoold 
afterwards  be  strained  and  poured  into  the  pudding),  may  be  inter- 
mingled with  the  meat. 

A  true  epicurean  receipt  for  this  dish  directs  the  paste  to  be  made 
with  oeo/'kidney  suet,  and  filled  with  alternate  layers  of  the  inside  oj 
the  eirloin^  sliced  and  seasoned,  and  of  fine  plump  native  oysto^ 
intermixed  with  an  occasional  small  slice  of  the  veal  fsA, 

SMALL   BEEF-STEAK   PUDDIKG. 

Make  into  a  very  firm  smooth  paste,  one  pound  of  flour,  tax  oontes 
of  beef-suet  fiinely  minced,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  and  half  a  pint 
of  cold  water.  Line  with  this  a  basin  which  holds  a  pint  and  a  faa]£ 
Season  a  pound  of  tender  steak,  free  from  bone  and  skin,  with  half 
an  ounce  of  salt  and  half  a  teaspoonful  of  pepper  well  mixed  together; 
lay  it  in  the  crust,  pour  in  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  water,  roll  oat  the 
cover,  close  the  pudding  carefully,  tie  a  floured  cloth  over,  and  boil  it 
for  three  hours  and  a  half.  We  give  this  receipt  in  additiofi  to  the 
preceding  one,  as  an  exact  guide  for  the  proportions  of  meat-pndifiagi

RUTH   pinch's   BREF-8TEAK   PUDDING. 

To  make  RM  PincK$  celebrated  pndding  (known  also  as  beef- 
steak pudding  a  la  Dickeiui),  substitute  six  ounces  of  butter  for  the 
saet  in  this  receipt,  and  moisten  the  paste  with  the  well-beaten  yolks 
of  four  ^gs,  or  with  three  whole  ones,  mixed  with  a  little  water ;

podding  is  to  be  turned  out  of  it  for  table.  In  all  else  proceed 
exactly  as  aboye. 

MUTTON  PUDDING. 

Mutton  freed  perfectly  from  fat,  and  mixed  with  two  or  three 
diced  kidneys,  xnakes  an  excellent  pudding.  The  meat  may  be 
sprinkled  with  fine  herbs  as  it  is  laid  into  the  crust  This  will 
require  rather  less  boiling  than  the  preceding  puddings,  but  it  is 
made  in  precisely  the  same  way. 

PARTRIDGE   PUDDING. 

{Very  Good.) 

Skin  a  brace  of  well-ke]^  partridges  and  cut  tbem  down  into 
fbiots;  line  a  deep  basin  with  suet  crust,  observing  the  directions 
giyen  in  the  preceding  receipts ;  lay  in  the  birds,  which  should  be 
father  highly  seasoned  with  pepper  or  cayenne,  and  moderately  with 
alt ;  pour  in  water  for  the  gravy,  close  the  pudding  with  care,  and 
boil  it  from  three  hours  to  three  and  a  half.  The  true  flavour  of  the 
^ame  is  admirably  preserved  by  this  mode  of  cookinff.  When  mush- 
xmiDs  are  plentiful,  put  a  layer  of  buttons,  or  small  flaps,  deaned  as 
or  piddin^,  alternately  with  a  layer  of  partridge,  in  filling  the 
mdoiiig,  which  will  then  be  most  excellent  eating :  the  crust  may  be 
eft  untouched,  and  merely  emptied  of  its  contents,  where  it  is 
objected  to,  or  its  place  may  be  supplied  with  a  richer  one  made  of 
otter.  A  seasoning  of  pounded  mace  or  nutmeg  can  be  used  at 
iflcretion.  Puddings  of  veal,  chickens,  and  young  rabbits,  may  all 
e  made  by  this  receipt,  or  with  the  addition  of  oysters,  which  we 
B^e  already  noticed. 

A   PEAS  PUDDING. 

(To  serve  with  hailed  pork.)

«  "vrorm-eaten ;  wash  the  remainder  well,  and  soak  them  for  a  night 
L  plenty  of  soft  water.  The  following  day  tie  them  up  in  a  thick 
Eftdding  doth,  giving  them  room  to  swell,  cover  them  well  with  cold 
It  -vrater  and  Soil  them  gently  from  two  hours  to  two  and  a  half: 

tixey  are  not  then  quite  tender,  they  are  of  bad  quality,  and  cannot

be  rendered  so.  Lift  them  into  a  cullender,  untie  tbe  dotb,  and 
crush  them  to  a  paste  with  a  wooden  spoon,  stir  in  a  good  slice  of 
butter,  and  a  seasoning  of  pepper  and  «dt,  tie  them  up  again  very 
tight,  and  boil  them  for  half  an  hour ;  turn  the  pudding  S^'^^  ^^^ 
a  dish  that  it  may  not  break,  and  serve  it  as  hot  as  possible.  This  is 
the  common  old-fashioned  mode  of  preparing  a  peas  pudding,  and 
many  persons  prefer  it  to  the  more  modern  one  wnich  foUow& 
Soak,  and  boil  the  peas  as  aboye,  drain  the  water  well  from  them

them  gradually  three  well  whisked  eggs,  tie  the  mixture  tightly  and 
closely  in  a  floured  cloth,  and  boil  it  for  one  hour.

They  must  have  room  to  swell  or  they  will  be  hard ;  but  if  too  much 
be  given  them  they  will  be  watery,  and  it  will  be  difficult  to  conveit 
them  into  a  pudding  at  all. 

WINE   SAUCB  FOR   8WEET   PUDDINGS. 

Boil  gently  together  for  ten  or  fifteen  minutes  the  very  thin  Had 
of  half  a  small  lemon,  about  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  sugar,  and  a 
wineglassful  of  water.  Take  out  the  lemon-peel  and  stir  into  the 
sauce  until  it  has  boiled  for  one  minute,  an  ounce  of  butter  smoothlr 
mixed  with  a  large  half-teaspoonful  of  flour ;  add  a  wineglassiiil  and 
a  half  of  sherry  or  Madeira,  or  other  good  white  wine,  and  when 
quite  hot  serve  the  sauce  without  delay.  Port  wine  sauce  is  made  in 
the  same  way  with  the  addition  of  a  dessertspoonful  of  lemon-jnioe, 
some  grated  nutmeg  and  a  little  more  sugar.  Orange-rind  and  joiee 
may  be  used  for  it  instead  of  lemon. 

COMMON   WINE   SAUCE. 

Sweeten  a  quarter-pint  of  good  melted  butter  with  an  ounce  aul 
a  half  of  sugar,  and  add  to  it  gradually  a  couple  of  glares  of  wine ; 
stir  it  until  it  is  at  the  point  of  boiling,  and  serve  it  immc^liatdf. 
Lemon-grate,  or  nutmeg,  can  be  added  at  pleasure.

A   GERMAN    CUSTARD   PUDDIMG-SAUCE. 

Boil  yery  gently  together  half  a  pint  of  new  milk  or  of  milk  and 
cream  mixed,  a  very  thin  strip  or  two  of  fresh  lemon-rind,  a  bit  of 
cinnamon,  half  an  inch  of  a  yanilla  bean,  and  an  ounce  and  a  half  or 
two  ounces  of  sugar,  until  the  milk  is  etrongly  flavoured  ;  then  strain, 
and  pour  it,  by  slow  decrees,  to  the  well-beaten  yolks  of  three  eggs, 
smoothly  mixed  with  a  Imife^end-fuU  (about  half  a  teaspoonful)  of 
flour,  a  grain  or  two  of  salt,  and  a  tablespoonful  of  cold  milk ;  and 
stir  these  very  quickly  round  as  the  milk  is  added.  Put  the  sauce 
again  into  the  stewpau,  and  whisk  or  stir  it  rapidly  until  it  thickens, 
and  looks  creamy.  It  must  not  be  placed  upon  the  fire,  but  should 
be  held  over  it,  when  this  is  done.  The  Germans  mill  their  sauces  to 
a  firoth  ;  but  they  may  be  whisked  with  almost  equally  good  effect, 
though  a  small  null  for  the  purpose— formed  like  a  chocolate  mill — 
may  be  had  at  a  very  trifling  cost. 

A   DELICIOUS   GERMAN   PUDDING-SAUCE. 

Dissolve  in  half  a  pint  of  sherry  or  of  Madeira,  from  three  to  four 
ounces  of  fine  sugar,  but  do  not  ulow  the  wine  to  boil ;  stir  it  hot  to 
the  well-beaten  yolks  of  six  fresh  eggs,  and  mill  the  sauce  over  a 
gentle  fire  until  it  is  well  thickened  and  highly  frothed ;  ^ur  it  over 
a  plum,  or  any  other  kind  of  sweet  boiled  pudding,  of  which  it  much 
improves  the  appearance.  Half  the  quantity  will  be  sufficient  for  one 
of  moderate  size.  We  recommend  the  addition  of  a  dessertspoonful 
of  strained  lemon-juice  to  the  wine. 

For  large  pud£ng,  sherry  or  Madeira,  }  pint ;  fine  sugar,  3  to  4 
oz. ;  yolks  of  egss,  6 ;  lemon-juice  (if  added),  1  dessertspoonful. 

Obe, — ^As  wehave  already  said  in  the  previous  receipt,  it  is  cus- 
tomary to  firoth  sweet  sauces  in  Germany  with  a  small  machine 
made  like  a  chocolate-mill.  Two  silver  forxs  fastened  together  at  the 
handles  may  be  used  instead  on  an  emergency,  or  the  sauce  may  be 
whisked  to  the  proper  state,  like  the  one  which  precedes  it. 

Great  care  must  oe  taken  not  to  allow  these  sauces  to  curdle.  The 
safer  plan  is  to  put  any  preparation  of  the  kind  into  a  white  jar,  and 
to  place  it  over  the  fire  m  a  pan  of  boiling  water,  and  then  to  stir  or 
miu  it  until  it  is  sufficiently  thickened :  the  jar  should  not  be  haJi

easily.  The  water  should  not  reach  to  within  two  or  three  inches  of 
the  brim.    We  give  these  minute  details  for  inexperienced  oooks.

above,  allowing  a  larger  prpportion  of  the  fruit,  as  it  yielos  leas  jmB 
than  the  currant.

COMMON   RASPBERRY-SAUGB. 

Put  three  ounces  of  sugar  broken  into  small  lumps,  and  a  irine- 
elassful  and  a  half  of  water  into  a  small  stewpan,  and  boil  them  far 
four  or  ^ve  minutes.  Add  half  a  pint  of  fresh  ripe  raspberries,  irell 
mashed  with  the  back  of  a  spoon.  Mix  them  with  the  sympi  sod 
boil  them  for  six  or  seven  mmutes ;  the  sauce  should  then  be  quite 
smooth  and  clear.  The  quantity  of  it  with  these  proportions  will  not 
be  large,  but  can  be  incrrased  at  pleasure.

PINE-APPLB   PUDDING-SAUCE. 

Basp  down  on  a  flhe  bright  grater  sufficient  of  the  flesh  of  a  ripe 
Jamaica  or  English  pine-apple  from  which  the  rind  has  been  thickly 
pared,  to  make  the  quantity  of  sauce  required.  Simmer  it  quite 
tender,  with  a  very  small  quantity  of  water;  then  mix  with  it  by 
degrees  from  half  to  three-quarters  of  its  weight  of  sugar,  give  it  five 
xoinutea  more  of  gentle  boiling,  and  pour  it  over  the  pudding. 

Basped  pineapple,  6  oz. ;  water,  2  tablespoonsiul :  10  to  15 
minutes  gentle  stewing.    Sugar,  4  oz. :  5  minutes. 

A  finer  sauce  may  be  made  with  half  a  pound  of  the  pine  first 
simmered  tender  in  its  own  juice,  and  one  tablespoonful  only  of 
water,  and  then  mixed  with  seven  ounces  of  sifted  sugar,  and  boiled 
ffently  until  it  looks  clear.  If  too  sweet,  the  strained  juice  of  half  a 
fiiige  flozed  lemon  may  be  stirred  to  it  before  it  is  served,  but  a  cer- 
tain weight  of  sugar  is  required  to  make  it  appear  bright.  This  pre- 
paration mav  be  Kept  for  some  time,  and  warmed  afresh  for  table 
when  needed.

GERMAN  CHERRY  BAUCE^ 

Beat  a  quart  of  cherries  in  a  mortar  until  the  stones  are  broken, 
then  boU  them  tender  with  half  a  pint  of  water  and  wine  mixed. 
Hub  them  through  a  sieve,  and  boil  them  again,  with  from  four  to 
six  ounces  of  fine  sugar,  some  grated  lemon-peel,  powdered  cinnamon, 
and  a  small  portion  of  pounded  cloves.  In  a  few  minutes  stir  to  the 
sauce  a  dessertspoonful  of  potato-flour,  smoothly  mixed  with  a  veiy 
little  cold  water ;  continue  to  stir  until  it  is  agam  ready  to  boil,  and 
serve  it,  either  poured  entirely  over  the  pud^g,  or  in  a  tureen. 

COMMON   BATTER   PUDDING. 

Beat  four  eggs  thoroughly,  mix  with  them  half  a  pint  of  milk,  and 
pass  them  through  a  sieve,  add  them  by  degrees  to  half  a  pound  of 
flour,  and  when  the  batter  is  perfectly  smooth,  thin  it  with  another 
half  pint  of  milk.  Shake  out  a  wet  pudding  cloth,  flour  it  well, 
pour  the  batter  in,  leave  it  room  to  swell,  tie  it  securely,  and  put  it 
immediatelv  into  plenty  of  fast-boiling  water.  An  hour  and  ten 
minutes  will  boil  it.  Send  it  to  table  tne  instant  it  is  dished,  with 
wine  sauce,  a  hot  compdte  of  fruit,  or  raspberry  vinegar :  this  last 
makes  a  delicious  pudding  sauce.  Unless  the  liquid  be  added  venr 
gradually  to  the  nour,  and  the  mixture  be  well  stirred  and  beaten 
as  each  portion  is  poured  to  it,  the  batter  will  not  be  smooth:  to 
render  it  very  light,  a  portion  of  the  whites  of  the  e^gs,  or  the  whole 
of  them,  should  De  whisked  to  a  froth  and  stirred  mto  it  just  before 
it  is  put  into  the  cloth. 

Flour,  i  lb.;  eggs,  4;  salt,  |  teaspoonful;  milk,  1  pint:  1  hoar 
and  10  minutes. 

Obs. — Modem  taste  is  in  favour  of  puddings  boiled  in  moulds,  bnt» 
as  we  have  already  stated,  they  are  seldom  or  ever  so  light  as  thoee 
which  are  tied  in  cloths  only.

BLACK-CAP  PUDDING. 

Make  a  good  light  thin  batter,  and  just  before  it  is  poured  into  the 
cloth  stir  to  it  half  a  pound  of  currants,  well  cleaned  and  dried : 
these  will  sink  to  the  lower  part  of  the  pudding  and  blacken  the 
surface.  Boil  it  the  usual  time,  and  dish  it  with  the  dark  side 
uppermost;  send  very  sweet  sauce  to  table  with  it.  Some  cooks 
batter  a  mould  thickly,  strew  in  the  currants,  and  pour  the  batter  on 
them,  which  produces  the  same  appearance  as  when  the  ingredients 
ire  tied  in  a  cloth. 

AU  batter  puddings  should  be  despatched  quickly  to  table  when 
they  are  once  ready  to  serye,  as  tney  speedily  become  heavy  if 
allowed  to  wait. 

BATTER   FRUIT   PUDDING, 

Butter  thickly  a  basin  which  holds  a  pint  and  a  hal^  and  fill  it 
nearly  to  the  bnm  with  good  boiling  apples  pared,  cored,  and  quar- 
tered; pour  over  them  a  batter  made  with  four  tablespoonsful  of 
flour,  two  large  or  three  small  eggs,  and  half  a  pint  of  milk.  Tie  a 
buttered  and  floured  cloth  over  the  basin,  which  ought  to  be  quite 
full,  and  boil  the  pudding  for  an  hour  and  a  quarter.  Turn  it  into 
a  hot  dish  when  done,  and  strew  sugar  thickly  over  it :  this,  if  added 
to  the  batter  at  first,  renders  it  heavY.  Morella  cherries  make  a 
Tery  superior  pudding  of  this  kind ;  ana  green  gooseberries,  damsons, 
and  various  other  frmts,  answer  for  it  extremely  well:  the  time  of 
boiling  it  must  be  varied  according  to  their  auality  and  its  size. 

For  a  pint  and  a  half  mould  or  basin  fillea  to  the  brim  with  apples 
or  other  fruit;  flour,  4  tablespoonsful;  ^gs,  2  large  or  3  small; 
milk,  )  pint:  1^  hour. 

Ois. — Apples  cored,  halved,  and  mixed  with  a  good  batter,  make 
an  excellent  baked  pudding,  as  do  red  currants,  cherries,  and  plums 
of  different  sorts  likewise.

▲  COMMON  APPLE  PUDDING. 

Hake  a  light  crust  with  one  pound  of  flour,  and  six  ounces  of 
▼enr  finely  minced  beef-suet ;  roll  it  thin,  and  fill  it  with  one  pound 
and  a  quarter  of  good  boiling  apples;  add  the  grated  rind  and 
strained  juice  of  a  small  lemon,  tie  it  in  a  cloth,  and  boil  it  one  hour 
and  twenty  minutes  before  Christmas,  and  from  twenty  to  thirty 
minutes  longer  after  Christmas.  A  small  slice  of  fresh  butter, 
Btined  into  it  when  it  is  sweetened  will,  to  many  tastes,  be  an 
acceptable  addition;  grated  nutmeg,  or  a  little  cinnamon  in  fine 
powder,  may  be  substituted  for  the  lemon-rind  when  either  is 
preferred.  To  convert  this  into  a  richer  pudding  use  half  a  pound 
of  butter  for  the  crust,  and  add  to  the  apples  a  spoonful  or  two  of 
orange  or  quince  marmalade. 

Crust:  flour,  1  lb.;  suet,  6  oz.  Fruit,  pared  and  cored,  lilb.; 
juice  and  rind  of  1  small  lemon  (or  some  nutmeg  or  cinnamon  in 
powder). 

Richer  pudding :  flour,  1  lb. ;  butter  }  lb. ;  in  addition  to  fruit, 
1  or  2  tablespooimul  of  orange  or  ouince  marmalade. 

HEBODOTUS'   PUDDING. 

(A  Oenvine  Classical  Receipt,') 

*  Prepare  and  mix  in  the  usual  manner  one  pound  of  fine  raisins 
itoned,  one  pound  of  minced  beef-suet,  half  a  pound  of  bread-crumbs, 
four  figs  chopped  small,  two  tablespoonsful  of  moist  sugar  Qwney^ 
in  the  oiiginay,  two  wine^lassesful  of  sherry,  and  the  rind  of  half 
I  hLTge  lemon  (grated^.    Boil  the  pudding  for  fourteen  hours.** 

Obs. — ^Xhis  receipt  is  really  to  be  found  in  Herodotus.  The  only 
rariations  made  in  it  are  the  substitution  of  sugar  for  honey,  and 
ilkerry  for  the  wine  of  ancient  Greece.  We  are  indebted  for  it  to 
in  accomplished  scholar,  who  has  had  it  served  at  his  own  table  on 
iDOire  than  one  occasion;  and  we  have  given  it  on  his  authority, 
iritliout  testing  it :  but  we  venture  to  suggest  that  seven  hours  would 
toil  it  quite  siuiciently.

THE   publisher's   PUDDING. 


This  pudding  can  scarcely  be  made  too  rich.  First  blanch,  tod 
then  beat  to  the  smoothest  possible  paste,  six  ounces  of  fresh  Jordan 
almonds,  and  a  dozen  bitter  ones ;  pour  very  gradually  to  them,  ia 
the  mortar,  three  quarters  of  a  pmt  of  boiling  cream ;  then  tnin

expression.  Heat  a  full  half  pint  of  it  afresh,  and  pour  it,  as  scwa 
as  it  boils,  upon  four  ounces  or  fine  bread-crumbs,  set  a  plate  oTer, 
and  leave  them  to  become  nearly  cold ;  then  mix  thoroughly  with 
them  four  ounces  of  maccaroons,  crushed  tolerably  small;  fire 
of  finely  minced  beef-suet,  five  of  marrow,  cleared  very  carefiiUy 
from  fibre,  and  from  the  splinters  of  bone  which  are  sometimes  fiiand 
in  it,  and  shred  not  very  small,  two  ounces  of  flour,  six  of  pounded 
sugar,  four  of  dried  cherries,  four  of  the  best  Muscatel  raisuB, 
weighed  after  they  are  stoned,  half  a  pound  of  candied  citron,  or  of

nutmeg,  the  yolks  only  of  seven  full-sized  <^gs,  the  grated  nnd  of  a 
lai^  lemon,  and  last  of  all,  a  glass  of  the  best  Cognac  brandy,  which 
must  be  stirred  briskly  in  by  slow  degrees.  Pour  the  mixture  into 
a  thickly  buttered  mould  or  basin,  which  contains  a  full  quart,  fill  it 
to  the  brim,  lay  a  sheet  of  buttered  writing-paper  over,  then  a  wcU-

HER  majesty's  PUDDING. 

Infuse  in  a  pint  of  new  milk  half  a  pod  of  ranilla,  cut  mto  sboit 
lengths,  and  bruised;  simmer  them  gently  together  for  twenty 
minutes,  and  strain  the  milk  through  muslin  to  half  a  pint  of  cream; 
put  these  again  on  the  fire  in  a  clean  saucepan,  with  three  oonoes  of 
fine  sugar,  and  pour  them  when  they  ooil,  to  the  beaten  3rolk9  of 
eight  very  fresh  e^gs.  Stir  the  mixture  often  until  it  is  nearly  or

battered  mould  or  basin  that  will  just  hold  it.  Let  it  stand  for  ^ve

vanilla.

COMMON   CUSTARD  PUDDINO. 

Whisk  three  egus  well,  j)ut  them  into  a  pint  basin,  and  add  to 
them  sufficient  milK  to  fill  it :  then  strain,  flavour,  and  sweeten  it

let  it  stand  a  few  minutes,  dish,  and  serve  it  with  sugar  sifled  over,

cherries  to  table  with  it.  A  small  quantity  of  lemon-brandy,  or  of 
ratifia  can  be  added,  to  give  it  flavour,  when  it  is  made,  or  the  sugar 
with  which  it  is  sweetened  may  be  rasped  on  a  lemon  or  an  orange, 
then  crushed  and  dissolved  in  the  milk ;  from  an  ounce  and  a  half  to 
two  ounces  will  be  sufficient  for  general  taste.

▲  CABINET  PUDDING. 

Split  and  stone  three  dozens  of  fine  jar  raisins,  or  take  an  equal 
number  of  dried  cherries,  and  place  either  of  them  regularly  in  a  sort 
of  pattern,  in  a  thickly-buttered  plain  quart  mould  or  basin ;  next, 
dice  and  lay  into  it  three  penny  sponge-cakes;  add  to  these  two 
ounces  of  ratifias,  four  macaroons,  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  candied 
citron  sliced  thin,  the  yolks  of  four  ^gs  with  the  whites  of  three 
only,  thoroughly  whisked,  mixed  with  half  a  pint  of  new  milk,  then 
strained  to  half  a  pint  of  sweet  cream,  and  sweetened  with  two  ounces 
and  a  half  of  pounded  susar :  these  ought  to  fill  the  mould  exactly. 
Steam  the  pudding,  or  bou  it  very  gently  for  one  hour ;  let  it  stand 
a  few  minutes  before  it  is  dished,  that  it  may  not  break ;  and  serve  it 
with  good  wine  or  brandy  sauce. 

Jar  raisins,  or  dried  cherries,  3  dozens  (quart  mould  or  basin) ; 
sponge  hiscnits,  3;  macaroons,  4;  ratifias,  2  oz. ;  candied  citron, 
1^  oz.;  yolks  of  4  eggs,  whites  of  3;  new  milk,  i  pint;  cream, 
k  pint ;  ragu*,  2^  oz. ;  steamed,  or  boiled,  1  hour. 

Oft#. — We  have  given  this  receipt,  for  which  we  are  indebted  to  a 
iriend,  without  any  variation  from  the  original,  because  on  testing  it 
we  have  found  it  very  exact  with  regard  to  quantity  and  tune ;  but 
though  an  extremely  delicate  and  excellent  pudding,  a  little  flavour- 
ing would,  we  think,  improve  it :  a  small  portion  of  the  milk  may  be 
omitted,  and  its  place  supplied  by  ratifia,  lemon-brandy,  or  aught 
else  that  is  preferred.

A   TERY   FWE  CABINET   PUDDING. 

Butter  thickly  a  mould  of  the  same  size  as  for  the  preoediiig 
pudding,  and  ornament  it  tastefully  with  dried  cherries,  or  with  the 
nnest  muscatel  raisins  opened  and  stoned;  lay  lightly  into  it  a 
quarter-pound  of  sponge  biscuit  cut  in  slices,  and  intermijEed  with  in 
equal  weight  of  ratifias;  sweeten  with  three  ounces  of  sugar  in 
lumps,  and  flavour  highly  with  vanilla,  or  with  the  thin  rind  of  hih 
a  fine  lemon,  and  six  sound  bitter  almonds  bruised  (should  these  he 
preferred),  three-quarters  of  a  pint,  or  rather  more,  of  thin  cream, 
or  of  cream  and  new  milk  mixed ;  strain  and  pour  this  hot  to  the 
well-beaten  yolks  of  six  eggs  and  the  whites  of  two,  and  when  the 
mixture  is  nearly  cold,  throw  in  gradually  a  win^lassful  of  good 
brandy ;  pour  it  ^ntly,  and  by  degrees,  into  the  mould,  and  tteam 
or  boil  tne  puddm^  very  softly  for  an  hour.  Serve  it  with  well 
made  wine  sauce.  J^ever  omit  a  buttered  paper  over  any  sort  at 
custard-mixture ;  and  remember  that  quick  boiling  will  destroy  the 
good  appearance  of  this  kind  of  pudding.  The  liquid  should  be 
quite  cold  before  it  is  added  to  the  cakes,  or  the  butter  on  the  moold 
would  melt  off,  and  the  decorations  with  it ;  preserved  ginger,  and 
candied  citron  in  slices,  may  be  used  to  vary  tnese,  and  the  syrup  of 
the  former  may  be  added  to  give  flavour  to  the  other  ingredients.

candied  citron  at  choice) :  steamed,  or  gently  boiled,  I  hour. 


8N0WD0N  PUDDING. 

(^Oemune  Receipt,) 

Ornament  a  well  buttered  mould  or  basin  with  some  fine  niani 
split  open  and  stoned,  but  not  divided,  pressing  the  cut  side  on  the 
butter  to  make  them  adhere;  next,  mix  half  a  pound  of  very  finely 
minced  beef-kidney  suet,  with  half  a  pound  of  bread  crumbs,  and  ui 
ounce  and  a  half  of  rice-flour,  a  pinch  of  salt,  and  six  ounces  of 
lemon  marmalade,  or  of  orange  when  the  lemon  cannot  be  procured; 
add  six  ounces  of  pale  brown  sugar,  six  thoroughly  whissed  eg«, 
and  the  grated  rinds  of  two  lemons.  Beat  the  whole  until  aliue 
ingredients  are  perfectly  mixed,  pour  it  gently  into  the  n^uld,  ooTer 
it  with  a  buttered  paper  and  a  floured  cloth,  and  boil  it  for  one  boor 
and  a  half.  It  will  turn  out  remarkably  well  if  carefully  prepand. 
Half  the  quantity  given  above  yrill  fill  a  mould  or  basin  which  will 
contain  rather  more  than  a  pint,  and  will  be  sufficiently  boUed  in  tea 
minutes  less  than  an  hour.  To  many  tastes  a  slight  diminution  in 
the  proportion  of  suet  w^uld  be  an  improvement  to  the  pudding; 


CHAP.  XX.]  BOILED  PUDDINQ8.  415 

ind  the  sabstitation  of  pounded  sugar  for  the  brown,  might  likewise 
be  considered  so.  Both  the  suet  and  eggs  used  for  it,  should  be  as 
fre&h  as  possible. 

This  pudding  is  constantly  served  to  travellers  at  the  hotel  at  the 
foot  of  the  mountain  from  which  it  derives  its  name.  It  is  probably 
well  known  to  many  of  our  readers  in  consequence.  Wine  sauce, 
arrowroot,  German  sauce,  or  any  other  of  the  sweet  pudding  sauces 
to  be  found  in  the  preceding  pages  of  this  chapter,  may  be  poured 
over,  or  sent  to  table  with  it. 


TBRT   GOOD   RAI8IN   PUDDINGS. 

To  three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  flour  add  four  ounces  of  fine 
erumbe  of  bread,  one  pound  of  beef-suet,  a  pound  and  six  ounces  of

veiT  sweet  wine,  or  punch  sauce ;  but  if  made  as  we  have  directed, 
will  be  much  lighter  than  if  sugar  be  mixed  with  the  other  ingre- 
dients before  it  is  boiled ;  and  we  have  found  it  generally  preferred 
to  a  richer  plum-pudding.

THB   ELEGANT   ECONOMIST'S   PUDDING. 

Butter  thickly  a  plain  mould  or  basin,  and  line  it  entirely  with

neatly  together ;  fill  it  quite  with  a  good  custard ;  lay,  first  a  buttered

the  pudding  gently  for  an  hour ;  let  it  stand  for  ten  minutes  ailer  it 
is  taken  up  b^ore  it  is  turned  out  of  the  mould.    This  is  a  more

YEOETABLE  PLUM   PUDDWO. 

{Cheap  and  good,) 

Mix  well  together  one  pound  of  smoothlv-maahed  potatoes,  half  a

they  are  stoned),  three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  susar,  eight  ounces  of

pudding  into  a  well-floured  cloth,  tie  it  up  very  closel^r,  and  boil  it 
for  four  hours.  The  correspondent  to  whom  we  are  indebted  for 
this  receipt  says,  that  the  cost  of  the  ingredients  does  not  exceed  half 
a  crown,  and  that  the  pudding  is  of  sufficient  sixe  for  a  party  of  six- 
teen persons.  We  can  vouch  for  its  excellence,  but  as  it  is  rather  apt 
to  break  when  turned  out  of  the  cloth,  a  couple  of  eggs  would  per- 
haps improve  it.  It  is  excellent  cold.  Sweetmeats,  brandy,  and 
spices  can  be  added  at  pleasure.

recommend  this  as  a  remarkably  li^t  small  rich  pudding :  it  may  be 
served  with  German,  wine,  or  punch  sauce.

A  KENTISH   WELL  PUDDING. 

Make  into  a  firm  smooth  paste,  with  cold  water,  one  pound  of  flour, 
nx  ounces  of  finely-minced  beef-suet,  three  quarters  of  a  pound  of 
emrants,  and  a  small  pinch  of  salt,  thoroughly  mixed  together., 

B  ft 


418  MODEBK  COOEEBT.  [chap.  XX. 

Form  into  a  ball  six  ounces  of  good  butter,  and  enclose  it  aecnrely 
in  about  a  third  of  the  paste  (rolled  to  a  half  inch  of  thickness),  ia 
the  same  way  that  an  apple-dumpling  is  made ;  roll  out  the  re- 
mainder of  the  paste,  and  place  the  portion  containing  the  butter  in 
the  centre  of  it,  with  the  part  where  the  edge  was  drawn  together 
turned  downwards :  gather  the  outer  crust  round  it,  and  after  oaTii^ 
moistened  the  edge,  close  it  with  ^eat  care.  Tie  the  padding 
tightly  in  a  well-floured  cloth,  and  boil  it  for  two  hours  and  a  halL 
It  must  be  dished  with  caution  that  it  may  not  break,  and  a  small  bit 
must  be  cut  directly  from  the  top,  aa  in  a  meat  pudding.  (See  page 
400). 

This  is  a  very  favourite  pudding  in  some  parts  of  England ;  the 
only  dijfficulty  in  making  or  in  serving  it,  is  to  prevent  the  escape  of 
the  butter,  which,  if  properly  secured,  will  be  found  in  a  liquid  stale 
in  the  inside,  on  opening  it.  Some  timid  cooks  fold  it  in  three 
coverings  of  paste,  the  better  to  guard  against  its  bursting  through ; 
but  there  is  no  danger  of  this  if  the  edges  of  the  crust  be  well  doeed. 
When  suet  is  objected  to,  seven  ounces  of  butter  may  be  sabatitated 
for  it.    The  currants  are  occasionally  omitted. 

Flour,  1  lb. ;  suet,  6  oz. ;  currants,  f  lb. ;  salt,  small  pinch ;  ball  of 
but      6  oz. :  2^  hours. 

ROLLED   PUDDING. 

Roll  out  thin  a  bit  of  light  puff  paste,  or  a  good  suet  crust,  and 
spread  equally  over  it  to  within  an  inch  of  the  edge,  any  kind  of  fruit 
jam.  Orange  marmalade,  and  mincemeat  make  excellent  varieties  of 
this  puddimr,  and  a  deep  layer  of  fine  brown  sugar,  flavoured  «ith 
the  grated  rind  and  strained  juice  of  one  very  large,  or  of  two  aoiall, 
lemons,  answers  for  it  extremely  well.  Roll  it  up  carefully,  pinch 
the  paste  together  at  the  ends,  fold  a  cloth  round,  secure  it  well  at 
the  ends,  and  boil  the  pudding  from  one  to  two  hours,  according  to 
its  size  and  the  nature  of  the  ingredients.  Half  a  pound  of  floor 
made  into  a  paste  with  suet  or  butter,  and  covered  with  preaervc, 
will  be  quite  sufficiently  boiled  in  an  hour  and  a  quarter. 

A   BREAD   PUDDING. 

Sweeten  a  pint  of  new  milk  with  three  ounces  of  fine  sasB, 
throw  in  a  few  grains  of  salt,  and  pour  it  boiling  on  half  a  poaod  of 
fine  and  lightly-grated  bread-crumbs ;  add  an  ounce  of  fresh  butter, 
and  cover  them  with  a  plate ;  let  them  remain  for  half  an  hour  or 
more,  and  then  stir  to  them  four  large  well-whisked  eggs,  and  a 
flavouring  of  nutmeg  or  of  lemon-rind;  pour  the  mixture  into  a 
thickly-buttered  mould  or  basin,  which  holds  a  pint  and  a  hall^  aad 
which  ou^ht  to  be  quite  full ;  tie  a  paper  and  a  cloth  tightlv  over, 
and  boil  the  pudding  for  exactly  an  hour  and  ten  minutes,  this  h 
quite  a  plain  receipt,  but  by  omitting  two  ounces  of  the  bread,  and 


CHAP.  XX.]  BOILED  PUDDINGS.  419 

idding  more  bntter,  one  egg,  a  small  ^lass  of  brandy,  the  grated  rind 
of  a  lemon,  and  as  much  sugar  as  will  sweeten  the  whole  richly,  a 
yeiy  excellent  pudding  will  be  obtained ;  candied  orange-peel  also 
has  a  good  effect  when  sliced  thinly  into  it ;  and  half  a  pound  of 
currants  is  generally  considered  a  further  improvement. 

New  milk,  1  pint ;  sugar,  3  oz. ;  salt,  few  grains ;  bread-crumbs,  *> 
lb. ;  eggs,  4  (5,  if  very  small) ;  nutmeg  or  lemon-rind  at  pleasure :  1 
hour  and  10  minutes. 

Or :  milk,  1  pint ;  bread-crumbs,  6  oz. ;  butter,  2  to  3  oz. ;  sugar, 
4  oz. ;  eggs,  5  ;  brandy,  small  glassful ;  rind.  1  lemon.  Further  ad*

A   BROWN   BREAD   PUDDING. 

To  half  a  pound  of  stale  brown  bread,  finely  and  lightly  crated,  add 
an  equal  weight  of  suet  chopped  small,  and  of  currants  deaned  and 
dried,  with  half  a  saltspoontul  of  salt,  three  ounces  of  sugar,  the 
third  of  a  small  nutmeg  grated,  two  ounces  of  candied  peel,  five 
well-beaten  eggs,  and  a  glass  of  brandy.  Mix  these  ingredients 
thoroughly,  and  boil  the  pudding  in  a  cloth  for  three  hours  and  a 
half.  Send  port  wine  sauce  to  table  with  it.  The  grated  rind  of 
a  large  lemon  may  be  added  to  this  pudding  with  good  effect 

Brown  bread,  suet,  and  currants,  each  8  oz. ;  sugar,  3  oz. ;  candied

1  wineglassful :  3^^  hours. 

A  GOOD  BOILED   RICE   PUDDING. 

Swell  gradually,*  and  boil  until  quite  soft  and  thick,  four  ounces 
and  a  half  of  whole  rice  in  a  pint  and  a  half  of  new  milk ;  sweeten 
them  with  from  three  to  four  ounces  of  sugar,  broken  small,  and  stir 
to  them  while  th^  are  still  quite  hot,  the  grated  rind  of  half  a  large 
lemon,  four  or  nve  bitter  almonds,  pounded  to  a  paste,  and  four 
lai^  well- whisked  eg^ ;  let  the  mixture  cool,  and  then  pour  it  into 
a  thickly-buttered  basm,  or  mould,  which  should  be  quite  full ;  tie  a 
buttered  paper  and  a  floured  clotli  over  it,  and  boil  the  pudding 
exactly  an  hour ;  let  it  stand  for  two  or  three  minutes  before  it  is 
turned  out,  and  serve  it  with  sweet  sauce,  fruit  syrup,  or  a  compote 
of  finesh  fruit.  An  ounce  and  a  half  of  candied  orange-rind  will 
improve  it  much,  and  a  couple  of  ounces  of  butter  may  be  added  to 
enrich  It,  when  the  receipt  without  is  considered  too  simple.  It  is 
excellent  when  made  with  milk  highly  flavoured  with  cocoa-nut,  or 
with  vanilla. 

Whole  rice,  4}  oz. ;  new  milk  (or  cocoa-nut-flavoured  milk),  H 
pint ;  sugar,  3  to  4  oz. ;  salt,  a  few  grains ;  bitter  almonds,  4  to  6 
rind  of  4  lemon;  eggs,  4 :  boiled  1  hour.

it  only  aisiuer  oxitil  it  is  done.

CHEAP   RICE  PUDDING. 

Wash  six  ounces  of  rice,  mix  it  with  three  quarten  of  a  poand  of 
raisiiis,  tie  them  in  a  well-floured  cloth,  giving  them  plenty  of  room 
to  swell ;  hoil  them  exactly  an  hour  and  three  quarters,  and  serre 
the  pudding  with  very  sweet  sauce :  this  is  a  nice  dish  for  the  nuiserj. 
A  pound  of  apples  pared,  cored,  and  quartered,  will  also  make  a 
very  wholesome  pudding,  mixed  with  the  rice,  and  boUed  from  as 
hour  and  a  quarter  to  an  hour  and  a  half;  and  suUana  raisins  and 
rice  will  give  another  good  variety  of  this  simple  pudding. 

Rice,  6  oz. ;  raisins,  j^  lb. :  2  hours.  Or,  rice,  6  oz. ;  apples,  1  lb.: 
li  to  1^  hour. 

RICE  AND   OOOSEBERRT   PUDDINCU 

Spread  six  ounces  of  rice  equally  over  a  moist  and  weU-fiomed 
pudding-cloth,  and  place  on  it  a  pint  of  green  gooseberries,  measoTBd 
after  the  heads  and  stalks  have  been  taken  off.  Gather  the  cloth  up 
carefully  round  the  fruit,  give  room  for  the  rice  to  swell,  and  boil  the 
pudding  for  an  hour  and  a  quarter.  Very  sweet  sauce,  or  plenty  of 
sugar,  should  be  eaten  with  it. 

liice,  6  oz. ;  green  gooseberries,  1  pint  *  li  hour. 

FASHIONABLE   APPLE  DUMPLINGS. 

These  are  boiled  in  small  knitted  or  closely-netted  cloths  (the 
former  have,  we  think,  the  prettiest  effect),  which  give  quite  an 
ornamental  appearance  to  an  otherwise  homely  dish.  Take  out  tbe 
cores  without  dividing  the  apples,  which  should  be  large,  and  of  a  good 
boiling  kind,  and  fill  the  cavities  with  orange  or  lemon  marmalade; 
enclose  them  in  a  good  crust  rolled  thin,  draw  the  cloths  round  them, 
tie  them  closely,  and  boil  them  for  three  quarters  of  an  hour.  Lenun 
dumplinss  may  be  boiled  in  the  same  way. 

i  to  1  bour,  if  the  apples  be  not  of  the  best  boiling  kind. 

ORANGE  SNOW-BALLS. 

Take  out  the  unhusked  grains,  and  wash  well  half  a  pound  of  rite; 
put  it  into  plenty  of  water,  and  boil  it  rather  quickly  for  tea 
minutes  ;  drain  and  let  it  cool.  Fare  four  large,  or  fire  small 
oranges,  and  clear  from  them  entirely  the  thick  white  inner  skis ; 
spread  the  rice,  in  as  many  equal  portions  as  there  are  oranges,  upon 
some  pudding  or  dumpling  cloths ;  tie  the  fruit  separately  in  these, 
and  boil  the  snow-balls  for  an  hour  and  a  half;  turn  them  carefidlr 
on  to  a  dish,  and  strew  plenty  of  sifted  sugar  over  them.  The 
oranses  carefully  pared  may  be  enclosed  in  a  thin  paste  and  boiled 
for  the  same  time. 

Kice,  8  oz. ;  China  oranges,  5 :  1  i  hour.
