APPLR  8N0W*BALL8. 

Pare  and  core  some  large  pudding-apples,  without  dividing  them, 
prepare  the  rice  as  in  the  foregoing  receipt,  enclose  them  in  it,  and 
lx>il  them  for  one  hour :  ten  minutes  less  will  be  sufficient  should  the 
fruit  be  but  of  moderate  size.  An  agreeable  addition  to  them  is  a 
slice  of  fresh  butter,  mixed  with  as  much  sugar  as  can  be  smoothly- 
blended  with  it,  and  a  flavouring  of  powdered  cinnamon,  or  of 
nutmeg :  this  must  be  sent  to  table  apart  from  them,  not  in  the  dish. 

LIGHT   CURRANT   BUMPLINOS. 

For  each  dumpling  take  three  tablespoonsful  of  flour,  two  of  finelj- 
minced  suet,  and  three  of  currants,  a  slight  pinch  of  salt,  and  as  much 
milk  or  water  as  will  make  a  very  thick  batter  of  the  ingredients. 
Tie  the  dumplings  in  well-floured  cloths,  and  boil  them  for  a  full 
hour :  they  may  oe  served  with  very  sweet  wine  sauce. 


LEMON   DUMPLINGS.      (lIGHT   AND  GOOD.) 

Mix,  with  ten  ounees  of  fine  bread-crumbs,  half  a  pound  of  beef- 
suet,  chopped  extremely  small,  one  large  tablespoon^  of  flour,  the 
grated  rinds  of  two  small  lemons,  or  of  a  very  large  one,  four  ounces 
of  pounded  sugar,  three  large  or  four  small  eggs  beaten  and  strained, 
and  last  of  al^  the  juice  of  the  lemons,  or  part  of  it,  also  strained. 
Divide  these  into  four  equal  portions,  tie  th«m  in  well-floured  cloths, 
and  bo3  them  an  hour.  The  dumplings  will  be  extremely  light  and 
delicate :  if  wished  very  sweet,  more  sugar  must  be  addea  to  them. 
The  sjmip  of  preserved  ginger  would  be  both  a  wholesome  and  ap- 
propriate sauce  for  them. 

SUFFOLK,   OR   HARD   DUMPLINGS. 

Mix  a  little  salt  with  some  flour,  and  make  it  into  a  smooth  and 
rather  lithe  paste,  with  cold  water  or  skimmed  milk;  form  it  into 
dumplings,  and  throw  them  into  boiling  water  :  in  half  an  hour  they 
will  be  ready  to  serve.  A  better  kmd  of  dumpling  is  made  by 
adding  sufficient  milk  to  the  flour  to  form  a  thick  batter,  and  then 
tying  the  dumplings  in  small  well -floured  cloths.  In  Suffolk  farm- 
houses, they  are  served  with  the  dripping-pan  gravy  of  roast  meat ; 
and  they  are  sometimes  made  very  small  indeed,  and  boiled  with 
stewed  shin  of  beef. 

NORFOLK  DUMPLINGS. 

Take  a  pound  of  dough  from  a  baking  of  very  light  white  bread, 
and  divide  it  into  six  equal  parts ;  mould  these  into  dumplings,  drop 
them  into  a  pan  of  fast  boiling  water,  and  boil  them  quicldy  from 


422  MODERN  COOKERY.  [cnir.xi 

twelve  to  fifteen  minutes.  Send  them  to  table  the  instcat  they  ait 
dished,  with  wine  sauce  or  raspberry  vinegar.  In  some  ooanties  thej 
are  eaten  with  melted  butter,  well  sweetened,  and  mixed  with  a  littk 
vinegar.  They  must  never  be  cut,  but  should  be  torn  apart  with  i 
cou^e  of  forks. 

SWEET   BOILED   PATTIES,      (goOd) 

Mix  into  a  very  smooth  paste,  three  ounces  of  finely-minced  snet 
with  eight  of  flour,  and  a  light  pinch  of  salt ;  divide  it  into  fbmteen 
balls  of  equal  size,  roll  them  out  quite  thin  and  round,  moisten  the 
edges,  put  a  little  preserve  into  each,  close  the  patties  very  seeardr 
to  prevent  Hs  escape,  throw  them  into  a  pan  of  boiling  water,  and  in 
from  ten  to  twelve  minutes  lift  them  out,  and  serve  them  instantlr. 
Butter-crust  may  be  used  for  them  instead  of  suet  but  it  will  not  be 
so  light. 

FE)ur,  8  oz. ;  suet,  3  oz. ;  liide  salt ;  divided  into  fourteen  portioDs: 
boiled  10  to  12  minutes. 

BOILED   RICE  TO   BE  SERVED   WITH   STEWED   FRUITS^  PBESKBTB^ 

OR   RASPBERRY   VINEGAR. 

Take  out  the  discoloured  grains  from  half  a  pound  of  good  liee; 
and  wash  it  in  several  waters ;  tie  it  very  loo8el)r  in  a  pudding  dotli, 
put  it  into  cold  water ;  heat  it  slowly,  and  boil  it  for  <juite  an  boor, 
it  will  then  be  quite  solid  and  resemble  a  pudding  m  appcaraoce. 
Sufficient  room  must  be  given  to  allow  the  grain  to  swell  to  its  fall 
size,  or  it  will  be  hard ;  but  too  much  space  will  render  the  whole 
watery.  With  a  little  experience,  the  cook  will  easily  ascertain  the 
exact  degree  to  be  allowed  for  it.  Four  ounces  of  rice  will  require 
quite  three  quarters  of  an  hour's  boiling ;  a  little  more  or  less  of  time 
will  sometimes  be  needed,  from  the  dif^rence  of  quality  in  the  grain. 
It  should  be  put  into  an  abundant  quantity  of  water,  which  raooU 
be  cold,  and  then  very  slowly  heated.

When  a  puddins  is  sufficiently  browned  on  the  surface  (that  is  to

which  is  sufficiently  solid  to  allow  of  it,  should  be  turned  on  to  a

A   BAKED   PLUM 'PUDDING  EN   MOULE,   OR   MOULDED. 

Mingle  thoroughly  in  a  large  pan  or  bowl  half  a  pound  of  th« 
nicest  beef-kidney  suet  minced  yery  small,  half  a  pound  of  carefully 
stoned  raisins,  as  many  currants,  u>ur  ounces  of  pounded  sugar,  half 
a  pound  of  flour,  two  ounces  of  candied  citron  and  lemon  or  oru^ 
rind,  four  large  well  whisked  eggs,  a  small  cup  of  milk,  a  glass  of 
brandy,  a  tiny  pinch  of  salt,  and  some  nutmeg  or  powdered  ginger. 
Beat  the  whole  up  lightly,  pour  it  into  a  well- buttered  mould  or 
cake-tin  and  bake  it  in  a  moderate  oven  from  an  hour  and  a  half  to 
two  hours.  Turn  it  from  the  mould  and  send  it  quickly  to  tahie 
with  Devonshire  cream^  or  melted  apricot  marmalade  for  sauce. 

THE  PBIKTEff's   PUDDIKG. 

Grate  yery  lightly  six  ounces  of  the  crumb  of  a  stale  loa(  and  pat 
it  into  a  deep  dish.  Dissolve  in  a  quart  of  cold  new  milk  four 
ounces  of  good  Lisbon  sugar ;  add  it  to  five  large,  well-whisked  ^ga, 
strain,  and  mix  them  with  the  bread-crumbs ;  stir  in  two  ounces  of  a 
fresh  finely-grated  cocoa-nut;  add  a  flavouring  of  nutmeg  or  of 
lemon-rind,  and  the  slightest  pinch  of  salt ;  let  the  puddins  stand  for 
a  couple  of  hours  to  soak  the  bread ;  and  bake  it  in  a  gentle  oyen  for 
three-quarters  of  an  hour :  it  will  be  excellent  if  carefully  made,  and 
not  too  quickly  baked.  When  the  cocoa-nut  is  not  at  hand,  as 
ounce  of  butter  just  dissolved,  should  be  poured  over  the  dish  before 
the  crumbs  are  put  into  it ;  and  the  rind  of  an  entire  lemon  may  be 
used  to  give  it  flavour ;  but  the  cocoa-nut  imparts  a  peculiar  ricliDei 
when  it  is  good  and  fresh. 

Bread-crumbs,  6  oz. ;  new  milk,  1  quart;  sugar,  4  oz.;  eggs,  Sj 
cocoa-nut,  2  oz.  (or  rind,  1  large  lemon,  and  1  oz.  butter) ;  su^btesl 
pinch  of  salt :  to  stand  2  hours.    Baked  in  gentle  oven  full  f  hour. 

0&«.— When  a  very  sweet  pudding  is  liked,  the  propoitioii  of 
sugar  mny  be  increased.

ALMOND   PT7DDINQ. 

On  two.  ounces  of.  fine  bread-crumbs  pour  a  pint  of  boiling  cream, 
aiid  let  them  remain  until  nearly  cold,  then  mix  them  very  gradually 
vrith  half  a  pound  of  sweet  and  six  bitter  almonds  pounded  to  the 
smoothest  paste,  with  a  little  orange-flower  water,  or  with  a  few  drops 
of  spring* water,  jUst  to  prevent  their  oiling ;  stir  to  them  by  degrees 
the  well-beaten  yolks  oi  seven  and  the  whites  of  three  eggs,  six 
ounces  of  sifted  sugar,  and  four  of  clarified  butter ;  turn  the  mixture 
into  A  very  dean  stewpan,  and  stir  it  without  ceasing  over  a  slow  fire 
until  it  becomes  thick,  but  on  no  account  allow  it  to  boil.  When  it 
is  tolerably  cool  add  a  glass  of  brandy,  or  half  a  one  of  noyau,  pour 
^e  pudding  into  a  disn  lined  with  very  thin  puff  paste,  and  bake  it 
half  an  hour  in  a  moderate  oven.

THE   YOUNQ   WIFe's   PUDPJ^G. 

(Avihor's  Receipts) 

Break  separately  into  a  cup  four  perfectly  sweet  eggs,  and  with 
the  point  of  a  small  three-pronged  fork  clear  them  from  the  specks. 
Throw  them,  as  they  are  done,  into  a  large  basin,  or  a  bowl,  and 
beat  them  up  lightly  for  four  or  five  minutes ;  then  add  by  degrees 
two  ounces  and  a  half  of  pounded  sugar,  with  a  very  small  pinch  of 
salt,  and  whisk  the  mixture  well,  holding  the  fork  rather  loosely 
between  the  thumb  and  fingers ;  next,  grate  in  the  rind  of  a  quite 
fresh  lemon,  or  substitute  for  it  a  tablespoon ful  of  lemon-brandy,  or 
of  orange-flower  water,  which  should  be  thrown  in  by  degrees,  and 
stirred  briskly  to  the  eggs.  Add  a  pint  of  cold  new  milk,  and  pour 
the  pudding  into  a  well  buttered  dish.  Slice  some  stale  bread,  some'* 
thing  more  than  a  quarter  of  an  inch  thick,  and  with  a  very  small 
cake-cutter  cut  suflicient  rounds  from  it  to  cover  the  top  of  the 
pudding ;  butter  them  thickly  with  good  butter ;  lay  them,  with  the 
dry  side  undermost,  upon  the  pudding,  sift  sugar  thickly  on  them, 
and  set  the  dish  gently  into  a  Dutch  or  American  oven,  which 
should  be  placed  at  the  distance  of  a  foot  or  more  from  a  moderate 
fire.  An  hour  of  very  slow  baking  will  be  just  sufficient  to  render 
the  pudding  firm  throughout ;  but  should  the  fire  be  fierce,  or  the 
oven  placea  too  near  it,  tne  receipt  will  fail. 

*  Obs. — We  give  minute  directions  for  this  dish,  because  though 
simple,  it  is  very  delicate  and  good,  and  the  same  instructions  will 
serve  for  all  the  varieties  of  it  which  follow.  The  cook  who  desires 
to  succeed  with  them,  must  take  the  trouble  to  regulate  properly  tha 


426  MODERN  COOKEBT.  -  [cHAF.  XZL 

heat  of  the  oven  in  which  they  are  baked.  When  it  is  necesarj  t« 
place  them  in  that  of  the  kitchen-range  the  door  should  be  left  <^iea 
for  a  time  to  cool  it  down  (should  it  be  very  hot),  before  they  are 
placed  in  it ;  and  they  may  be  set  upon  a  plate  or  dish  rcvernd,  if 
the  iron  should  still  remain  greatly  heated. 

THE  GOOD   daughter's   MINCEMEAT   PDDDING. 

(^Auihar^s  Receipt,^ 

Lay  into  a  rather  deep  tart-dish  some  thin  slices  of  French  idl 
very  slightly  spread  with  butter  and  covered  with  a  thick  layer  of 
mincemeat ,  place  a  second  tier  lightly  on  these,  covered  in  the  suat 
way  with  the  mincemeat ;  then  pour  gently  in  a  custard  made  irith 
three  well- whisked  e^gs,  three-quarters  of  a  pint  of  new  milk  or  thin 
cream,  the  slightest  pinch  of  salt,  and  two  ounces  of  sugar.  Let  the 
pudding  stand  to  soak  for  an  hour,  then  bake  it  genUy  until  it  is 
quite  firm  in  the  centre :  this  will  be  in  firom  three-quarters  of  aa 
hour  to  a  full  hour. 

MRS.   HOWITT's  pudding, 

(^Authar^s  Receipt,^ 

Butter  lightly,  on  both  sides,  some  evenly  cut  slices  of  roll,  or  of 
light  bread  freed  from  crust,  and  spread  the  tops  thickly  but  uni- 
formly with  good  orange-marmalade.  Prepare  as  much  only  in  thii 
way  as  will  cover  the  surface  of  the  pudding  without  the  edges  of 
the  bread  overlaying  each  other,  as  this  would  make  it  sink  to  the 
bottom  of  the  dish.  Add  the  same  custard  as  for  the  minoemeit- 
pudding,  but  flavour  it  with  French  brandy  only.  Let  it  stand  for 
an  hour,  then  place  it  gently  in  a  slow  oven  and  bake  it  until  it  is 
quite  sei,  but  no  longer.  It  is  an  excellent  and  delicate  pudding 
when  properly  baked ;  but  like  all  which  are  composed  in  pait  i 
custard,  it  will  be  spoiled  by  a  fierce  degree  of  heat.  The  brad 
should  be  of  a  light  clear  brown,  and  the  custard,  under  it,  smoodi 
and  firm.  This  may  be  composed,  at  choice,  of  the  yolks  of  four  lod 
whites  of  two  eggs,  thoroughly  whisked,  first  without  and  then  with 
two  tablespoonsful  of  fine  sugar :  to  these  the  milk  or  cream  say 
then  be  added. 

AN  EXCELLENT  LEMON  PUDDING, 

Beat  well  together  four  ounces  of  fresh  butter  creamed,  and  eight 
of  sifted  sugar ;  to  these  add  gradually  the  yolks  of  six  sod  the 
whites  of  two  eggs,  with  the  grated  rind  and  the  strained  juice  of  one 
large  lemon : — this  last  must  be  added  by  slow  degrees,  and  rtined

I.EMON  SVET   PUDDING. 

To  half  a  pound  of  finely  grated  bread-crumbs,  add  six  ounces  of 
fresh  beef-kidney  suet,  free  from  skin,  and  minced  very  small,  a 
quarter  of  a  pound  of  castor  sugar,  six  ounces  of  currants,  the  grated 
rind  and  the  strained  juice  of  a  large  lemon,  and  four  full-sized  or 
five  small  well-beaten  esgs;  pour  these  ingredients  into  a  thickly- 
battered  pan,  and  bake  the  pudding  for  an  hour  in  a  brisk  oven,  but 
draw  it  towards  the  mouth  when  it  is  of  a  fine  brown  colour.  Turn 
it  from  the  dish  before  it  is  served,  and  strew  sifted  sugar  over  it  or 
not,  at  pleasure :  two  ounces  more  of  suet  can  be  added  when  a 
larger  proportion  is  liked.  The  pudding  is  very  good  without  the 
carrants. 

Bread-crumbs,  8  oz. ;  beef-suet,  6  oz.;  pounded  sugar,  3^  os.; 
kmon,  1  iarge;  currants,  6  oz. ;  eggs,  4  large,  or  6  smiall :  1  hour, 
brisk  oven. 

BAKEWELL   PUDDING. 

This  pudding  is  famous  not  onl^  in  Derbyshire,  but  in  several  of 
our  northern  counties,  where  it  is  usually  served  on  all  holiday- 
occasions.  Line  a  shallow  tart-dish  with  quite  an  inch-deep  layer  of 
several  kinds  of  good  preserve  mixed  together,  and  intermingle  with 
them  from  two  to  three  ounces  of  candied  citron  or  orange-rind. 
Beat  well  the  yolks  of  ten  eggs,  and  add  to  them  gradually  half  a 
pound  of  sifted  sugar ;  when  they  are  well  mixed,  pour  in  by  degrees 
naif  a  pound  of  good  clarified  butter,  and  a  little  ratifia  or  any  other 
flavour  that  may  be  preferred ;  fill  the  dish  two-thirds  full  with  this 
mixture,  and  bake  tne  pudding  for  nearly  an  hour  in  a  moderate 
oven.    Half  the  quantity  will  be  sufficient  for  a  small  dish. 

Mixed  preserves,  1)  to  2  lbs. ;  yolks  of  e^gs,  10 ;  sugar,  |  lb. ; 
butter,  \  lb. ;  ratifia,  lemon-brandy,  or  other  flavouring,  to  the  taste : 
baked,  moderate  oven,  f  to  1  hour. 

Obs. — ^This  is  a  rich  and  expensive,  but  not  very  refined  pudding. 
A  variation  of  it,  known  in  the  south  as  an  Alderman's  Pudding,  is 
live  think,  superior  to  it.  It  is  made  without  the  candied  peel,  and 
with  a  layer  of  apricot-jam  only,  six  ounces  of  butter,  six  of  sugar, 
the  yolks  of  six,  and  the  whites  of  two  eggs.

from  thirty  minutes  to  a  full  hour,  according  to  the  quantity  aod

A  GOOD  BAKED  BREAD  PUDDING. 

Pour,  quite  boiling,  on  six  ounces  (or  three  quarters  of  a  pint)  of

to  a  firm  froth,  and  mixed  at  the  instant  with  three  large  tablespoons- 
fnl  of  sifted  sugar.  Bake  the  puddins  for  half  an  hour  in  a  moderate 
DTen.  The  icing  may  be  omitted,  and  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  butter, 
jtist  warmed,  put  into  the  dish  before  the  pudding,  and  plenty  of 
sugar  sifted  over  it  just  as  it  is  sent  to  the  oven,  or  it  may  be  made 
iritbout  either.

A   GOOD   SEMOULINAy   OR   80UJEE  PUDDING.

tablespoonsful  of  semouuna,  and  stir  them  toother  as  this  is  done,

oven.    Boil  the  soujee  exactly  as  the  semoulina.

FRENCH  SEMOULINA   PUDDING. 

Or  Gateau  de  Semoute. 

Infuse  by  the  side  of  the  fire  in  a  quart  of  new  milk,  the  very 
thin  rind  of  a  fine  fresh  lemon,  and  when  it  has  stood  for  half  aa 
hour  bring  it  slowly  to  a  boil :  simmer  it  for  four  or  five  minutes,

stirring  it  all  the  time,  five  ounces  of  the  best  quality  of  semoulina  ;* 
let  it  boil  over  a  gentle  fire  for  ten  minutes,  then  add  four  ounces  of

quarter-teaspoonful  of  salt;  boil  the  mixture  for  two  or  thne 
additional  minutes,  keeping  it  stirred  without  ceasing ;  take  it  firm

bitter  almonds,  pounded  with  a  little  sugar,  will  heighten  the  fiavoar

8AXE-aOTHA  PUDDING,   OR   TOURTE. 

Blanch  and  pound  to  the  smoothest  possible  paste,  a  couple  of 
dances  of  Jordan  almonds,  and  four  or  five  bitter  ones ;  add  to  them, 
spoonful  by  spoonful  quite  at  first,  four  eggs  which  have  been 
whisked  very  light;  throw  in  gradually  two  ounces  of  pounded 
sugar,  and  then  four  ounces  of  the  finest  bread  crumbs.  Just  melt, 
but  without  heating,  two  ounces  of  fresh  butter,  and  add  it  in  vezy 
small  portions  to  the  other  ingredients,  beating  each  well  to  them 
until  it  ceases  to  appear  on  the  surface.  Pour  the  paste  thus 
prepared  upon  a  pint  of  red  currants,  ready  mixed  in  a  tart-dish  with 
four  ounces  of  pounded  sugar,  and  bake  them  gently  for  about  half 
an  hour.  Baspberries  and  currants  mixed,  and  Kentish  or  morella 
cherries,  will  make  most  excellent  varieties  of  this  dish :  the  Kentish 
cherries  should  be  stoned  for  it,  the  morellas  left  entire.  Should  the 
paste  be  considered  too  rich,  a  part  or  the  whole  of  the  butter  can  be 
omitted ;  or  again,  it  may  on  occasion  be  made  without  the  almonds ; 
but  the  reader  is  recommended  to  try  the  receipt  in  the  first  instance 
without  any  variation  from  it-  The  crust  wilt  be  found  delicious  if 
well  made.  Like  all  mixtures  of  the  kind  it  must  be  kept  light  by 
constant  beating,  as  the  various  ingredients  are  added  to  the  eggs, 
which  should  themselves  be  whisked  to,  a  very  light  froth  before 
they  are  used.

BADEN-BADEN   PUDDINQS. 

Prepare  the  same  paste  as  for  the  preceding  receipt,  and  add  to  it 
by  degrees  a  couple  of  tablespoonsfuls  of  fine  raspberry,  strawberry, 
or  apricot  jam,  which  has  previously  been  worked  smooth  with  the 
back  of  a  spoon ;  half  fill  some  buttered  patty-pans  or  small  cups 
with  the  mixture  and  bake  the  puddings  in  a  gentle  oven  from 
fifteen  to  twenty  minutes,  or  rather  longer  should  it  be  very  slow. 
For  variety,  omit  the  preserve,  and  flavour  the  puddings  with  the 
lightly  grated  rind  of^a  fresh  lemon,  and  with  an  ounce  or  so  of 
candied  peel  shred  small;  or  with  a  little  vanilla  pounded  with  a 
lump  or  two  of  sugar,  and  sifted  through  a  hair  sieve ;  or  with  from 
three  to  four  drachms  of  orange  flowers  pralijieis  reduced  to  powder ; 
or  serve  them  quite  plain  with  a  iruit  sauce.

SUTHERLAND   OR  CASTLE  FITDDIN08. 

Take  an  equal  weight  of  eggs  in  the  shell,  of  good  hatter,  of  fine 
dry  flour,  and  of  sifted  sugar.  First,  whisk  the  eggs  for  ten  minutes 
or  until  thej  appear  extremely  lights  then  throw  in  the  sugar 
hy  degrees,  and  continue  the  whisking  for  four  or  five  minutes; 
next,  strew  in  the  flour,  also  gradtudly,  and  when  it  appean 
smoothly  hlended  with  the  other  ingredients,  pour  the  butter  to 
them  in  small  portions,  each  of  which  should  be  beaten  in  until 
there  is  no  appearance  of  it  left.  It  should  previously  be  just 
liquefied  with  the  least  possible  degree  of  heat:  tnis  may  be  effected 
by  putting  it  into  a  well-warmed  saucepan,  and  shaking  it  round 
until  it  is  dissolved.  A  grain  or  two  of  salt  should  be  thrown  in 
with  the  flour;  and  the  rind  of  half  a  fine  lemon  rasped  on  sugar  or 
grated,  or  some  pounded  mace,  or  any  other  fiavour  can  be  added  at 
choice.  Four  the  mixture  directly  it  is  ready  into  well-buttered 
cups,  and  bake  the  puddings  from  twenty  to  twenty-flve  minutei. 
When  cold  they  resemble  good  pound  cakes,  and  may  be  served  ss 
such.    Wine  sauce  should  be  sent  to  table  with  them. 

Eggs,  4;  their  weight  in  flour,  sugar,  and  butter;  UtUe  ali; 
flavouring  of  pounded  mace  or  lemon-rind. 

06«.~  Three  egg8  are  sufficient  for  a  small  dish  of  these  pudding 
They  may  be  varied  with  an  ounce  or  two  of  candied  citron;  or 
with  a  spoonful  of  brandy,  or  a  little  orange-flower  water.  The 
mode  we  have  given  of  making  them  will  be  found  perfectly  sne- 
oessful  if  our  directions  be  followed  with  exactness.  In  a  alow  oven 
they  will  not  be  too  much  baked  in  half  an  hour. 

MADELEINE  PUDDINGS. 

To  he  served  cold. 

Take  the  same  ingredients  as  for  the  Sutl^rland  puddings,  but 
clarify  an  additional  ounce  of  butter ;  skim,  and  then  fill  some  round 
tin  pattypans  with  it  almost  to  the  brim ;  pour  it  from  one  to  the 
other  until  all  have  received  a  sufficient  coating  to  prevent  the 
puddings  from  adhering  to  them,  and  leave  half  a  teaspoonful  in 
each ;  mix  the  remainder  with  the  eggs,  sugar,  and  flour,  beat  the 
whole  up  ver^  lightly,  fill  the  pans  about  two-thirds  full,  and  pat 
them  directly  into  a  rather  brisk  oven,  but  draw  them  towards  the 
mouth  of  it  when  they  are  sufficiently  coloured;  from  fifteen  to 
eighteen  minutes  will  bake  them.  Turn  them  out,  and  drain  them 
on  a  sheet  of  paper.  When  they  are  quite  cold,  with  the  pcmit  ot 
the  knife  take  out  a  portion  of  the  tops,  hollow  the  puddini;^  a  little, 
and  fill  them  with  rich  apricot-jam,  well  mixed  with  half  its  weight 
of  pounded  almonds,  of  which  two  in  every  ounce  should  be  biner 
ones.

A   GOOD   GROUND   RICB   PUDDING. 

IVIiz  yery  smoothly  five  ounces  of  flour  of  rice  (or  of  ground  rice,

half  more  which  is  boiling  fast ;  keep  it  stirred  constantly  over  a 
gentle  fire  from  ten  to  twelve  minutes,  and  be  particularly  careful 
not  to  let  it  bum  to  the  pan ;  add  to  it  before  it  is  taken  from  the 
fire,  a  quarter  of  a  pound  of  good  butter,  from  five  to  six  ounces  of 
sugar,  roughly  powdered,  and  a  few  grains  of  salt ;  turn  it  into  a 
pan,  and  stir  it  for  a  few  minutes,  to  prevent  its  hardening  at  the 
top ;  then  mix  with  it,  by  degrees  but  quickly,  the  yolks  of  eight 
eggs,  and  the  whites  of  two,  the  grated  or  rasped  rind  of  a  fine 
lemon,  and  a  glass  of  brandy.  Lay  a  border  of  rich  paste  round  a 
buttered  dish,  pour  in  the  pudding,  strain  a  little  clarified  butter  over 
the  top,  moisten  the  paste  with  a  brush,  or  small  bunch  of  feathers 
dipped  in  cold  water,  and  sift  plenty  of  sugar  on  it,  but  less  over  the 
pudding  itself.  Send  it  to  a  very  gentle  oven  to  be  baked  for  three- 
quarters  of  an  hour. 

Rice-flour  (or  ground  rice),  5  oz. ;  new  milk,  1  quart :  10  to  12 
minutes.  Butter,  4  oz. ;  sugar,  5  to  6  oz. ;  salt,  i  saltspoonful ;  yolks, 
8  ^gs ;  whites,  2 ;  rind,  1  large  lemon ;  brandy,  large  wineglassful : 
f  hour,  slaw  oven. 

Obs. — These  proportions  are  sufficient  for  a  pudding  of  larser  size 
than  those  served  usually  at  elegant  tables;  they  will  mSLe  two 
small  ones ;  or  two-thirds  of  the  quantity  may  be  taken  for  one  of 
moderate  size.  Lemon-brandy  or  ratifia,  or  a  portion  of  each,  may 
be  used  to  give  it  flavour,  witn  good  effect ;  and  it  may  be  enriched, 
if  thb  be  desired,  by  adding  to  the  other  ingredients  from  three  to 
four  ounces  of  Jordan  almonds,  finely  pounded,  and  by  substituting 
cream  for  half  of  the  milk. 

COMMON   GROUND   RICR   PUDDING. 

One  pint  and  a  half  of  milk,  three  ounces  and  a  half  of  rice,  three 
of  Lisbon  sugar,  one  and  a  half  of  butter,  some  nutmeg,  or  lemon- 
grate,  and  four  eggs,  baked  slowly  for  half  an  hour,  or  more,  if  not 
quite  fijrm. 

GREEN   GOOSEBERRY   PUDDING. 

Boil  together,  from  ten  to  twelve  minutes,  a  pound 'of  green 
gooseberries,  five  ounces  of  sugar,  and  rather  more  than  a  quarter  of 
a  pint  of  water :  then  beat  the  fruit  to  a  mash,  and  stir  to  it  an 
ounce  and  a  half  of  fresh  butter ;  when  nearly,  or  quite  cold,  add

until  tender,  sweeten  it  with  three  onnces  of  incur,  stir  to  H. 
gradually,  three  large,  or  four  small  eggs,  beaten  and  strained;  add 
Krated  nutmeg,  lemon  rind,  or  cinnamon  to  giye  it  flavonr,  ud 
oake  it  one  hour  in  a  gentle  oven. 

Rice,  6  oz.;  in  water,  8  to  10  minutes.  Milk,  1  quart:  |  to  1

lemon-rind,  or  cinnamon :  bake  1  hour,  gentle  oven. 

QUITE   CHEAP   BICB   PUDDING. 

Boil  the  rice  in  water,  as  for  a  currie,  and  while  it  is  still  wann, 
mix  with  it  a  pint  and  a  half  of  milk,  and  three  fresh  or  four  or  five 
French  eggs  (at  many  seasons  of  the  year  these  last,  which  ire 
always  cheap,  are  very  good^  and  answer  excellently  for  puddings.) 
Sweeten  it  with  pale  brown  sugar,  grate  nutm^  on  the  top,  sod 
bake  it  dowly  until  it  is  firm  in  every  part. 

RICHER   RICE   PUDDING. 

Wash  very  clean  four  ounces  of  whole  rice,  pour  on  it  a  i»nt  and 
a  half  of  new  milk,  and  stew  it  slowly  till  quite  tender ;  before  it  is 
taken  from  the  fire,  stir  in  two  ounces  of  nxxi  butter,  and  three  c£ 
sugar ;  and  when  it  has  cooled  a  little,  add  four  well-whisked  Cjggs, 
and  the  grated  rind  of  half  a  lemon.  Bake  the  pudding  in  a  gentk 
oven  from  thirty  to  forty  minutes.  As  rice  requires  long  boilug  ta 
render  it  soft  in  milk,  it  may  be  partially  stewed  in  water,  the 
quantity  of  milk  diminished  to  a  pint,  and  a  little  thick  sweet  creiDi 
mixed  with  it,  before  the  other  ingredients  are  added. 

Kice,  4  oz  ;  new  milk,  1^  pint ;  butter,  2  oz. ;  sugar,  3  oz. ;  eggs,  4; 
rind  of  i  lemon :  30  to  40  minutes,  slow  oven. 

RICE   PUDDING   MERINQui. 

Swell  gently  four  ounces  of  Carolina  rice  in  a  pint  and  a  quarter 
of  milk  or  of  thin  cream ;  let  it  cool  a  little,  and  stir  to  it  an  ouooe 
and  a  half  of  butter,  three  of  pounded  sugar,  a  grain  or  two  of  sut, 
the  grated  lind  of  a  small  lemon,  and  the  yolks  of  four  lai^  or  cl 
five  small  eggs.  Four  the  mixture  into  a  well-buttered  d^,  and 
lay  lightly  and  equally  over  the  top  the  whites  of  four  eggs  benea 
as  for  sponge  cakes,  and  mixed  at  the  instant  with  from  four  to  five 
heaped  tablespoonsful  of  sifted  sugar.  Bake  the  pudding  half  ta 
hour  in  a  moderate  oven,  but  do  not  allow  the  meringue  to  be  too 
deeply  coloured;  it  should  be  of  a  clear  brown,  and  very  cn^ 
Berve  it  directly  it  is  taken  from  the  oven. 

Rice,  4  oz. ;  miik,  or  cream,  1^  pint;  butter,  1|  oz. ;  sugar,  3  ol; 
rind,  1  lemon ;  yolks  of  eggs,  4  or  6  ;  the  whites  beaten  to  snow,  and 
mixed  with  as  many  tablespoonsful  of  sifted  sugar :  baked  {  boor, 
moderate  oven.

CAKB   AND  CUSTARD,   AND   VARIOUS  OTHER   INEXPEf^SIVB 

PUDDINGS. 

Even  when  very  dry,  the  remains  of  a  sponffe  or  a  Savoy  cake 
will  serve  excellently  for  a  pudding,  if  lightly  hroken  up,  or 
erumbledf  and  intermixed  or  not,  with  a  few  ratinas  or  macaroons, 
which  should  also  be  broken  up.  A  custard  composed  of  four  eggs 
to  the  pint  of  milk  if  small,  and  three  if  very  large  and  fresh,  and 
not  very  highly  sweetened,  should  be  poured  over  the  cake  half  an 
hour  at  least  before  it  is  placed  in  the  oven  (which  should  be  slow') ; 
and  any  flavour  given  to  it  which  may  be  liked.  An  economical  and 
clever  cook  will  seldom  be  at  a  loss  for  compounding  an  inexpensive 
and  good  pudding  in  this  way.  More  or  less  of  the  cake  can  be 
used  as  may  be  convenient.  Fart  of  a  mould  of  sweet  rice  or  the 
remains  of  a  dish  of  Aroci  Doci  (see  Chapter  XXIII.),  and  various 
ether  preparations  may  be  turned  to  account  in  a  similar  manner ; 
but  the  custard  should  be  perfectly  and  equally  mingled  with  what- 
ever other  ingredients  are  used.  Macaroni  boiled  tender  in  milk, 
or  in  milk  and  water,  will  make  an  excellent  pudding;  and  sago 
stewed  very  thick,  will  supply  another ;  the  custard  may  be  mixed 
with  this  last  while  it  is  still  just  warm.  Two  ounces  well  washed, 
and  slowly  heated  in  a  pint  of  liquid,  will  be  tender  in  from  fifteen 
to  twenty  minutes.  All  these  puddings  will  require  a  gentle  oven, 
and  will  be  ready  to  serve  when  they  are  firm  in  the  centre,  and  do 
not  stick  to  a  knife  when  plunged  into  it 

BAKED   APPLB   PUDDING^   OR   CUSTARD. 

Weigh  a  pound  of  good  boiling  apples  after  they  are  pared  and 
cored,  and  stew  them  to  a  perfectly  smooth  maniialade,  with  six 
ounces  of  sugar,  and  a  spoonful  or  two  of  wine ;  stir  them  often  that 
they  may  not  stick  to  the  pan-  Mix  with  them  while  they  are 
still  quite  hot,  three  ounces  of  butter,  the  grated  rind  and  the 
■trained  juice  of  a  lemon,  and  lastly,  stir  in  by  degrees  the  well- 
beaten  yolks  of  five  eggs,  and  a  dessertspoonful  of  flour,  or  in  lieu  of 
the  last,  three  or  fourNaples*  biscuits,  or  macaroons  crushed  small. 
Bake  the  pudding  for  a  full  half  hour  in  a  moderate  oven,  or  longer

two  ounces  aad  a  half  of  very  fine  bread-crumbs,  and  foor  vdl 
whisked  eggs.  Bake  the  pudding  gently  from  half  to  three-quarten 
of  an  hour.  To  make  a  finer  one  of  the  kind,  work  the  fridi 
through  a  sieve,  mix  it  with  four  or  five  crushed  Naples  hiscnits, 
and  use  double  the  quantity  of  butter. 

Green  gooseberries,  1  lb. ;  sugar,  6  oz. ;  water,  full  {  pint:  10  to

POTATO   PUDDING.

crated  rind  of  a  small  lemon.  Four  the  mixture  into  a  well-buttend 
dish,  and  bake  it  in  a  moderate  oven  for  nearly  three-quarten  of  ii 
hour.  It  should  be  turned  out  and  sent  to  table  with  fine  sogar 
sifted  over  it ;  or  for  variety,  red  currant  jelly,  or  any  other  preserve, 
may  be  spread  on  it  as  soon  as  it  is  dished. 

Potatoes,  H  lb.;  butter,  3  oz. ;  sugar,  5  or  6  oz.;  ^gs,  5  or  6;

DUTCH   CUSTARD^   OR   BAKED   RASPBERRY   FJTDDISQ, 

Lay  into  a  tart-dish  a  border  of  puff-paste,  and  a  pint  and  a  bslf  of 
freshly-gathered  raspberries,  well  mixed  with  three  ounces  of  sngir. 
Whisk  thoroughly  six  lar^e  eggs  with  three  ounces  more  of  sogir, 
and  pour  it  over  the  fruit :  biuce  the  pudding  from  twenty-five  to 
thirty  minutes  in  a  moderate  oven. 

Break  the  eggs  one  at  a  time  into  a  cup,  and  with  the  point  of  a 
small  three-pronged  fork  take  off  the  specks  or  germs,  before  tb^ 
are  beaten,  as  we  have  directed  in  page  424. 

Raspberries,  li  pint;  sugar,  6  oz.;  eggs,  6  :  25  to  30  minutes. 

OABRIELLe's   pudding,   or   sweet   casserole    of   RICE- 

Wash  half  a  pound  of  the  best  Carolina  rice,  drain  it  on  a  bnr- 
sieve,  put  it  into  a  very  clean  stewpan  or  saucepan,  and  pour  on  k  a 
quart  of  cold  new  milk.  Stir  them  well  together,  and  place  thai 
near  the  fire  that  the  rice  may  swell  very  gradually ;  then  ki  it 
simmer  as  gently  as  possible  for  about  half  an  hour,  or  nntil  it  \xgm 
to  be  quite  tender ;  mix  with  it  then,  two  ounces  of  fresh  butter  la^ 
two  and  a  half  of  pounded  sugar,  and  let  it  continue  to  simmeT  softh 
until  it  is  dry  and  sufficiently  tender,*  to  be  easily  crushed  to  a 
smooth  paste  with  a  strong  wooden  spoon.  Work  it  to  this  pood 
and  then  let  it  cool.  Before  it  is  taken  from  the  fire,  scrape  into  it 
the  outside  of  some  sugar  which  has  been  rubbed  upon  the  lind  of  a 
fresh  lemon.  Have  r^y  a  tin  mould  of  pretty  form,  well  buttcfed 
in  every  part ;  press  the  rice  into  it  while  it  is  still  warm,  smooth  tk 
surface,  and  let  it  remain  until  cold.  Should  the  mould  be  one  wiixk 
opens  at  the  ends,  like  that  shown  in  the  plate  at  page  344,  the  puddjoi 
will  come  out  easily ;  but  if  it  should  be  in  a  plain  comnion  one,  jait 
dip  it  into  hot  water  to  loosen  it ;  turn  out  the  rice,  and  tiien  agn 
reverse  it  on  to  a  tin  or  dish,  and  with  the  point  of  a  knife  innk 
round  the  top  a  rim  of  about  an  inch  wide ;  then  brush  some  darifid 
butter  over  the  whole  pudding,  and  set  it  into  a  brisk  OTea.  Whei 
it  is  of  an  equal  light  golden  brown  draw  it  out,  raise  the  cover  care- 
fully where  it  is  marked,  scoop  out  the  rice  from  the  inside,  leaviof 

*  Unless  the  rio«  be  boiled  slowly,  and  very  dry,  it  will  not  answer  &r  tia 
tasserole. 


CBAP.  XXI.]  BAKED  PUDBINGS.  439 

only  a  crust  of  about  an  inch  thick  in  every  part,  and  pour  into  it 
some  preserred  fruit  wanned  in  its  own  syrup,  or  fill  it  with  a  cam' 
poie  of  plums  or  peaches  (see  Chapter  XaIII.)  ;  or  with  some  good 
apples  boiled  with  fine  sugar  to  a  smooth  rich  marmalade.  This  is  a 
very  good  as  well  as  an  elegant  dish :  it  may  be  enriched  with  more 
butter,  and  by  substituting  cream  for  the  milk  in  part  or  entirely, 
but  it  is  excellent  without  either. 

Rice,  \  lb.;  new  milk,  1  quart:  ]  hour.  Fresh  butter,  2  oz.; 
pounded  sugar,  2)  oz. ;  rasped  rind,  1  lemon :  ^  hour  or  more. 

Ohs, — The  precise  time  of  baking  the  pudding  cannot  well  be 
specified :  it  only  requires  colour. 


▼BRMICELLI  PUDDING  WITH  APPLES  OR  WITHOUT^  AND  PUDDINGS 

OF  SOUJEE  AND  8EM0LA. 

Drop  gradually  into  an  exact  quart  of  boiling  milk  four  ounces  of 
Tery  fresh  vermicelli,  crushing  it  slightly  with  one  hand  and  letting 
it  fall  gently  from  the  fingers,  and  stirring  the  milk  with  a  spoon 
held  in  the  other  hand,  to  prevent  the  vermicelli  from  gathering  into 
lumps.  Boil  it  softly  until  it  is  quite  tender  and  very  thick,  which  it 
mill  be  usually  in  about  twenty  minutes,  during  which  time  it  must 
be  veiy  frequently  stirred ;  then  work  in  two  ounces  of  fresh  butter 
and  four  of  pounded  sugar ;  turn  the  mixture  into  a  bowl  or  pan, 
and  stir  it  occasionally  until  it  has  cooled  down.  Whisk  five  good 
^gs  until  they  are  very  light,  beat  them  gradually  and  quickly  to 
the  other  ingredients,  add  the  finely  grated  rind  of  a  lemon  or  a  little 
lemon-brandy  or  ratifia,  and  pour  the  pudding  when  nearly  cold  into 
a  buttered  dish,  and  just  cover  the  surface  wiUi  apples  pared,  cored, 
and  Quartered ;  press  them  into  the  pudding-mixture,  to  the  top  of 
whicli  they  ynli  immediately  rise  again,  and  place  the  dish  in  a 
very  gentle  oven  for  three  -  <iuarters  of  an  hour,  or  longer  if 
needed  to  render  the  fruit  ouite  tender.  The  apples  should  be 
of  the  best  quality  for  cooking.  This  is  an  exceedingly  nice 
puddine  if  well  made  and  well  baked.  The  butter  can  be  omitted 
to  simplify  it. 

Milk,  1  quart ;  vermicelli,  4  oz. :  boiled  about  20  minutes.  Butter, 
2  oz. ;  (when  used)  pounded  sugar,  i  lb. ;  eggs,  5 :  baked  slowly/  f 
hour  or  more. 

For  a  plain  common  vermicelli  pudding  omit  the  apples  and  one 
tgg :  for  a  very  good  one  use  six  eggs,  and  the  butter ;  and  flavour  it 
delicately  with  orange-flower  water,  vanilla,  or  aught  else  that  may 
'be  preferred.  We  have  often  had  an  ounce  or  two  of  candied  citron 
sliced  very  thin  mingled  with  it. 

Puddings  of  soujee  and  semola  are  made  in  precisely  the  same 
manner,  with  four  ounces  to  the  quart  of  milk,  and  ten  minutes 
toiling*

decorated  with  small  thin  leaves  of  citron-rind,  cut  with  a  minvfie 
paste-cutter.  The  same  preparation  may  be  used  also  for  GabrieOe  J 
pudding,  and  filled  with  hot  preserved  fruit,  the  rice  scooped  fnxD  the 
inside  being  mixed  with  the  syrup. 

GOOD  TORKSHIRE  PUDDING. 

To  make  a  very  good  and  light  Yorkshire  pudding,  take  an  eqoal 
number  of  eggs  and  of  heaped  tablespoonsful  of  flour,  with  a 
spoonful  of  sfut  to  six  of  these.  Whisk  the  ^gs  well^  strain,  and 
them  gradually  with  the  flour,  then  pour  in  by  degrees  as  mndi 
milk  as  will  reduce  the  batter  to  the  consistence  of  rather  thin  en 
The  tin  which  is  to  receive  the  pudding  must  have  been  placed  &r 
■some  time  previously  imder  a  jomt  that  has  been  put  down  to  tou0L  . 
one  of  beef^is  usually  preferrea.  Beat  the  batter  Driskly  and  l^thr 
the  instant  before  it  is  poured  into  the  pan,  watch  it  carefully  trat  & 
may  not  bum,  and  let  the  edges  have  an  equal  share  of  the  fire. 
When  the  pudding  is  quite  firm  in  every  part,  and  well-coloiued

the  sar&oe,  tuiB  it  to  brown  the  under  side.  This  is  best  accom-

mnch  thinner  than  in  the  south,  roasted  generally  at  an  enormous 
fire^  and  not  turned  at  all :  currants  there  are  sometimes  added

COMMON   YORKSHIRE   PUDDIMQ. 

Half  a  pound  of  flour,  three  eggs  (we  would  recommend  a  fourth), 
rather  more  than  a  pint  of  milk,  and  a  teaspoonful  of  salt. 

NORMANDY  PUDDING.      (OOOD.) 

Boil,  until  very  soft  and  dry,  eight  ounces  of  rice  in  a  pint  and 
a  half,  or  rather  more,  of  water,*  stir  to  it  two  ounces  of  fresn  butter 
and  three  of  sugar,  and  simmer  it  for  a  few  minutes  ailer  they  are 
added ;  then  pour  it  out,  and  let  it  cool  for  use.    Strip  from  the 
stalks  as  many  red  currants,  or  Kentish  cherries,  as  will  fill  a  tart- 
dish  of  moderate  size,  and  for  each  pint  of  the  fruit  allow  from  three 
to  four  ounces  of  sugar.    Line  the  bottom  and  sides  of  a  deep  dish 
with  part  of  the  rice ;  next,  put  in  a  thick  layer  of  fruit  and  sugar ; 
then  one  of  rice  and  one  of  fruit  alternately  until  the  dish  is  fulL 
Suflicient  of  the  rice  should  be  reserved  to  form  a  rather  thick  layer 
at  the  top :  smooth  this  equally  with  a  knife,  sift  sugar  thickly  on  it, 
or  brush  it  with  good  cream,  and  send  the  pudding  to  a  moderate 
OTen  for  half  an  hour,  or  longer,  should  it  be  large.    Morella  cher- 
ries, with  a  little  additional  sugar,  make  an  exceUent  pudding  of  this 
Jdnd. 

•  COMMON  BAKED  RAISIN  PUDDING. 

Beat  well  to{;ether  three-quarters  of  a  pound  of  flour,  the  same 
quantity  of  raisins,  six  ounces  of  beef- suet,  finely  chopped,  a  small 
pinch  of  ult,  some  grated  nutmeg,  and  three  eggs  which  have  been 
tfaoroaghly  whisked  and  mixed  with  about  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of 
milk,  or  less  than  this,  should  the  eggs  be  large.  Pour  the  whole 
into  a  buttered  dish,  and  bake  it  an  hour  and  a  quarter.  For  a  large 
pudding,  increase  the  quantities  one  half.

A    RICHER   BAKKD   RAISIN   PUDDING. 

Mix  and  whisk  well,  and  lightly  together,  a  pound  of  raisinf 
weighed  after  they  are  stoned,  ten  ounces  of  finely  minced  beef-euet, 
three-quarters  of  a  pound  of  flour,  a  little  salt,  half  a  small  nutmeg, 
or  the  grated  rind  of  a  lemon,  four  large  eggs,  and  as  much  milk  as 
may  be  needed  to  make  the  whole  into  a  very  thick  batter :  bake  the 
pudding  a  few  minutes  longer  than  the  preceding  one.  The  adi^tioD 
of  sugar  will  be  found  no  improvement  as  it  willrender  it  much  less 
light. 

Sultana  raisins  are  well  adapted  to  these  puddings,  as  they  contain 
no  pips,  and  from  their  delicate  size  sooner  become  tender  in  the 
baking  than  the  larger  kinds. 

THE  POOR  author's  PUDDING. 

Flavour  a  quart  of  new  milk  by  boiling  in  it  for  a  few  minutes 
half  a  stick  of  well-bruised  cinnamon,  or  the  thin  rind  of  a  small 
lemon ;  add  a  few  grains  of  salt,  and  three  oxmces  of  su^ar,  and  torn 
the  whole  into  a  deep  basin :  when  it  is  <}uite  cold^  stir  to  it  three 
well-beaten  eg^  and  strain  the  mixture  mto  a  pie-dish.  CoTer  the 
top  entirely  with  slices  of  bread  free  from  crust,  and  half  an  inch 
thick,  cut  so  as  to  join  neatly,  and  buttered  on  both  sides :  bake  the 
pudding  in  a  moderate  oven  for  about  half  an  hour,  or  in  a  Dutch 
oven  before  the  fire. 

New  milk,  1  quart ;  cinnamon,  or  lemon-rind ;  sugar,  3  oz. ;  little 
salt ;  eggs,  8 ;  buttered  bread :  baked  }  hour. 

PUDDING   A   LA   PAYSANNB. 

{Cheap  and  Oood.) 

Fill  a  deep  tart-dish  with  alternate  layers  of  well-sugared  fmlt 
and  very  thin  slices  of  the  crumb  of  a  light  stale  loaf;  let  the  uppo* 
layer  be  of  fruit,  and  should  it  be  of  a  dry  kind,  sprinkle  over  it 
about  a  dessertspoonful  of  water,  or  a  little  lemon-juice :  nis^lierries, 
currants,  and  cherries,  will  not  require  this.  Send  the  pudding  to  a 
somewhat  brisk  oven  to  be  baked  for  about  half  an  honr.  The  pro-

ounces  will  be  sufficient. 

THE   curate's  PUDDING. 

This  is  but  a  variation  of  the  pudding  d  la  Paysanne  wbkh,  piic- 
ccdes  it,  but  as  it  is  both  good  and  inexpensive  it  may  be  aooepULblA 


CHAP.  XXI.]  BAKED  PUBDIN69.  443 

to  some  of  our  readers.  Wash,  wipe,  and  pare  some  quickly  grown 
rbabarb-stalks,  cut  them  into  short  lengths,  and  put  a  layer  of  them 
into  a  deep  dish  with  a  spoonful  or  two  of  Lisbon  sugar;  cover  these 
evenly  with  part  of  a  penny  roll  sliced  thin ;  add  another  thick  layer 
of  fruit  and  sugar,  then  one  of  bread,  then  another  of  the  rhubarb ; 
cover  this  last  with  a  deep  layer  of  fine  bread-crumbs  well  mingled 
with  about  a  tablespoonful  of  sugar,  pour  a  little  clarified  butter  over 
them,  and  send  the  pudding  to  a  brisk  oven.  From  thirty  to  forty 
minutes  will  bake  it.  Good  boiling  apples  sliced,  sweetened,  and 
flavoured  with  nutmeg  or  grated  lemon-rind,  and  covered  with  well 
buttered  slices  of  bread,  make  an  excellent  pudding  of  this  kind,  and 
80  do  black  currants  likewise,  without  the  butter. 

A   LIGHT   BAKED   BATTER   PUDDING. 

With  three  heaped  tablespoonsful  or  about  six  ounces  of  flour  mix 
a  small  saltspoonf  ul  of  salt,  and  add  very  gradually  to  it  three  fresh 
^gs  which  have  been  cleared  in  the  usual  way  or  strained,  and 
wmsked  to  a  light  froth.  Beat  up  the  batter  well,  then  stir  to  it  by 
d^rees  a  pint  of  new  milk,  pour  it  into  a  buttered  dish,  set  it  imme- 
diately into  a  rather  brisk  oven,  and  bake  it  three-quarters  of  an  hour. 
If  properly  mana^d,  it  will  be  extremely  light  and  delicate,  and  the 
surface  wul  be  crisp.  When  good  milk  cannot  be  had  for  it,  another 
egg,  or  the  yolk  of  one  at  least,  should  be  added.  Send  preserved  or 
stewed  fruit  to  table  with  it.  The  same  mixture  may  be  baked  in 
buttered  cups  from  twenty  to  thirty  minutes,  turned  out,  and  served 
with  sugar  sifted  thickly  over. 

In  some  counties  an  ounce  or  tw^o  of  very  finely  minced  suet  is 
usually  mixed  with  baked  batter  puddings,  which  are  enriched,  but 
not  improved,  we  think,  by  the  addition;  but  that  is  entirely  a 
matter  of  taste

TO  COOK  EGGS  IN   THE  SHELL   WITHOUT   BOILING  THEM. 

(An  admirahU  receipt.) 

This  mode  of  dressing  eggs  is  not  new ;  it  seems,  indeed,  to  have 
been  known  in  years  long  past,  but  not  to  have  received  the  attention 
which  its  excellence  deserved.  We  saw  it  mentioned  with  much 
commendation  in  a  most  usefUl  little  periodical,  called  the  Cottage 
Gardener^  and  had  it  tested  immediately  with  various  modifications 
and  with  entire  success.  A^r  many  tnals,  we  give  the  following  as 
the  best  and  most  uniform  in  its  results  of  our  numerous  experiments, 
first,  put  some  boiling  water  into  a  large  basin — a  slop-basin  for 
example — and  let  it  remain  for  a  few  seconds,  then  turn  it  out,  lay  in 
the  ^g  (or  ^gs),  and  roll  it  over,  to  take  the  chill  off  the  e^ell,  that 
it  may  not  crack  from  the  sudden  application  of  heat ;  and  pour  in — 
and  upon  the  egg— ^ite  boiling  water  from  a  kettle,  until  it  is  com- 
pletely immers^;  put  a  plate  over  it  instantlv,  and  let  it  remain, 
upon  the  table,  for  twelve  minutes,  when  it  will  be  found  perfectly 
and  beautifully  cooked,  entirely  free  from  all  flavour  and  appearance 
ofraumesgy  and  yet  so  lightly  and  delicately  dressed  as  to  suit  even 
peraons  who  cannot  take  eggs  at  all  when  boiled  in  the  usual  way. 
It  should  be  turned  when  something  more  than  half  done,  but  the 
plate  should  be  replaced  as  quickly  as  possible.  Two  eg^  will  re- 
quire scarcely  more  time  than  one ;  but  some  additional  minutes  must 
be  allowed  for  any  number  beyond  that.  The  process  may  always 
be  quickened  by  chanf;ing  the  water  when  it  has  cooled  a  little,  for 
more  that  is  fast  boiling :  the  eggs  may,  in  fact,  be  rendered  quite 
hard  by  the  same  means,  but  then  no  advantage  is  obtained  over  the 
old  method  of  cooking  them. 

12  minutes. 

Obs. — ^This  is  one  of  the  receipts  which  we  have  re-produced  here 
from  our  cookexy  for  invalids,  on  account  of  its  adaptation  to  the 
taste  generally. 

TO   BOIL  EGGS  IN   THE   SHELL. 

Even  this  very  simple  process  demands  a  certain  degree  of  care,  for 
if  the  qsgs  be  brought  from  a  cold  larder,  and  sudden!  v  plunged  into 
boiling  water  tiiey  will  frequently  break  immediately,  and  a  large 
poition  wUl  often  escape  from  the  wells.    In  winter  they  should  be 

*  For  a  sea-store,  an  old  and  ezperienoed  cook  from  on  board  a  man-of-war, 
directs  eggs  to  be  mbbed  vith  salt  butter,  and  packed  in  layers  with  plenty  of 
bran  between  them.  He  says  that  the  salt  penetrates  the  shell,  and  tends  to 
preeerre  the  eggs,  which  will  require  no  additional  salt  when  eaten.  We  giye  the 
information  to  the  reader  as  we  receired  it. 


446  MODEBK  COOKERY.  [cBAT.xm 

held  for  an  instant  oxer  the  steam  from  the  saucepan  before  they 
are  laid  in,  and  they  should  be  put  gently  into  it  Three  minnta 
will  boil  them  sufficiently  for  persons  who  like  the  whites  in  a  par- 
tially liquid  state.  Five  minutes,  exact  timey  if  they  be  fresh  and  fine, 
will  harden  the  whites  only,  and  leave  the  yolks  still  liquid.  Few 
eaters  require  them  more  dr^sed  than  this ;  but  eight  or  ten  nunutes 
will  render  them  hard.  Eggs  should  always  be  cooked  in  soffideot 
water  to  cover  them  completely. 

To  boil  very  lightly,  8  minutes ;  to  render  the  whites  firm,  ^VDi 
minutes ;  hard  eggs,  8  to  10  minutes :  ( 15  minutes  for  salad 
dressing.) 


TO   DRESS   THE   EGQS   OF   THE   GUINEA  FOWL   AND    BANTAM. 

The  eggs  of  the  Guinea-fowl — 
which  are  small,  very  prettily 
shaped,  and  of  a  pale  or  full  fawn- 
colour  (for  in  this  they  vary)— are 
much  esteemed   by  epicures,  being  , 

very  rich  and  excellent  eating.  They  /?^%^ 

are  generally  somewhat  higher  in  /^S" 

price  than  the  common  hens*  eggs, 
even  in  Norfolk,  Suffolk,  and  other 
counties  where  they  most  abound ;  and  in  T.iondon  they  are  usniBj 
expensive.  They  may  be  cooked  in  the  shell  without  boiling  by  the 
method  we  have  already  given:  eight  or  nine  minutes  will  cook 
them  so.  About  three  and  a  half  of  gentle  boillnff  will  render  the 
whites  firm,  and  ten  will  harden  them  quite  through.  They  are  oftea 
served  instead  of  plovers*  ^S^  ftod  are  sent  to  table  embedded  ia 
moss  in  the  same  manner.  They  may  also  be  shelled,  and  used  wfaok 
to  decorate  a  salad. 

The  eggs  of  the  bantam/ which  are  scarcely  more  than  half  the 
size  of  these,  and  of  which  the  shells  are  much  thinner,  will  reqaiit 
less  time  to  cook.  They  form  an  elegant  decoration  for  a  calad.  if 
boiled  hard,  which  they  \nll  become  in  five  or  six  minutes ;  and  for  a 
mince  of  fowl,  or  veal  and  oysters,  when  poached. 

Two  minutes*  poaching  in  an  enamelled  saucepan*  will  be  sofficieol 
for  these  delicate  little  eggs,  without  positive  boiling.  Thej  shooU 
be  carefully  broken  and  put  gently  into  water  at  boiling  point,  bit 
which  has  ceased  to  move,  and  left  undisturbed  by  the  side  of  the  &t 
until  the  yolks  are  just  set  on  the  surface. 

Guinea-fowls*  eggs,  quite  hard,  10  minutes.    For  eating  (by 
method,  8  to  9  minutes),  3  to  4  minutes. 

Bantams*,  hard,  6  minutes ;  soft,  2i  to  3  minutes. 

*  In  any  other  kind,  an  additional  half  minute  may  be  raqnirei.

TO   DRESS   turkeys'   EGGS. 


Turkeys'  eggs  are  not,  we  believe,  broaght  very  abundantly  into 
the  London  market,*  but  their  superiority  to  those  of  the  common 
fowl  is  well  known  in  the  counties  where  the  birds  are  principally 
reared.  Though  of  larse  size  they  are  delicate  in  flavour,  and  are 
equally  valuable  for  the  breakfast-table— cooked  simply  in  the  shell — 
or  for  compounding  any  of  the  dishes  for  which  hens'  eggs  are  com- 
monly in  request.  They  make  super-excellent  sauce,  omlets,  cus- 
tards, and  puddings ;  and  are  especially  to  be  recommended  poached, 
or  served  by  any  other  of  the  following  receipts.    Those  of  the

FORCED   turkeys'   EOGS   (oR  SWANs'). 

(An  Excellent  Entremets,) 

Boil  gently  for  twenty  minutes  in  plenty  of  water,  that  they  may 
be  entirely  covered  with  it,  five  or  six  fresh  turkeys*  eggs,  and  when 
they  are  done  lift  them  into  a  large  pan  of  water  to  cool.  By  chang- 
ing the  water  once  or  twice  they  will  become  cold  more  rapidly,  and 
they  must  not  be  used  until  they  are  perfectly  so. 

Roll  them  in  a  cloth,  pressing  lightly  on  them  to  break  the  shells ; 
dear  them  off,  and  halve  the  eggs  evenly  lengthwise.  Take  out  the 
yolks  with  care,  and  pound  them  to  a  smooth  paste  in  a  mortar  with 
an  ounce  and  a  half,  or  two  ounces  at  the  utmost,  of  pure-flavoured 
butter  to  the  half  dozen,  a  small  half-tea/<poonful  of  salt,  a  little  finely 
grated  nutmeg,  and  some  cayenne,  also  in  fine  powder :  a  little  mace, 
— one  of  the  most  delicate  of  all  seasonings  when  judiciously  used — 
may  be  added  with  good  effect.  Blend  these  ingredients  thoroughly, 
and  then  add  to  them  by  degrees  one  raw  hen's  egg  slightly  whisked, 
and  the  yolk  of  a  second,  or  a  dessertspoonful  or  two  of  sweet  rich 
cream.  One  common  egg  is  sufiicient  for  four  of  the  turkey  egg- 
yolks.  Beat.up  the  mass,  which  will  now  be  of  the  consistence  of  a 
thick  batter,  well  and  lightly,  and  proceed  to  fill  the  whites  with  it, 
having  first  cut  a  small  slice  from  each  half  to  make  it  stand  evenly 
on  the  dish,  and  hollowed  the  inside  with  the  point  of  a  sharp  knife, 
BO  as  to  render  it  of  equal  thickhess  throughout.    Fill  them  uiU  and

TO   BOIL   ▲  swan's   ego  HARD. 

Swans*  eggs  are  much  more  delicate  than  from  their  size,  and  fitn 
the  tendency  of  the  birds  to  feed  on  fish  might  be  supposed ;  and 
when  boiled  hard  and  shelled,  their  appearance  is  beauttfiuy  the  white 
being  of  remarkable  purity  and  transparency.  Take  as  much  water 
as  will  cover  the  egg  (or  eggs)  well  in  every  part,  let  it  boil  qnicklj, 
then  take  it  from  the  fire,  and  as  soon  as  the  water  ceases  to  move 
put  in  the  egg,  and  leave  it  by  the  side  of  the  fire — ^without  allowing 
it  to  boil— for  twenty  minutes,  and  turn  it  gently  once  or  twice  in 
the  time ;  then  put  on  the  cover  of  the  stewpan  and  boil  it  gently  far 
a  quarter  of  an  nour ;  take  it  quite  from  the  fire,  and  in  five  miaofies 
put  it  into  a  basin  and  throw  a  cloth,  once  or  twice  folded,  over  it, 
and  let  it  cool  slowly.  It  will  retain  the  heat  for  a  yery  long  time, 
and  as  it  should  be  quite  cold  before  it  is  cut,  it  should  be  boiled 
early  if  wanted  to  serve  the  same  day.  Halve  it  evenly  with  a  shaip 
knife  lengthwise,  take  out  the  yolk  with  care,  and  prepare  it  for 
table,  either  by  the  receipt  which  follows,  or  by  that  for  forced  eggsi

TO   POACH   EGOS. 

Take  for  this  purpose  a  wide  and  delicately  clean  pan  about  half- 
filled  with  the  clearest  spring- water ;  throw  in  a  small  saltspoonful 
of  salt,  and  place  it  over  a  fire  quite  free  from  smoke.  Break  some 
new-laid  eggs  into  separate  cups,  and  do  this  with  care,  that  the  yolks 
may  not  be  injured.  When  the  water  boils,  draw  back  the  pan,  glide 
the  eggs  gently  into  it,  and  let  them  stand  until  the  whites  appear 
almost  set,  which  will  be  in  about  a  minute;  then,  without  shaking 
them,  move  the  pan  over  the  fire,  and  just  simmer  them  from  two 
minutes  and  a  half  to  three  minutes.  Lift  them  out  separately  with 
a  slice,  trim  quickly  off  the  ragged  edges,  and  serve  them  upon 
dressed  spinach,  or  upon  minced  veal,  turkey,  or  chicken ;  or  dish 
them  for  an  invalid,  upon  delicately  toasted  bi'ead,  sliced  thick,  and 
freed  from  crust :  it  is  an  improvement  to  have  the  bread  buttered, 
but  it  is  then  les8«vholesome. 

Comparative  time  of  poaching  eggs.  Swans*  ^KS^  5  to  6  minutes. 
(In  basin,  10  minutes).  Turkeys*  eg^s,  4  minutes  Hens'  eggs,  3  to 
Zi  minutes.    Guinea-fowls*,  2  to  3  mmutes.    Bantams*,  2  minutes. 

0^9. — All  eggs  may  be  poached  without  boiling  if  kept  just  at  sim- 
mering point,  but  one  boil  quite  at  last  will  assist  to  detach  them  from 
the  stewpan,  from  which  they  should  always  be  very  carefully  lifted 
on  what  is  called  a  fish  or  egg-slice.  There  are  pans  made  on  pur- 
pose for  poaching  and  frying  them  in  good  form ;  but  they  do  not, 
we  believe,  answer  particularly  well.  If  broken  into  cups  slightly 
rubbed  with  butter,  and  simmered  in  them,  their  roundness  of  shai«e 
will  be  best  preserved*

CEufs  Poches  au  Jus. 

Dress  the  eggs  as  above,  giving  them  as  good  an  appearance  as 
possible,  lay  them  into  a  very  hot  dish,  and  sauce  them  with  some 
rich,  clear,  boiling  veal  gravy,  or  with  some  JSspaenole.  Each  egg, 
for  variety,  may  t>e  dished  upon  a  crouton  of  bread  cut  with  a  fluted 
paste-cutter,  and  fried  a  pale  brown :  the  sauce  should  then  be  poured 
round,  not  over  them. 

Foaching  is  the  best  mode  of  dressing  a  swan's  egg,*  as  it  renders  it 
more  than  any  other  delicate  in  flavour :  it  is  usually  served  on  a  bed 
of  spinach.    Only  the  eggs  of  quite  young  swans  are  suited  to  the

(EUFS   AU   PLAT. 

A  pewter  or  any  other  met^l  plate  or  dish  which  will  bear  the  fire, 
must  be  used  for  these.  Just  melt  a  slice  of  butter  in  it,  then  put  in

and  salt  on  the  top  of  each,  and  place  them  over  a  gentle  lire  uutii 
the  whites  are  quite  set,  but  keep  them  free  from  colour. 

This  is  a  very  common  mode  of  preparing  eggs  on  the  continent; 
but  there  is  generally  a  slight  rawness  of  tne  surface  of  the  yolks 
which  is  in  a  measure  removed  by  ladling  the  boiling  butter  ova 
them  with  a  spoon  as  they  are  cooking,  though  a  saTamander  bdd 
above  them  for  a  minute  would  have  a  better  effect.  Four  or  five 
minutes  will  dress  them.

MILK  AND  CREAM. 

Without  possessing  a  dairy,  it  is  quite  possible  for  families  to  bare 
always  a  sufficient  provision  of  milk  and  cream  for  their  cob- 
sumption,  provided  there  be  a  clean  cool  larder  or  pantry  where  it 
can  be  kept.  It  should  be  taken  from  persons  who  can  be  depended 
on  for  supplying  it  pure,  and  if  it  can  be  obtained  from  a  dairy  neu 
at  hand  it  will  be  an  advantage,  as  in  the  summer  it  is  less  easy  to 
preserve  it  sweet  when  it  has  been  conveyed  from  a  distance.  It 
should  be  poured  at  once  into  well-scalded  pans  or  basins  kept  ex- 
clusively for  it,  and  placed  on  a  very  dean  and  airy  shelf,  apart  £raa 
all  the  other  contents  of  the  larder.  The  fresh  milk  as  it  come?  in 
should  be  set  at  one  end  of  the  shelf,  and  that  for  use  should  be 
taken  from  the  other,  so  that  none  may  become  stale  from  being  mis- 
placed or  overlooked.  The  cream  should  be  removed  with  a  per- 
forated skimmer  (or  skimming-dish  as  it  is  called  in  dairy-counties) 
which  has  been  dipped  into  cold  water  to  prevent  the  cream,  when 
thick,  from  adhering  to  it.  Twelve  hours  in  stumner,  and  twenty- 
four  in  winter,  will  be  sufficient  time  for  the  milk  to  stand  for 
**•  creaming^*  though  it  may  often  be  kept  longer  with  advant^^ 
Between  two  and  three  pints  of  really  good  milk  will  prodaee  abNit 
a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  cream.  In  frosty  weather  the  pans  for  it 
should  be  warmed  before  it  is  poured  in.  If  boiled  when  fini 
brought  in,  it  will  remain  sweet  much  longer  than  it  otberrat 
would ;  but  it  will  then  be  unfit  to  serve  with  tea ;  though  it  may  bt 
heated  afresh  and  sent  to  table  with  coffee ;  and  used  also  for  pod- 
dings,  and  all  other  varieties  of  milk-diet 


CHAP.  XXII.]  EGOS  AND  MILK.  451 


DEYONSHERE^   OR  CLOTTED   CREAM. 

From  the  mode  adopted  in  Devonshire,  and  in  some  other  coun- 
ties, of  scalding  the  muk  in  the  following  manner,  the  cream  becomes 
Tery  rich  and  thick,  and  is  easily  converted  into  excellent  butter.  It 
is  strained  into  larse  shallow  metal  pans  as  soon  as  it  is  brought  into 
the  dairy  and  left  for  twelve  hours  at  least  in  summer,  and  thirty-six 
in  cold  weather.  It  is  then  gently  carried  to  a  hot  plate — heated  by 
a  fire  from  below — and  brought  slowly  to  a  quite  scalding  heat  but 
without  being  allowed  to  boil  or  even  to  simmer.  When  it  is 
ready  to  be  removed,  distinct  rings  appear  on  the  surface,  and  small 
bubbles  of  air.  It  must  then  be  carried  carefully  back  to  the  dairy, 
and  may  be  skimmed  in  twelve  hours  afterwards.  The  cream  should 
be  weU  drained  from  the  milk— which  will  be  very  poor— as  this 
is  done  It  may  then  be  converted  into  excellent  butter,  merely  by 
beating  it  with  the  hand  in  a  shallow  wooden  tub,  which  is,  we  are 
informed,  the  usual  manner  of  making  it  in  small  Devonshire 
dairies. 

DU   LAIT   A   MADAME. 

Boil  a  quart  of  new  milk,  and  let  it  cool  sufiicienily  to  allow  the 
cream  to  be  taken  off;  then  rinse  an  earthen  jar  well  in  every  part 
with  buttermilk,  and  while  the  boiled  milk  is  still  rather  warm,  pour 
it  in  and  add  the  cream  gently  on  the  top.  Let  it  remain  twenty- 
four  hours,  turn  it  into  a  deep  dish,  mix  it  with  pounded  sugar,  and 
it  will  be  ready  to  serve,  lliis  preparation  is  much  eaten  abroad 
during  the  summer,  and  is  considered  very  wholesome.  The  milk, 
by  the  foregoing  process,  becomes  a  very  soft  curd,  slightly,  but  not 
at  all  unpleasantly,  acid  in  flavour.  A  cover,  or  thick  folded  doth, 
should  be  placed  on  the  jar  after  the  milk  is  poured  in,  and  it  should 
be  kept  in  a  moderately  warm  place.  In  very  sidtry  weather  less 
time  may  be  allowed  for  the  milk  to  stand. 

Obs. — We  give  this  and  the  following  receipt  from  an  unpublished 
work  which  we  have  in  progress,  being  always  desirous  to  make  such 
information  as  we  possess  generally  useful  as  far  as  we  can. 

CURDS   AND   WHEY. 

Bennet  is  generally  prepared  for  dairy-use  by  butchers,  and  kept 
in  farmhouses  hung  in  the  chimney  comers,  where  it  will  remain 
good  a  long  time.  It  is  the  inner  stomach  of  the  calf,  from  which 
the  curd  is  removed,  and  which  is  salted  and  stretched  out  to  dry  on 
rolinters  of  wood,  or  strong  wooden  skewers.  It  should  be  preserved 
from  dust  and  smoke  (by  a  naper-bag  or  other  means),  and  portions 
of  it  cut  off  as  wanted.  Soak  a  small  bit  in  half  a  teacupfhl  of  warm 
water,  and  let  it  remain  in  it  for  an  hour  or  two ;  then  pour  into 


452  MODEBN  COOKERY.  [cbap.  un 


ft  quart  of  warm  new  milk  a  dessertspoonfbl  of  the  rennet-li^ 
and  keep  it  in  a  warm  place  until  the  whey  appears  eeparated  fnm 
the  curd,  and  looks  clear.  The  smaller  the  proportion  of  lennet 
used,  the  more  soft  and  delicate  will  be  the  curd.  We  write  te 
directions  from  recollection,  havinfi^  often  had  the  dish  thus  prepmi 
but  having  no  memorandum  at  this  moment  of  the  precise  propordaiDi

think,  for  a  gallon  of  milk,  if  some  hours  were  allowed  for  it  to  torn. 
When  rermet'Whey^  which  is  a  most  valuable  beverage  in  many  c«i 
of  illness,  is  required  for  an  invalid  to  drink,  a  bit  of  the  rennei,iftff 
being  quickly  and  slightly  rinsed,  may  be  stirred  at  onoe  into  the 
warm  milk,  as  the  curd  becoming  hard  is  then  of  no  consequence,  it 
must  be  kept  warm  until  the  whey  appears  and  is  clear.  It  may  then  be 
strained,  and  given  to  the  patient  to  drink,  or  allowed  to  become  crid 
before  it  is  taken.  In  feverish  complaints  it  has  often  the  most  benign 
effect.

sediment.

TO   CLARIFY   CJlLF's   FEET  STOCK. 

Break  up  a  quart  of  the  stock,  put  it  into  a  clean  stewrpaa  w^ 
the  whites  of  five  lai^e  or  of  six  small  eggs,  two  ounces  of  sugar,  and 
the  strained  juice  of  a  small  lemon ;  place  it  over  a  gentle  fire,  and  do 
not  stir  it  after  the  scum  begins  to  form ;  when  it  has  boiled  fiTe  or 
six  minutes,  if  the  liquid  part  be  clear,  turn  it  into  a  jelly-bag,  and 
pass  it  through  a  second  time  should  it  not  be  perfectly  traospareit 
the  first.  To  consumptive  patients,  and  others  requiring  restoratives 
but  forbidden  to  take  stimulants,  the  jell^r  thus  prepared  is  oCien  verf 
acceptable,  and  may  be  taken  with  impunity,  when  it  would  be  h^h^ 
injurious  made  with  wine.  More  white  of  egg  is  required  fx>  diiinr 
it  than  when  sugar  and  acid  are  used  in  larger  quantities,  as  both  « 
these  assist  the  process.  For  blanc-mange  omit  the  lemon-juice,  and 
mix  with  the  clarified  stock  an  equal  proportion  of  cream  (for  aa 
invalid,  new  milk^,  with  the  usual  flavouring,  and  weight  of  sugar; 
or  pour  the  boilmg  stock  very  gradually  to  some  finely  pounded 
almonds,  and  express  it  from  them  as  directed  for  Quince  BlamaiigCb 
allowing  from  six  to  eight  ounces  to  the  pint, 

Stock,  1  quart ;  whites  of  eggs,  6 ;  sugar,  2  oz. ;  juice,  1  snnfl 
lemon :  5  to  8  minutes. 

TO  CLARIFY  ISINGLASS. 

The  finely-cut  purified  isinglass,  which  is  now  in  general  use,  le- 
quixas  no  clarifying  except  for  clear  jellies :  for  all  otl^r  dishes  it  a 


CHAP.xxm.]     SWEET  DISHES,  OR  ENTREMETS.  455 

sufficient  to  dissolve,  skim,  and  pass  it  through  a  muslin  strainer. 
When  two  ounces  are  required  for  a  dish,  put  two  and  a  half  into  a 
ddicately  clean  pan,  and  pour  on  it  a  pint  of  spring  water  which  has 
been  gradually  mixed  with  a  teaspoonful  of  beaten  white  of  egg ;  stir 
these  thoroughly  together,  and  let  them  heat  slowly  by  the  side  of  a 
gentle  fire,  but  do  not  allow  the  isinglass  to  stick  to  the  pan.  When 
the  scum  is  well  risen,  which  it  will  be  after  two  or  three  minutes* 
simmering,  clear  it  off,  and  continue  the  skimming  until  no  more 
appears ;  then,  should  the  quantity  of  liquid  be  more  than  is  needed, 
reduce  it  by  quick  boiling  to  the  proper  point,  strain  it  through  a 
thin  muslin,  and  set  it  by  for  use  :  it  will  be  perfectly  transparent, 
and  may  be  mixed  lukewarm  with  the  clear  and  ready  sweetened 
juice  oi  various  fruits,  or  used  with  the  necessary  proportion  of  syrup, 
for  jellies  flavoured  with  choice  liqueurs.  As  the  clarifying  reduces 
the  strength  of  the  isinglass— or  rather  as  a  portion  of  it  is  taken  up 
by  the  white  of  egg — an  additional  quarter  to  each  ounce  must  be 
allowed  for  this :  if  the  scum  be  laid  to  drain  on  the  back  of  a  fine 
sieve  which  has  been  wetted  with  hot  water,  a  little  very  strong  jelly 
^rill  drip  from  it. 

Isinglass,  2|  oz.;  water,  1  pint;  beaten  white  of  egg^  1  tea- 
spoonful. 

Obs. — At  many  Italian  warehouses  a  preparation  is  now  sold  under 
the  name  of  isinglass,  which  appears  to  us  to  be  highly  purified 
gelatine  of  some  other  kind.  It  is  converted  without  trouble  into  a 
very  transparent  jelly,  is  free  from  flavour,  and  is  less  expensive  than 
the  genuine  Russian  isinglass ;  but  when  taken  for  any  length  of 
time  as  a  restorative,  its  different  nature  becomes  perceptible.  It 
answers  well  for  the  table  occasionally;  but  it  is  not  suited  to 
invalids. 

SPINACH   GREEN;  FOR   COLOUniNO  SWEET  DISHES^  CONFECTIONARY^ 

OR   SOUPS. 

Pound  quite  to  a  pulp,  in  a  marble  or  Wedgewood  mortar,  a  hand- 
ful or  two  of  young  freshly-gathered  spinach,  then  throw  it  into  a 
hair  sieve,  and  press  through  all  the  juice  which  can  be  obtained  from 
it ;  pour  this  into  a  clean  white  jar,  and  place  it  in  a  pan  of  water 
that  is  at  the  point  of  boiling,  and  which  must  be  allowed  only  to 
JTist  simmer  afterwards ;  in  three  or  four  minutes  the  juice  will  be 
poached  or  set :  take  it  then  gently  with  a  spoon,  and  lay  it  upon  the 
back  of  a  fine  sieve  to  drain.  If  wanted  for  immediate  use,  merely 
mix  it  in  the  mortar  with  some  finely-powdered  sugar  ;♦  but  if  to  be 
kept  as  a  store,  pound  it  with  as  much  as  will  render  the  whole 
tolerably  dry,  boil  it  to  candy-height  over  a  very  clear  fire,  pour  it 
out  in  cakes,  and  keep  them  in  a  tm  box  or  canister.  For  this  last 
preparation  consult  the  receipt  for  orange-flower  candy. 

•  For  soap,  dilute  it  first  with  a  little  of  the  boiling  stock,  and  stir  it  to  the 
nmaijder.

same  way,  and  with  the  same  proportions  of  fruit  and  water,  but  tkj

following  day,  when  the  juice  will  be  rich  and  clear.  They  dMcM

weighed,  as  the  air  will  so<m  discolour  them.  The  juice  will  ibnn  a 
jelly  much  more  easily  if  the  cores  and  pips  be  left  in  the  fruit

COCOA-NUT   FLAVOCRED   MILK. 

(For  sweet  dishes^  g-c.) 

Pare  the  dark  outer  rind  from  a  very  fresh  nut,  and  grate  it  as  i 
fine  and  exceedingly  clean  grater ,  to  every  three  ounces  pour  a  qout 
of  new  milk,  and  simmer  them  very  softly  for  three  quarters  cf  «■ 
hour,  or  more,  that  a  full  flavour  of  the  nut  may  be  imparted  to  the 
milk  without  its  being  much  reduced :  strain  it  through  a  fine  sieve,  v 
cloth,  with  suflicient  pressure  to  leave  the  nut  almost  dry:  it  mar 
then  be  used  for  blanc-mange,  custards,  rice,  and  other  poddii^ 
light  cakes  and  bread. 

To  each  quart  new  milk,  3  oz.  grated  cocoa-nut :  }  to  1  hoar.

(Or  Fruit  stewed  in  Syrup.')

preparations  for  trial  to  such  of  our  readers  as  may  be  unacqnaiiiteii

-with  them,  as  well  as  to  those  who  may  have  a  distaste  to  the  com- 
mon ^^gltwed  fndV*  of  English  cookery.  If  well  made  they  are 
peculiarly  delicious  and  refreshing,  preserving  the  pure  flavour  oi 
the  fruit  of  which  they  are  coroposied ;  while  its  acidity  is  much 
softened  hy  the  small  quantity  of  water  added  to  form  the  syrup  in 
which  it  18  boiled.  Ihey  are  also  more  economical  than  tarts  or 
puddings,  and  infinitely  more  wholesome.  In  the  second  course 
pastry-crust  can  always  he  served  with  them,  if  desired,  in  the  form 
of  ready  baked  leaves,  round  cakes,  or  any  more  fanciful  shapes ;  or  a 
border  of  these  may  be  fastened  with  a  little  white  of  egg  and  flour 
round  the  edge  of  the  dish  in  which  the  compote  is  served ;  but  rice, 
or  macaroni  simply  boiled,  or  a  very  plain  pudding  is  a  more  usual 
accompaniment. 

Compote*  will  remain  good  for  two  or  three  days  in  a  cool  store- 
room, or  somewhat  longer,  if  gently  boiled  up  for  an  instant  a  second 
time ;  but  they  contain  generally  too  small  a  proportion  of  sugar  to 
preserve  them  from  mould  or  fermentation  for  many  days.  The 
syrup  should  be  enriched  with  a  larger  quantity  when  they  are  in- 
tended for  the  desserts  of  formal  dinners,  as  it  will  increase  the  trans- 
parency of  the  fruit :  the  juice  is  always  beautifully  clear  when  the 
compotes  are  carefully  prepared.  They  should  be  served  in  glass 
dishes,  or  in  compotiers,  which  are  of  a  form  adapted  to  them. 

Compote  of  spring  fruit, — (Rhubarb).  Take  a  pound  of  the  stalks 
af^r  they  are  pared,  and  cut  them  into  short  lengths ;  have  ready  a 
quarter  of  a  pint  of  water  boiled  gently  for  ten  minutes  with  five 
ounces  of  su^r,  or  with  six  should  the  fruit  be  very  acid ;  put  it  in, 
and  simmer  it  for  about  ten  minutes.  Some  kinds  will  be  tender  in 
rather  less  time,  some  will  require  more. 

Obs,— Good  sugar  in  lumps  should  be  used  for  these  dishes. 
Lisbon  sugar  will  answer  for  tnem  very  well  on  ordinarv  occasions, 
but  that  which  is  refined  will  render  them  much  more  delicate. 

Compote  of  green  currants. — Spring  water,  half-pint;  sugar,  ^ve 
ounces ;  boiled  together  ten  minutes.  One  pint  of  green  currants 
etripped  from  the  stalks ;  simmered  five  minutes. 

Compote  of  green  gooseberries, — This  is  an  excellent  compdte  if 
made  with  fine  sugar,  and  very  good  with  any  kind.  Break  ^\e 
ounces  into  small  lumps  and  pour  on  them  half  a  pint  of  water ;  boil 
these  gently  for  ten  mmutes,  and  clear  off  all  the  scum ;  then  add  to 
them  a  pint  of  fresh  gooseberries  freeil  from  the  tops  and  stalks, 
washed,  and  well  drain^.  Simmer  them  gently  from  eight  to  ten 
minutes,  and  serve  them  hot  or  cold.  Increase  the  quantity  for  a 
laroe  dish. 

Compote  of  green  apricots. — Wipe  the  down  from  a  pound  of  quite 
^oung  apricots,  and  stew  them  very  gently  for  nearly  twenty  minutes 
in  syrup  made  with  eight  ounces  of  sugar  and  three-quarters  of  a  pint 
of  water,  boiled  together  the  usual  time. 

Compdte  of  red  currants, — ^A  quarter  of  a  pint  of  water  and  ^ye 
ounces  of  sugar :  ten  minutes.    One  pint  of  currants  freed  from  the

stalks  to  be  just  simmered  in  the  syrup  from  five  to  seyeii  miiiiite& 
This  receipt  will  serve  equally  for  raspberries,  or  for  a  compoU  of 
the  two  fruits  mixed  together.  Either  of  them  will  be  found  an  ad- 
mirable accompaniment  to  a  pudding  of  batter,  custard,  brad,  or 
ground  rice,  and  also  to  various  other  kinds  of  puddings^  as  wdi 
as  to  whole  rice  plainly  boiled. 

Compote  of  Kentish  or  Flemish  cherries. — Simmer  five  ounces  of 
sugar  with  half  a  pint  of  water  for  ten  minutes ;  throw  into  the  s^rmp 
a  pound  of  cherries  weighed  after  they  are  stalked,  and  let  them  stew 
gently  for  twenty  minutes :  it  is  a  great  improvement  to  stone  the 
fruit,  but  a  larger  quantity  will  then  be  required  for  a  dish. 

Compote  of  Morella  cherries.— BoH.  together  for  fifteen  minutes, 
six  ounces  of  sugar  with  half  a  pint  of  water ;  add  a  pound  and  a 
quarter  of  ripe  Morella  cherries,  and  simmer  them  very  softly  from 
five  to  seven  minutes  :  this  is  a  delicious  compdte.  A  larger  propor- 
tion of  sugar  will  often  be  required  for  it,  as  the  fruit  is  \'ery  acid  in 
some  seasons,  and  when  it  is  not  fully  ripe 

Compete  of  dasnsons, — Four  ounces  of  sugar  and  half  a  pint  of 
water  to  be  boiled  for  ten  minutes ;  one  pound  of  damsons  to  be 
added,  and  simmered  gently  from  ten  to  twelve  minutes. 

Compote  of  the  green  magnwn-homun  or  Mogul  plum. — The  green 
Mogul  plums  are  often  brought  abundantly  into  the  market  when  the 
fruit  is  thinned  from  the  trees,  and  they  make  admirable  tarts  or 
compotes^  possessing  the  fine  slight  bitter  flavour  of  the  unripe  apricot, 
to  which  uiey  are  quite  equal.  Measure  a  pint  of  the  plums  without 
their  stalks,  and  wash  them  very  clean;  then  throw  them  into  a 
syrup  made  with  seven  ounces  of  sugar  in  lumps,  and  half  a  pint  of 
water,  boiled  together  for  eight  or  ten  minutes.  Give  the  plums  one 
quick  boil,  and  then  let  them  stew  quite  softly  for  about  five  mimita»<, 
or  until  they  are  tender,  which  occasionally  will  be  in  less  time  evco. 
Take  off  the  scum,  and  serve  the  compote  hot  or  cold. 

Compote  of  the  magfium-Jtonumy  or  other  large  plums, — ^B<h1  six 
ounces  of  sugar  with  half  a  pint  of  water  the  usual  time ;  take  the 
stalks  from  a  pound  of  plums,  and  simmer  them  very  sofUy  for 
twenty  minutes.  Increase  the  proportion  of  sugar  if  needed,  and 
regulate  the  time  as  may  be  necessary  for  the  different  varieties  of 
fruit. 

Compote  of  bullaees.^The  large,  or  shepherds*  bullace,  is  Tery 
good  stewed,  but  will  require  a  considerable  portion  of  sagar  to 
render  it  palatable,  unless  it  be  quite  ripe.  Make  a  syrup  with  faatf 
a  pound  of  sugar,  and  three-quarters  of  a  pint  of  water,  and  boil  in 
it  gently  from  fifteen  to  twenty  minutes,  a  pint  and  a  half  of  the 
bullaces  freed  from  their  stalks. 

Compdte  of  Siberian  crabs, — To  three-quarters  of  a  pint  of 
add  six  ounces  of  ^ne  sugar,  boil  them  for  ten  or  twelve  minutes, 
skim  them  well.  Add  a  pound  and  a  half  of  Siberian  crabs  without 
their  stalks,  and  keep  them  just  at  the  point  of  boiling  for  twenty 
minutes  they  will  then  become  tender  without  bursting.     A  lb«

this  compote, 

Oh$, — ^In  a  dry  wann  smnmer,  when  fruit  ripens  freely,  and  is  rich 
in  quality,  the  proportion  of  sugar  directed  for  these  compotes  would 
generally  be  found  sufficient ;  but  in  a  cold  or  wet  season  it  would
