alight  difference  in  the  cost  of  sugars,  renders  it  a  poor  economy  to 
use  the  raw  for  dishes  of  this  class,  instead  of  that  which  is  well  re- 
fined. To  make  a  clear  syrup  it  should  be  broken  into  lumps,  not 
crushed  to  powder.  Almost  every  kind  of  fruit  may  be  converted 
into  a  good  compote. 

COMPETE  OP  PEACHES. 

Parehalf  adosen  ripe  peaches,  and  stew  them  very  softly  from 
eighteen  to  twenty  minutes,  keeping  them  often  turned  in  a  light 
syrup,  made  with  five  ounces  of  sugar,  and  half  a  pint  of  water  boucd 
together  for  ten  minutes.  Dish  the  fruit ;  reduce  the  symp  by  quick 
boiling,  pour  it  over  the  peaches,  and  serve  them  hot  for  a  second- 
course  dish,  or  cold  for  dessert.  They  should  be  <]|uite  ripe,  and  will 
be  found  delicious  dressed  thus.  A  little  lemon-juice  may  be  added 
to  the  syrup,  and  the  blanched  kernels  of  two  or  three  peach  or 
apricot  stones. 

Sugar,  5  oz.;  water,  ^  pint*  10  minutes.  Peaches,  6 :  18  to  20 
minutes. 

Obe. — Nectarines,  without  being  pared,  may  be  dressed  in  the  same 
way,  but  will  require  to  be  stewed  somewhat  longer,  unless  they  be 
quite  ripe. 

AlfOTHER   RECEIPT   FOR  STEWED   PEACHES. 

Shofold  the  fruit  be  not  perfectly  ripe,  throw  it  into  boiling  water 
and  keep  it  just  simmering,  until  the  skin  can  be  easily  stripped  off. 
Have  r^y  half  a  pound  of  fine  sugar  boiled  to  a  light  syrup  with 
three-quarters  of  a  pint  of  water ;  throw  in  the  peaches,  let  them 
stew  softly  until  quite  tender,  and  turn  them  often  that  they  may  be 
equally  done ;  after  they  are  ^hed,  add  a  little  strained  lemon-juice 
to  the  syrup,  and  reduce  it  by  a  few  minutes*  very  quick  boihng. 
The  fruit  is  sometimes  pared,  divided,  and  stoned,  then  gently  stewed 
tuxti]  it  is  tender. 

Sugur,  8  oz. ;  water,  }  pnt :  10  to  12  minutes.  Peaches,  6  or  7 ; 
lemon-juioe,  1  large  teaspoonful.

nearly  its  weight  of  sugar  to  render  it  palatable ;  but  after  hanging 
MHoe  time  upon  the  trees  it  becomes  much  mellowed  in  flavour,  and

may  be  sufficiently  sweetened  with  a  smaller  pr:  portion.  Aooording 
to  the  state  of  the  fruit  then,  take  for  eacn  pound  (leaving  it  in

BLACK  CAPS  PAR   EXCELLENCE. 

(For  the  Second-Bourse^  or  for  Dessert.') 

Cut  a  dozen  fine  Norfolk  biffins  in  two  without  paring  them,  soood 
out  the  cores,  and  fill  the  cavities  with  thin  strips  of  fresh  lemon-rina 
and  with  candied  orange-peel.  Cover  the  bottom  of  a  fiat  shallow  tm 
with  a  thick  layer  of  fine  pale  brown  sugar,  press  the  two  halves  </ 
each  apple  together,  and  place  them  closely  in  the  tin ;  poor  luJf  a 
bottle  of  raisin  or  of  any  other  sweet  wine  over  them,  and  be  careful 
to  moisten  the  tops  of  fdl ;  sift  white  sugar  thickly  on  them,  and  set 
the  tin  into  a  very  hot  oven  at  first,  that  the  outsides  of  the  anpks 
may  catch  or  become  black ;  then  draw  them  to  the  moutii  of  the 
oven,  and  bake  them  gently  until  they  are  soft  quite  through.  The 
Norfolk  biffin  answers  for  this  dish  far  better  than  any  other  kind  of 
apple,  but  the  winter  queening,  and  some  few  firm  sorts  beside,  can 
be  used  for  it  with  fair  success.  These  for  variety  may  be  cofed 
without  being  divided,  and  filled  with  orange  mannalade.  The  Uaek 
caps  served  hot,  as  a  second-course  dish,  are  excellent. 

Norfolk  biffius,  12 ;  rinds  fresh  lemons,  1  to  2 ;  candied  oiange^ 
rind,  2  to  3  oz. ;  pale  brown  sugar,  )  lb. ;  raisin  or  other  wine^  | 
bottle  ;  little  sifted  sugar    ]  to  1  hour,  or  more. 

Obs. — The  apples  dressed  as  above  resemble  a  rich  eonfectitm^  and 
will  remain  good  for  ten  days  or  a  fortnight ;  sometimes  much  longer 
even.    The  receipt  is  an  admirable  one. 

GATEAU   DE   POMMES. 

Boil  together  for  fifteen  minutes  a  pound  of  well-refined  sugar  and 
half  a  pint  of  water ;  then  add  a  couple  of  pounds  of  nonsuches,  or  of 
any  otner  finely-fiavoured  apples  which  can  be  boiled  easOy  to  a 
smooth  pulp,  and  the  juice  oi^a  couple  of  small,  or  of  one  very  hrgs 
lemon.  Stew  these  gently  until  the  mixture  is  perfectly  free  frooi 
lumps,  then  boil  it  quickly,  keeping  it  stirred,  without  quitting  it» 
until  it  forms  a  veiy  thick  and  dry  marmalade.  A  few  minolea 
before  it  is  done  add  the  finely  grated  rinds  of  a  couple  of  lemons  $ 


CHAP,  xxm.]      SWEET  DISHES,  OB  ENTREMETS.  461 

when,  it  leaya  the  hottom  of  the  preserrnie-pan  yisible  and  dry, 
pre»  it  into  moulds  of  tasteful  form ;  and  either  store  it  for  vriptcr 
use,  or  if  wanted  for  table,  serve  it  plain  for  dessert,  or  ornament  it 
with  spikes  of  blanched  almonds,  and  pour  a  custard  round  it  for  a 
second-course  dish  (entremets). 

Sugar,  1  lb. ;  water,  |  pint :  15  minutes.  Nonsuches  or  other 
apples,  2  lbs. ;  juice,  1  large  or  2  small  lemons :  2  hours  or  more. 

GATEAU   OF   MIXED   FRUITS.      (gOOd). 

Extract  the  juice  from  some  fresh  red  currants  by  simmering  them 
TeiT  gently  for  a  few  minutes  over  a  slow  fire :  strain  it  through  a 
folded  muslin,  and  to  one  pound  of  it  add  a  pound  and  a  half  of  non- 
suches or  of  freshly  gathered  codlings,  pared,  and  rather  deeply  cored, 
that  the  fibrous  part  of  the  apple  may  be  avoided.  Boil  these  quite 
slowly  until  the  mixture  is  perfectly  smooth,  then,  to  evaporate  part 
of  the  moisture,  let  the  boiling  be  quickened.  In  firom  twenty-five 
to  thirty  minutes  draw  the  pan  from  the  fire,  and  throw  in  gradually 
a  pound  and  a  quarter  of  sugar  in  fine  powder :  mix  it  well  with  the 
fruit,  and  when  it  is  dissolved  continue  the  boiling  rapidly  for  twenty 
minutes  loviger,  keeping  the  mixture  constantly  stirred ;  put  it  into  a 
mould,  and  store  it,  when  cold,  for  winter  use,  or  serve  it  for  dessert, 
or  for  the  second  course :  in  the  latter  ca^e  decorate  it  with  spikes  of 
blanched  almonds,  or  pistachio-nuts,  and  heap  solid  whipped  cream 
round  it,  or  pour  a  custard  into  the  dish.  For  dessert  it  may  be 
garnished  witn  dice  of  the  palest  apple-jelly. 

Juice  of  red  currants,  1  lb. ;  nonsuches,  or  codlin^^s  (pared  and 
cored),  1|  lb. ;  25  to  30  minutes.    Sugar,  1)  lb. :  20  minutes. 

OljHt. — A  portion  of  raspberries,  if  still  in  season,  may  be  mixed 
irith  the  currants  for  this  gateau,  should  the  flavour  be  liked. 

For  other  and  excellent  varieties  of  gateaux  of  fruit,  see  Newton 
solid,  and  damson  solid.  Chapter  XXIV.  Ripe  peaches  and  non- 
floches  will  likewise  do  well  for  it.  Codlings  answer  perfectly  for  the 
preceding  receipt,  and  the  preparation  is  of  fine  colour  and  very 
pleasant  flavour :  it  ought  to  cut  in  clear  firm  slices.  Other  varieties 
of  frxdt  can  be  mingled  in  the  same  manner.

CHikP.  zxni.]      SWEET  DISHES,   OR  ENTBEMEIS.  463 

and  let  tliexn  stew  until  it  is  reduced  to  rather  less  than  two  quarts ; 
or  if,  after  the  flesh  has  quite  fallen  from  the  bones,  the  liquor  on 
being  strained  off  should  exceed  that  quantity,  reduce  it  by  rapid 
boiling  in  a  clean  uncovered  pan  over  a  very  clear  Arc.  When  it  is 
perfectly  firm  and  cold,  take  it  clear  of  fat  and  sediment,  and  add  to 
It  a  bottle  of  sherry,  which  should  be  of  good  quality  (for  poor,  thin 
wines  are  not  well  adapted  to  the  purpose),  three-quarters  of  a  pound 
of  sugar  broken  small,  the  iuice  of  five  large  or  of  six  moderate-si^ed 
lemons,  and  the  whites,  with  the  shells  finely  crushed,  of  seven  eggs, 
or  of  more  should  they  be  very  small.  The  rinds  of  three  lemons, 
pared  exceedingly  thin,  may  be  thrown  into  the  jelly  a  few  minutes 
before  it  is  taken  from  the  fire  ;  or  they  may  be  put  mto  the  jelly-bag 
previously  to  its  being  poured  through,  when  they  will  impart  to  it  a 
slight  and  delicate  flavour,  without  deepening  its  colour  much.  If  it 
is  to  be  moulded,  something  more  than  half  an  ounce  of  isinglass 
should  be  dropped  lightly  in  where  the  liquid  becomes  visible  through 
tl:e  head  of  scum,  when  the  mixture  begins  to  boil ;  for  if  not  sum- 
dently  firm,  it  will  break  when  it  is  dished.  It  may  be  roughed,  or 
served  in  glasses  without  this  addition ;  and  in  a  liquid  state  will  be 
found  an  admirable  ingredient  for  Oxford,  or  other  punch. 

Calf*8  feet,  4 ;  water,  1  gallon :  to  be  reduced  more  than  half. 
Sherry,  1  bottle ;  sugar, )  lb.  (more  to  taste)  ;  juice  of  5  large  lemons, 
or  of  six  moderate-sized ;  whites  and  shells  of  7  eggs,  or  more  if 
small ;  rinds  of  lemons,  3  (for  moulding,  nearly  }  oz.  of  isinglass) : 
15  to  20  minutes. 

Obs. — An  excellent  and  wholesome  jelly  for  young  people  may  be 
made  with  good  orange  or  raisin  wine,  instead  of  sherry  ;  to  either  of 
the^  the  juice  of  three  or  four  oranges,  with  a  small  portion  of  the 
rind,  may  be  added  instead  of  part  of  the  lemons. 


HOQBRN   VARIETIES   OF   CALp's  FEET  JELLY. 

In  modem  cookery  a  number  of  excellent  jellies  are  made  with 
the  stock  of  calves*  feet,  variously  flavoured.  Many  of  them  are  com- 
pounded entirely  without  wine,  a  small  quantity  of  some  fine  limteur 
hems  used  as  a  substitute ;  and  sometimes  cinnamon,  or  vanilla,  or 
Seville  orange-rind  with  a  slight  portion  of  acid,  takes  place  of  thi& 
For  aristocratic  tables,  indeed,  it  is  the  Present  fashion  to  serve  them 
very  lightly  and  delicately  flavoured.    Their  cost  is  thus  materially 
diminisned.    Fresh  strawberries  dropped  into  clear  calf  *s  feet  jelly 
just  before  it  sets,  impart  a  delicious  fragrance  to  it,  when  they  are  of 
a  choice  kind ;  and  other  fruit  is  mingled  with  it  often ;  but  none 
has  80  good  an  effect,  though  many  sorts  when  tasteftiUr  employed 
g^ive  an  excellent  appearance  to  it.    The  Belgrave  mould,  of  which 
the  description  will  be  found  at  page  470,  is  well  adapted  for  highly 
omameotai  jellies;  and  we  reconunen4  its  adoption  for  this  daRS  of

APPLB   calf's   feet  JELLY. 


Four  a  quart  of  prepared  apple-juice  (see  page  456),  on  a  pound 
of  fresh  apples  pared  and  cored,  and  simmer  tnem  until  thej  are  well 
broken  ;  strain  the  juice,  and  let  it  stand  until  cold ;  then  measure, 
and  put  a  pint  and  a  half  of  it  into  a  stewpan  with  a  quart  of  calf  s 
feet  stock  (see  pa^e  453),  nine  ounces  of  sugar  broken  small,  or 
roughly  pounded,  tne  juice  of  two  fine  lemons,  and  the  thin  rinds  of 
one  and  a  half,  with  the  whites  and  shells  of  cieht  eggs-  Let  it  boil 
gently  for  ten  minutes,  then  strain  it  througn  a  flannel-bag,  and 
when  cool  put  it  into  moulds.  It  will  be  very  clear,  and  firm,  and  of 
pleasant  ilavour.  Apples  of  good  qualitv  should  be  used  for  it,  aod 
the  quantity  of  sugar  must  be  regulated  by  the  time  of  year,  as  tbe 
fruit  will  have  lost  much  of  its  acidity  during  the  latter  part  of  the 
season.  This  receipt,  which  is  the  result  of  our  own  experiment,  aod 
which  we  have  found  very  successful,  was  first  tried  just  afier 
Christmas,  with  pearmains  and  llipstone  pippins.  A  little  syrup  of 
preserved  ginger,  or  a  small  glass  of  fine  white  brandy,  would, 
perhaps,  to  some  tastes,  improve  the  jelly ;  but  we  give  it  simply  at 
we  have  had  it  proved  ourselves. 

Prepared  apple  juice,  1  quart ;  fresh  apples,  1  lb. :  i  to  }  hour. 
Strained  juice,  1|  pi^^  i  calf^s  feet  stock,  1  quart ;  sugar,  9  oz. ;  juioe 
of  lemons,  2  ;  rind  of  1|;  whites  and  shells  of  eggs,  8  :  10  minutes. 

0Z>«.— We  would  recommend  the  substitution  of  quinces  for  apples 
in  this  receipt  as  likely  to  afford  a  very  agreeable  variety  oi  the 
jelly :  or  equal  portions  of  the  two  fruits  might  answer  well. 

Unless  the  stock  be  very  stiff,  add  isinglass  to  this,  as  to  the  calf  s 
feet  jelly,  when  it  is  to  be  moulded. 


ORANGE   CALF*S   FEBT  JELLY. 


(Author^s  Receipt) 


To  a  pint  and  a  half  of  firm  calfs  feet  stock,  put  a  pint  of  strained 
China-orange  juice  mixed  with  that  of  one  or  two  lemons ;  add  to 
these  six  ounces  of  sugar,  broken  small,  the  very  thin  rinds  of  three 
oranges  and  one  lemon,  and  the  whites  of  six  eggs  with  half  tbe 
shells  crushed  small.  Stir  these  gently  over  a  clear  fire  antil  tbe 
head  of  scum  begins  to  form,  but  not  at  all  afterwards.  Simmer  the 
jelly  for  ten  minutes  from  the  first yy/  boil ;  take  it  from  the  fire,  lei 
It  stand  a  little,  then  pour  it  through  a  jelly-bag  until  perfectly 
clear.  This  is  an  original,  and  entirely  new  receipt,  which  we  can 
recommend  to  the  reader,  the  jelly  beine  very  pale,  beautifully 
transparent,  and  delicate  in  flavour :  it  would,  we  think,  be  peculiarly 
acceptable  to  such  invalids  as  are  forbidden  to  take  wine  in  any 
form. 

The  proportions  both  of  sugar  and  of  lemon-juice  must  he 
what  varied  according  to  the  season  in  whicli  the  oranges  are  used.

ORANGE  ISINGLASS  JELLT. 

To  render  this  perfectly  transparent  the  juice  of  the  fruit  must  he 
filtered,  and  the  isinglass  clarified ;  but  it  is  not  usual  to  take  so  much 
trouble  for  it.  Stram  as  clear  as  possible,  first  through  a  sieve  or 
muslin,  then  through  a  thick  cloth  or  jelly  bag,  one  quart  of  China 
orange-joice,  mixed  with  as  much  lemon-juice  as  will  give  it  an  agree- 
able degree  of  acidity,  or  with  a  small  proportion  of  Seville  orange- 
juice.  ^  Dissolve  two  ounces  and  a  half  of  isinglass  in  a  pint  of  water, 
skim  it  well,  throw  in  half  a  pound  of  sugar,  and  a  few  strips  of 
the  orange-rind,  pour  in  the  orange-juice,  stir  the  whole  well 
together,  skim  it  clean  without  allowing  it  to  boil,  strain  it  through  a 
doth  or  through  a  muslin,  many  times  folded,  and  when  nearly  cold 
put  it  into  the  moulds.*  This  jelly  is  sometimes  made  without  any 
water,  by  dissolving  the  isinglass  and  sugar  in  the  juice  of  the  fruit. 

Qrange-juioe,  1  quart ;  water,  1  pint ;  isinglass,  2^  oz. ;  sugar,  |  lb. 

YEBY   FINE  ORANGE  JELLY. 

{Sussex  Place  Receipt) 

On  two  ounces  and  a  half  of  the  finest  isinglass,  pour  a  full  but  an 
exact  pint  of  spring  water ;  press  down  the  isinglass  and  turn  it  over 
until  the  whole  is  well  moistened ;  then  place  it  over  a  gentle  fire  and 
let  it  dissolve  gradually ;  remove  the  scum  after  it  has  simmered  for 
two  or  three  minutes,  then  pour  it  out,  and  set  it  aside  to  cool.  In 
another  pint  of  spring  water  boil  a  pound  of  hiffhly  refined  su^  for 
five  or  SIX  minutes ;  turn  this  syrup  into  a  bowl,  and  when  it  is  only 
just  warm,  throw  into  it  the  very  thinly  pared  rinds  of  two  fine 
lemons,  of  two  Seville  oranges,  and  of  two  China  oranges,  with  the 
juice  of  five  China,  and  of  two  Seville  oranges,  and  of  three  lemons. 
vVhen  this  mixture  is  cold,  but  not  beginning  to  thicken,  mix  it  well 
with  the  liquid  isinglass,  and  strain  it  through  a  fine  lawn  sieve,  or 
through  a  square  of  muslin  folded  in  four ;  pour  it  into  moulds 
which  have  been  laid  in  cold  water,  and  when  wanted  for  the  table, 
loosen  it  from  them  by  wrapping  about  them,  dosel;^,  a  doth  which  has 
been  dipped  into  boihng  water,  and  by  passing  a  kmfe  round  the  edges. 

Nothing  can  be  more  refined  and  delicate  in  fiavour  than  the 
above ;  but  the  appearance  of  the  jelly  may  be  improved  by  dari- 
fying  the  isinglass,  and  its  colour  by  boiling  the  fruit-rinds  in  the 
syrup  for  three  or  four  minutes,  and  by  leaving  them  in  it  until  it  is

TO  MAKE  ORANGE  BASKETS  FOR  JELLY. 
The  oranges  for  these  should  be  large.  First,  mark  the  handle  of 
the  basket  evenly  across  the  stalk  end  of  the  fruit  with  the  hMik  of  a, 
Binull  knife,  or  with  a  silver  one,  and  let  it  be  quite  half  mu  n,^ 
wide ;  then  trace  a  line  across  from  one  end  of  the  handle  to  tbc 
other  exactly  in  the  middle  of  the  orange,  and  when  the  other  liiik 


CHAP.zxui.]    SWEET  DISHES,  OB  ENTBEMETS.  467 

is  marked  in  the  same  way,  cut  just  through  the  rind  with  the  point 
of  a  penknife,  being  careml  not  to  pierce  tne  fruit  itself;  next,  with 
a  tea  or  dessertspoon,  take  of  the  quartered  rind  on  either  side  of  the 
handle ;  jmss  a  penknife  under  the  handle  itself;  work  the  pomt  of  a 
Bpoon  gently  between  the  oranee  and  the  basket,  until  they  are 
separated  in  every  part ;  then  taS^e  the  fruit  between  the  thumb  and 
fingers,  and  press  it  carefuUy  out  through  one  of  the  spaces  on  either 
ode  of  the  handle. 

Baskets  thus  made  may  be  filled  with  any  of  the  jellies  of  which 
the  receipts  are  given  here :  but  they  should  be  nearly  cold  before 
they  are  poured  in ;  and  thejr  ought  also  to  be  very  clear.  Some  of 
the  baskets  may  be  filled  with  ratifias,  and  dished  alternately  with 
those  which  contain  the  jelly. 

LEMON   calf's   FEET  JELLY. 

Break  up  a  quart  of  strong  calf  *s  feet  stock,  which  should  have 
been  measured  while  in  a  liquid  state ;  let  it  be  quite  clear  of  fat  and 
sediment,  for  which  a  small  additional  quantity  should  be  allowed; 
add  to  it  a  not  very  full  half-pint  of  strained  lemon- juice,  and  ten 
ounces  of  si^ar,  broken  small  (rather  more  or  less  according  to  the 
state  of  the  miit),  the  rind  of  one  lemon  pared  as  thin  as  possible,  or 
of  from  two  to  three  when  a  full  flavour  of  it  is  liked,  and  the  whites 
with  part  of  the  shells  crushed  small,  of  five  large  or  of  six  small 
eggs.  Proceed  as  for  the  preceding  jellies,  and  when  the  mixture 
has  boiled  five  minutes  throw  in  a  small  pinch  of  isinglass ;  continue 
the  boiling  for  five  or  six  minutes  longer,  draw  the  pan  from  the  fir^ 
let  it  stand  to  settle;  then  turn  it  into  the  jelly-bag.  We  have 
found  it  always  perfectly  clear  with  once  passing  tnrough ;  but 
should  it  not  be  so,  pour  it  in  a  second  time. 

Strong  cairs  feet  stock,  1  quart;  strained  lemon-juice,  short 
i  pint ;  sugar,  10  oz.  Qnore  or  less  according  to  state  of  fruit) ; 
rind  of  from  1  to  3  large  lemons ;  whites  and  part  of  shells  of  6  large 
or  6  small  eggs :  6  minutes.    Finch  of  isinglass :  6  minutes  longer. 

Obs. — About  seven  large  lemons  will  produce  the  half  pmt  of 
juice.  This  quantity  is  for  one  mould  only.  The  jelly  will  be  found 
almost  colourless  unless  much  of  the  Tiaaa  be  used,  and  as  perfectly 
transparent  as  clear  spring  water :  it  is  alw  very  agreeable  in  flavour. 
For  variety,  part  of  the  juice  of  the  fruit  mignt  be  omitted,  and  its 
place  supplicMl  by  maraschino,  or  any  other  rich  white  liqueur  of 
appropriate  flavour ;  and  to  render  it  safer  eating,  some  syrup  of 
preserved  ginger  would  be  an  excellent  addition. 

COMSTAMTIA.  JELLY. 

Infuse  in  a  pint  of  water  for  five  minutes  the  rind  of  half  a  Seville 
orange,  pared  extremely  thin ;  add  an  ounce  of  isinglass ;  and  when 
this  is  dissolved  throw  in  four  oimces  of  good  sugar  in  lumps ;  stii

well,  and  simmer  the  whole  for  a  few  miniites,  then  mix  with  it  four 
large  wineglassesful  of  Constantia,  and  strain  the  jelly  through  a  fine 
doth  of  close  texture ;  let  it  settle  and  oool,  then  pour  it  gratly  fitsn 
any  sediment  there  may  be,  into  a  mould  which  has  been  laid  for  ii 
hour  or  two  into  water.  We  had  this  jelly  made  in  the  first  instame 
for  an  invalid  who  was  forbidden  to  take  acids,  and  it  proved  ao 
agreeable  in  flavour  that  we  can  recommend  it  for  the  table.  Hie 
isinglass,  with  an  additional  quarter  of  an  ounce,  might  be  daiificd, 
and  the  sugar  and  orange-rind  boiled  with  it  afleiwards. 

Water,  1  pint;  rind,  4  Seville  orange:  5  minutes.  Tsingiii, 
1  oz.;  sugar,  4  oz. :  5  to  7  minutes.  Constantia,  4  large  nine-

RHUBARB   ISINGLASS  JELLT. 

(Auihors  Original  Beceipt.     Oood^ 

A  jelly  of  beautiful  tint,  and  excellent  flavour,  may  be  made  irith 
fresh  youne  rhubarb-stems,  either  of  the  giant  or  dwarf  kind,  if  tbey 
be  of  a  bright  pink  colour.  Wash,  and  drain  or  wipe  them;  ilice 
without  paring  them,  taking  them  quite  free  from  any  ooane  or 
discoloured  parts.  Put  two  pounds  and  a  half,  and  a  quart  of 
water  into  an  enamelled  stewpan,  which  is  more  suitable  to  tbe

and  boil  the  rhubarb  very  gently  for  twenty  minatea,  or  until 
it  is  thoroughly  stewed,  but  not  sufficiently  so  to  thicken  the  jdoe. 
Strain  it  through  a  muslin  folded  in  four ;  measure  a  pint  and  a  half 
of  it ;  heat  it  alresh  in  a  clean  pan;  add  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  tbe 
finest  isinglass,  and  six  ounces  or  more  of  the  best  sugar  in  bife 
lumps ;  stir  it  oflen  until  the  isinglass  is  entirely  dissolved,  then  kc 
it  boil  ouickly  for  a  few  minutes  to  throw  up  the  scam ;  clear  thii 
off  carerully,  and  strain  the  jelly  twice  through  a  moalin  stnaiia,* 
folded  as  the  first ;  let  it  cool,  and  mould  it  as  usual.

tastefully  garnished  with  presenred,  or  with

of  the  originators,  Messrs.  Temple  and  Rey- 
nolds, Princes  Street,  Cavendish  Square,  and

The  space  occupied  by  the  larger  cylinder  may  be  left  empty,  or 
filled, 'before  the  jelly  is  served,  with  white  or  with  pMde-tmted 
whipped  cream.  Water,  only  sufficiently  warm  to  detach  the  jelly 
from  them  without  heating  or  melting  it,  must  be  poured  into  the 
cylinders  to  un/£r  them ;  and  to  loosen  the  whole  so  as  to  nnmonkl 
it  easily,  a  cloth  wrung  out  of  very  hot  water  must  be  woond  roiud 
it,  or  the  mould  must  be  dipped  quickly  into  some  which  is  nearly  or 
Quite  Doiling.  A  dish  should  then  be  laid  on  it,  it  should  be  caic- 
mlly  reversed,  and  the  mould  lifted  from  it  gently.  It  will  aonw- 
times  require  a  slight  sharp  blow  to  detach  it  quite. 

Italian  jelly  is  made  by  half  filling  a  mould  of  convenient  fonn, 
and  laying  round  upon  it  in  a  chain,  as  soon  as  it  is  set,  some  blane- 
mance  m^  rather  firm,  and  cut  of  equal  thickness  and  size,  with  a 
small  round  cutter ;  the  mould  is  then  filled  with  the  remainder  of 
the  jelly,  which  must  be  nearly  cold,  but  not  beginning  to  let 
Brandied  morella  cherries,  drained  very  dry,  are  sometimes  dn^iped 
into  moulds  of  pale  jelly ;  and  fruits,  either  firesh  or  preserved,  are 
arranged  in  them  with  exceediiijgly  good  effect  when  dcilfclly 
managed ;  but  this  is  best  accomplished  by  having  a  mould  for  the 
purpose,  with  another  of  smaller  size  fixed  in  it  by  means  of  alicfeit 
wires,  which  hook  on  to  the  edge  of  the  outer  one.  By  po«ung 
water  into  this  it  may  easily  be  detached  from  the  jelly ;  the  fruit  is 
then  to  be  placed  in  the  -space  left  by  it,  and  the  whole  fiUed  up  with 
more  jelly :  to'  give  the  proper  effect,  it  must  be  recollected  that  the 
dish  will  be  reversed  when  sent  to  table. 

QUEEN   MAB's   PUDDINO. 

{An  Elegcmt  Summer  Diih.) 

Throw  into  a  pint  of  new  milk  the  thin  rind  of  a  small  lemon,  ani; 
SIX  or  eight  bitter  almonds,  blanched  and  bruised ;  or  substitute  fat^ 
these  half  a  pod  of  vanilla  cut  small,  heat  it  slowly  by  the  side  of  the] 
(ire,  and  keep  it  at  the  point  of  boiling  until  it  is  strongly  flavouivdtj

finest  igiiiglass,  or  a  full  ounce  should  the  weather  be  extremely 

warm;  when  this  is  dissolyed,  strain  the  milk  through  a  muslin,  and 

put  it  into  a  clean  saucepan,  with  from  four  to  five  ounces  and  a  half 

of  sugar  in  lumps,  and  half  a  pint  of  rich  cream ;  give  the  whole  one 

boil,  and  then  stir  it,  briskly  and  by  degrees,  to  the  well-beaten  yolks 

of  six  fresh  eggs;   next,  thicken  the  mixture  as  a  custard,  over  a 

gentk  fire,  but  do  not  hazard  its  curdling ;  when  it  is  of  tolerable 

consistence,  pour  it  out,  and  continue  the  stirring  until  it  is  half  cold, 

then  mix  with  it  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  candied  dtron,  cut  in  small 

spikes,  and  a  couple  of  ounces  of  dried  cherries,  and  pour  it  into  a 

mould  rubbed  with  a  drop  of  oil :  when  turned  out  it  will  have  the 

appearance  of  a  pudding.    From  two  to  three  ounces  of  preserved 

ginger,  well  drained  and  sliced,  may  be  substituted  ibr  the  cherries, 

and  an  ounce  of  pistachio-nuts,  blanched  and  split,  for  the  citron ; 

these  wiU  maJte  an  elegant  variety  of  the  dish,  and  the  syrup  of  the 

giDger,  poured  round  as  sauce,  will  be  a  further  improvement. 

Clurrants  steamed  until  tender,  and  candied  orange  or  lemon-rind,  are 

often  used  instead  of  the  cherries,  and  the  well-sweetened  juice  of 

fltrawberries,  raspberries  (white  or  red),  apricots,  peaches,  or  syrup  of 

pine-apnle,  will  make  an  agreeable  sauce ;  a  small  quantity  of  this 

Lkst  will  also  rive  a  delicious  flavour  to  the  pudding  itself,  when 

mixed   with   the   other    ingredients.    Cream  may  be   substituted 

entirely  for  the  milk,  when  its  richness  is  considered  desirable. 

New  milk,  1  pint;  rind  1  small  lemon;  bitter  almonds,  6  to  8 
(or,  vanillay  i  pod) ;  salt,  few  grains ;  isinelass,  }  oz.  (1  oz.  in  sultry 
weather) ;  sugar,  4i  oz. ;  cream,  i  pint ;  yolks,  6  eggs ;  dried  cherries, 
2  oz. ;  candied  citron,  l\  oz. ;  (or,  preserved  ginger,  2  to  3  oz.,  and 
the  syrup  as  sauce,  and  1  oz.  of  blanched  pistachio-nuts ;  or  4  oz. 
currants,  steamed  20  minutes,  and  2  oz.  candied  orange-rind).  For 
sauce,  sweetened  juice  of  strawberries,  raspberries,  or  plums,  or  pine 
apple  syrup. 

Obs.—Tae  currants  should  be  steamed  in  an  earthen  cullender, 
placed  over  a  saucepan  of  boiling  water,  and  covered  with  the  lid. 
It  will  be  a  great  improvement  to  place  the  pudding  over  ice  for  an 
hour  before  it  is  served.

neherchi  by  a  flavouring  of  maraschino,  but  must  then  have  a  little 
addition  of  isinglass.  Tne  preparation,  without  this  last  ingredient, 
will  be  found  excellent  iced. 

AN   EXCELLBZ9T  TRIFLB. 

Take  equal  parts  of  ^line  and  brandy,  about  a  wineglassful  of  each, 
or  two-thirds  of  good  sherry  or  Madeira,  and  one  of  spirit,  and 
•oak  in  the  mixture  four  sponge-biscuits,  and  half  a  pound  of  maca- 
roons and  ratifias ;  coyer  the  bottom  of  the  trifle-dish  with  part  of 
these,  and  pour  upon  them  a  full  pint  of  rich  boiled  custard  made 
with  three-quarters  of  a  pint,  or  rather  more,  of  milk  and  cream 
taken  in  equal  portions,  and  six  eggs;  and  sweetened,  flavoured 
and  thickened  by  the  receipt  of  page  481 ;  lay  the  remainder  of  the 
waked  cakes  upon  it,  and  pile  over  the  whole,  to  the  depth  of  two  or 
three  inches,  the  whipped  syllabub  of  page  476,  previously  well 
drained ;  then  sweeten  and  flavour  slightly  with  wine  only,  less  than 
half  a  pint  of  thin  cream  (or  of  cream  and  milk  mixed) ;  wash  and 
wipe  the  whisk,  and  whip  it  to  the  lightest  possible  froth :  take  it  off 
with  a  skimmer  and  heap  it  gently  over  the  trifle.

6WI88  CREAM;   OR  TRIFLE* 

{Very  Good.) 

Flavour  pleasantly  with  lemon  rind  and  cinnamon,  a  pint  of  rich 
cream,  after  having  taken  from  it  as  much  as  will  mix  smoothly  to 
a  thin  batter  four  teaspoonsful  of  the  finest  flour ;  sweeten  it  with 
ax  ounces  of  well-refined  sogar  in  lumps ;  place  it  over  a  clear  fire 
in  a  delicately  clean  saucepan,  and  when  it  boils  stir  in  the  flour,  and 
sjfnmer  it  for  four  or  five  minutes,  stirring  it  gently  without  ceasing ; 
then  pour  it  out,  and  when  it  is  quite  cold  mix  with  it  by  degrees 
the  strained  juice  of  two  moderate-sized  and  very  fresh  lemons. 
Tnke  a  quarter  of  a  pound  of  macaroons,  cover  the  bottom  of  a 
glass  dish  with  a  portion  of  them,  pour  in  a  part  of  the  cream,  lay 
the  remainder  of  the  macaroons  upon  it,  add  the  rest  of  the  cream,

the  day  before  it  is  wanted  for  table.  The  requisite  flavour  may  be 
riven  to  this  dish  by  infusing  in  the  cream  the  very  thin  rind  of  a 
Emon,  and  part  of  a  stick  of  cinnamon  slightly  bruised,  and  then 
straining  it  before  the  flour  is  added ;  or,  these  and  the  sugar  may  be 
boiled  together  with  two  or  three  spoonsful  of  water,  to  a  strongly

TEBT   GOOD   LEMON   CREAMS   MADE   WITHOUT   CREAM. 

Ponr  over  the  yery  thin  rinds  of  two  moderate-sized  but  perfectly 
sound  fresh  lemons  and  six  ounces  of  sugar,  half  a  pint  of  spring 
water,  and  let  them  remain  for  six  hours:  then  ada  the  strained 
juice  of  the  lemons,  and  five  fresh  eggs  well  beaten  and  also  strained ; 
take  out  the  lemon-rind,  and  stir  the  mixture  without  ceasing  oyer  a 
gentle  fire  until  it  has  boiled  softly  from  six  to  eight  minutes :  it  will 
not  curdle  as  it  would  did  milk  supply  the  place  of  the  water  and 
lemon-juice.  The  creams  are,  we  think,  more  delicate,  though  not 
quite  so  thick,  when  the  yolks  only  of  six  eggs  are  used  for  them. 
The^  will  keep  well  for  nearly  a  week  in  really  cold  weather.

preferred) ;  cold  water,  }  pint :  6  hours.  Juice  of  lemons,  2 ;  eggs, 
5 :  to  be  boiled  sofUy  6  to  8  minutes.

the  lemon-juice,  sugar,  and  water,  before  they  are  stirred  to  the 

FRUIT  CREAMS;   AND   ITALIAN   CREAMS. 

These  are  yery  quickly  and  easily  made,  by  mixing  with  good 
cream  a  sufficient  proportion  of  the  sweetened  juice  of  fresh  fruit,  or 
of  well-made  fruit  jelly  or  jam,  to  flavour  it :  a  few  drops  of  prepared 
cochineal  may  be  added  to  deepen  the  colour  when  it  is  required  for 
any  particular  purpose.  A  quarter  of  a  pint  of  strawberry  or  of 
raspberry  jelly  will  fully  flavour  a  pint  of  cream :  a  very  little  lemon- 
juice  improves  almost  all  compositions  of  this  kind.  When  jam  is 
used  it  must  first  be  gradually  mixed  with  the  cream,  and  then 
worked  through  a  sieve,  to  take  out  the  seed  or  skin  of  the  fruit. 
All  fresh  juice,  for  this  purpose,  must  of  course,  be  cold ;  that  of 
strawberries  is  best  obtamed  by  crushing  the  fruit  and  strewing 
sugar  over  it.  Peaches,  pine-apple,  apricots,  or  nectarines,  may  be 
simmered  for  a  few  minutes  in  a  little  syrup,  and  this,  drained  well 
from  them,  will  serve  extremely  well  to  mix  with  the  cream  when  it 
has  become  thoroughl;^  cold :  the  lemon-juice  should  be  added  to  all 
of  these.  When  the  ingredients  are  well  blended,  lightly  whisk  or 
mill  them  to  a  froth ;  take  this  off  with  a  skimmer  as  it  rises,  and  lay 
it  upon  a  fine  sieve  reversed,  to  drain,  or  if  it  is  to  be  served  in 
glasses,  fill  them  with  it  at  once. 

Italian  creams  are  either  fruit-flavoured  only,  or  mixed  with  wine 
like  syllabubs,  then  whisked  to  a  stiff  froth  and  put  into  a  perforated 
mould,  into  which  a  muslin  is  first  laid ;  or  into  a  small  nair-sieve 
(which  must  also  first  be  lined  with  the  muslin),  and  left  to  drain 
until  the  following  day,  when  the  cream  must  be  very  gently  turned 
Dut,  and  dished,  and  garnished,  as  fancy  may  direct

RICHER  BLAIYC-MAKOB* 

A  pint  of  good  cream  with  a  pint  of  new  milk,  sweetened  and 
flavourod  aa  above  (or  in  any  other  manner  which  good  taste  may 
dictate),  with  a  little  additional  sugar,  and  the  same  proportion  of 
isingla^  will  make  very  good  blanc- mange.  Two  ounces  of  Jordan 
almonds  may  be  pounded  and  mixed  with  it,  but  they  are  not  needed 
with  the  cream. 

JAUMANGE^   OR  JAUNE   MANGER,   SOMETIMES   CALLED   DUTCH 

FLUMMERY. 

Four  on  the  very  thin  rind  of  a  large  lemon  and  half  a  pound  of 
sngar  broken  small,  a  pint  of  water,  and  keep  them  stirred  over  a 
gentle  fire  until  they  have  simmered  for  three  or  four  minutes,  then 
kave  the  saucepan  by  the  side  of  the  stove  that  the  syrup  may  taste 
well  of  the  lemon.  In  ten  or  fifteen  minutes  afterwards  add  two 
ounces  of  isinglass,  and  stir  the  mixture  often  until  this  is  dissolved, 
then  throw  in  the  strained  juice  of  four  sound  moderate-sized  lemons, 
and  a  pint  of  sherry ;  mix  the  whole  briskly  with  the  beaten  yolks  of 
eight  fresh  eggs,  and  pass  it  through  a  delicately  clean  hair-sieve : 
next  thicken  it  in  a  jar  or  jug  placra  in  a  pan  of  boiling  water,  turn 
it  into  a  bowl,  and  when  it  has  become  cool  and  been  allowed  to  settle 
for  a  minute  or  two,  pour  it  into  moulds  which  have  been  laid  in 
water.  Some  persons  add  a  small  glass  of  brandy  to  it,  and  deduct 
so  much  from  tne  quantity  of  water. 

Kind  of  1  lemon;  sugar,  8  oz.;  water,  1  pint:  3  or  4  minutes. 
Jsinglass,  2  oz. ;  juice,  4  lemons ;  yolks  of  eggs,  8 ;  wine,  1  pint ; 
brandy  (at  pleasure),  1  ivineglassful.

Obs. — We  have  retained  here  the  old-fashioned  name  of  blanc-

QUINCE  BLANC-MAlfOB. 

{DeHeiousJ) 

This,  if  carefhlly  made,  and  with  ripe  quinces,  is  one  of  the  moii 
richly-flavoured  preparations  of  fruit  that  we  have  ever  tasted ;  and 
the  receipt,  we  may  venture  to  say,  will  be  altogether  new  to  the 
reader.  Dissolve  in  a  pint  of  prepared  juice  of  quinces  (aee  page 
456),  an  ounce  of  the  best  isinglass ;  next,  add  ten  ounces  of  sugar, 
roughly  pounded,  and  stir  these  together  gently  over  a  clear  fire, 
from  twenty  to  thirty  minutes,  or  until  the  juice  jellies  in  ^lii^ 
from  the  spoon.  Kemove  the  scum  carefUUy,  and  pour  the  boiliDg 
jelly  gradually  to  half  a  pint  of  thick  cream,  stirring  them  briskly 
together  as  they  are  mixed :  they  must  be  stirred  until  veiy  nearly 
cold,  and  then  poured  into  a  mould  which  has  been  rubbed  m  every 
part  with  the  smallest  possible  quantity  of  very  pure  salad  oil,  or  if 
more  convenient,  into  one  that  has  been  dipped  into  cold  water. 

06f.~ThiB  blancmanser  which  we  had  made  originally  on  the 
thought  of  the  moment  for  a  friend,  proved  so  Yerv  rich  in  flavour, 
that  we  inserted  the  exact  receipt  for  it,  as  we  had  had  it  made  <n 
our  first  trial ;  but  it  might  be  simplified  by  merely  boiling  the  juiee, 
sugar,  and  isinglass,  togetner  for  a  few  mlnutesi  and  then  mudng  thai 
with. the  cream.  An  ounce  and  a  half  of  isinglass  and  three-quarteis 
of  a  pint  of  cream  might  then  be  used  for  it.  The  juice  of  other 
fruit  may  be  substitutedfor  that  of  the  quinces. 

Juice  of  quinces,  1  pint ;  isinglass,  1  oz. :  5  to  10  minutes.  Sugv, 
10  oz. :  20  to  30  minutes.    Cream,  j[  pint. 

QUIKCE   BLAMC-MAMGE,   WITH   ALMOND   CREAM. 

When  cream  is  not  procurable,  which  will  sometimes  happen  ia 
the  depth  of  winter,  almonds,  if  plentifully  used,  will  afford  a  very 
good  substitute,  though  the  finer  bianc-mange  is  made  from  the  fore- 
going receipt.  On  four  ounces  of  almonds,  blanched  and  beaten  to 
ihe  smoothest  paste,  and  moistened  in  the  pounding  with  a  few  drops 
of  water,  to  prevent  their  oiling,  pour  a  pint  of  boilinff  quin  -juice ; 
itir  them  together,  and  torn  them  into  a  strong  cloth,  en  whidi  let  the 


uiAr.xxinJ  SWEET  DISHES.  479 

ends  be  held  and  twisted  different  ways  by  two  persons,  to  express 
the  cream  from  the  almonds ;  put  the  juice  again  on  the  fire,  with 
half  ajwund  of  sugar,  and  when  it  boils,  throw  in  nearly  an  ounce  of 
fine  winglass ;  simmer  the  whole  for  ^ve  minutes,  take  off  the  scum, 
stir  the  blanc-mange  until  it  is  nearly  cold,  then  mould  it  for  table. 
Increase  the  quantity  both  of  this  and  of  the  preceding  blanc-mange, 
when  a  large  dish  of  either  is  required. 

Quince-juice,  1  pint;  Jordan  almond,  4oz. ;  sugar, 4  lb. :  isinglass, 
nearly  I  oz, :  6  minutes. 

APRICOT   BLANC-MANOEy   OR   CRiaiE   PARISIENNB. 

Dissolve  gently  an  ounce  of  fine  isinglass  in  a  pint  of  new  milk  or 
of  thin  cream,  and  strain  it  through  a  folded  muslin ;  put  it  into  a 
clean  saucepcm,  with  three  ounces  of  sugar,  broken  into  small  lumps, 
and  when  it  boils,  stir  to  it  half  a  pint  of  rich  cream ;  add  it,  at  first 
by  spoonsful  only,  to  eight  ounces  of  the  finest  apricot  jam,  mix  them 
Texy  smooUily,  and  stir  the  whole  until  it  is  nearly  cold  that  the  jam 
may  not  sink  to  the  bottom  of  the  mould :  a  tablespoonful  of  lemon- 
juioe  will  improve  the  flavour. 

When  cream  is  scarce,  use  milk  instead,  with  an  additional  quarter 
of  an  ounce  of  isinglass,  and  enrich  it  by  pouring  it  boiling  on  the 
§Biiie  proportion  of  almonds  as  for  the  second  quince  blanc-manee  (see 
page  478).  Cream  can  in  all  cases  be  substituted  entirely  for  the 
milk,  when  a  vei^  rich  preparation  is  desired.  Peach  jam  will  answer 
admirably  for  this  receipt ;  but  none  of  any  kind  should  be  used  for 
it  which  has  not  been  passed  through  a  sieve  when  made. 

Isinglass,  1  oz. ;  new  milk,  1  pint ;  cream,  i  pint :  sugar,  3  oz. 
apricot  jam,  i  lb ;  lemon-juice,  1  tablespoonful.      Or,  peach  jam,  ^ 
Id.;  cream,  li  pint. 


CURRAKT   BLAIVC-MANGB. 

In  three-quarters  of  a  pint  of  clear  currant-juice,  drawn  from  the 
fruit  as  for  jelly,  and  stramed,  dissolve  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  isin- 
glass ;  add  nine  ounces  of  sugar  broken  small,  give  the  whole  a  boil, 
strain  it,  and  stir  it  by  slow  degrees  to  three-quarters  of  a  pint  of 
thick  cold  cream ;  when  it  is  less  than  milk-warm  pour  it  into  the 
moulds.  The  proportions  of  juice  and  cream  can  be  varied  to  the 
taste,  and  a  portion  of  raspberries  or  strawberries  added  to  the  cur- 
rants. Black  currants  would,  we  think,  make  an  agreeable  variety  of 
this  blanc-mange  for. persons  who  like  their  peculiar  flavour,  but  we 
have  not  tried  them.

cream,  }  pint.

VERY  GOOD  OLD-FASHIONED   BOILED   CUSTARD* 

Throw  into  a  pint  and  a  half  of  new  milk,  the  very  thin  rind  of  a 
fresh  lemon,  and  let  it  infuse  for  half  an  hour,  then  simmer  them 
together  for  a  few  minutes,  and  add  four  ounces  and  a  half  of  white 
ragar.  Beat  thoroughly  the  yolks  of  fourteen  fresh  e^^  mix  with 
theni  another  half-pint  of  new  milk,  stir  the  hoiling  muk  quickly  to 
them,  take  out  the  lemon-peel,  and  turn  the  custard  into  a  deep  jug ; 
set  this  over  the  fire  in  a  pan  of  boiling  water,  and  keep  the  custard 
Etirred  gently,  hut  without  ceasing,  until  it  begins  to  thicken ;  then 
move  tne  spoon  rather  more  quickly,  making  it  always  touch  the 
bottom  of  the  jug,  until  the  mixture  is  brought  to  the  point  of  boil- 
ing, when  it  must  be  instantly  taken  from  the  fire,  or  it  will  curdle 
m  a  moment.  Four  it  into  a  bowl,  and  keep  it  stirred  until  nearly 
cold,  then  add  to  it  by  degrees  a  wineglassml  of  good  brandy,  and 
two  ounces  of  blanched  almonds,  cut  into  spikes ;  or  omit  these,  at 
pleasure.  A  few  bitter  ones,  bruised,  can  be  boiled  in  the  milk  in 
lien  of  lemon-peel,  when  their  flavour  is  preferred. 

New  milk,  I  quart ;  rind  of  1  lemon  ;  sugar,  4i^  oz. ;  yolks  of  eggs, 
14;  salt,  less  than  ^  saltspoonful. 

RICH   BOILED  CUSTARD. 

Take  a  small  cupful  from  a  ^uart  of  fresh  cream,  and  simmer  the 
remainder  for  a  few  minutes  with  four  ounces  of  sugar  and  the  rind 
of  a  lemon,  or  give  it  any  other  flavour  that  may  be  preferred.  Beat 
and  strain  the  yolks  of  eight  eggs,  mix  them  witn  the  cupful  of  cream, 
and  stir  the  rest  boiling  to  them :  thicken  the  custard  like  the  pre- 
ceding one. 

Cream,  1  quart ;  sugar,  4  oz. ;  yolks  of  eggs,  8. 

THE   queen's   custard.

|bt  and  a  half  of  boiling  cream  which  has  been  sweetened,  with  three 
mmces  of  sugar;  add  tibe  smallest  pinch  of  nit,  and  thicken  the 
CDfftard  as  usmd.  When  nearly  cold,  flavour  it  with  a  glass  and  a 
half  of  noyau,  maraschino,  or  cuirasseau,  and  add  the  sliced  almonds 
or  not,  at  pleasure.

CURRANT  CUSTARD.

beaten  eggs ;  thicken  the  custard  in  a  jug  set  into  a  pan  of  vHa; 
pour  it  out,  stir  it  till  nearly  cold,  then  add  to  it  carefull j,  tnd  W 
degrees,  half  a  pint  of  rich  cream,  and  last  of  all  two  tabkqnon 
of  strained  lemon-juice.  When  the  currants  are  very  ripe  omit  oae 
ounce  of  the  sugar. 

White  currants  and  strawberries,  cherries,  red  or  white  Tu^tenks 
or  a  mixture  of  any  of  these  fruits,  may  be  used  for  these  eostMr^ 
with  good  effect :  they  are  excellent.

i^UINGE  OR  APPLE  CUSTARDS.

of  strained  lemon-juice,  and  from  four  to  six  ounces  of  sngar  afioad- 
ing  to  the  acidity  of  the  fruit ;  stir  these  boiling,  quickly,  and  in  sail 
portions,  to  eight  well-beaten  eggs,  and  thicken  the  custard  in  a  j^ 
placed  in  a  pan  of  boiling  water,  in  the  usual  manner.  A  Imrga  p** 
portion  of  lemon-juice  and  a  high  flavouring  of  a  rind  can  ht  gna 
when  approved.  For  quince  custards,  which  if  well  made  are  eiaf 
lent,  observe  the  same  directions  as  for  the  apple,  but  omit  the  kaoi- 
juioe.  As  we  have  before  observed,  all  custards  are  much  finer  «^ 
made  with  the  yolks  only  of  the  eggs,  of  which  the  number  msift  he 
increased  nearly  half,  when  this  is  done.

spoonml ;  susar,  4  to  6  os. ;  egg^  8.  Quince  custards,  saiiie  pnpv- 
tions,  but  no  lemon-juice. 

Ois. — In  making  lemon-creams  the  apple-juice  may  he  saltf^ 
tuted  very  advantageously  for  water^  without  varying  the  neeqfLm

CHOCOLATE  CUSTARDS. 

Dissolve  gently  by  the  side  of  the  fire  an  ounce  and  a  half  of  the 
hest  chocolate  in  rather  more  than  a  wineglaasful  of  water,  and  then 
boil  it  until  it  is  perfectly  smooth ;  mix  with  it  a  pint  of  milk  well 
flavoured  with  lemon  peel  or  vanilla,  add  two  ounces  of  fine  sugar, 
and  when  the  whole  boils,  stir  it  to  five  well-beaten  eggs  which  have 
been  strained.  Put  the  custard  into  a  jar  or  jug,  set  it  into  a  pan  of 
boiling  water,  and  stir  it  without  ceasmg  until  it  is  thick.  Vo  not 
put  it  into  glasses  or  a  dish  until  it  is  nearly  or  quite  cold.  These, 
as  well  as  all  other  custards,  are  infinitely  finer  when  made  with  the 
yolks  only  of  the  ^gs,  of  which  the  number  must  then  be  increased. 
Two  ounces  of  chocolate,  a  pint  of  milk,  half  a  pint  of  cream,  two 
or  three  ounces  of  su^ar,  and  eight  yolks  of  eggs,  will  make  very 
superior  custards  of  this  kind. 

Kasped  chocolate,  1^  oz. ;  water,  1  large  wineglassful :  5  to  8 
minutes.  New  milk,  1  pint ;  eggs,  5 ;  sugar,  2  oz.  Or :  chocolate, 
2  oz.;  water,  i  pint ;  new  milk,  1  pint :  sugar,  2^  to  3  oz. ;  cream,  ^ 
pint ;  yolks  of  eggs,  8. 

Obs. — Either  of  these  may  be  moulded  by  dissolving  from  half  to 
three  quarters  of  an  ounce  of  isinglass  in  the  milk.  The  proportion 
of  chocolate  can  be  increased  to  the  taste. 

COMMON   BAKED   CUSTARD. 

Mix  a  quart  of  new  milk  with  eight  well  beaten  eggs,  strain  the 
mixture  through  a  fine  sieve,  and  sweeten  it  with  from  five  to  eight 
ounces  of  sugar,  according  to  the  taste ;  add  a  small  pinch  of  salt,  and 
pour  the  custard  into  a  deep  dish  with  or  without  a  lining  or  rim  of 
paste,  grate  nutmeg  or  lemon  rind  over  the  top,  and  buEe  it  in  a 
tery  slow  oven  from  twenty  to  thirty  minutes,  or  longer,  should  it 
Dot  be  firm  in  the  centre.  A  custard,  if  well  made,  and  properly 
baked,  will  be  quite  smooth  when  cut,  without  the  honey-combed 
ippearance  which  a  hot  oven  gives ;  and  there  will  be  no  whey  in  the 
lisn. 

New  mOk,  1  quart ;  eggs,  8 ;  sugar,  5  to  8  oz. ;  salt,  ^  saltspoon- 
Tul ;  nutm^  or  lemon-grate :  baked,  slow  oven,  30  to  40  minutes,  or 
Biore.

irell'beaten  yolks  of  ten  fresh  eggn,  and  the  whites  of  four ;  strain  the

FRENCH  CUSTARDS  OB  CBEAH8.

the  stewpan  to  prevent  the  steam  falling  from  it  into  the  «» 
When  none  is  at  hand  of  a  form  to  allow  of  this,  it  is  better  to  wi 
charcoal  fire,  and  to  lay  an  oven-leaf,  or  tin,  over  the  pan,  m^  •

there  is  always  more  or  less  of  difficulty  in  keeping  a  coal  fire  eols^

as  to  form  a  border  an  inch  deep.    A  few  petals  of  fresh  oia^

very  superior  to  that  derived  from  the  distilled  water.    Half  apwij 
vanilla,  cut  in  short  lengths,  and  well  bruised,  may  be  used  os" 
of  either ;  but  the  milk  should  then  stand  some  time  by  tk* 
before  or  after  it  boils,  and  it  must  be  strained  throiJ^h  a  bsv 
before  it  is  added  to  the  eggs,  as  the  small  seed  of  the  vanilhw 
probably  pass  through  a  sieve.

yolks  of  six,  and  the  whites  of  three  eggs.  Dissolve  in  half  a  pint  of

A   MERINGUE  OF   RHUDARBy   OR   GREEN   GOOSEBERRIES. 

Weigh  a  pound  of  delicate  young  rhubarb-stems  after  they  have 
been  carefully  pared  and  cut  into  short  lengths ;  mix  eight  ounces  of 
pounded  sugar  with  them,  and  stew  them  gently  until  they  form  a 
emooth  pulp;  then  quicken  the  boiling,  and  stir  them  often  until  they 
are  xeducea  to  a  tolerably  dry  marnudade.  When  the  fruit  has 
reached  this  point  turn  it  from  the  pan  and  let  it  stand  until  it  is 
quite  cold.  Separate  the  whites  of  four  fresh  eggs  carefully  from  the 
volks,  and  whisk  them  to  a  froth  sufficiently  solid  to  remain  standing 
in  points  when  it  is  dropped  from  the  whisk  or  fork.  Conmion  cooks 
sometimes  fail  entirely  in  very  light  preparations  from  not  properly 
understanding  this  extremely  easy  process,  which  requires  nothing 
beyond  plenty  of  space  in  the  bowl  or  basin  used,  and  regular  but 
not  violent  whisking  until  the  eg^s  whiten,  and  gradually  assume  the 
appearance  of  snow.  No  drop  ofliquid  must  remain  at  the  bottom  of 
toe  basin,  and  the  mass  must  be  firm  enough  to  stand  up,  as  has  been 
flaid,  in  points.  When  in  this  state,  mingle  with  it  four  neaped  table- 
spoonsful  of  dry  sifted  sugar,  stir  these  gently  together,  and  when 
they  are  quite  mixed,  lay  them  lightly  over  the  rhubarb  in  a  rather 
deep  tart-dish.  Place  the  meringue  in  a  moderate  oven  and  bake  it 
for  about  half  an  hour,  but  ascertain,  before  it  is  served,  that  the 
centre  is  quite  firm.  The  crust  formed  by  the  white  of  egg  and 
PQgar,  which  is  in  fact  the  meringue^  should  be  of  a  light  equal 
brown,  and  crisp  quite  through.  If  placed  in  an  exceedingly  slow 
oven,  the  underpart  of  it  will  remain  half  liquid,  and  give  an  unin- 
\iting  appearance  to  the  fruit  when  it  is  served.  Unless  the  rhubarb 
should  be  very  acid,  six  ounces  of  sugar  will  be  sufficient  to  sweeten 
it  for  many  tastes.  It  is  a  great  improvement  to  this  dish  to  diminish 
the  proportion  of  fruit,  and  to  pour  some  thick  boiled  custard  upon  it 
before  the  meringne  is  laid  on. 

06«. — When  gooseberries  are  substituted  for  spring-fruit,  a  pint 
ind  a  half  will  he  sufficient  for  this  preparation,  or  even  a  smaller 
{ffoportion  when  only  one  of  quite  moderate  size  is  required.    In  the

Boil  down  the  rhubarb  with  seyen  ounces  of  sogar,  alter  hsraf

CHAP,  xzm.]  BWEET  DISHES.  487 

a  proper  degree  of  colour,  and  it  will  be  liable  to  break  in  the 
disning.  The  strips  of  bread  must  of  course  join  very  perfectly,  for 
if  any  spaces  were  left  between  them  the  syrup  of  the  fruit  would 
escape  and  destroy  the  good  appearance  of  the  dish :  should  there  not 
have  been  sufficient  marmalade  prepared  to  fill  the  mould  entirely,  a 
jar  of  quince  or  apricot  jam,  or  of  preserved  cherries  even,  may  be 
added  to  it  with  advantap;e.  The  butter  should  be  well  drained  from 
the  Charlotte  before  it  is  taken  from  the  mould;  and  sugar  may  be 
sifted  thickly  over  it  before  it  is  served,  or  it  may  be  covered  with 
any  kind  of  clear  red  jelly. 

A  more  el^ant,  and  we  think  an  easier  mode  of  forming  the  crust, 
is  to  line  the  mould  with  small  rounds  of  bread  stamped  out  with  a 
plain  cake  or  paste  cutter,  then  dipped  in  butter,  and  placed  with  the 
edges  sufficiently  one  over  the  other  to  hold  the  fruit  securely :  the 
strips  of  bread  are  sometimes  arranged  in  the  same  way. 

f  to  1  hour,  quick  oven. 

MARMALADE  FOR   THE   CHARLOTTE. 

Weigh  three  pounds  of  good  boiling  apples,  after  they  have  been 
pared,  cored,  and  quartered;  put  them  into  a  stewpan  with  six 
ounces  of  fresh  butter,  three  quarters  of  a  pound  of  sugar  beaten  to 
powder,  three  quarters  of  a  teaspoonful  of  pounded  cinnamon,  and 
the  strained  juice  of  a  lemon ;  let  these  stew  over  a  gentle  fire,  until 
they  form  a  perfectly  smooth  and  dry  marmalade;  keep  them  often 
atiired  that  tney  may  not  burn,  and  let  them  cool  before  they  are 
put  into  the  crust    This  quantity  is  for  a  moderate-sized  Charlotte. 

A   CHARLOTTE  A   LA   PARISIENNE. 

This  dish  is  sometimes  called  in  England  a  Vienna  cake ;  and  it 
is  known  here  also,  we  believe,  as  a  Gateaux  de  Bordeaux,  Cut 
horizontally  into  half-inch  slices  a  Savoy  or  sponge  cake,  and  cover 
each  slice  with  a  different  kind  of  preserve ;  replace  them  in  their 
original  form,  and  spread  equally  over  the  cake  an  icing  made  with 
the  whites  of  three  eggs,  and  four  ounces  of  the  finest  pounded  sugar ; 
rift  more  sugar  over  it  in  every  part,  and  put  it  into  a  very  gentle 
oven  to  dry.  The  eggs  should  be  whisked  to  snow  before  they  are 
used.  One  kind  of  preserve,  instead  of  several,  can  be  used  for  this 
dish;  and  a  rice  or  a  pound  cake  may  supply  the  place  of  the 
Savoy  or  sponge  biscuit. 

A   GERTRUDE  A   LA   CREME. 

Slice  a  plain  pound  or  rice  cake  as  for  the  Charlotte  a  la  Parisienne^ 
and  take  a  round  out  of  the  centre  of  each  slice  with  a  tin-cutter 
before  the  preserve  is  laid  on ;  replace  the  whole  in  its  original  form,  ice 
the  outside  with  a  green  or  rose  coloured  idng  at  pleasure,  and  dry

to  table,  fill  it  with  wull-drained  whipped  cream,  flaToiii«d  w  Ibia

AROCB    DOCE  (OR  SWEET   RICE^   A   LA   PORTUGAISE.) 

Wash  thoroughly,  then  drain,  and  wipe  dry  in  a  soft  cloth,  half  a 
pound  of  the  best  Carolina  rice.  Pour  to  it  three  pints  of  new  milk, 
and  when  it  has  gently  stewed  for  half  an  hour,  add  eight  ounces  of 
sugar  broken  into  small  lumps,  let  it  boil  until  it  is  dry  and  tender, 
and  when  it  is  nearly  so,  stir  to  it  two  ounces  of  blanched  almonds, 
chopped*  or  pounded.  Turn  the  rice  when  done  into  shallow  dishes 
or  soup  plates,  and  shake  it  until  the  surface  is  smooth ;  then  sift 
over  it  rather  thickly  through  a  muslin,  some  freshly-powdered 
cinnamon,  which  will  give  it  the  appearance  of  a  baked  pudding. 
Serve  it  cold.  It  will  remain  good  for  several  days.  This  is  quite 
the  best  sweet  preparation  of  rice  that  we  have  ever  eaten,  and  it  is 
a  very  favourite  dish  in  Portugal,  whence  the  receipt  w^as  derived. 
One  or  two  bitter  almonds,  pounded  with  the  sweet  ones,  might  n 
little  improve  its  flavour,  and  a  few  spoonsful  of  rich  cream  could 
occasionally  be  substituted  for  a  small  portion  of  the  milk,  but  it 
should  not  be  added  until  the  preparation  is  three  parts  done. 

Rice,  8  oz. ;  milk,  3  pints :  30  minutes.  Sugar,  8  oz. :  1  hour  or 
more.     Pounded  almonds,  2  oz, ;  cinnamon,  1  teaspoonful. 

Obs — The  rice  must  be  frequently  stirred  w'uile  boiling,  par* 

^  The  Portugaese  use  them  not  rery  finely  chopped.

BDTTERED   CHERRIRS.      (CERISBB   AU   BEURBE.)

hot  on  the  fried  bread.  Two  pounds  of  the  fruit,  when  it  ia  stoi»d, 
will  be  required  for  a  full-sizea  dish. 

SWEET   MACARONT. 

Drop  gently  into  a  pint  and  a  half  of  new  milk,  when  it  is  boOo^ 
fast,  four  ounces  of  fine  pipe  macaroni,  add  a  grain  or  two  of  salt,  aaS 
Bome  thin  strips  of  lemon  or  orange  rind :  cinnamon  can  be  sob- 
fitituted  for  these  when  preferred.  Simmer  the  macaroni  by  a  gentle 
fire  until  it  is  tolerably  tender,  then  add  from  two  to  three  ounces 
of  sugar  broken  small,  and  boil  it  till  the  i>ipe8  are  soft,  and 
swollen  to  their  full  size ;  drain,  and  arrange  it  in  a  hot  dish  ;  stir 
the  milk  quickly  to  the  well-beaten  yolks  of  three  large*  or  of 
four  small  eggs,  shake  them  round  briskly  over  the  fire  until  ther 
thicken,  pour  them  over  the  macaroni  and  serve  it  immediately ;  or 
instead  of  the  eggs,  heat  and  sweeten  some  very  rich  cream,  ponr  it 
on  the  Irained  macaroni,  and  dust  finely-powdered  dnnamon  over

through  a  muslin,  or  strew  it  thickly  with  crushed  macaroons.  For

BERMUDA   WITCHES. 

Slice  equally  some  rice,  pound,  or  Savoy  cake,  not  more  than  the 
sixth  of  an  inch  thick ;  take  off  the  hrown  edges,  and  spread  one  half 
of  it  with  Guava  jelly,  or,  if  more  convenient,  with  fine  strawberry, 
raspberry,  or  currant  jelly  of  the  best  quality  (see  Norman  receipt, 
478) ;  on  this  strew  thicldy  some  fresh  cocoa-nut  srated  small  and 
lightly ;  press  over  it  the  remainder  of  the  cake,  and  trim  the  whole 
into  good  form ;  divide  the  slices  if  large,  pile  them  slopingly  in  the 
centre  of  a  dish  upon  a  very  white  napkin  folded  flat,  and  garnish  or 
intersperse  them  xnth.  small  sprigs  of  m3Ttle.  For  very  young 
people  a  Frenca  roll  or  two,  and  good  currant  jeUy,  red  or  white, 
will  supply  a  wholesome  and  inexpensive  dish. 

NESSELRODE   PUDDING. 

We  give  Monsieur  Car^me*s  own  receipt  for  this  favourite  and 
fashionable  dish,  not  having  ourselves  had  a  ^ood  opportimity  of 
proving  it;  but  as  it  ori^ated  with  him  he  is  the  best  authority 
for  it.  It  may  be  varied  m  many  ways,  which  the  taste  or  ingenuity 
of  the  reader  will  easily  suggest.  Boil  forty  fine  sound  Spanish 
chestnuts  quite  tender  in  plenty  of  water,  take  off  the  husks,  and 
pound  the  chestnuts  })erfectly  with  a  few  spoonsful  of  syrup ;  rub 
them  through  a  fine  sieve,  and  mix  them  in  a  basin  ivith  a  pint  of 
syrup  made  with  a  pound  of  sugar  clarified,  and  highly-flavoured 
>vith  a  nod  of  vanilla,  a  pint  of  rich  cream,  and  the  yolks  of  twelve 
eggs ;  thicken  the  mixture  like  a  boiled  custard ;  when  it  is  cold  put 
it  into  a  freezing  pot,  adding  a  glass  of  maraschino,  and  make  it  set  as 
an  iced  cream ;  then  add  an  ounce  of  preserved  citron  cut  in  dice,  two 
ounces  of  currants,  and  as  many  fine  raisins  stoned  and  divided  (all 
of  which  should  be  soaked  from  the  day  before  in  some  maraschmo 
vrith  a  httle  sugar) ;  the  whole  thus  mingled,  add  a  plateful  of 
-whipped  cream,  and  the  whites  of  three  eggs  prepared  as  for  Italian 
menngue.  When  the  pudding  is  perfectly  frozen,  mould  it  in  a 
pewter  mould  of  the  form  of  a  pine-apple,  and  place  it  again  in  the 
^  till  wanted  to  serve.  Preserved  cherries  may  be  substituted  for 
the  raisins  and  currants. 

Chestnuts,  40;  syrup,  1  pint  some  spoonsful;  vanilla,  1  pod; 
cream,  1  pint;  yolk  of  eggs,  12 ;  maraschino,  1  glassful ;  citron,  1  oz. ; 
currants,  2  oz. ;  raisins,  2  oz. ;  whipped  cream,  1  plateful ;  whites  of 
eggs  beaten  to  snow,  3. 

Obi' — As  Monsieur  Car^me  directs  the  eggs  for  his  Italian  me- 


492  MODERN  COOKE KT.  [cbat.  xxm. 

ringues  to  be  prepared  as  follows,  he  probably  intends  tbat  tber 
should  be  mixed  with  the  syrup  before  they  are  added  to  the  pudding 
Boil  together  half  a  pound  of  the  finest  sugar,  and  half  a  pint  of 
water,  until  they  begin  to  be  very  thick ;  then,  with  a  wooden  spcoa, 
work  the  sugar  against  the  side  of  the  pan  till  it  whitens ;  leare  it  I0 
cool  a  little,  work  it  again,  and  then  with  a  whisk  mingle  with  it  the 
eggs  whipped  to  a  very  firm  froth,  which  ought  to  pn^noe  a  pt^a- 
ration  very  white,  smooth,  and  brilliant 

STEWED   FIGS.      (a.   VERY  NICE  COMPOTE.) 

Put  into  an  enamelled  or  a  copper  stewpan,  four  onnoes  of  refined 
sugar,  the  very  thin  rind  of  a  large  and  fresh  lemon,  and  a  pint  of 
cold  water.  When  the  sugar  is  dissolved,  add  a  pound  of  fine  Tu-kg" 
figs,  and  place  the  steyrpan  on  a  trivet  above  a  moderate  fire,  or  upoo 
a  stove,  where  they  can  neat  and  swell  slowly,  and  be  very  gently 
stewed.  When  they  are  quite  tender,  add  to  them  two  glassesfoi  oif 
port  wine,  and  the  strained  juice  of  the  lemon ;  arrange  them  in  1 
glass  dish,  and  serve  them  cold.  From  two  hours  to  two  and  a  haH 
of  the  gentlest  stewing  will  generally  be  sufficient  to  render  the  6^ 
fit  for  table.  Orange-juice  and  rind  can  be  used  for  them  at  pleasoA 
instead  of  the  lemon ;  two  or  three  bitter  almonds  may  be  boiled  is 
the  syrup  to  give  it  flavour,  and  any  wine  can  be  used  for  it  wkica 
mav  be  preferred,  but  port  is  best. 

This  compdUi  may  be  served  in  the  second  course  hot,  in  a  rice- 
border  ;  or  cold  for  dessert.

or  with  parcnment  and  thick  paper  when  it  is  not  secured  from  the 
air  with  corks. 


▲   FEW   GENERAL  RULES   AND  DIRECTIONS   FOR    PRESERTING. 

1.  Let  everything  used  for  the  purpose  be  delicatelj  dean  asd 
dry ;  bottles  especially  so. 

2.  Never  place  a  preserving  pan  Jlai  a/xm  ihe  fire^  as  this  vi!l 
render  the  preserve  liable  to  1mm  to^  as  it  is  called ;  that  is  to  say,  to 
adhere  closely  to  the  metal,  and  then  to  bum ;  it  should  rest  alwqpi 
on  a  trivet  (that  shown  with  the  French  furnace  is  voy  conreinsl, 
even  for  a  common  grate),  or  on  the  lowered  bar  of  a  kitchen  raa^ 
when  there  is  no  regular  preserving  stove  in  a  house. 

3.  Afrer  the  su^ar  is  added  to  them,  stir  the  preserves  gentl;^  at 
first,  and  more  quickly  towards  the  end,  without  quitting  them  nstil 
they  are  done :  this  precaution  will  always  prevent  the  chsnee  e£ 
their  being  spoiled. 

4.  All  preserves  should  be  perfectly  cleared  from  the  scnm  as  it  ri«a 

5.  Fruit  which  is  to  be  preserved  in  syrup  must  first  be  hhnrhffd 


CHAP.  XXIV.]  PRESERVES*  497 

or  boiled  gently,  until  it  is  sufficiently  softened  to  absorb  the  sugar ; 
and  a  thin  syrup  must  be  poured  on  it  at  first,  or  it  will  shrivel 
instead  of  remaining  plump,  and  becoming  clear.  Thus,  if  its  weight. 
of  sugar  is  to  be  allowed,  and  boiled  to  a  syrup  with  a  pint  of  water 
to  the  pound,  only  half  the  weight  must  be  taken  at  first,  and  this 
must  not  be  boiled  with  the  water  more  than  fifteen  or  twenty 
minutes  at  the  commencement  of  the  process ;  a  part  of  the  remain- 
ing sugar  must  be  added  every  time  tne  syrup  is  reboiled,  unless  it 
should  be  otherwise  directed  in  the  receipt. 

6.  To  preserve  both  the  true  flavour  and  the  colour  of  fruit  in 
jams  and  jellies,  boil  them  rapidly  until  they  are  well  reduced,  before 
the  sugar  is  added,  and  quickly  ailerwards,  but  do  not  allow  them  to 
become  so  much  thickened  that  the  sugar  will  not  dissolve  in  them 
easily,  and  throw  up  its  scum.  In  some  seasons,  the  juice  is  so  much 
richer  than  in  others,  that  this  effect  takes  place  almost  before  one  is 
aware  of  it ;  but  the  drop  which  adheres  to  the  skimmer  when  it  is 
held  up,  will  show  the  state  it  has  reached. 

7.  ifever  use  tin,  iron,  or  pewter  spoons,  or  skimmers,  for  pre- 
serves, as  they  will  convert  the  colour  of  red  fruit  into  a  dingy 
purple,  and  impart,  besides,  a  very  unpleasant  flavour. 

8.  When  cheap  jams  or  jellies  are  required,  make  them  at  once 
with  Lisbon  sugar,  but  use  that  which  is  voeU  refined  always,  for 
preserves  in  general;  it  is  a  false  economy,  as  we  have  elsewhere 
observed,  to  purchase  an  inferior  kind,  as  there  is  great  waste  from  it 
in  the  quantit^r  of  scum  which  it  throws  up.  The  best  has  been  used 
for  all  the  receipts  given  here. 

9.  Let  fruit  for  preserving  be  gathered  always  in  perfectly  dry 
weather,  and  be  free  both  from  the  morning  and  evening  dew,  and  as 
much  so  as  possible  from  dust.  When  bottled,  it  must  be  steamed  or 
baked  during  the  day  on  which  it  is  gathered,  or  there  will  be  a  great 
loss  from  the  bursting  of  the  bottles ;  and  for  jams  and  jellies  it 
cannot  be  too  soon  boiled  down  after  it  is  taken  from  the  trees. 

TO   EXTRACT   THE  JUICE   OP   PLUMS   FOR  JELLY. 

Take  the  stalks  from  the  fruit,  and  throw  aside  all  that  is  not 
perfectly  sound :  put  it  into  very  clean,  large  stone  jars,  and  give 
part  of  the  harder  kinds,  such  as  buUaces  and  damson,  a  gash  with  a 
knife  as  they  are  thrown  in ;  do  this  especially  in  filling  the  upper 
part  of  the  jars.  Tie  one  or  two  folds  of  thick  paper  over  them,  and 
set  them  for  the  night  into  an  oven  from  whicn  the  bread  has  been 
drawn  four  or  five  hours ;  or  cover  them  with  bladder,  instead  of 
paper,  place  them  in  pans,  or  in  a  copper*  with  water  which  will  reach 
to  quite  two-thirds  of  their  height,  and  boil  them  gently  from  two  to 
three  hours,  or  until  the  fruit  is  quite  soft,  and  nas  yielded  all  the 
juice  it  will  afford :  this  last  is  the  safer  and  better  mode  for  jellies  of 
delicate  colour. 

*  The  fruit  Bteams  perfecUj  in  this,  if  the  cover  be  placed  over  it.

TO   WEIGH    THE  JUICE  OP  FRUIT- 

Fut  a  bann  into  one  scale,  and  its  weight  into  the  other ;  add  ti 
this  last  the  weight  which  is  required  of  the  juice,  and  poor  into  the 
basin  as  much  as  will  balance  the  scales.  It  is  always  bc^r  to  weigk 
than  to  measure  the  juice  for  preserving,  as  it  can  genefsUy  be  doe 
with  more  exactness. 

RHUBARB  JAM. 

The  stalks  of  the  rhubarb  (or  spring-fruit,  as  it  is  called)  sboaM 
be  taken  for  this  preserve,  which  is  a  very  good  and  useful  one,  vkik 
they  are  fresh  and  young.  Wipe  them  very  dean,  psre  dim 
quickly,  weigh,  and  cut  them  into  half-inch  lengths ;  to  eveir  pound 
add  an  equtu  weight  of  good  sugar  in  fine  powder ;  nux  than  vel 
together,  let  them  remain  for  ten  minutes  or  a  quarter  of  an  boor  ts 
draw  out  the  iuice  a  little,  then  turn  them  into  a  preserving  pan,  Itf 
them  heat  rather  slowly,  but  as  soon  as  the  stalks  are  tender  boil  tk 
preserve  rapidly,  stirring  it  well  for  about  half  an  hour.  It  vill  k 
of  excellent  flavour,  and  will  serve  admirably  for  tarts. 

A  somewhat  cheaper  mode  of  making  tne  jam  is  to  stew  it  lad 
tender  in  its  own  juices,  and  then  to  boil  it  rapidly  until  it  is  toknikT 
dry,  to  add  to  it  only  half  its  weight  of  sugar,  and  to  give  it  fro* 
twenty  to  thirty  minutes  boiling. 

Spring  fruit  (rhubarb),  4  \h&, ;  sugar,  4  lbs. :  heated  slowly,  lai 
when  tender,  boiled  quickly,  ZO  minutes. 

ORERN   GOOSEBERRY  JELLT. 

Wash  some  freshly  gathered  gooseberries  very  clean ;  after  faariair 
taken  off  the  tops  and  stalks,  then  to  each  pound  pour  three-qwtn 
of  a  pint  of  spring  water,  and  simmer  them  until  they  are  wel 
broken ;  turn  the  whole  into  a  jellv-bag  or  doth,  and  let  alt  ck 
juice  drain  through ;  weigh  and  boil  it  rapidly  for  fifteen  woMa. 
Draw  it  from  the  fire,  and  stir  in  it  until  entirely  dissolved,  an  e^ 
weight  of  good  sugar  reduced  to  powder ;  boil  the  jelly  from  fifka 
to  twenty  minutes  longer,  or  until  it  jellies  strongly  on  the  spoca  « 
skimmer ;  clear  it  perfectly  from  scum,  and  pour  it  into  small  wi 
moulds  or  glasses.  It  ought  to  be  very  pale  and  transparent.  1^ 
sugar  may  be  added  to  the  juice  at  first,  and  the  presierve  boiled  fiva 
twenty-five  to  thirty-five  minutes,  but  the  colour  will  not  then  be» 
good.  When  the  fruit  abounds,  the  juice  may  be  drawn  from  it  viti^ 
very  little  water,  as  directed  for  apples,  page  523,  when  it  ail 
require  much  less  boiling.

ORBEN     OOOSEBERRT    JAM. 

(Firm  and  qfgaad  colour.) 

Cut  the  stalks  and  tops  from  the  fruit,  weigh  and  bruise  it  slightly, 
boil  it  for  six  or  seven  minutes,  keeping  it  well  turned  during  the 
time,  then  to  every  three  pounds  of  gooseberries  add  two  and  a  half 
of  sugar  beaten  to  powder,  and  boil  tne  preserve  quickly  for  three- 
quarters  of  an  hour.  It  must  be  constantly  stirred,  and  carefully 
cleared  from  scum.  This  makes  a  fine,  firm,  and  refreshing  preserve 
if  the  fruit  be  rubbed  through  a  sieve  before  the  sugar  is  aidded.  K 
well  reduced  afterwards,  it  may  be  converted  into  a  gateau^  or 
gooseberry-solid,  with  three  pounds  of  sugar,  or  even  a  smaller  pro- 
portion. The  preceding  jam  will  often  turn  in  perfect  form  from  the 
moulds  or  jars  which  contain  it;  and  if  freed  irom  the  seeds,  would 
be  very  excellent :  it  is  extremely  good  even  made  as  above.  For 
all  preserves,  the  reduction,  or  boiling  down  to  a  certain  consistence, 
should  take  place  principally  before  the  sugar  is  mingled  with  them ; 
pnd  this  has  the  best  effect  when  added  to  the  fruit  and  dissolved  in 
it  by  defpcees. 

Green  gooseberries,  6  lbs.:  6  to  7  minutes.  Sugar,  6  lbs.;  I 
b«>ur. 

TO   DRY  GREEN   GOOSEBERRIES. 

Take  the  finest  green  gooseberries,  fully  grown,  and  freshly 
gathered ;  cut  off  the  buds,  split  them  across  the  tops  half  way  down, 
and  with  the  small  end  of  a  tea  or  of  an  egg  spoon,  scoop  out  the 
seeds.  Boil  together  for  fifteen  minutes  a  pound  and  a  half  of  the 
finest  sugar,  and  a  pint  of  water ;  skim  this  syrup  thoroughly  and 
throw  into  it  a  pound  of  the  seeded  gooseberries ;  sumner  them  from 
five  to  seven  minutes,  when  they  ought  to  be  clear. and  tender;  when 
they  are  so,  lift  them  out,  and  throw  as  many  more  into  the  syrup ; 
dram  them  a  little  when  done,  spread  them  singly  on  dishes,  and  dry 
them  very  gradually  in  a  quite  cool  stove  or  oven,  or  in  a  sunny 
-window.  They  will  keep  well  in  the  syrup,  and  may  be  potted  in 
it,  and  dried  when  wanted  for  use. 

Green  gooseberries  without  seeds,  2  lbs. ;  water,  1  pint ;  sugar,  1^ 
lb. :  boil^  15  minutes.    Gooseberries  simmered,  ^  to  7  minutes. 

GREEN   GOOSEBERRIES  FOR   TARTS. 

Fill  very  clean,  dry,  wide-necked  bottles  with  gooseberries  gathered 
tbe  same  day,  and  before  they  have  attained  their  full  growth. 
Cork  them  lightly,  wrap  a  little  hay  round  each  of  them,  and  set 
them  up  to  their  necks  m  a  copper  of  cold  water  which  should  be 
brought  very  gradually  to  boil.    Let  the  fruit  be  gently  simmered 


500  MODERK  COOK^Br.  [cBAr.xxxf. 

until  it  appears  shninken  and  perfectly  scalded ;  then  take  out  tk 
bottles,  and  with  the  contents  of  one  or  two  fill  up  the  remainder,  tad 
use  great  care  not  to  break  the  fruit  in  doing  this.  When  ill  iit 
ready  pour  scalding  water  into  the  bottles  and  cover  the  goosebEniB 
entirely  with  it,  or  they  will  become  mouldy  at  the  top.  Cork  tk 
bottles  well  immediately,  and  cover  the  necks  iivith  melted  resia; 
keep  them  in  a  cool  place  ;  and  when  the  goosebeTries  are  osed 
pour  off  the  greater  part  of  the  water,  and  add  sugar  as  for  the  fiesh 
fruit,  of  which  they  will  have  the  flavour  and  appearance ;  and  tk^ 
will  be  found  more  wholesome  prepared  in  this  manner  tbaa  i 
simply  baked  or  steamed  in  the  bottles.

JELLY  OF  RIPE  GOOSEBERRISS. 

(Excellent) 

Take  the  tops  and  stalks  from  a  gallon  or  more  of  any  kind  of  veS*

dear  fire  until  they  have  yielded  all  their  juice,  which  should  then  be 
poured  off  without  pressing  the  fruit,  and  passed  first  through  a  fine 
ideTe,  and  afterwards  through  a  double  muslin-strainer,  or  a  jell^- 
bag.  Next  weigh  it,  and  to  every  three  pounds  add  one  of  white 
currant  juice,  which  has  previously  been  prepared  in  the  same  way ; 
boil  these  quickly  for  a  quarter  of  an  hour,  then  draw  them  from  the 
fire  and  stir  to  them  half  their  weight  of  good  sugar ;  when  this  is 
dissolved,  boil  the  jelly  for  six  minutes  longer,  skim  it  thoroughly, 
and  pour  it  into  jars  or  moulds.  If  a  very  large  quantity  be  made,

8u^r  is  added.

15  minutes.    Sugar,  2  lbs. :  6  minutes. 

Ohs. — The  same  proportion  of  red  currant  juice,  mixed  with  that 
of  the  gooseberries,  makes  an  exceedingly  nice  jelly. 

UNMIXED   GOOSEBERRT  JELLY. 

Boil  rapidly  for  ten  minutes  four  pounds  of  the  juice  of  red 
gooseberries,  prepared  as  in  the  preceding  receipt ;  take  it  from  the 
fire,  and  stir  in  it  until  dissolved  three  pounds  of  sugar  beaten  to 
powder ;  boil  it  a^in  for  five  minutes,  keeping  it  constantly  stirred 
and  thoroughly  skmuned. 

Juice  of  red  gooseberries,  4  lbs. :  10  minutes.  Sugar,  3  lbs.  :  5 
minutes. 

GOOSEBERRY   PASTE. 

Press  througli  a  sieve  the  gooseberries  from  which  the  juice  has 
been  taken  for  jelly,  without  naving  been  drained  very  closely  from

quarter,  or  untU  it  forms  a  dry  paste  in  the  pan ;  stir  to  it,  off  the 
fire,  six  ounces  of  good  pounded  sugar  for  each  pound  of  the  fruit, 
and  when  this  is  nearly  dissolved  boil  the  preserve  from  twenty  to 
twenty-five  minutes,  keeping  it  stirred  without  cessation,  as  it  will  be 
liable  to  burn  should  tnis  be  neglected.  Put  it  into  moulds,  or 
shallow  pans,  and  turn  it  it  out  when  wanted  for  table. 

Pulp  of  gooseberries,  4  lbs. :  1^  to  If  hour.  Sugar,  1}  lb. :  20  to 
25  minutes.

and  tender,  in  syrup  made  witn  a  pound  of  sugar  to  the  pint  of 
water,  boiled  untd  rather  thick.

TO   DRY  CHERRIES  WITH   SUGAIU 

(A  quick  and  easy  method.) 

Stone  some  fine,  sound,  Kentish  or  Flemish  cherries;  pat 
them  into  a  preserving  pan,  with  six  ounces  of  sugar  reduced  ts 
powder,  to  each  pound  of  the  fruit ;  set  them  over  a  moderate  fire. 
and  simmer  them  gently  for  nearly  or  quite  twenty  minntes;  hi 


CHAF.  ZZIY.]  PRESERVES.  603 

them  remain  in  the  syrnp  nntil  they  are  a  little  cooled,  then  turn 
them  into  a  sieve,  and  before  they  are  cold  lav  them  singly  on  dishes, 
and  dry  them  verjr  gradually,  as  directed  for  other  fruits.  When 
the  cherries  are  quite  ripe  the  stones  may  generally  be  drawn  out  with 
the  stalks,  by  pressing  the  fruit  gently  at  the  sanie  time ;  but  when 
this  method  fails,  they  must  be  extracted  with  a  new  quill,  cut  round 
at  the  end :  those  or  the  very  short-stalked,  turnip-shaped  cherry, 
which  abounds,  and  is  remarkably  fine  in  many  parts  of  JN^omiandy, 
and  which  we  have  occasionally  met  vrith  here,  though  it  is  not,  we 
believe,  very  abundant  in  our  markets,  are  easily  removed  with  a 
large  pin,  on  the  point  of  which  the  stone  may  be  caught  at  the  stalk 
end,  just  opnosite  the  seam  of  the  fruit,  and  drawn  out  at  the  top, 
leaving  the  cnerry  apparently  entire. 

DRIED   CHERRIES. 

(^Superior  Receipt.^ 

To  each  pound  of  cherries  weighed  after  they  are  stoned,  add  eight 
ounces  of  good  sugar,  and  boil  them  very  softly  for  ten  minutes: 
j<our  them  into  a  large  bowl  or  pan,  and  leave  them  for  two  days  in 
the  syrup;  then  simmer  them  again  for  ten  minutes,  and. set  them 
by  in  it  for  two  or  three  days ;  drain  them  slightly,  and  dry  them 
very  slowly,  as  directed  in  the  previous  receipts.  Keep  them  in  jars 
or  tin  canisters,  when  done.  These  cherries  are  generally  preferred 
to  such  as  are  dried  with  a  larger  proportion  of  sugar ;  but  when  the 
taste  is  in  favour  of  the  latter,  from  twelve  to  sixteen  ounces  can  be 
allowed  to  the  pound  of  fruit,  which  may  then  be  potted  in  the 
syrup  and  dried  at  any  time ;  though  we  think  the  flavour  of  the 
cherries  is  better  preserved  when  tms  is  done  within  a  fortnight  of 
their  being  boiled. 

Cherries,  stoned,  8  lbs. ;  sugar,  4  lbs. :  10  minutes.  Left  two  or 
three  days.  Boiled  again,  10  minutes ;  left  two  days ;  drained  and 
dried. 

CHERRIES  DRIED   WITHOUT   SUGAR. 

These  are  often  more  pleasant  and  refreshing  to  invalids  and  tra- 
vellers than  a  sweetened  confection  of  the  fruit,  their  flavour  and 
agreeable  acidity  being  well  preserved  when  they  are  simply  spread 
on  dishes  or  hamper-lids,  and  slowly  dried.*  Throw  aside  the 
bruised  and  decayed  fruit,  and  arrange  the  remainder  singly,  and 
with  the  stalks  uppermost  on  the  dishes.  The  Kentish  cherries  are 
best  for  the  purpose,  but  morellas  also  answer  for  it  excellently. 
The  former  are  sometimes  stoned,  and  simmered  until  quite  tender  in 
their  own  juice,  before  they  are  dried ;  but  this  is  scarcely  an  im- 
provement on  the  more  usual  method  of  leaving  them  entire. 

•  The  diflhes  on  which  they  are  laid  should  be  changed  daOv.
