A   DELICIOUS   CREAM-CAKE   AND   BWERt    RUSKS. 

When  in  very  sultry  weather  cream  becomes  acid  from  bein^  seat 
to  a  distance,  or  from  other  causes,  it  may  still  be  made  aTailaUe  far 
delicate  pastry-crust,  and  superlatiye  cakes,  biscuits,  and  bread;  Ins 
if  ever  so  slightly  putrid  it  will  be  fit  only  to  be  thrown  away.  The 
following  receipt  is  given  exactly  as  it  was  used  with  perfect  soeeei 
on  the  thought  of  the  moment,  when  we  first  had  it  tried.  GcinAie 
down  five  ounces  of  good  butter  into  a  pound  of  fine  flour,  then  lox 
thoroughly  with  them  half  a  pound  of  sifted  sugar,  a  few  gnamd 
salt,  and  two  ounces  of  candied  citron  or  orange-nnd  sliced  tmn ;  add 
something  more  than  half  a  pint  of  thick  and  rather  sour  aam 
mixed  with  two  well  whisked  eggs,  and  just  before  the  paste  b  pK 
into  the  moulds,  which  should  be  buttered  in  every  part  and  wf 
two- thirds  filled,  beat  thoroughly  into  it  half  a  teaspooni\il  of  tk 
very  best  carbonate  of  soda,  which  has  been  perfectly  blended  wA 
twice  the  quantity  of  sugar  and  of  flour,  and  rubb^  through  a  fee 
sieve,  or  worked  to  the  smoothest  powder  in  a  mortar,  or  in  ai^ 
other  way. 

For  the  convenience  of  having  it  baked  in  a  small  iron  otcd,  tb 
quantity  was  divided  into  two  oikes,  one  of  which  was  gently  polkd 
apart  with  a  couple  of  forks  while  still  hot,  and  then  set  again  iatt 
the  oven  and  crisped  with  a  gentle  heat  quite  through :  it  was  tbe 
converted  into  the  very  nicest  sweet  rusks.  Sufficient  cream  dioaU 
be  used  for  the  cakes  to  convert  the  ingredients  into  a  verr  Uik 
paste  or  thick  batter,  which  can  be  properly  worked  or  mixed  «itb  > 
wooden  spoon,  with  the  back  of  wmch  it  should  be  Teiy  lishdr 
beaten  up  before  it  is  moulded.  About  three-quarters  of'  an  aos 
will  bake  it  in  a  moderate  oven.  It  should  be  firm  on  the  sorftce^ 
as  all  li^ht  cakes  should  be — that  it  may  not  sink  aud  become  Imbtt 
after  it  is  drawn  out.  Turn  it  from  the  mould,  and  lay  it  on  is  odr 
upon  a  sieve  reversed,  to  cooL 

A   aOOD    LIGHT   LUNCHEON-CAKB   AND   BROWN   BRACX. 

Break  down  four  ounces  of  butter  into  a  couple  of  pounds  of  taa^ 
ind  work  it  quite  into  crumbs,  but  handle  it  very  lightly;  Buxka 


CHAP.  XXVI.]  CAKES*  555 

pinch  of  salt  and  four  ounces  of  pounded  snra*;  hollow  the  centre, 
and  stir  into  it  a  large  tablespoonful  of  solid  well- washed  yeast  (or 
an  ounce  of  Grerman  yeast  which  will  ferment  more  quickly),  diluted 
with  three-quarters  of  a  pint  of  warm  new  milk ;  when  sufficient  of 
the  surrounding  floor  is  mixed  with  it  to  form  a  thick  batter  strew 
more  flour  on  the  top,  lay  a  cloth  once  or  twice  folded  together  over 
the  pan,  and  let  it  remain  until  the  leaven  has  become  very  light : 
this  it  will  generally  be  in  an  hour  and  a  quarter,  or,  at  the  utmost, 
in  an  hour  and  a  half.  The  fermentation  may  be  quickened  by  in- 
creasing the  proportion  of  yeast,  but  this  is  better  avoided,  as  it  may 
chance  to  render  the  cake  bitter;  additional  time,  however,  must 
always  be  allowed  for  it  to  rise  when  but  a  small  quantity  is  used. 
When  the  leaven  is  at  the  proper  height,  add  to  a  couple  of  well 
whisked  ^gs,  sufficient  nearly-boiling  milk  to  warm  them,  and  mix 
them  with  the  other  ingredients ;  then  beat  well  into  the  cake  by 
degrees,  eight  ounces  more  of  pounded  sugar,  and  half  a  grated  nut- 
meg ;  cut  from  two  to  three  ounces  of  candied  citron  thin,  and  strew 
over  it ;  leave  it  again  to  rise,  as  before,  for  about  three-qjuarters  of 
an  hour ;  mix  the  citron  equally  with  it,  put  it  into  a  thickly  but- 
tered tin  or  earthen  pan,  and  bake  it  in  a  quick  oven  for  an  hour  and 
ten  minutes  at  the  least,  and  after  it  is  placed  in  it  let  it  not  be  moved 
until  it  is  quite  set,  or  it  will  possibly  be  heavy  at  the  top.  The 
grated  rinds  of  a  couple  of  lemons  will  improve  its  flavour.  Fine 
Lisbon  sugar  can  be  used  to  sweeten  it  instead  of  pounded,  but  the 
difference  of  expense  would  be  very  slight,  and  the  cake  would  not  be 
so  good;  the  quantity  can,  of  course,  be  diminished  when  it  is  con- 
sidered too  much.  Three-quarters  of  a  pound  of  currants  can,  at 
dioioe,  be  substituted  for  the  citron.  Three  ounces  of  carraway 
seeds  vnll  convert  it  into  common  bravm  bracks  or  Irish  seed-cake. 
For  the  manner  of  purifying  yeast,  see  Chapter  XXXL 


A    VERY   CHEAP   LUNCHEON   BISCUIT,   OR   NURSERY  CAKE. 

Two  or  three  pounds  of  white  bread  dough  taken  when  ready  for 
the  oven,  will  make  a  good  light  biscuit  if  well  managed,  with  the 
addition  of  from  half  to  three-quarters  of  a  pound  of  sugar,  a  very 
small  quantity  of  butter,  and  a  few  currants,  or  carraway-seeds,  or  a 
teaspoonful  of  mixed  spices.  The  dough  should  be  rather  firm ;  the 
butter  should  first  be  well  kneaded  into  it  in  small  portions,  then  the 
sugar  added  in  the  same  way,  and  next  the  currants  or  spice.  The 
whole  should  be  perfectly  and  equally  mingled,  flour  being  slightly 
dredged  upon  it  as  it  is  worked,  if  needful.  It  must  then  be  allowed 
to  rise  until  it  is  very  light,  when  it  should  again  be  kneaded  down, 
but  not  heavily ;  ana  when  it  has  once  more  risen,  it  should  be  sent 
without  delay  to  the  oven.  An  ounce  of  butter  to  the  pound  of 
dough  will  be  sufficient  for  it.  Much  richer  cakes  can  be  made  thus, 
and  they  will  be  extremely  good  if  care  be  taken  to  let  them  rise 


556  MODEBK  COOKERY.  [chap,  xx^ 

euffidently  before  they  are  baked.  We  regret  that  we  cannot  mul- 
tiply our  receipts  for  them.  Sultana  raisins  are  an  ezoellent  sub- 
stitute for  currants  in  these  and  other  common  cakes. 

ISLB  OF  WIGHT  DOUGH-NUTS. 

Work  smoothly  together  with  the  fingers  four  ounces  of  good  krd, 
and  four  pounds  of  nour ;  add  half  a  pound  of  fine  brown  sugar,  two 
tablespoonsful  of  allspice,  one  drachm  of  pounded  cinnamon,  half  as 
much  of  cloves,  two  large  blades  of  mace,  beaten  to  powder,  two 
tablespoonsful  of  fresh  yeast  which  has  been  watered  for  one  night, 
and  which  should  be  solid,  and  as  much  new  milk  as  will  make  the 
whole  into  a  rather  firm  dough ;  let  this  stand  from  an  hour  to  aa 
hour  and  a  half  near  the  fire,  Uien  knead  it  well,  and  make  it  into 
balls  about  the  size  of  a  small  apple  ;  hollow  them  with  the  thumb,  and 
enclose  a  few  currants  in  the  middle ;  gather  the  paste  well  oTer 
them,  and  throw  the  dough-nuts  into  a  saucepan  naif  filled  with 
boiling  lard ;  when  they  are  equally  coloured  to  a  fine  brown,  lift 
them  out  and  dry  them  before  the  fire  on  the  back  of  a  sieve.  A\li«i 
they  are  made  in  lar^  Quantities,  as  they  are  at  certain  seasons  in  the 
island,  they  are  dramed  upon  very  clean  straw.  The  lard  should 
boil  only  just  before  they  are  dropped  into  it,  or  the  outsides  will  be 
scorched  before  the  insides  are  siifiiciently  done. 

Flour,  4  lbs. ;  lard,  4  os. ;  sugar,  i  lb. ;  allspice,  2  tablespoonsful ; 
pounded  cinnamon,  1  drachm ;  cloves  and  mace,  each  |  drachm ; 
yeast  (solid),  two  lai^e  tablespoonsful:  to  rise,  1  to  labour.  Cur- 
rants, at  choice :  dough-nuts  boiled  in  lard,  5  to  7  minutes. 

QDEEN   CAKES. 

To  make  these,  proceed  exactly  as  for  the  pound  currant-cake  of 
page  546,  but  bake  the  mixture  in  small  well-buttered  tin  pans 
(heart-shaped  ones  are  usual),  in  a  somewhat  brisk  oven,  for  about 
twenty  minutes. 

JUMBLES. 

Rasp  on  some  good  sugar  the  rinds  of  two  lemons ;  dry,  reduce  it 
to  powder,  and  sift  it  with  as  much  more  as  will  make  up  a  pound  in 
weight ;  mix  with  it  one  pound  of  flour,  four  well-beaten  ^gs,  and 
six  ounces  of  warm  butter :  drop  the  mixture  on  buttered  tins,  and 
bake  the  jumbles  in  a  very  slow  oven  from  twenty  to  thir^  minutes. 
They  should  be  pale,  but  perfectly  crisp. 

A   GOOD  SODA   CAKE. 

Break  dovm  half  a  pound  *  of  fresh  butter  into  a  pound  of  fiix 

*  Six  oTinceB  would  to  many  tastes  be  quite  gofBcient,  and  the  l«sa  butter  tbt 
cake  contains  the  better. 


CHAP.  XXVI.]  CAKES,  657 

dry  flour,  and  work  it  into  very  small  crumbs ;  mix  well  with  these 
half  a  pound  of  sifted  sugar,  and  pour  to  them  first,  a  quarter  of  a 
pint  of  boiling  mUk,  and  next,  three  well-whisked  eggs ;  add  some 
grated  nutmes,  or  fresh  lemon-rind,  and  eight  ounces  of  currants, 
cleaned  and  c&ied ;  beat  the  whole  well  and  lightly  together,  then 
strew  in  a  very  small  teaspoonful  of  sood  carbonate  of  soda  in  the 
finest  powder,  which  has  been  rubbed  through  a  sieve  and  well  mixed 
with  a  little  su^,  and  again  beat  the  (»ke  well  and  lightly  for 
three  or  four  mmutes;  put  it  into  a  buttered  mould,  and  bake  it 
from  an  hour  to  an  hour  and  a  quarter ;  or  divide  it  in  two,  when 
three-quarters  of  an  hour  will  be  sufficient  for  each  part. 

Flour,  1  lb. ;  butter,  i  lb. ;  sugar,  ^  lb. ;  boiling  milk,  full  i  pint ; 
eggs,  3 ;  currants,  i  lb. ;  good  carbonate  of  soda,  1  very  small  tea- 
spoonful  :  1  to  1|  hour.    Or :  divided  in  two,  1  to  }  hour. 

Obs. — This,  ii'  carefully  made,  resembles  a  pound  cake,  but  is 
much  less  expensive,  and  far  more  wholesome,  while  it  has  the 
advantage  of  being  very  expeditiously  prepared.  Great  care,  how- 
ever, must  be  taken  to  avoid  mixing  with  it  too  large  a  proportion, 
or  a  coarse  quality  of  soda ;  as  either  will  impart  to  it  a  far  from 
agreeable  flavour. 

GOOD   SCOTTISH   SHORTBREAD, 

With  one  pound  of  flour  mix  well  two  ounces  of  sifled  sugar,  and 
one  of  candied  orange-rind  or  citron,  sliced  small ;  make  these  into 
a  paste  with  from  eight  to  nine  ounces  of  good  butter,  made  suf- 
ficiently warm  to  be  liquid  ;  press  the  paste  together  with  the  hands, 
and  mould  it  upon  tins  into  large  cakes  nearly  an  inch  thick,  pinch 
the  edges,  and  bake  the  shortbread  in  a  moderate  oven  for  twenty 
minutes,  or  longer,  should  it  not  be  quite  crisp,  but  do  not  allow  it  to 
become  deeply  coloured. 

Flour,  1  lb. ;  sugar,  2  oz. ;  candied  orange  or  citron,  1  oz. ;  butter, 
8  to  9  oz. :  20  minutes  or  more. 

Obs. — This,  to  many  persons,  is  a  very  indigestible  compound, 
though  agreeable  to  the  taste. 

A   O ALETTE. 

The  galettc  is  a  favourite  cake  in  France,  and  may  be  made  rich 
and  comparatively  delicate,  or  quite  common,  by  using  more  or  less 
butter  for  it,  and  by  augmenting  or  diminishing  the  size.  Work 
lightly  three-quarters  of  a  pound  of  good  butter  into  a  pound  of 
flour,  add  a  large  saltnpoonful  of  salt,  and  make  these  into  a  paste 
with  the  yolks  of  a  couple  of  eggs  mixed  with  a  small  cupful  of  gockl 
cream,  or  simply  with  water ;  roll  this  into  a  complete  round,  three- 
quarters  of  an  inch  thick ;  score  it  in  small  diamonds,  brush  yolk  of 
^gg  over  the  top,  and  bake  the  galette  for  about  half  an  hour  in  a 


S5S  MODEBK  COOKEBT.  [cuLP.un 

tolerably  quick  oven :  it  is  usually  eaten  hot,  but  is  serred  eold  tbo. 
An  ounce  of  sifted  sugar  is  sometimes  added  to  it. 

A  good  galette:  flour,  1  lb.;  butter,  $  lb.;  salt,  1  sahmonftl: 
yolks  of  eggs,  2 ;  cream,  small  cupful :  baked  |  boor.  CanasB 
galette :  flour,  2  lbs. ;  butter,  f  to  1  lb. ;  no  ^gs. 

SMALL  BUOAR  CAKES   OF   TARIOUS    KINDS. 

To  make  very  sweet  rich  sugar  cakes  mingle,  first  working  it  tbt 
small  with  the  fingers,  half  a  pound  of  butter  with  each  pound  i 
flour :  if  more  than  this  proportion  be  used  the  paste  will  be  too  scfl 
to  permit  the  addition  of  the  proper  number  of  eggs.  Next,  hlzflA 
thoroughly  with  these  three-quarters  of  a  pound  of  dry  sifted  sugii, 
and  the  grated  rinds  of  two  small  fresh  lemons  (for  /«in0«-cakes  tbe 
strained  juice  of  one  is  generally  added),  or  a  dessertspooofol  d 
cinnamon  freshly  pounded ;  or  from  one  ounce  to  two  ounces  of  cff- 
raway- seeds ;  or  a  similar  proportion  of  the  finest  powdered  ginger; 
or  three-quarters  of  a  poimd  of  very  dry  well  cleaned  currants.  A 
slight  pinch  of  salt  should  be  thrown  in  with  the  sugar.  If  to  he 
made  into  flat  cakes  proceed  to  moisten  these  ingredients  gndnsHj 
with  from  two  eggs  to  four  slightly  whisked,  and  when  tbey  fono's 
Jirm  paste,  proceed  quickly  to  roll  and  to  stamp  them  out  with  a  cske 
tin ;  for  as  the  sugar  dissolves  with  the  moisture  of  the  ^gs,  tbe 
paste  will  otherwise  become  so  lithe  as  to  adhere  to  tbe  board  sid 
roller.  When  it  is  to  be  merely  dropped  on  the  bakinc-sheets,  it 
will  require  an  additional  egg  or  more.  The  cakes  shoiud  then  be 
placed  quite  two  inches  apart,  as  they  will  spread  in  the  baking. 

Five  ounces  of  butter  with  six  of  sugar  to  the  pound  of  flour,  tvo 
large  eggs,  and  a  small  quantity  of  milk,  will  be  sufficient  for  cdle 
cheap  sugar  cakes :  any  flavour  can  be  given  to  them  as  to  tboa

LIGHT  BUNS  OF  DIFFERENT  KINDS. 

Quite  plain  buns  unthout  butter.-^YeTj  good  light  buns  may  be  made 
entirely  without  butter,  but  they  must  be  tolerably  fresh  when 
served.  To  make  them,  dilute  very  smoothly  an  ounce  of  sweet 
German  yeast  or  a  large  tablespoonful  of  quite  solid  and  well 
washed  English  yeast  with  a  pint  of  warm  new  milk ;  mix  this 
immediately  with  as  much  flour  as  it  will  convert  into  a  rather  thick 
batter,  throw  a  double  cloth  over  the  pan,  and  place  it  where  the 
warmth  of  the  fire  will  search,  \vdthout  heating  it.  When  it  is  well 
risen  and  bubbles  apnear  on  the  top,  add  a  little  salt,  some  pounded 
sugar,  and  as  much  nour  as  will  form  it  into  a  light  dough.  Leave  it 
to  rise  again,  when  it  will  probably  be  too  little  firm  for  moulding 
with  the  fingers,  and  must  be  beaten  up  with  a  strong  wooden  spoon 
and  put  into  cups  or  tin  pans  slightly  buttered,  to  be  baked.  The 
bans  should  be  sent  to  a  quick  oven,  and  baked  until  the  entire 
surface  is  well  browned.  These  directions  may  appear  to  the  reader 
somewhat  vague ;  but  we  must  frankly  state  that  we  have  no  precise 
memorandum  by  us  of  this  receipt,  though  we  have  had  buns  made 
by  it  very  successfully  in  former  years :  we  cannot,  however, 
exactly  recall  the  proportion  of  flour  which  was  used  for  them,  but 
believe  it  was  about  two  pounds.  For  this  quantity  half  a  pound  of 
sugar  would  be  sufficient.  The  batter  will  be  a  long  time  rising  to 
the  proper  height;  an  hour  and  a  half  or  two  hours.  Currants, 
carraways,  nutmeg,  or  mixed  spices,  can  always  be  added  at  dis- 
cretion. 

It  is  usual  to  strew  a  few  currants  on  the  tops  of  the  buns  before 
the}''  are  baked. 

To  render  them  richer  and  firmer,  it  is  merely  necessary  to  di- 
minish the  proportion  of  milk,  and  to  crumble  up  very  small  two  oi 
more  ounces  oi  butter  in  the  flour  which  is  added  to  the  batter  after 
it  has  risen.  When  again  quito  light,  the  dough  may  then  be  rolled 
into  balls,  and  placed  on  flat  tins  some  inches  apart  until  they  have 
Bpread  to  the  proper  shape.  Confectioners  generally  wash  the  tops 
with  milk,  and  sill  a  little  sugar  over  them. 

JSxeter  Bum, — These  are  somewhat  celebrated  in  the  city  whose 
name  they  bear,  especially  those  of  one  maker  whose  secret  for  them 
we  have  recently  obtained.  Instead  of  being  made  into  a  dough  with 
milk,  Devonshire  cream  is  used  for  them,  either  entirely  or  in  part. 
If  very  thick,  a  portion  of  water  should  be  added  to*  it,  or  the  yeast 
would  not  ferment  freely.  The  better  plan  is  to  dilute  it  with  a 
quarter  of  a  pint  or  rather  more  of  warm  water,  and  when  it  is  suffi- 
ciently risen  to  make  up  the  buns  lightly,  like  bread,  with  the  creamy

which  most  also  be  warm;  then  to  proceed  by  the  receipt  giTO 
ftbove.

THE   colonel's   BISCUITS. 

Mix  a  slight  pinch  of  salt  vnth  some  fine  sifted  flour ;  make  it  into 
a  smooth  paste  with  thin  cream,  and  bake  the  biscuits  gently,  after

them  as  soon  as  thev  are  cold  in  a  dry  canister,  to  preserve  them 
crisp :  they  are  excellent. 

AUNT   charlotte's   BISCUITS. 

These  biscuits,  which  are  very  simple  and  very  good,  may  be  made 
with  the  same  dough  as  fine  white  bread,  with  tne  addition  of  from 
half  to  a  whole  ounce  of  butter  to  the  pound  kneaded  into  it  after  it 
has  risen.  Break  the  butter  small,  spread  out  the  dough  a  little, 
knead  it  in  well  and  equally,  and  leave  it  for  about  half  an  hour  to 
rise ;  then  roll  it  a  quarter  of  an  inch  thick,  prick  it  well  all  over, 
cut  out  the  biscuits,  and  bake  them  in  a  moderate  oven  from  ten  to 
fifteen  minutes :  they  should  be  crisp  quite  through,  but  not  deeply 
coloured. 

White-bread  dough,  2  lbs. ;  butter,  1  to  2  oz. :  to  rise  }  hour. 
Baked  in  moderate  oven  10  to  15  minutes. 

Obs.— To  make  the  biscuits  by  themselves,  proceed  as  for  Bordyke 
bread ;  but  use  new  milk  for  them,  and  work  three  ounces  of  butter 
into  two  pounds  of  flour  before  the  yeast  is  added. 

EXCELLENT   SODA   BUNS. 

"Work  into  half  a  pound  of  flour  three  ounces  of  butter,  until  it  is 
quite  in  crumbs ;  mix  thoroughly  with  them  four  ounces  of  sugar, 
me  slightest  pinch  of  salt,  an  ounce,  or  rather  more,  of  candied 
orange  or  lemon  rind,  shred  extremely  small,  and  a  little  grated  nut- 
meg ;  to  these  pour  boiling  a  smaU  teacupful  of  cream,  or  of  milk 
when  this  cannot  be  had;  mix  them  a  little,  and  add  immediately 
two  eggs,  leaving  out  the  white  of  one,  and  when  the  whole  is  well 
mingled,  dust  over,  and  beat  well  into  it,  less  than  half  a  teaspoonful 
of  good  carbonate  of  soda,  perfectly  free  from  lumps ;  rub  an  oven- 
tin  with  butter,  drop  the  buns  upon  it  with  a  spoon,  and  send  them 
to  a  moderate  oven.  When  they  are  firm  to  the  touch  in  every  part, 
and  well  coloured  underneath,  they  are  done.  They  resemble  good 
cakes,  if  properly  made,  although  in  reality  they  are  not  rich :  to 
render  them  so  the  proportion  of  sugar  and  of  butter  can  be  in- 
creased, and  currants  added  also.  It  is  immaterial,  we  find,  whether 
thpy  be  put  into  the  oven  as  soon  as  they  are  mixed,  or  an  hour 
afterwards.  They  are  equally  light.  These  proportions  make  just 
a  dozen  of  small  buns.

1  oz.  or  more;  grated  nutmeg;  cream  (or  milk)  1  small  teacupful; 
eg^-yolks  2,  white  1 ;  good  carbonate  of  soda  about  the  third  of  a  tea- 
Bpooiiful :  15  to  25  minutes,  moderate  oven.

not  to  a  strong  frotb),  and  af^rwards  with  the  water,  about  half  ibt

TO   BOIL   SUGAR   FROM   SYRUP   TO   CANDY,   OR   TO   CARAMEL. 

The  technicalities  by  which  confectioners  distinguish  the  different 
degrees  of  sugar-boiling,  seem  to  us  calculated  rather  to  puzzle  than 
to  assist  the  reader ;  and  we  shall,  therefore,  confine  ourselves  to  such 
plain  English  terms  as  may  suffice,  we  hope,  to  explain  them.  Af\er 
having  boiled  a  certain  time,  the  length  of  which  will  in  a  measure 
depend  upon  the  quality  of  the  sugar  as  well  as  on  the  quantity  of 
water  added,  it  becomes  a  thin  syrup,  and  will  scarcely  form  a  short 
thread  if  a  drop  be  pressed  between  the  thumb  and  finger  and  they 
are  then  drawn  apart ;  from  five  to  ten  minutes  more  of  rapid  boiling 
will  bring  it  to  a  thick  syrup,  and  when  this  degree  is  reached  the 
thread  may  be  drawn  from  one  hand  to  the  other  at  some  length 
without  breaking ;  but  its  appearance  in  dropping  from  the  skimmer 
will  perhaps  best  denote  its  being  at  this  point,  as  it  hangs  in  a  sort 
of  string  as  it  falls.  After  this  the  sugar  will  soon  begin  to  whiten, 
and  to  lorm  large  bubbles  in  the  pan,  when,  if  it  be  intended  for 
barley-sugar,  or  caramel,  some  lemon-juice  or  other  acid  must  be 
added  to  it,  to  prevent  its  graining  or  becoming  sugar  again ;  but  if 
wanted  to  candy,  it  must  be  stirred  without  ceasing  until  it  rises 
almost  to  the  top  of  the  pan,  in  one  large  white  mass,  when  it  must 
be  used  immediately  or  laded  out  into  paper  cases  or  on  to  dishes, 
with  the  utmost  expedition,  as  it  passes  m  an  instant  almost,  from 
this  state  to  one  in  which  it  forms  a  sort  of  powder,  which  will  render 
it  necessary  to  add  water,  to  stir  it  until  dissolved,  and  to  reboil  it  to 
the  proper  point.  For  barley-suOT  likewise  it  must  be  constantly 
stirred,  and  carefully  watched  aner  the  lemon-juice  is  added.  A 
small  quantity  should  be  dropped  from  time  to  time  into  a  large 
basin  of  cold  water  by  those  who  are  inexperienced  in  the  process ; 
when  in  falling  into  this  it  makes  a  bubbling  noise,  and  if  taken  out 
immediately  after,  it  snaps  clean  between  the  teeth  without  sticking 
to  them,  it  must  be  poured  out  instantly :  if  wanted  for  sugar-spin- 
ning, the  pan  must  be  plunged  as  quickly  as  possible  into  a  vessel  of 
cold  water. 

CARAMEL, 

(^The  quickest  way,) 

Put  into  a  brass  skillet,  or  preserving-pan,  some  silled  sugar  of  the 
finest  quality,  and  stir  it  softly  with  a  wooden  spoon  or  spatula,  over 


564  MODEBN  COOKERT.  [cHAP.  xxm 

a  very  gentle  fire  until  it  has  become  liquid ;  a  pale  or  a  deqi  tilt 
may  then  be  given  to  it,  according  to  the  purpose  for  which  it  is  ie> 
quired :  so  soon  as  it  is  entirely  melted,  and  looks  clear,  it  is  reMij 
for  use.  Pastry-cooks  glaze  small  pastry  by  just  dipping  the  snife 
into  it ;  and  tney  use  it  also  for  nougaty  and  other  confectioBizTi 
though  it  is  not  in  general  quite  so  brilfiant  as  that  which  is  made  if 
the  preceding  receipt.  When  the  sugar  first  b^^s  to  melt,  it  should 
be  stirred  only  just  in  that  part,  or  it  will  not  ^  equally  ookored. 

BARLEY-SUGAR. 

Add  to  three  pounds  of  hi^hly-refined  sugar  one  pint  and  a  quarter 
of  spring  water,  with  sufficient  white  of  egg  to  clarify  it  in  tk 
manner  directed  in  the  last  page  but  one ;  pour  to  it,  when  it  b^giss 
to  whiten,  and  to  be  very  thick,  a  dessertspoonfxil  of  the  stnioed 
juice  of  a  fresh  lemon ;  and  boil  it  quickly  until  it  is  at  the  poiat 
which  we  have  indicated  above.  A  few  drops  of  essence  of  leDua 
may  be  added  to  it,  just  as  it  is  taken  from  the  fire.  Pour  it  on  tot 
marble  slab,  or  on  to  a  shallow  dish  which  has  been  slightly  oikd,*? 
rubbed  with  a  particle  of  fresh  butter ;  and  when  it  b^ns  to  hardei 
at  the  edges  form  it  into  sticks,  lozenges,  balls,  or  any  other  shape?  tf 
pleasure.  While  it  is  still  liquid  it  may  be  used  for  various  purpoaei 
such  as  Chantilly  baskets,  palace  bonbons,  eroqtumieSy*  ceriMe*  m 
caramel^  &c. :  for  these  the  vessel  containing  it  must  be  set  into  a  pn 
of  water,  and  it  must  again  be  liquefied  with  a  very  gentle  degree  d 
heat  should  it  cool  too  quickly.  As  it  soon  dissolves  if  exposed  M 
damp,  it  should  be  put  mto  very  dry  canisters  as  soon  as  it  is  ooli 
and  these  should  be  kept  in  a  dry  place. 

Best  sugar,  3  lbs. ;  water,  1^  pint;  white  of  egg,  |  of  1 ;  lemoe- 
juiee,  1  dessertspoonful. 

NOUGAT. 

This  is  a  preparation  of  barley  sugar,  and  almonds,  filberts,  or 
pistachio-nuts,  or  which  good  confectioners,  both  foreifi[n  and  EochslL 
make  a  great  variety  of  nighly  ornamental  dishes.  We  mnstrwy<r- 
ever,  confine  our  directions  to  the  most  common  and  simple  mode  4^ 
serving  it.  Blanch  twelve  ounces  of  fine  Jordan  almonds  m  tbe 
usual  way,  wipe  them  very  dry,  split  them  in  halves,  and  spreii 
them  upon  tins  or  dishes ;  dry  them  in  a  very  gentle  oven,  withobS 
allowing  them  to  brown ;  or  if  the  flavour  be  liked  better  to,  fcc 
them  be  equally  coloured  to  a  pale  gold  tint :  they  should  then  ic 
of\en  turned  while  in  the  oven.  Boil  to  barley  sugar  in  a  sb^ 
pre.serving-pan  six  ounces  of  highly-refined  sugar,  throw  in  tbe 
almonds,  mix  them  with  it  well  wiwout  breaking  them,  turn  the 

•  These  are  formed  of  small  cakes,  roasted  chestnuts,  and  tmritias  ^Sex 
thin^,  just  dipped  singly  into  the  barley-sugar,  and  then  arranged  in  c<mA 
and  joined  in  a  mould,  from  which  they  are  turned  out  f^r  table. 


CI14P.  xxvn.]  CONFECTIONARY.  5C5 

nougat  on  to  a  dish  slightly  rubhed  with  oil,  spread  it  out  quickly, 
mark  it  into  squares,  and  cut  it  before  it  is  cold ;  or  pour  it  into  a 
mould,  and  with  an  oiled  lemon  spread  it  quickly,  and  very  thin  over 
it,  and  turn  it  out  when  cool.  It  must  at  all  times  be  carefully  pre- 
served from  damp ;  and  should  be  put  into  a  dry  tin  box  as  soon  as 
it  is  cold. 

Sugar,  6  oz. ;  almonds,  12  oz. 

Another  and  more  expeditious  way  of  making  it,  is  to  boil  ftie 
sugar  to  caramel  without  any  water,  as  directed  at  page  563 :  the 
proportion  of  almonds  can  be  diminished  at  pleasure,  but  the  noLgat 
should  always  be  well  filled  with  them. 

GINGER   CANDV. 

Break  a  pound  of  highly-refined  sugar  into  lumps,  put  it  into  a 
preserving-pan,  and  pour  over  it  about  the  third  of  a  pint  of  spring 
water;  let  it  stand  until  the  sug^ar  is  nearly  dissolved,  then  set  it 
over  a  perfectly  clear  fire,  and  boil  it  until  it  becomes  a  thin  syrup. 
Have  ready  in  a  large  cup  a  teaspoonful  of  the  very  best  ginger  in 
powder ;  mix  it  smoothly  and  gradually  with  two  or  three  spoonsful 
of  the  syrup,  and  then  stir  it  well  into  the  whole.  Watch  the  mix- 
ture carefufly,  keep  it  stirred,  and  drop  it  often  from  a  spoon  to 
ascertain  the  exact  point  of  boiling  it  has  reached.  When  it  begins 
to  fall  in  fizkes,  throw  in  the  freshly-grated  rind  of  a  very  large 
lemon,  or  of  two  small  ones,  and  work  the  sugar  round  quickly  as  it 
is  added.  The  candy  must  now  be  stirred  constantly  until  it  is  done : 
this  will  be  when  it  falls  in  a  mass  from  the  spoon,  and  does  not 
sJTth  when  placed  in  a  small  heap  on  a  dish.  It  must  be  poured,  or 
laded  out,  as  expeditiously  as  possible  when  readv,  or  it  will  fall 
quite  into  powder.  If  this  should  happen,  a  little  water  must  be 
added  to  it,  and  it  must  be  reboiled  to  the  proper  point.  The  candy, 
if  dropped  in  cakes  upon  sheets  of  very  dry  foolscap  or  other  thick 
writins-paper  laid  upon  cold  dishes,  may  be  moved  off  without 
difficulty  while  it  is  still  iust  warm,  but  it  must  not  be  touched  while 
quite  hot,  or  it  will  breax. 

Sugar,  highly  refined,  1  lb.;  water,  1  of  a  pint;  ginger,  1  tea- 
spoomul ;  rind  of  1  large  lemon. 

ORANGE-FLOWER  CANDY. 

Beat  in  three-ouarters  of  a  pint,  or  rather  more,  of  water,  about 
the  fourth  part  oi  the  white  of  an  egg,  and  pour  it  on  two  pounds  of 
the  best  sugar  broken  into  lumps.  When  it  has  st<»od  a  little  time, 
place  it  over  a  very  clear  fire,  and  let  it  boil  for  a  few  minutes,  then 
set  it  on  one  side,  imtil  the  scum  has  subsided ;  clear  it  off,  and  boil 
the  sugar  until  it  is  very  thick,  then  strew  in  by  degrees  three  ounces 
of  the  petals  of  the  orange-blossom,  weighed  after  they  are  picked 
from  their  stems.    Continue  to  stir  the  candy  until  it  rises  m  cue

white  mass  in  the  pan.  then  lay  it,  as  quickly  as  it  can  be  done,  is 
cakes  with  a  large  spoon,  upon  thick  and  very  dry  sheets  of  wntai^

COCOA-NUT   CANDY. 

Hasp  very  fine  a  sound  fresh  cocoa-nut,  spread  it  on  a  dish,  nd 
let  it  dry  naturally  for  two  or  three  days,  as  it  will  not  bear  the 
heat  of  an  oven,  and  is  too  oily  for  use  when  freshly  broken.  >oar 
ounces  of  it  will  be  sufficient  for  a  pound  of  sugar  for  most  tastiS, 
but  more  can  be  used  at  pleasure.  IBoil  the  sugar  as  for  the  oruife- 
flower  candy,  and  when  it  begins  to  be  very  thick  and  white,  stiw  a 
the  nut,  stir  and  mix  it  weU^  and  do  not  quit  it  for  an  instant  lus^ 
it  is  finished.  The  pan  should  not  be  placed  upon  the  fire  bat  fficr 
it,  as  the  nut  is  liable  to  bum  with  too  fierce  a  heat. 

For  almond-candy  proceed  in  exactly  the  same  way,  but  let  tk 
almonds,  either  whole  or  split,  be  perfectly  well  dried  in  a  ffeetk 
oven,  and  do  not  throw  them  into  tne  sugar  until  it  approadws  ihs 
candying  point.

used  for  the  purpose.

PALACE-BONBONS. 

Take  some  fine  firesh  candied  orange-rind,  or  citron,  clear  off  the 
sngar  which  adheres  to  it,  cut  it  into  inch-squares,  stick  these  singly  on 
the  prong  of  a  silver  fork  or  on  osier-twigs,  dip  them  into  liquid 
barley -sugar,  and  place  them  on  a  dish  rubbed  with  the  smallest 
possible  quantity  of  very  pure  salad  oil.  When  cold,  put  them  into 
tin  boxes  or  canisters  well  dried,  with  paper,  which  should  also  be 
very  dry,  between  each  layer. 

EYERTON   TOPFIE. 

No.  1. — Put  into  a  brass  skillet  or  small  preserving  pan  three 
onncesof  very  fresh  butter,  and  as  soon  as  it  is  just  melted  add  a 
pound  of  brown  suffar  of  moderate  quality;  keep  these  stirred 
gently  over  a  very  clear  fire  for  about  fifteen  minutes,  or  until  a 
little  of  the  mixture,  dropped  into  a  basin  of  cold  water,  breaks  clean 
between  the  teeth  without  sticking  to  them :  when  it  is  boiled  to  this 
pointy  it  must  be  poured  out  immediately,  or  it  will  bum.  The 
grated  rind  of  a  lemon,  added  when  the  toffie  is  half  done,  improves 
It  much  ;  or  a  small  teaspoonful  of  powdered  ginger  moistened  with 
a  little  of  the  other  ingredients  as  soon  as  the  sugar  is  dissolved  and 
then  stirred  to  the  whole,  will  vary  it  pleasantly  to  many  tastes. 
The  real  Everton  tofiie  is  made  witn  a  much  larger  proportion  of 
butter,  but  it  is  the  less  wholesome  on  that  very  account.  If  dropped 
upon  dishes  first  rubbed  with  a  buttered  paper,  the  toffie  when  cold 
can  be  raised  from  them  easily. 

Butter,  3  oz.;  sugar,  1  lb.:  15  to  18  minutes.  Or,  sugar,  1  lb.; 
butter,  5  oz. ;  almonds,  2  oz. :  20  to  30  minutes. 

No.  2. — Boil  together  a  pound  of  sugar  and  five  ounces  of  butter 
for  twenty  minutes ;  then  stir  in  two  ounces  of  almonds  blanched, 
divided,  and  thoroughly  dried  in  a  slow  oven,  or  before  the  fire. 
Let  the  toffie  boil  after  they  are  added,  till  it  crackles  when  dropped 
into  cold  water,  and  snaps  between  the  teeth  without  sticking. 

Sugar,  1  lb. ;  butter,  5  oz. ;  almonds,  2  oz. :  20  to  30  minutes. 

CHOCOLATE   DROPS. 

Throw  into  a  well  heated  metal  mortar  from  two  to  four  ounces  of 
the  best  quality  of  cake-chocolate  broken  small,  and  pound  it  with  a 
warm  pestle  until  it  resembles  a  smooth  paste  or  very  thick  batter ; 
then  add  an  equal  weight  of  sugar  in  the  finest  powder,  and  beat 
them  until  they  are  thorouffhly  blended.  Roll  the  mixture  into 
small  balls,  lay  them  uppn  sheets  of  writing  paper  or  upon  clean 
dishes,  and  take  them  off  when  they  are  nearly  cold.  The  tops  may 
be  covered  with  white  nonpareil  comfits,  or  the  drops  may  be  shaken 
in  a  paper  containing  some  of  these,  and  entirely  encrusted  with 


568  MODERN  COOKERr*  [chat.  xxfiL 

them ;  but  it  must  be  recollected  that  they  will  not  adhere  to  tbea 
after  they  become  hard.  More  or  less  sugar  can  be  worked  mto  the 
chocolate  according  to  the  taste ;  and  a  Wedgewood  mortar  mar  be 
used  for  it  when  no  other  is  at  hand,  but  one  of  beU-metai  vili 
azuwer  the  purpose  better. 

CHOCOLATE   ALMONDS. 

When  the  chocolate  has  been  softened,  and  mixed  with  an  eqasl 
proportion  of  sugar,  as  directed  in  the  forgoing  receipt,  enekK 
singly  in  small  portions  of  it  some  almonds  previously  iMif  dned,  or 
even  slightly  coloured  in  the  oven,  after  haWng  been  blandied. 
KoU  them  very  smooth  in  the  hand,  and  cover  them  with  the  oooi- 
fits,  or  form  them  like  the  almond  shamrocks  of  page  574.  FUbem 
and  pistachio-nuts  may  be  substituted  for  the  almoncls  with  good 
effect ;  but  they  also  must  be  perfectly  dry. 

SEVILLE   ORANOB   PASTE. 

Wipe,  and  pare  in  the  thinnest  possible  strips,  some  Senfis 
orange-rinds,  and  throw  them  into  plenty  of  boiling  water.  Wbsi 
they  are  extremely  tender,  lift  them  on  to  a  large  sieve  revened  ti 
dram ;  press  the  water  from  them  a  little,  and  before  tbey  are  qiss 
cold,  pound  them  to  the  smoothest  paste,  and  blend  thoroughly  yn± 
them  as  much  fine  sifted  sugar  as  can  possibly  be  mixed  with  thoa 
Roll  the  mass  out  extremely  thin,  and  with  a  very  smsdl  tin-cutter 
form  it  into  cakes  or  leaves,  or  anv  other  shapes,  and  then  dry  it  ia  t 
VERT  gentle  oven.  Store  it  in  close-shutting  boxes  as  soon  as  it  a 
cold.  A  little  choice  prepared  ginger  may  be  added  to  it  in  the 
pounding  when  it  is  liked. 

Paste  of  lemon  or  citron-rind  may  be  made  in  the  same  way.

ORANGE   SALAD. 

Take  off  the  outer  rinds,  and  then  strip  away  entirely  the  white 
inside  skin  from  some  fine  China  oranges;  slice  them  thin,  and 
remove  the  seeds,  and  thick  skin  of  the  cores,  as  this  is  done ;  strew 
over  them  plenty  of  white  sifted  sugar,  and  pour  on  them  a  ^lass  or 
more  of  brandy :  when  the  sugar  is  dissolved  serve  the  oranges.  In 
France  ripe  pears  of  superior  quality  are  sometimes  sliced  up  with 
the  oranges.  Powdered  sugar-candy  used  instead  of  sugar,  is  an 
improvement  to  this  salad ;  and  the  substitution  of  port,  sherry,  or 
Madeira,  for  the  brandy  is  oflen  considered  so.  The  fruit  may  be 
used  without  being  pared,  and  a  little  curouqao  or  any  other  liqueur 
may  be  added  to  the  brandy;  or  this  last,  when  unmixed,  may  be 
burned  after  it  is  poured  on  the  oranges. 

TANGERINE   ORANGES. 

These  beautiful  little  oranges,  of  which  the  rinds  have  a  most 
peculiar,  and  to  many  tastes  not  a  very  agreeable  flavour,  are  re- 
markably sweet  and  delicate  when  in  their  perfection ;  but  they  come 
later  into  the  market  than  the  more  common  varieties  of  the  orange, 
and  disappear  from  them  sooner.  They  make  a  very  refined  salui, 
and  also  an  ornamental  dessert  dish :  their  cost  is  somewhat  higher 
than  that  of  the  Malta  and  St.  Michael  oranges. 

There  is  another  species  of  this  fruit  known  commonly  as  the 
hlood-orange  which  has  many  admirers,  but  it  is  not  we  should  say 
greatly  superior  to  the  more  abundant  kinds  usually  served  at  our 
tables. 

PEACHES   IN   BRANDT. 

{Rotterdam  Receipt,) 

Prepare  and  stew  some  .fine  full-flavoured  peaches  by  the  receipt 
of  page  459,  but  with  two  ounces  more  of  sugar  to  the  half  pint  of 
water ;  when  they  are  tender  put  them,  with  their  syrup.  into  glass 
or  new  stone  jars,  which  they  should  only  half  fill ;  and  when  they 
are  quite  cold  pour  in  white,  or  very  pale,  French  brandy  to  within 
an  inch  and  a  half  of  the  brims :  a  few  peach  or  apricot  kernels  can 
be  added  to  them.    The  jars  must  be  corxed  down. 

BRANDIED   MORELLA   CHERRIES.

space  for  four  ounces  of  pounded  white  sugar-candy  (or  of  brown,  if 
better  liked);  fill  them  up  entirely  with  the  best  French  brandy,

DRIED   NORFOLK   BIFFINS. 

The  Norfolk  biffin  is  a  hard  and  very  red  i^ple,  the  flesh  of  t^

Obs. — These  pippins,  if  stewed  with  care,  will  be  converted  into  a 
rich  confection :  but  they  will  be  very  good  and  more  refreshing  with 
less  sagar.  They  are  now  exceedingly  cheap,  and  may  be  converted 
into  excellent  second  course  dishes  at  small  expense.  Half  a  pound, 
as  they  are  light  and  swell  much  in  the  stewing,  will  be  sufficient  to 
serve  at  once.  Rinse  them  quickly  with  cold  water,  and  then  soak 
them  for  an  hour  in  the  pan  in  which  they  are  to  be  stewed,  in  a 
quart  of  fresh  water ;  place  them  by  the  side  of  the  stove  to  heat 
gradually,  and  when-  they  begin  to  soften  add  as  much  sugar  as  will 
sweeten  them  to  the  taste :  they  require  but  a  small  portion.  Lemon- 
rind  'can  be  added  to  them  at  pleasure.  We  have  many  receipts  for 
other  ways  of  preparing  them,  to  which  we  cannot  now  give  placQ 
here.  It  answers  well  to  bake  them  slowly  in  a  covered  jar.  TheJ 
may  be  served  hot  in  a  border  of  rice. 

8TSWED   PRUNE AUX   DE   TOURS^   OR   TOURS   DRIED   PLUMS. 

These  plums,  which  resemble  in  form  small  dried  Norfolk  biffins, 
make  a  delicious  compote:  they  are  also  excellent  served  dry.  In 
France  they  are  stewed  until  tender  in  equal  parts  of  water,  and  of 
the  light  red  wine  of  the  country,  with  about  four  ounces  of  sugar 
to  the  pound  of  fruit :  when  port  wine  is  used  for  them  a  smaller 
proportion  of  it  will  suffice.  The  sugar  should  not  be  added  in  stew- 
uie  any  dried  fruits  until  they  are  at  least  half-done,  as  they  will  not 
Bouen  by  any  means  so  easily  in  syrup  as  in  unsweetened  liquid. 

Dried  }  lums,  1  lb. ;  water,  }  pint,  and  light  claret,  i  pint,  or 
water,  I  pmt,  and  port  wine,  ^pint:  I J  hour.  Sugar,  4  oz. :  2  hours, 
or  more. 

Obs. — Common  French  plums  are  stewed  in  the  same  way  with 
or  without  vrine.  A  little  experience  will  teach  the  cook  the  exact 
quantity  of  liquid  and  of  sugar  which  they  require. 

TO   BAKE   PEARS. 

Wipe  some  large  sound  iron  pears,  arrange  them  on  a  dish  with 
the  stalk  end  upwards,  put  them  into  the  oven  after  the  bread  is 
withdrawn,  and  let  them  remain  all  night.  If  well  baked,  they  will  be 
excellent,  very  sweet,  and  juicy,  and  much  finer  in  flavour  than 
those  which  are  stewed  or  baked  with  sugar :  the  bon  chritien  pear 
also  is  delicious  baked  thus. 

STEWED   PEARS. 

Pare,  cut  in  halves,  and  core  a  dozen  fine  pears,  put  them  into  a 
close  shutting  stewpan  with  some  thin  strips  of  lemon -rind,  half  a 
pound  of  sugar  in  lumps,  as  much  water  as  will  nearly  cover  them, 
and  should  a  very  bright  colour  be  desired,  a  dozen  grains  of  cochi- 
neal,  bruised,  and  tied  in  a  m  islin ;  stew  the  fruit  as  gently  as  pos^ 


574  MODERN  COOKERT.  [chap.  XxniL 

Bible,  four  or  five  hours,  or  longer  should  it  not  be  perfectlT  tends. 
Wine  is  sometimes  added  both  to  stewed  pears  and  to  baked  ooo. 
If  put  into  a  covered  jar,  well  tied  down  and  baked  for  some  boonk 
with  a  proper  quantity  of  liquid  and  sugar,  they  will  be  rery  good. 

BOILED   CHESTNUTS. 

Make  a  slight  incision  in  the  outer  skin  only,  of  each  chestnut,  lo 
prevent  its  bursting,  and  when  all  are  done,  throw  thezn  into  pkoiy 
of  boiling  water,  with  about  a  dessertspoonful  of  salt  to  tbe  ban 
gallon.  Some  chestnuts  will  require  to  be  boiled  nearly  or  qii&e 
an  hour,  others  little  more  than  half  the  time:  tbe  cook  sbooU 
try  them  occasionally,  and  as  soon  as  they  are  soft  throagb,  drua 
them,  wipe  them  in  a  coarse  cloth,  and  send  them  to  table  qokklj  ia 
a  hot  napkin. 

Obs. — The  best  chestnuts  are  those  which  have  no  internal  Sr 
visions :  the  finest  kinds  are  quite  entire  when  shelled. 

ROASTED    CHESTNUTS. 

The  best  mode  of  preparing  these  is  to  roast  them,  as  in  Spain,  ia 
a  coffee-roaster,  after  having  first  boiled  them  from  seven  to  tea 
minutes,  and  wiped  them  dry.  They  should  not  be  allowed  to  eoc^ 
and  will  require  but  firom  ten  to  fifteen  minutes*  roasting.  Tbcr 
may,  when  more  convenient,  be  finished  over  the  fire  as  nsnal,  or  ii 
a  Dutch  or  common  oven,  but  in  all  cases  the  previous  boiling  viH 
be  found  an  improvement.  Never  omit  to  cut  the  rind  of  emSi  ast 
slightly  before  it  is  cooked.  Serve  the  chestnuts  veiy  hot  in  a  uf- 
kin,  and  send  salt  to  table  with  them. 

ALMOND  SHAMROCKS. 

(  Very  goody  and  very  pretty.) 

Whisk  the  white  of  a  very  fresh  egg  to  a  froth  sufiScientlv  solid  to 
remain  standing  in  high  points  when  dropped  from  the  whisk ;  v«k 
into  it  from  half  to  three-quarters  of  a  pound  of  very  fine  diy  siftrf 
sugar,  or  more  should  it  be  needed,  to  bring  the  mixture  to  a  coe- 
sistency  in  which  it  can  be  worked  with  the  fingers.  Hare  readt 
some  fine  Jordan  almonds  which  have  been  blanched,  and  thoiva^h^ 
dried  at  the  month  of  the  oven ;  roll  each  of  these  in  a  small  portioa 
of  the  icing  until  it  is  equally  covered,  and  of  good  form ;  then  hj 
them  jn  sheets  of  thick  writing  paper,  placing  three  together  in  the  fatm 
of  the  shamrock,  or  trefoil,  with  a  small  bit  of  sugar  tvristed  from  t^ 
centre  almond  to  form  the  stalk.  When  all  are  ready,  set  then 
into  a  very  slow  oven  for  twenty  minutes  or  longer :  they  diGoid 
become  quite  firm  without  taking  any  colour.  They  make  an  ex- 
cellent and  very  ornamental  dish.  To  give  them  flavour  and  vaiietT. 
use  for  them  sugar  which  has  been  rasped  on  the  linda  of  s

ound  lemons,  or  Seville  oranges,  or  upon  citron,  and  dried  before  it

FINB  CURRANT   8TRUP,   OR   8IR0P   DB  OROSEILLES. 

Express  the  juice  from  some  fine  ripe  red  currants,  which  have  been 
gathered  in  dry  weather,  and  stripped  from  the  stallu ;  strain,  and 

{>ut  it  into  a  new,  or  a  perfectly  cl^m  and  dry  earthen  pitcher,  and 
et  it  stand  in  a  cellar  or  in  some  cool  place  for  twenty-four  hours,  or 
longer,  should  it  not  then  appear  perfectly  curdled.  Pour  it  gently 
into  a  fine  hair-sieve,  and  let  the  clear  juice  drain  through  without 
pressure ;  pass  it  through  a  jelly-bag,  or  a  closely- woven  cloth,  weigh 
it,  and  add  as  much  good  sugar  broken  small  as  there  is  of  the  juice, 
and  when  this  is  dissolved  turn  the  syrup  into  a  preserving-pan  or 
stewpan,  and  boil  it  gently  for  four  or  five  minutes  being  careful  to 
dear  off  all  the  scum.  In  twelve  hours  afterwards  the  syrup  may  be 
put  into  small  dry  bottles,  and  corked  and  stored  in  a  cool,  but  dry 
place.  It  is  a  most  agreeable  preparation,  retaining  perfectly  the 
flavour  of  the  fresh  fruit;  and  mixed  with  water,  it  affords,  like 
strawberry  or  raspberry  vinegar,  a  delicious  summer  beverage,  and 
one  which  is  peculiarly  adapted  to  invalids.  It  makes  also  a  fine 
isinglass  jelly,  and  an  incomparable  sweet-pudding  sauce.  A  portion 
of  raspberry  or  cherry-juice  may  be  mixed  with  that  of  the  currants 
at  pleasure.

CAMBRIDGE   MILK   PUNCH. 

Throw  into  two  quarts  of  new  milk  the  very  thinly-pared  rind  of 
a  fine  lemon,  and  half  a  pound  of  good  sugar  in  lumps ;  bring  it 
slowly  to  boil,  take  out  the  lemon-rind,  draw  it  from  the  fire,  and 
stir  quickly  in  a  couple  of  well-whisked  eggs  which  have  been  mixed 
with  less  than  half  a  pint  of  cold  milk,  and  strained  though  a  sieve; 
the  milk  must  not  of  course  be  allowed  to  boil  after  these  are  mixed 
with  it.  Add  gradually  a  pint  of  rum,  and  half  a  pint  of  brandy ; 
mill  the  punch  to  a  froth,  and  serve  it  immediately  with  quite  warm 
glasses.  At  the  University  the  lemon-rind  is  usually  omitted,  but  it 
18  a  great  improvement  to  the  flavour  of  the  beverage.  The  sugar 
and  spirit  can  be  otherwise  apportioned  to  the  taste ;  and  we  would 
recommend  the  yolks  of  three  eggs,  or  of  four,  in  preference  to  the 
two  whole  ones. 

New  milk,  2  quarts ;  rind,  1  large  lemon ;  fresh  eggs,  2 ;  cold  milk, 
i  pint ;  rum,  1  pint ;  brandy,  )  pint. 

TO   MULL  WINE. 

{An  excellent  French  Receipt^ 

Boil  in  a  wineglassful  and  a  half  of  water,  a  quarter  of  an  ounce 
of  spice  (cinnamon,  ginger  slightly  bruised,  and  cloves),  with  three 
ounces  of  fine  sugar,  untU  they  form  a  thick  syrup,  which  must  not 
on  any  account  be  allowed  to  bum.  Pour  in  a  pint  of  port  wine,  and 
stir  it  gently  until  it  is  on  the  point  of  boiling  only :  it  should  then  be 
servai  immediately.  The  addition  of  a  strip  or  two  of  orange- rind 
cut  extremely  thin,  gives  to  this  beverage  tne  flavour  of  bishop.  In 
France  light  claret  takes  the  place  of  port  wine  in  making  it,  and  the 
better  kinds  of  tnn  ordinaire  are  very  palatable  thus  prepared. 

Water,  1^  win^lassful ;  spice,  i  oz.,  of  which  fine  cloves,  24,  and 
of  remainder,  rather  more  ginger  than  cinnamon ;  sugar  3  oz. :  15  to 
20  minutes.  Port  wine  or  claret,  1  pint ;  orange-rind,  if  used,  to  be 
boiled  with  the  spice. 

Obs, — Sherry,  or  vety  fine  raisin  or  ginger  wine,  prepared  as 
above,  and  stirred  hot  to  the  yolks  of  four  fresh  eggs,  will  make  good 
^g-wine

DELICIOUS   MILK   LKMONADB. 

Dissolve  six  ounces  of  loaf  sugar  in  a  pint  of  boiling  water,  and 
mix  with  them  a  quarter  of  a  pint  of  lemon-juice,  and  the  same 
quantity  of  sherry ;  then  add  three-quarters  of  a  pint  of  cold  milk, 
stir  the  whole  well  together,  and  pass  it  through  a  jelly-bag  till 
clear. 

EXCELLENT   PORTABLE   LEMONADE. 

Basp,  with  a  quarter-pound  of  sugar,  the  rind  of  a  rerj  fine  juicy 
lemon,  reduce  it  to  powder,  and  pour  on  it  the  strained  juice  of  the 
fruit.  Press  the  mixture  into  a  jar,  and  when  wanted  for  use  dissolve 
a  tablespoonful  of  it  in  a  glass  of  water.  It  will  keep  a  considerable 
time.  If  too  sweet  for  the  taste  of  the  drinker,  a  very  small  portion 
of  citric  add  may  be  added  when  it  is  taken.

mmutes ;  then  cover  it  do¥m,  and  let  it  stand  until  perfectly  cold. 
In  twelve  hours,  or  less,  it  will  be  fit  for  use ;  but  it  is  better  when 
made  over  night.  If  Ihese  directions  be  followed,  the  barley-water 
will  be  compajratively  dear,  and  very  soft  and  pleasant  to  drmk.  A 
glass  of  calrs  feet  jelly  added  to  the  barley  is  an  infinite  improve- 
ment ;  but  as  lemon-rind  is  often  extremely  unpalatable  to  invalids, 
their  taste  should  be  consulted  before  that  ingredient  is  added,  as  it 
should  be  also  for  the  degree  of  sweetness  that  is  desired.  After  the 
barley-water  has  been  poured  off  once,  the  jug  may  be  filled  with 
boiling  water  a  second  tmie,  and  even  a  third  time  with  advantage

EXCELLENT  ORANGE  WINE. 

Take  half  a  chest  of  Seyille  oranges,  pare  off  the  rinds  as  thin  as 
possible,  put  two-thirds  of  them  into  six  gallons  of  water,  and  let 
them  remain  for  twenty-four  hours.  Squeeze  the  oranges  (which 
ought  to  yield  seven  or  eight  quarts  of  juice)  through  a  sieve  into  a 
pan,  and  as  they  are  done  throw  them  into  six  gallons  more  of  water ; 
let  them  be  washed  well  in  it  with  the  hands,  and  then  put  into 
another  six  gallons  of  water  and  left  until  the  following  day.  For' 
each  gallon  of  wine,  put  into  the  cask  three  pounds  and  a  quarter  of 
loaf  sugar,  and  the  liquor  strained  clear  from  the  rinds  and  pulp. 
Wash  tnese  again  and  again,  should  more  liquor  be  re(}uired  to  ml 
the  cask;  but  do  not  at  any  time  add  raw  water.  Stir  the  wine 
daily  until  the  sugar  is  perfectly  dissolved,  and  let  it  ferment  from 
four  to  five  weeks ;  add  to  it  two  bottles  of  brandy,  stop  it  down, 
and  in  twelve  months  it  will  be  fit  to  bottle. 

Obs. — The  excellence  of  all  wine  depends  so  much  upon  the 
fermentation  being  properly  conducted,  that  unless  the  mode  of 
regulating  this  be  understood  by  the  niaker,  there  will  always  be 
great  danger  of  failure  in  the  operation.  There  is,  we  believe,  an 
excellent  work  upon  the  subject  by  Mr.  McCulloch,  which  the 
reader  who  needs  information  upon  it  will  do  well  to  consult :  our 
own  experience  is  too  slight  to  enable  us  to  multiply  our  receipts.

good  light  white  wine,  thoroughly  cooled  down,  will  giTe  anotka 
agreeable  beverage  for  warm  weather ;  but  a  much  smiuler  propor- 
tion of  wine  would  better  adapt  it  to  many  tastes.

A  FEW  GENERAL  DIRECTIONS  FOR  MAKING  COFFEE. 

"When  good  coffee  is  desired,  let  it  be  procured  if  possible  of  a  first* 
"ate  London  house*  which  can  be  depended  on;  and  we  would  re* 
lommend  that  it  should  be  of  the  finest  quality  that  can  be  obtained ; 
or  there  is  no  real  economy  in  using  that  which  is  nominally  cheaper, 
kS  a  larger  quantity  will  be  required  to  give  the  same  amount  of 
trensthy  and  the  flavour  will  be  very  inferior.  It  should  always  be 
¥eMy  roasted ;  but  when  a  constant  and  large  demand  for  it  exists, 

«  We  conld  indicate  seyeral  houses  where  nnadalterated  coffee  maj  be  procured, 
)Ot  it  is  not  always  to  be  had  from  them  so  choice  in  quality  as  it  might  be ;  and 
t  is  in  general  too  highly  roasted.  By  Car  the  finest  we  have  ever  tasted  we  had 
•n  two  occasions,  some  years  since,  from  Mr.  Cobbett,  of  Pall  Mall.  The  fi-a* 
Xtnce  of  it  was  too  remarkable  to  be  easily  forgotten,  and  the  flavour  was  ezqui- 
ite ;  but  it  was  apparently  an  accidental  sample  which  he  had  met  with  in  the 
ckarket,  for  though  very  good,  that  with  which  we  were  supplied  afterwards  never 
quailed  it. 

Messrs.  Stauiforth  and  Co.,  138,  Oxford-street,  are  deservedly  noted  for  the 
xceUence  of  their  coffee.  It  is  always  ground  at  the  instant  of  serving  it  to  a 
ostomer;  and  they  have  the  complauance  of  roasting  even  so  small  a  quantity 
•  two  pounds,  to  suit  the  taste  at  the  nurchaser :  it  may  therefore  be  procured 
f  them  as  pale-dried  as  it  can  be  wished. 

The  house  of  Messrs.  Decastro  and  Peaeh,  next  door  to  Hatchett's  Hotel, 
•iccadilly,  may  likewise,  we  think,  be  quite  depended  on  for  suppljiiig  genuine' 
offee  to  iht  public ;  and  they  have  an  immense  demand  for  it.

it  will  be  easy  to  have  it  so.  When  it  has  been  stored  for  any  knftk

heated  through,  either  in  the  oven  or  in  a  stewpttn  held  hi^  above 
the  fire.  It  should  be  often  turned  while  it  is  warming,  and  graoid 
as  soon  as  it  is  cold  again.  Never  fmrchcue  it  reatfy  gromd  aaks 
compelled  to  do  so.  When  no  proper  mill  for  it  is  fitted  up  in  the

with  quite  the  same  facility  as  those  which  are  fastened  firmly  to  t

too  coarse  the  essence  will  be  only  partially  extracted  finom  it  bf 
filtering ;  and  if  it  be  extremely  fine  the  water  will  not  pezcobte 
through  it,  and  it  will  not  be  clear. 

We  say  nothing  about  mingling  chicory  with  it.  Oar  directiDBi 
are  for  making  ptir«  coffee;  which,  when  not  taken  in  exoeaS)ii,  ve 
believe,  a  wholesome  as  well  as  a  most  agreeable  beverage.  "Hk 
effect  of  chicory  is,  we  believe,  to  impart  a  shght  bitter  flavour  to  tbe 
infusion,  and  to  deepen  its  colour  so  much  as  to  make  it  appear  mack 
stronger  than  it  really  is  True  connoisseurs,  however,  do  not  attack 
any  importance  to  the  dark  hue  of  cofiee,  the  very  choicest  that  caa 
be  tasted  being  sometimes  of  quite  a  pale  tint. 

Always  serve  hot  milk  or  cream,  or  hot  milk  and  odid  ereaa,  i 
preferred,  with  breakfast  cofiee.  In  the  evening,  when  milk  ia  sorei 
at  all  with  it,  it  should  likewise  be  boiling. 

Do  not,  tn  any  way,  make  use  of  the  residue  of  one  day's  eofiee  k 
preparing  that  of  the  next ;  you  would  but  injure  the  parity  of  iei 
flavour  by  doing  so,  and  effect  next  to  nothing  in  the  nntaer  of 
economy.* 

EXCELLENT  BREAKFAST  COFFEE. 

A  simple,  well-made  English  filter,  or  percolater^  as  it  is  eaDed, 
will  answer  perfectly  for  making  cofiee ;  but  from  amongst  the  masv 
of  more  recent  invention  which  are  on  sale,  the  reader  who  pivte 
one  of  ornamental  appearance,  and  of  novel  eonstructioii,  will  eady 
be  suited.  The  size  of  the  filter  must  be  adapted  to  the  nnmker 
of  persons  for  whom  the  cofiee  is  to  be  prepared;  for  if  a  hip 
ouantity  of  the  powder  be  heaped  into  an  insufficient  space  &r  a, 
tnere  voll  not  be  room  for  it  to  swelL  and  the  water  wul  not  psn 
through.  Put  three  ounces  of  cofiee  into  one  which  will  oontaia  k 
the  lower  compartment  two  pints  and  a  half;  shake  the  po«dff 
quite  level  and  press  it  closely  down ;  remove  the  presser,  pat  on  the 
top  strainer,  and  pour  round  and  round,  so  as  to  wet  the 


•  When  the  coffee  hM  heen  filtered  in  a  proper  menner,  vster , 
warde  on  the  "  grounds "  as  they  are  termed,  will  have  searcely  aisT 
•olt^or :  this  is  not  the  oaie  when  it  has  been  boiled. 


CHAP.  XXX.]    COFFEE,  CHOCOLATE,  ETC.  691 

equally,  about  the  third  part  of  a  measured  pint  of  fast  boiling 
water.  Let  this  drain  quite  through  before  more  is  added ;  then 
pour  in  —  still  quite  boiling — in  the  same  manner  as  much  more 
water,  and  when  it  has  passed  through,  add  the  remainder ;  let  it 
drain  entirely  through,  tnen  remove  the  top  of  the  filter,  put  the 
cover  on  the  part  wmch  contains  the  coffee,  and  serve  it  immediately. 
It  will  be  very  strong,  and  perfectly  clear.  Fill  the  breakfast  cups 
two  parts  full  of  new  boiling  milk,  and  add  as  much  of  the  infusion 
as  will  give  it  the  degree  of  strength  which  is  agreeable  to  those  for 
whom  it  is  prepared.  When  it  is  liked  extremely  strong,  the  pro- 
portion of  milk  must  be  diminished,  or  less  water  be  poured  to  the 
coffee. 

If  nearly  an  additional  half  pint  of  water  be  added  before  the  tof 
of  the  percolater  is  taken  off,  it  will  still  be  very  good,  provided  that 
the  coffee  used  be  really  of  first-rate  quality. 

To  make  cheaper  breakfast  coffee  to  be  served  in  the  usual  English 
mode,  the  same  process  should  be  followed,  but  the  proportion  of 
water  must  be  considerably  increased :  it  should  always,  however,  be 
added  by  slow  degrees. 

Good  breakfast  coffee  (for  three  persons).  Best  Mocha,  in  mo- 
derately fine  powder,  ground  at  the  instant  of  using  it,  8  oz. ;  boiling 
water  added  oy  degrees,  1  pint ;  (more  at  pleasure).  Boiling  milk 
served  with  it,  1^  pint  to  1  quart.  Common  English  coffee :  coffee- 
powder,  3  oz. ;  water,  1  quart,  to  be  slowly  filtered ;  hot  milk,  half 
to  whole  pint.    Cream  in  addition  to  either  of  the  above,  at  choice. 

TO   BOIL  COFFEE, 

To  boil  coffee  and  refine  it,  put  the  necessary  quantity  of  water  into 
a  pot  which  it  will  not  fill  by  some  inches ;  when  it  boils  stir  in  the 
coffee ;  for  unless  this  be  at  once  moistened,  it  will  remain  on  the  top 
and  be  liable  to  fly  over.  Give  it  one  or  two  strong  boils,  then  raise 
it  from  the  fire,  and  simmer  it  for  ten  minutes  only ;  pour  out  a  large 
cupful  twice,  hold  it  high  over  the  coffee  pot  and  pour  it  in  again, 
then  set  it  on  the  stove  where  it  will  keep  hot  without  simmering  or 
moving  in  the  least,  for  ten  minutes  longer.  It  will  be  perfectly 
clear,  unless  mismanaged,  without  any  other  fining.  Should  more, 
however,  be  deemed  necessary,  a  verv  small  pinch  of  isinglass,  or  a 
clean  egg-shell,  with  a  little  of  the  white  adhering  to  it,  is  the  bei^ 
that  can  be  used.  Never  use  mustard  to  fine  coffee  with.  It  is  a 
barbarous  custom  of  which  we  have  heard  foreigners  who  have  been 
in  England  vehemently  complain. 

Coffee,  2  oz. ;  water,  1  pint  to  1  quart,  according  to  the  strength 
required.    Boiled  10  minutes ;  left  to  clear  10  minutes. 

ISmarA.— Filtering  is,  we  should  say,  a  far  more  economical,  and  in 
every  way  a  superior  mode  of  making  coffee  to  boiling  it ;  but  as 
some  persons  still  prefer  the  old  method,  we  insert  the  receipt  for  it.

TO   HAKB   COGOA. 

Directions  for  making  it  are  usually  sold  with  the  prepared,  or 
best  quality  of  cocoa,  which  is  merely  mixed  with  boilmg  water  in 
the  proportions  indicated  on  the  packets.  That  which  is  prepared 
from  the  nibs  requires  several  hours*  boiling,  and  should  be  left 
until  it  is  quite  cola,  that  the  oil  which  will  be  found  on  the  surface 
may  be  (&ared  from  it  before  it  is  again  heated  for  table :  this  is 
particularly  needful  when  it  is  to  be  served  to  persons  in  delicate 
lealth.

REMARKS   OX   HOME-MADE   BREAD. 

It  is  surely  a  singular  fact  that  the  one  article  of  our  dail  j  food  m 
which  health  depends  more  than  on  any  other,  is  precttely  thit 
which  is  obtained  in  England  with  ihe  greatest  difficulty — god, 
lights  and  pure  bread — yet  nothing  can  be  more  simple  and  ea^  thn 
the  process  of  making  it,  either  in  large  quantities  or  in  smtlL 
From  constant  failure,  it  is  nevertheless  considered  so  difficult  ia 
many  families,  that  recourse  is  had  to  the  nearest  baker,  both  xi 
town  and  country,  as  a  means  of  escape  from  the  heavy,  or  bitter,  or 
ill-baked  masses  of  dough  which  appear  at  table  under  the  name  d 
household  or  home-made  bread;  and  which  are  well  calculated  to 
create  the  distaste  which  they  often  excite  for  eyerythin^  wbic^ 
bears  its  name.  Without  wishing  in  the  slightest  degree  to  dispaiige 
the  skill  and  labour  of  bread-mucers  by  trade,  truth  compels  us  to 
assert  our  conviction  of  the  superior  wholesomeness  of  bread  madeia 
our  own  homes.  When  a  miller  can  be  depended  on  to  supply  flffff 
of  good  quality,  and  the  other  ingredients  used  in  preparmg  it  sft 
also  fresn  and  good,  and  mingled  with  it  in  due  proportionsi  and  the 
kneading,  fermentation,  and  baking,  are  conducted  with  care  aad 


CHAP.  XXXI.]  BREAD.  695 

iDtelligCDce,  the  result  will  uniformly  he  excellent  hread.  Every 
cook,  therefore, — and  we  might  almost  say  every  female  servant-^ 
ought  to  be  perfectly  acquainted  with  the  proper  mode  of  making  it ; 
and  skill  in  preparing  a  variety  of  dishes,  is  poor  compensation  for 
ignorance  on  this  one  essential  point.*  Moreover,  it  presents  no  more 
real  difficulty  than  boiling  a  dish  of  potatoes,  or  making  a  rice 
pudding;  and  the  neglect  with  which  it  is  treated  is  thereiore  the 
less  to  be  comprehended  or  excused. 


TO   PURIFY   YEA8T   FOR   BREAD   OR   CAKES. 

The  veast  procured  from  a  public  hrewery  is  often  so  extremely 
bitter  that  it  can  onl^  be  rendered  fit  for  use  by  frequent  washings, 
md  ailer  these  even  it  should  be  cautiously  employed.  Mix  it,  when 
&rst  brought  in,  with  a  large  quantity  of  cold  water,  and  set  it  by 
until  the  following  morning  in  a  cool  place ;  then  drain  off  the  water, 
md  stir  the  yeast  up  well  with  as  much  more  of  fresh :  it  must  again 
itand  several  hours  before  the  water  can  be  poured  clear  from  it. 
3y  changing  this  daily  in  winter,  and  both  night  and  morning  in  very 
lot  weather,  the  yeast  may  be  preserved  fit  for  use  much  longer  than 
I  would  otherwise  be ;  and  should  it  ferment  rather  less  freely  after 

time,  a  small  portion  of  brown  su^r  and  a  little  warm  milk  or  other 
iquid,  stirred  to  it  a  quarter  of  an  hour  or  twenty  minutes  before  it 
I  required  for  bread-making,  will  restore  its  strength. 

The  German  yeast,  of  which  we  have  spoken  in  detul  in  another 
art  of  this  chapter,  makes  exceedingly  light  bread  and  buns,  and  is 
ever  bitter ;  it  is  therefore  a  valuable  substitute  for  our  own  beer- 
3ast,  but  cannot  be  procured  in  all  parts  of  the  country,  for  the 
iasons  which  we  have  stated. 

THE   OVEN. 

A  brick  oven,  heated  with  wood,  is  far  superior  to  any  other  for 
king  bread,  as  well  as  for  most  other  purposes.  The  iron  ovens, 
w  commonly  attached  to  kitchen-ranges— the  construction  of  which 
s  within  these  few  years  been  wonderfully  improved — though  ex- 
idingly  convenient,  from  the  facility  whicn  they  afford  for  baking 
all  hours  of  the  'day,  do  not  in  general  answer  well  for  bread,  unless 
be  made  into  very  small  loaves  or  rolls,  as  the  surface  becomes 
ndened  and  browned  long  before  the  heat  has  sufficiently  penetrated 

Only  those  persons  who  live  liabitaally  on  good  home-made  bread,  can  form 
dea  of  the  extent  to  which  health  is  affected  by  their  being  deprived  of  it. 
have  been  appealed  to  on  several  occasions  for  household  loaves— which  we 
i  sent  to  a  considerable  distance — by  Mends  who  complained  of  being  ren> 
(d  really  ill  by  the  bread  which  they  were  compelled  to  eat  in  the  sea^de 
3s  and  in  other  places  of  fashionable  resort ;  and  in  London  we  have  heard 
ssant  complaints  both  from  foreigners  and  habitual  residents,  of  the  im- 
ibility  of  obtaining  7  loi^y  wkolaotne  bread. 


696  MODERN  COOKERY.  [cHA.p.  xxxL 

to  the  centre  of  the  dough.  The  same  objection  often  exists  to  inm- 
ovens  oi  larser  size,  which  require  care  and  management,  to  ensoro 
the  successfiu  use  of  them.  A  brick  oven  should  be  well  bnled  with 
faffgot  wood,  or  with  a  faggot,  and  two  or  three  solid  logs ;  and  after 
it  18  cleared,  the  door  shomd  be  closely  shut  for  quite  half  an  hour 
before  the  baking  commences :  the  heat  will  then  be  well  sustained 
for  a  succession  of  bread,  pies,  cakes,  and  small  pastry.  The  servant 
who  habitually  attends  at  an  oven  will  soon  become  acquainted  with 
the  precise  quantity  of  f\iei  which  it  requires,  and  all  other  pecu- 
liarities which  may  be  connected  with  it. 

A   FEW   RULES   TO  BE  OBSERVED   IN  MAKINO  BREAD. 

Never  use  too  large  a  proportion  of  yeast,  as  the  bread  will  not 
only  become  dry  very  speedily  when  this  is  done,  but  it  will  be  far 
less  sweet  and  pleasant  in  flavour  than,  that  which  is  more  slowly 
I'ermented,  and  the  colour  will  not  be  so  good :  there  will  also  be  a 
great  chance  of  its  being  bitter  when  brewer's  yeast  is  used  for  it. 

Hemember  that  milk  or  water  of  scalding  heat  poured  to  any  kind 
of  yeast  will  render  the  bread  heavy.  One  pint  of  either  added  quite 
boiling  to  a  pint  and  a  half  of  cold,  will  bring  it  to  about  the  d^^ree 
of  warmth  required.  In  frosty  weather  the  proportion  of  the  h^ted 
liquid  may  be  increased  a  little. 

When  only  porter-yeast — which  is  dark-coloured  and  bitter-Hssa 
be  procured,  use  a  much  smaller  proportion  than  usual,  and  allow 
much  longer  time  for  it  to  rise.  Never  let  it  be  sent  to  the  oven 
until  it  18  evidently  light  Bitter  bread  is  unpalatable,  but  not 
really  unwholesome ;  but  heavy  bread  is  particularly  so. 

Let  the  leaven  be  kneaded  up  quickly  with  the  remainder  of  the 
flour  when  once  it  is  well  risen,  as  it  should  on  no  account  be 
allowed  to  sink  affain  before  this  is  done,  when  it  has  reached  the 
proper  point ;  and  in  making  the  dough,  be  particularly  careful  not 
to  render  it  too  lithe  by  adding  more  liquid  than  is  requisite.  It 
should  be  quite  firm,  and  entirely  free  from  lumps  and  crumbs 
throughout  the  mass,  and  on  the  surfiMse  also,  which  ought  to  be 
perfectly  smooth. 

in  winter,  place  the  bread  while  it  is  rising  sufficiently  dose  to  the 
fire  to  prevent  its  becoming  cold,  but  never  so  near  as  to  render  it 
hot  A  warm  thick  cloth  should  be  thrown  over  the  pan  in  which  it 
is  made  immediatelv  after  the  leaven  is  mixed,  and  kept  on  it  until 
the  bread  b  ready  for  the  oven. 


HOUSEHOLD  BREAD. 

•Put  half  a  bushel  (more  or  less,  according  to  the  consumption  of  the 
family)  of  flour  into  the  kneading  tub  or  ti^ugh,  and  hollow  it  well  in 
the  middle;  dilute  a  pint  of  yeast  as  it  is  brought  from  the  breweiy 


CHAP.  XXXI.]  BREAD.  697 

or  half  the  quantity  if  it  has  been  washed  and  rendered  solid,  with, 
four  qoarts  or  more  of  lukewarm  milk  or  water,  or  a  mixture  of  the 
two ;  stir  into  it,  from  the  surrounding  part,  with  a  wooden  spoon, 
as  much  flour  as  will  make  a  thick  batter;  throw  a  handful  or 
two  over  it,  and  leave  this,  which  is  called  the  leaven,  to  rise  before 
proceeding  further.  In  about  an  hour  it  will  have  swollen  con- 
siderably, and  have  burst  through  the  coating  of  flour  on  the  top ; 
then  pour  in  as  much  more  warm  liquid  as  will  convert  the  whole, 
with  good  kneadine,  and  this  should  not  be  spared,  into  a  Arm  dough, 
of  which  the  surface  should  be  entirely  fVee  from  lumps  or  crumbs. 
Throw  a  cloth  over,  and  let  it  remain  untU  it  has  risen  very  much  a 
second  time,  which  will  be  in  an  hour,  or  something  more,  if  the 
batch  be  large.  Then  work  it  lightly  up,  and  mould  it  into  loaves 
of  from  two  to  three  pounds  weight ;  send  them  directly  to  a  well 
heated  oven,  and  bake  them  from  an  hour  and  a  half  to  an  hour  and 
three-quarters. 

Plour,  ^  bushel ;  salt  (when  it  is  liked),  4  to  6  oz. ;  yeast,  1  pint 
unwashed,  or  i  pint  if  purified;  milk,  or  water,  2  quarts:  1  to  I4 
hour.    Additional  liquia  as  needed. 

Obs. — Brown  bread  can  be  made  exactly  as  above,  either  with  half 
meal  and  half  flour  mixed,  or  with  meal  only.  This  will  absorb 
more  moisture  than  fine  flour,  and  will  retain  it  rather  longer. 
Brown  bread  should  always  be  thorovghh/  baked. 

Remark, — We  have  seen  it  very  erroneously  asserted  in  one  or 
two  works,  that  bread  made  with  milk  speedily  becomes  sour.  This 
is  never  the  case  when  it  is  properly  baked  and  kept,  and  when  the 
milk  used  for  it  is  perfecUy  sioeet.  The  experience  of  many  years, 
enables  us  to  speak  positively  on  this  point. 


BORDYKE   BREAD. 

(Autkor's  Receipt,') 

I^Gx  with  a  gallon  of  flour  a  large  teaspoonful  of  fine  salt,  make  a 
bellow  in  the  centre,  and  pour  in  two  tablespoonsf\il  of  solid,  well  pu- 
rified yeast,  enidually  diluted  with  about  two  pints  and  a  half  of  milk, 
and  work  it  mto  a  thick  batter  with  the  surrounding  fiour,  strew  a 
thick  layer  over  and  leave  it  to  rise  from  an  hour  to  an  hour  and  a 
half;  then  knead  it  up  with  aeT  much  more  warm  skimmed  milk,  or 
half  new  milk  and  half  water,  as  will  render  it  quite  firm  and  smooth 
without  being  very  stifi*;  let  it  rise  another  hour,  and  divide  it  into 
three  loaves;  put  them  into  square  tins  slightly  buttered,  or  into 
round  baking  pans,  and  bake  them  about  an  hour  and  a  quarter  in  a 
well-heated  oven.  The  dough  can  be  formed  into  household  loaves 
if  preferred,  and  sent  to  the  oven  in  the  usual  way.  When  a  finer 
and  more  sfrangy  kind  of  bread  is  required  for  immeoiate  eating,  sub- 
stitute new  milk  for  skimmed,  dissolve  in  it  about  an  ounce  of  butter.

GERMAN   YEA6T. 

(And  Bread  made  with  German  Yeati^ 

This  has  very  generally  superseded  the  use  of  English  beo'-jest 
in  London,  and  other  places  conveniently  situated  for  reeavii^ 
quickly  and  regularly  the  supplies  of  it  which  are  imported  froci 
abroad ;  but  as  it  speedily  becomes  putrid  in  sultry  weather,  and  does 
not  in  any  season  remain  good  long  aiter  its  arrival  here,  it  is  im- 
suited  for  transmission  to  remote  parts  of  the  country.  Bread  noide 
with  it  while  it  is  perfectly  sweet,  is  extremely  light  and  good,  and  ^ 
answers  remarkably  well  for  light  cakes  and  biscuits.  An  onnee  ii 
the  proportion  which  we  have  always  had  used  for  a  quartern  (half 
a  gallon  or  three  pounds  and  a  half)  of  flour,  and  this,  with  the  ad- 
dition of  some  salt  and  nearly  a  quart  of  milk,  or  milk  and  water,  b» 
produced  excellent  bread  when  it  has  been  made  with  care.  Tbe 
yeast  should  be  very  gradually  and  perfectly  moistened  and  blenled 
with  the  warm  liqmd ;  for  unless  this  be  done,  and  the  whole  ren- 
dered smooth  as  cream,  the  dough  will  not  be  of  the  uniform  textsre 
which  it  ought,  but  will  be  full  of  large  hollow  spaces,  which  are 
never  seen  in  well-made  bread.  The  mass  should  be  mixed  npfinal; 
and  toeU  kneaded  at  once,  then  left  to  rise  for  about  an  hour ;  »ia 
kneaded  thoroughly,  and  again  left  to  rise  from  three-quarters  «  aa 
hour  to  an  hour ;  then  divided,  and  Kghtly  worked  up  into  loaves 
put  into  round  slightly  buttered  earthen  pans,  and  sent  immediafeJj 
to  the  oven.* 

A  leaven  may  be  first  laid  with  the  yeast,  and  part  of  the  liquid 
when  it  is  preferred,  as  directed  for  bread  made  with  becr-jeast,  but 
the  result  will  be  equally  good  if  the  whole  be  kneaded  up  at  oooe,  if 
it  be  made  quite  Jirm.

PROFESSOR  LIEBIG's  BAVARIAN  BROWN  BREAD. 

(^Very  nutritious  and  wholesome,) 

Baron  Liebig  pronounces  this  bread  to  be  very  superior  to  that 
rhich  is  made  'with  fine  flour  solely,  both  in  consequence  of  the 
;reater  amount  of  nutriment  which  it  contains,  and  from  its  slight 
nedicinal  effect,  which  renders  it  valuable  to  many  persons  accus- 
omed  to  have  frequent  recourse  to  drugs,  of  which  it  supersedes  the 
lecessity.  It  is  made  with  the  wheat  exactly  as  it  is  CTound,  no  part 
leing  subtracted,  nor  any  additional  flour  mingled  with  it.  He  directs 
hat  the  wheat  should  not  be  damped  before  it  is  prepared ;  btit  few 
oillers  can  be  found  who  will  depart  from  their  oranary  practice  to 
hlige  private  customers ;  and  this  determined  adherence  to  established 
isage  intervenes  constantly  between  us,  and  all  improvement  in  our 
Qoaes  of  preparing  food.  The  bread  is  made  in  the  usual  way,  with 
rater  only,  or  with  a  portion  of  milk  added  to  the  yeast,  as  taste  or 
onvenience  may  dictate.  The  loaves  should  be  well  baked  at  all 
tmcs ;  and  the  dough  should  of  course  be  perfectly  light  when  it  is 
(laced  in  the  oven.  Salt  should  be  mixed  with  the  meal  before  the 
east  is  added. 

ENGLISH   BROWN   BREAD. 

This  is  often  made  with  a  portion  only  of  the  unbolted  meal  re- 
ommended  in  the  preceding  receipt,  mixed  with  more  or  less  of  fine 
lour,  according  to  the  quality  of  bread  required ;  and  in  many  fami- 
ies  the  coarse  bran  is  always  sifted  from  the  meal,  as  an  impression 
xists  that  it  is  irritating  to  the  stomach.  If  one  gallon  of  meal  as  it 
omes  from  the  mill,  be  well  mixed  with  an  equal  measure  of  flour, 
nd  made  into  a  dough  in  the  manner  directed  for  white  household 
•read,  the  loaves  will  still  be  sufficiently  brown  for  the  general  taste 
1  this  country,  and  they  will  be  good  and  wholesome,  though  not, 
erhaps,  so  entirely  easy  of  digestion  as  Baron  Liebig*s  Bavarian 
read« 

UNFERMENTED   BREAD. 

This  bread,  in  which  carbonate  of  soda  and  muriatic  acid  are  sub- 
tituted  for  yeast  or  other  leaven,  has  within  these  few  years  been 
ighly  recommended,  and  much  eaten.  It  may  possibly  suit  many 
ersons  better  than  that  which  is  fermented  in  the  usual  way,  but  it 
I  not  in  general  by  any  means  so  pleasant  in  flavour ;  and  there  is 
luch  more  chance  of  failure  in  preparing  it  in  private  families,  as  it 
Hmlres  some  skill  to  mix  the  ingredients  with  exactness  and  despatch; 
nd  it  is  absolutely  necessaiy  that  the  dough  should  be  set  into  the 
ven  the  instant  it  is  ready.    In  some  hy£*opathic  and  other  large
